author: Chris Marti
summary: Chris discovers his mysterious 'winking out' experiences are Theravada fruitions following stream entry and begins deliberately cycling through the insight stages, cataloguing third-eye energetics and each cessation's shifting aftereffects.
interesting_sentence: "They used to be Big Events, then speed bumps, now they're kind of like running over a garden hose with my car." —cmarti
helpful_sentence: "Note to self: you can overshoot a cessation. You can be concentrating too hard and in the wrong "way." Hold things lightly. There is no need to bear down. Let the breath breathe itself!" —cmarti
who_should_read: New stream-enterers wanting a granular, first-person account of recognizing fruition/cessation and the pitfalls of over-efforting past it.

author: Chris Marti
summary: Chris moves into second path, reporting intense dissolution, hyperactive lucid sleep, on-demand jhanas and self-induced 'tickle' cessations, while Kenneth coaches him through free-fall integration and probes for anagami markers.
interesting_sentence: "You'll just keep falling and you'll never hit the ground because there's no ground." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "If there is a trick to integration, it's to avoid drawing conclusions." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners entering second-path territory wanting to understand jhana access, nirodha samapatti, and the 'trying too hard' trap.

author: Chris Marti
summary: Chris settles into anagami/3rd-path territory - centerless 'big' perception, loss of the self as reference point, The Witness practice and NS in daily life - while discussing Tozan's ranks and guilt over jhanic bliss.
interesting_sentence: "Now there's a tiny little "me" rattling around in an enormous field of experience." —cmarti
helpful_sentence: "ALL experiences -- here and there, up and down, in and out, mundane or extraordinary -- are fundamentally the same. How we deal with, or relate to, experiences is the only thing that changes." —Gozen
who_should_read: Post-second-path yogis approaching 3rd path who want a candid map of centerless perception, jhana mastery, and integrating radical shifts into ordinary life.

author: Chris Marti
summary: After a 'tectonic shift' he calls bigger than stream entry, Chris drops concentration/vipassana for Kenneth's 'watching the dummy' practice (locus of awareness behind and above the head), accessing non-dual 'is,' spontaneous emotional surges, and a heart-driven letting-go practice amid a family crisis.
interesting_sentence: "It's like there's a dial somewhere in hyperspace that has settings on it marked "angry, sad, happy" and so on, and someone, not me, is turning the dial and thus altering my moods." —cmarti
helpful_sentence: "There is, then, an intellectual component of not self that arises, usually as a mental image for me, and a felt, in-the-bones sense of not self, which is the absence of any conscious or unconscious assumptions of a static, permanent me." —cmarti
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry practitioners exploring non-dual awareness and identity who want to see how vipassana gives way to a heart-centered, surrender-based practice.

author: Chris Marti
summary: Chris works on 'flow' and radical non-attachment (even to awareness and the unconditioned), weathers a Dark Night relapse and heavy work travel, and closes the thread describing a May 6th airplane 'click' in which seeking ceased, the 'conveyor belt' shut off, and awareness became permanently fused with perception.
interesting_sentence: "So somehow seeking is that which obscures finding." —cmarti
helpful_sentence: "When you believe you have uncovered it all, objectified all the stuff... then it's time to look a lot deeper and a lot harder." —cmarti
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners near the 'last mile' who want a first-person account of how relentless seeking gives way to effortless awareness at the fourth-path transition.

author: Chris Marti
summary: In extended post-fourth-path integration, Chris reports his career-bound sense of self has collapsed (the insight 'conveyor belt' is off), wrestles with losing his work 'edge,' warns that one can 'hide from life using awakening,' supports a loved one through recurring depression, and argues awakening is about re-entering ordinary human life rather than chasing states.
interesting_sentence: "You can hide from your life using your own awakening." —cmarti
helpful_sentence: "All the deepest jhanas and all the wildest experiences put together are still not awakening or enlightenment. They're just deep jhanas and wild experiences. They're states. Awakening is not a state." —cmarti
who_should_read: Anyone at or past fourth path (or curious what lies beyond it) who wants a grounded look at integrating awakening into career, marriage, and family instead of escaping into states.

author: ClaytonL
summary: Recovery-focused practitioner reflects on radical, non-dual acceptance while doing service work at AA meetings, briefly experiments with astral-plane trance work, and reports that dharma insight held steady through a month of 12-hour oil-spill shifts with almost no formal sitting.
interesting_sentence: "The delusion that I can transcend myself and these worldy circumstances was smashed." —ClaytonL
helpful_sentence: "Understand that your ability to control these unacceptable things is limited in a sense as well. Work clear eyed within these limitations." —ClaytonL
who_should_read: Practitioners in 12-step recovery who want to see dharma practice integrated with AA service work and radical acceptance teachings.

author: Dadriance
summary: Recaps a 3-day Goenka course where shifting from scanning to noting deepened insight, recurring pelvic-to-spine shaking intensified, a bleak disgust-laden dip hit on day two, and a sudden vertigo-inducing recognition of "the lunacy of the illusion of self" arose on day three despite fighting a head cold throughout.
interesting_sentence: "As I was walking back to my room from a sitting, these words suddenly popped into my head: 'the lunacy of the illusion of self'." —Dadriance
helpful_sentence: "I understand now why some people have suggested that scanning may reduce the ability to associate physical sensations with emotions, thoughts, feelings - noting seems to make a more direct linkage and that leads to insights about the same." —Dadriance
who_should_read: Goenka retreatants curious how switching from scanning to noting shifts insight, and how illness during intensives gets framed via sankaras.

author: Yadid
summary: Recounts a 7-day retreat with Christopher Titmuss in Israel during which the author cycles from A&P (with a vibratory headache and a 'WOW! life force' sensation) through Dark Night into deepening Equanimity by day five, followed by a post-retreat reflection on anger and shame as layered suffering.
interesting_sentence: "its funny how A&P can have variations but it always includes a WOW moment, and it always fades into the dark night." —Yadid
helpful_sentence: "knowing them is great for motivation, but one has to be extremely clear and trusting the process that the insights and progress ARISE ON THEIR OWN TIME, so all one has to do is just practice well and leave all else to trust, because thinking that you can somehow facilitate progress simply adds more suffering into the mix." —Yadid
who_should_read: Practitioners returning to sitting after a break who want a concise account of cycling through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity on a short retreat.

author: chuanose
summary: An eleven-month beginner-to-stream-entry journal tracking daily vipassana and jhana sits marked by escalating head/face pulsation and pressure sensations, culminating in Kenneth Folk confirming stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "I suddenly had a vision of an arm moving towards me to grab my right hand, and i feel fear and energy shooting up my head" —chuanose
helpful_sentence: "Be a tortoise, not a hare. Constancy, not heroics." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Complete beginners wanting a highly granular, encouraging model of how daily noting and jhana practice can lead to confirmed stream entry within about a year.

log: A Zen Meditator Crosses the Arising & Passing Away
author: notbrian
key_words: Doing It vs Getting It Done podcast
summary: Recounts an A&P breakthrough triggered by a 'vaster observer' technique from the Doing It vs. Getting It Done podcast, followed by dukkha nanas and a formless, bodiless meditative state the author explicitly does not identify as fruition.
interesting_sentence: "It felt like the crown of my head opened up and the universe flowed into it, melting my concept of self-and-other." —notbrian
helpful_sentence: "I then decided to try out the technique described in the above-mentioned podcast, where you invite a vaster observer to look at your achieved state of meditation." —notbrian
who_should_read: Zen practitioners moving toward insight-map practices who want a first-person account of an A&P event and the dukkha nanas that followed.

author: RevElev
summary: RevElev logs roughly a year of daily 45-90 minute noting/samatha sits tracking recurring third-eye pressure, energy surges, and eye-crossing as he cycles through A&P, dark night, and equanimity before concluding he attained stream entry and then continues cycling toward second path.
interesting_sentence: "Feel like someone crapped in my skull, then beat me with a hose...and not in a good way!" —RevElev
helpful_sentence: "Trust that the noting is doing its work, untangling the misperceptions one tiny knot at a time." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Beginning noting practitioners curious about the granular, day-by-day felt sense of A&P, dark night, and stream entry.

author: Chris_TK
summary: Post-stream-entry practitioner logs out-loud noting sits marked by facial squeezing, full-body kundalini shaking, a night of intense fear/disgust, and a persistent burning-coolness sensation up the spine, while querying kennethfolk about distinguishing review fruitions from a new insight cycle.
interesting_sentence: "In the middle of the night I woke up with feelings of INTENSE fear, misery and disgust, emphasis on disgust." —Chris_TK
helpful_sentence: "Yes, at any given time your are either in review or the new cycle. In the new cycle, you just ride the arc up to your cutting edge and back down." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry yogis wanting a concrete example of distinguishing review-phase fruitions from a fresh insight cycle, explained directly by a teacher.

author: AaronS01
summary: AaronS01 recounts a 10-day IMS Samadhi retreat with Richard Shankman where he reached the first two jhanas under daily guidance, then describes a subsequent month-and-a-half practice break followed by aversion, anger, and uncontrollable eye-rolling piti energy on returning to his cushion, which he suspects is the dark night.
interesting_sentence: "It feels ragged and prickly and electric, like someone has stuck a wire into my veins." —AaronS01
helpful_sentence: "But having problems making a distinction between attention naturally going to what I'm noting, or if I'm actually noting a split second before my attention moves on." —AaronS01
who_should_read: Practitioners transitioning from an intensive jhana retreat back to solo practice who want to recognize early dark-night aversion and uncontrolled piti.

author: Chris_TK
summary: Over several months of anapana-into-noting sits, kasina work, and qi gong energy practices, the author cycles through A&P-like pulsing, harsh Re-Observation vibrations, and dark-night anxiety before a sit in which his head 'un-caps' and thoughts lose their weight, which he concludes was stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "...just that same action of my body snapping to attention. It seemed like I was reacting to something, except the only thing I can say is that it wasn't an experience -- I want to say a non-experience..." —Chris_TK
helpful_sentence: "I would stick with noting as a main practice until you get path. It's the powerhouse." —mumuwu
who_should_read: Yogis cycling toward stream entry who want a detailed, energy-phenomena-heavy account of the run-up to a first cessation.

author: Ciocoiu
summary: Ciocoiu keeps a long, day-by-day samatha log of breath-counting sits tracking chills, piti, and drifting concentration, experimenting with kasina discs and noting, without ever claiming a formal insight attainment.
interesting_sentence: "So its been several months I've stopped. Suppose I did not see much progress, lost motivation, dissapointed, doubtful, etc." —Ciocoiu
helpful_sentence: "If the eyes become a distraction, make them the object. There are no hindrances or distractions, only objects." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners wanting a long-form, disciplined samatha/jhana log heavy on piti tracking and kasina experimentation rather than insight-stage drama.

author: abideasthat
summary: abideasthat describes a twice-daily pranayama-mantra-silent-abidance routine destabilized by a grueling physical job, and debates with forum members whether to add noting/vipassana to his concentration-based 'I AM' practice.
interesting_sentence: "The stick you're stirring the fire with, the I, is burning away as we speak which is why things utterly suck right now, you've made progress and are now in, what we generally call, Dark Night." —TommyMcNally
helpful_sentence: "One methodological criterion for having achieved access consciousness is simply that you come to a place where it becomes easier and more natural than usual to stay with your meditation object." —TommyMcNally
who_should_read: Concentration/neo-advaita practitioners burning out under life stress who want to see forum members make the case for adding noting practice.

author: anaj
summary: anaj asks how to recognize and develop access concentration after five months of daily shamatha-vipassana practice, and receives detailed technique advice on breath focus, counting, and kasina objects from several forum members.
interesting_sentence: "For me, I feel a 'ballooning' in my head and a 'whoosh' as my field of focus tightens substantially." —tomotvos
helpful_sentence: "Instead of feeling like you are waging a constant battle against mind wandering to keep coming back to the object, it may feel as though you can stay with your object for longer durations and with less effort." —brianm2
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate meditators struggling to identify or stabilize access concentration who want concrete breath-focus and kasina tips.

author: lbcongo
summary: lbcongo, working with a private teacher and preparing for his first Goenka retreat, logs near-daily samatha-into-noting sits detailing twitching releases, absorption states, and several brief 'blank out' moments he suspects but never confirms as fruitions.
interesting_sentence: "All sensations went 'blank' for a brief instant. I don't think this could have been a fruition because I didn't feel any paradigm shift afterward." —lbcongo
helpful_sentence: "When doing vipassana/noting practice you note any sensation that you recognize in the current moment... If it happens to be the rising of your breath, then fine, note 'rising', if you itch note 'itching', etc." —Aquanin
who_should_read: Yogis working with a personal teacher toward first path who want a granular, week-by-week account of absorption, noting, and ambiguous cessation-like events.

log: Adam's Mindfulness in Daily Life journal
author: JAdamG
summary: Tracks a practitioner's efforts to extend noting into daily activities such as medication-taking, waiting in lines, showering, and meals, using post-it note reminders and Kenneth Folk's 'disembedding' framework to build off-cushion mindfulness.
interesting_sentence: "I realized what Kenneth meant when he said that 'if you disembed from something, it isn't you.'" —JAdamG
helpful_sentence: "Staying mindful and note on all this with a real light gentle touch." —telecaster
who_should_read: Practitioners who sit well formally but struggle to extend noting into daily life and want concrete environmental triggers and reminders.

log: Alan's [enter catchy title here] Meditation Notes
author: AlanSmithe
summary: AlanSmithe logs near-daily 1.5-4 hour sits combining anapanasati, intensive body scanning, and passenger-style noting, describing a clear A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity cycle at a 10-day Goenka retreat and ongoing attempts to reach Equanimity in daily practice while experimenting with formless-realm vipassana jhanas.
interesting_sentence: "It occurred to me today that if folks who have achieved path can enter any nana at any point easily, then they can induce Equanimity at will, which would be a terrific thing to do when stressed out, etc." —AlanSmithe
helpful_sentence: "When things get chilled out in equanimity, especially when transitioning from groser vibrations or unpleasant sensations, there is a relief-like sensations and general chill-out." —Seekr
who_should_read: Self-guided vipassana practitioners wanting a granular, technique-by-technique log of cycling through A&P, dark night, and equanimity outside of retreat.

author: APrioriKreuz
summary: Blends Tibetan Ngondro (refuge, bodhicitta, mandala offering, purification, guru yoga) with Kenneth Folk-style noting, 'paying the toll,' and self-inquiry into the 'I,' reporting progressive surrender, dissolving tension, and an equanimous response to a real-life car mirror collision.
interesting_sentence: "A year ago that would've been enough to get really angry, yell at the guy, and get into an argument with the person. This time, nothing like that happened." —APrioriKreuz
helpful_sentence: "The trick: ask myself \"why is this unpleasant?\" and then acknowledge that pleasant, unpleasant and neutral are arbitrary beliefs." —APrioriKreuz
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to blending Vajrayana devotional practice with MCTB-style noting and self-inquiry will find a candid, technique-rich cross-tradition experiment.

author: APrioriKreuz
summary: A Vajrayana practitioner recounts childhood glimpses of emptiness, recovery from addiction, a year of intense jhana practice with physical pain, a brief dark night, and growing equanimity experienced as an automatic witnessing 'third gear.'
interesting_sentence: "The movie Inception shows this very well: a sign/idea can have healthy or harmful consequences: an idea can be planted in the mind and this idea grows (cause and effect at work)." —APrioriKreuz
helpful_sentence: "I confirmed that every single jhana, Ã±ana, insight, etc. can be used again as self: delusion." —APrioriKreuz
who_should_read: Vajrayana practitioners curious how tantric ritual, mantra, and visualization map onto the A&P-dark night-equanimity insight cycle will find this account illuminating.

log: Altitude's Practice Journal Prior to Month-long Retreat
author: altitude
summary: Altitude, a working householder with roughly eight years of retreat experience mostly at Spirit Rock, prepares for an upcoming month-long retreat with John Travis by describing a Mahasi/Pandita-inspired practice of tracking rapid arising-and-passing in the body done largely without explicit noting.
interesting_sentence: "It often seems that some residual and specific momentum from the last retreat carries over into the current retreat... and the nature of the momentum seems unpredictable but somehow related to the previous retreat." —altitude
helpful_sentence: "Also, be very clear about what practices you are doing before you go and commit yourself to just them." —richardweeden
who_should_read: Experienced householder yogis preparing for a long retreat who want a peer account of pre-retreat practice calibration and teacher selection.

author: Seekr
summary: A months-long series of kasina-plus-verbal-noting sits documenting shifting noting speeds, recurring throat/chest heaviness and pressure-on-head sensations, and cycling between striving, doubt, misery and brief patches of calm without any reported cessation.
interesting_sentence: "Oh M.A.N! Buzzing vibratory, chest heaviness hell! Didn't know I can get so intensely through this territory." —Seekr
helpful_sentence: "Kasina seems useful to tune down the mental scenario thought chatter." —Seekr
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about pairing kasina concentration with verbal noting who want an unvarnished look at cycling between striving, doubt and fleeting calm.

author: AndyW45
summary: Tracks a shift from vipassana into Adyashanti-style non-meditation and Mahamudra noting, detailed jhana-arc mapping (1st through 8th) under Kenneth Folk's guidance, retreat reports from Gaia House and Cittaviveka, and a Dark Night/A&P cycle that Kenneth Folk ultimately confirms as 2nd Path.
interesting_sentence: "I experienced a very brief landslide of joy that was so overwhelming I actually had to stop the practice." —AndyW45
helpful_sentence: "You name whatever arises, just like vipassana, then say 'I welcome you.' For example, 'anxiety... I welcome you,' 'heaviness... I welcome you,' 'doubt... I welcome you.'" —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry practitioners wanting a detailed model of jhana-arc mapping and a real teacher-confirmed path review process.

author: andymr
summary: A software engineer's multi-year, extremely detailed noting journal (formal sits, walking, and driving-while-noting) that tracks jhana experimentation with candle-flame kasina, repeated cessation/fruition events, confirmed stream entry and second path, a tentatively claimed third path, and a later burnout with depression before a renewed equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "I realized I was noting the tornado siren. Grabbed my wallet and phone and got my butt downstairs into the basement." —andymr
helpful_sentence: "Get used to the sensations, visual impressions, and mental impressions, that go with each jhana. Together, these form the 'handle' for the jhana. You can get back to any jhana by simply recalling the handle." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners who want a granular, years-long noting log illustrating jhana handles, repeated fruition/cessation phenomenology, and the emotional grind (doubt, depression, burnout) that can follow multiple path claims.

author: AndyW
summary: A post-2nd-path yogi alternates outloud Mahasi noting with Adyashanti-style doing-nothing, tracking a slump in daily practice and exploring the interplay of effort, surrender, and aversion.
interesting_sentence: "Then the thought arose: why does there need to be an observer?" —AndyW
helpful_sentence: "If you spend your time doing nothing by training, you never enter the rest/recovery phase, and performance suffers." —Andy
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry yogis wrestling with motivation dips will appreciate this candid account of balancing striving with surrender.

author: ClaytonL
summary: A 21-year-old practitioner logs a year of scanning-and-noting sits, confirms a likely stream entry with Kenneth Folk, has a probable second-path fruition during a 10-day Goenka retreat in Malaysia complete with a terrifying demon dream, and moves on to mastering the jhanic arc, witness dwelling, and Pure Land jhanas while staying sober in a 12-step program.
interesting_sentence: "I see a cloaked figure and I think 'wow so thats what a demon looks like' Immediately I Feel geyser of cosmic crud, strongest ever, strongest fear every, and my body being ripped apart." —ClaytonL
helpful_sentence: "Track your progress up and down the arc in each sitting, paying careful attention to the highest state you attain before you start back down the arc." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners moving past stream entry who want a long-form, candid account of Goenka-style noting combined with witness practice and jhana work, including how recovery from addiction intersects with the path.

author: JAdamG
summary: JAdamG restarts daily practice, cycling through letting-go jhana attempts and noting sessions that stall around the Misery/Dissolution nanas, with forum advice pushing him from effortful control toward gentle noting.
interesting_sentence: "The trap of \"maybe if I surrender enough, I'll stop suffering\" (bargaining) gets me every time, even though I know it's coming." —JAdamG
helpful_sentence: "The experiences you need to have to make progress will arise on their own. You don't need to do ANYTHING except be there (i.e., gently note) for them." —betawave
who_should_read: Yogis who over-control their jhana practice and want to see how excessive striving can be reframed as gentle noting.

author: Antero.
summary: Riding the jhanic arc and 'the Wheel' between formal sits and a solo retreat, Antero reports fruitions consistent with 2nd path, repeated Nirodha Samapatti cessations, Pure Land jhana exploration, and an emergent centerless Mahamudra/3rd-gear awareness he provisionally maps to 3rd path.
interesting_sentence: "There was a point of light at the end of the funnel, a blip and a strange trembling sensation as some kind of shock wave went through me." —Antero.
helpful_sentence: "The experience of lights, energy, and shaking you experienced during sleep (post 13) was very likely an A&P event rather than a Path moment." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring post-path jhana work, Nirodha Samapatti, and non-dual 'gears' practice under close teacher guidance will find a detailed technical account here.

author: Antero.
summary: Continuing well past his claimed 4th path, Antero describes stabilizing seamless no-self and effortless non-conceptual awareness through Rigpa/Dzogchen and Mahamudra pointers, while cataloguing residual throat/head vibrations, energetic sensing of other yogis' states, and increasingly vivid lucid dreaming.
interesting_sentence: "no-self is an undeniable empirical fact, but for some reason the experience may still be almost-no-self." —Antero.
helpful_sentence: "Once you are sure you understand your teacher's instructions, do exactly as he says. Do not try to improve the technique or tweak it in any way." —Antero.
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners past 4th path curious about post-attainment refinement, energetic vibration phenomena, and Dzogchen/Mahamudra pointers for resting in non-conceptual awareness.

author: Antero.
summary: A beginner methodically logs daily noting and asana practice while mapping the jhanic arc (1st-8th jhana) onto the nanas, adopts Kenneth Folk's Witness and eye-flickering techniques amid intensifying kundalini-type energy, and reports an unconfirmed 'Event' on 25 July followed by two ambiguous fruition-like blackouts.
interesting_sentence: "I can imagine that computer could feel like that after booting the CPU." —Antero.
helpful_sentence: "Every moment of noting is a moment of freedom." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Meditators who want a granular, day-by-day account of correlating jhanas with Progress-of-Insight nanas alongside kundalini phenomena and Kenneth Folk's Witness/eye-flickering method.

author: Antero.
summary: Antero traces his post-4th-path development through techniques like Direct Mode, binary noting of silence/noise, and Journalistic Self Inquiry, reporting recurring energetic phenomena, a progressively fading sense of self, and growing equanimity with his wife, kids, and coworkers.
interesting_sentence: "Could someone please tell me how can there potentially be any suffering when I can clearly see that there is no one present to suffer?" —Antero.
helpful_sentence: "The best advice that I have ever been given is: 'Note everything' and it does not change through the paths." —Antero.
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring life after 4th path who want a granular, technique-rich account of post-path 'stages' (Direct Mode, binary noting, Journalistic Self Inquiry) and their effects on daily emotional and family life.

author: Antero.
summary: Antero practices Tibetan Tummo with chakra-point light visualization and breath retention up to 150 seconds, reporting bright vibratory void states resembling 5th jhana before shortening retentions due to head tension and adverse after-effects.
interesting_sentence: "During the breath retention and simultaneous visualization of light at the chakra point, the body dissolves into a cloud of bright vibrations and this is mingled with the feeling of being suspended in a dark void." —Antero.
helpful_sentence: "Now I have actually shortened the retentions, because of some adverse effects of tension in the head and unpleasant vibrations later on during the day." —Antero.
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about physical Tummo/chakra breath-retention practice and its kundalini-like energetic side effects.

author: Antero.
summary: Antero, well past his earlier path attainments, describes continuous non-dual emptiness practice where formal and informal meditation merge, powerful spontaneous raptures during sitting, and gives detailed instruction on using ashtanga yoga's bandhas and drishti gazing points as concentration practice.
interesting_sentence: "I do not practice anymore and there is not a moment when I don't." —Antero.
helpful_sentence: "Drstis enable one to create a bubble of concentration regardless where one practices and controlling the sight controls the mind." —Antero.
who_should_read: Advanced, post-path practitioners interested in non-dual integration of practice into daily life, and yogis wanting to use ashtanga drishti/bandha work as a concentration technique.

author: Antero
summary: Candidly catalogs six 'mistakes' from a period of practice decline (over-conceptualizing emptiness, forcing stillness, dissociation) then details somatic bodywork inspired by Reggie Ray's full-body pranayama and Dzogchen Trekchod instruction attributed to Tsoknyi Rinpoche.
interesting_sentence: "in one instance of disorientation I could not even access my own memories which left me feeling totally disconnected from my own beingness." —Antero
helpful_sentence: "Creating something artificial is always moving away from the natural state." —Antero
who_should_read: Post-fourth-path meditators who felt ungrounded or dissociated and want to see how somatic bodywork and Dzogchen Trekchod instruction restored embodiment.

author: AnthonyYeshe
summary: AnthonyYeshe alternates between noting, self-inquiry, and Kenneth Folk's three-gear surrender practice while taking up Tummo breath retention and Tsa Lung, cycling through equanimity, dark-night-ish tension, and a months-long spontaneous baseline shift marked by charisma, sharpened intellect, and effortless positive decisions.
interesting_sentence: "During 3rd path I was so obsessed with them but right after 4th path I kinda forgot about them." —AnthonyYeshe
helpful_sentence: "1st Gear practices objectify, 2nd Gear practices inquire, and 3rd Gear practices surrender." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring Kenneth Folk's three-gear surrender practice or combining insight meditation with Tummo/energy work will find a candid, detailed account of baseline shifts and post-path cycling.

author: AnthonyYeshe
summary: A Kinesiology grad chronicles intensive Mahasi-style noting practice, moving from a Goenka retreat A&P through repeated dark-night cycling into a vivid 'made of sand' A&P confirmed by Kenneth Folk, culminating in a diagonal 'vision sliced in half' pop experience during a two-week solitary cabin retreat in Colorado that he suspects was a fruition.
interesting_sentence: "The very next moment I realized I was still walking and I felt like I was three feet taller and all of a sudden my vision sliced in half diagonally." —AnthonyYeshe
helpful_sentence: "Remember, the A&P can be spectacular, while stream entry is an anti-climax." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners cycling through post-A&P dukkha nanas who want a candid, blow-by-blow noting log paired with Kenneth Folk's live nana-by-nana corrections.

author: AnthonyYeshe
summary: A KFD practitioner cycles through kasina concentration, out-loud noting, Mahamudra Noting, and self-inquiry questioning over roughly a year, culminating in a Cooper retreat breakthrough where a chest-centered anxiety wave gives way to a lasting dissolution of self-contraction that he tentatively calls 4th path.
interesting_sentence: "Something's missing! What was it?" —AnthonyYeshe
helpful_sentence: "Resistance is exactly what is between you and awakeness at any moment." —Antero.
who_should_read: Practitioners curious how kasina concentration, noting, and Mahamudra Noting combine over a long arc culminating in a retreat-triggered path shift.

author: postreptilian
summary: postreptilian, a stream-enterer since April 2009, journals sporadically about noting practice with Kenneth Folk's 'wheel' technique, his difficulty sharing about his own practice, and his discovery that subtle anger and 'disabling attitudes' are a recurring blind spot behind his dark-night cycling.
interesting_sentence: "It's helping me understand what Dan Ingram means when he says that his practice was oftentimes very anger-powered." —postreptilian
helpful_sentence: "if you're resisting it, it must be moving" —postreptilian
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry practitioners wrestling with self-doubt about their attainment who want an example of using resolutions to call up and review path fruitions.

log: Arran Retreat Reports: April 2011 - Audio Trancripts
author: TommyMcNally
summary: Audio-transcribed notes from a solo three-day retreat on the Isle of Arran describe rising through jhanas to roughly the eighth, a detailed mapping of the dukkha ñanas onto specific body locations, a review confirming 1st and 2nd path fruitions, and a severe, distinctive fruition he suspects but doubts is 3rd path.
interesting_sentence: "we're in the no-dog and even here there's just pure subjectivity with no centre point to it" —TommyMcNally
helpful_sentence: "the more you pay attention to them, the longer they'll seem to last when, really, the only thing which keeps them going is your own consciousness of them" —TommyMcNally
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in fine-grained phenomenology of the ñanas and jhanas will find a vivid, body-mapped account of a short solo retreat useful.

log: Attaining Fruition After Attaining to 2nd Path
author: foolbutnotforlong
summary: After months of migraine-like headaches, deep jhana practice up through Pure Land Jhana 1, and an all-dry vipassana push that culminated in a four-pop fruition, the author (Jorge, aka foolbutnotforlong) claims second path and opens a month-long review period to study fruition via resolutions and Kenneth Folk's eye-flick technique.
interesting_sentence: "My depression continued however, until one day when I said to myself "enough! this is it! I'm done living this life!" —foolbutnotforlong
helpful_sentence: "Try it. Resolve to experience it and let go of the intention and keep meditating." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Practitioners near or past second path who want a candid account of post-attainment review practices, fruition-recall resolutions, and the eye-flick technique.

log: Back from retreat with 2nd path attained
author: Sugato
summary: Recounts a 7-day monastery retreat where the author cycled from confusion through A&P, dark night, and equanimity into a cessation event confirming second path, followed by questions on deliberately inducing fruitions.
interesting_sentence: "The first four days was like heaven and heeeellllll." —Sugato
helpful_sentence: "When you next sit, make the resolution to experience entry into a cessation moment through one of the doors." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Post-first-path practitioners wanting a first-person account of powering through the dukkha ñanas on retreat to reach a second-path cessation and learning to induce fruitions afterward.

author: Bill H
summary: Logs an extended dark-night period marked by intense physical pain and suspected PTSD activation during noting, experimenting with Mahasi and choiceless noting, TWIM, metta, and somatic experiencing therapy to stabilize practice without wrecking his job and marriage.
interesting_sentence: "I have found some small but very painful spots in my neck that have been 3/4 cleared out now from awareness - some really odd emotions and memories tied to those spots in my neck." —Bill H
helpful_sentence: "Maybe it only feels ineffective because you are in reob?  That's what it does to you. It seems like you are resisting the 'bad states' and only going for the good ones. Watch that resistance. It is the key." —Russell
who_should_read: Meditators dealing with trauma-linked physical pain during noting who want concrete stabilization techniques (TWIM, somatic work, essence noting) rather than pure vipassana grinding.

author: Bill29ish
summary: Describes a multi-year deepening practice involving somatic meditation, earth breathing, and a self-devised pitch-dark-room practice, recounting a dramatic 2012 experience of the sense of a separate observer vanishing while walking the dog, followed by ongoing thinning of self-referential emotion.
interesting_sentence: "It would be interesting to see how it effects someone at third path." —Bill29ish
helpful_sentence: "It's basically formless practice, no technique practice, done in the absolute dark." —Bill29ish
who_should_read: Long-term practitioners exploring Vajrayana and somatic approaches (tonglen, earth breathing, darkness retreats) who want an account of gradual self-dissolution outside the standard noting-based path model.

author: WF566163
summary: Chronicles a body-centered 'grounding' and binary-noting practice that cycles through A&P-like and Dark-Night-like states before culminating in a sustained loss of the sense of a separate observer and eventual abandonment of formal sitting.
interesting_sentence: "I dont know anything & neither do you" —WF566163
helpful_sentence: "Instead of riding the mind states, BE the mind states" —Antero.
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in a body-based 'Direct Mode' grounding approach and detailed accounts of self-referencing dissolving beyond stream entry.

author: WF566163
summary: Chronicles daily fruitions and cycling through review-phase Dark Night and Equanimity a month after stream entry, alternating Mahamudra noting with Metta practice under Vince Horn's guidance.
interesting_sentence: "...this incredible weightlessness as though all the air had been sucked out of the sense of \"me\"." —WF566163
helpful_sentence: "I had always thought of \"stillness\" as being something outside of myself to acquire and tonight while I sitting I could see clearly that stillness was an inherent part of my experience when I can just be with my experience, in other words, not something separate from me that I have to get, but something that is already present in experience." —WF566163
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry meditators wanting concrete, blow-by-blow descriptions of review-phase cycling between Dark Night and Equanimity with recurring fruitions.

author: boeuf
summary: Boeuf logs months of kasina, pranayama, and noting-aloud sessions featuring vibration, fear, and A&P-like phenomena, witness/turiya discussions with the KFD community, and recurring uncertainty about whether his calm, spacious states qualify as equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "Then a distinct feeling that \"something\" was behind me--which was genuinely creepy to me and while I could investigate it, I could only bear to check out the edges of that one on a sensate level." —boeuf
helpful_sentence: "Whenever you have a question, reduce it to its simplest possible form and post it on this thread in its own little box, all by itself." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Meditators uncertain whether they've reached equanimity who want a detailed, month-by-month record of kasina, noting, and witness-practice phenomenology to compare against.

author: brian.ananda
summary: Brian Ananda posts a brief note redirecting readers from this forum thread to an external WordPress blog for his ongoing practice journal.
interesting_sentence: "Kenneth & I both found it a little difficult to read at times." —brian.ananda
helpful_sentence: "So...I made a simple wordpress blog for my Journal here: ananda2010.wordpress.com" —brian.ananda
who_should_read: Readers looking for Brian Ananda's ongoing journal should follow the linked external blog rather than this forum thread, which contains no practice content.

author: brianm2
summary: A dated, day-by-day log tracking a shift from kasina/vipassana practice into intensive 'who am I'-style self-enquiry and cittanupassana aimed at directly locating the witness, ending with Kenneth Folk cautioning against over-intellectualizing the process.
interesting_sentence: "It felt kind of funny in a surreal way, like someone devoting hours each day to looking for their own nose." —brianm2
helpful_sentence: "Your insistence upon understanding the territory you hope someday to visit is keeping you from seeing what is in front of you." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to 2nd-gear self-enquiry and witness investigation who want a philosophically rigorous, granular day-by-day account.

author: notbrian
summary: notbrian reports crossing into second path via noting practice combined with Zen mu-koan work, describing a cessation marked by a cracking sound, a 'WHOOSH,' an electrical jolt, and waves of cool bliss, followed by resolution of a lingering dark night.
interesting_sentence: "I heard some kind of noise. I am still not sure what it was--maybe a subtle crack of something in the room or outside and then WHOOSH." —notbrian
helpful_sentence: "He gave me a technique that he called mahamudra noting that helped me deepen my experience of serenity." —notbrian
who_should_read: Practitioners curious how a cessation-based path attainment can align with Zen koan practice alongside a noting-based path model.

author: BrunoLoff
summary: After 'brain frying' from an intensive 4hr/day regime forces a practice pause marked by head pain and motor-coordination lapses, Bruno recovers via swimming, walking and Chi Kung before resuming vipassana, reporting PCEs, multiple fruitions, chakra-centered energy shifts, and a tentative third-path claim under Kenneth Folk's guidance.
interesting_sentence: "*blip blip*, my eyes were opened it was really weird and then the characteristic bliss and relief." —BrunoLoff
helpful_sentence: "Attention fatigue is the neurological equivalent of pushing your muscles too hard and never giving them a chance to recover." —JAdamG
who_should_read: Practitioners dealing with meditation-induced burnout or 'brain frying' who want a concrete recovery protocol blending rest, movement and gradual practice resumption.

author: chrispapa
summary: A near-daily, months-long noting journal detailing four-level labelling practice, recurring energetic 'avalanches between the ears,' a persistent 20-minute concentration 'wall,' and gradual deepening under Kenneth Folk's guidance including a private jhana tour.
interesting_sentence: "It's supposed to be liberating (as in relief), but right now it feels like being slowly flayed alive." —chrispapa
helpful_sentence: "Any essential element will be common to all awakened people. That means there is NO single theory or thought that is essential to awakening. The only essential thing is the practice." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: New noting practitioners who want a granular, week-by-week account of building concentration and noting technique under a teacher's feedback.

author: chuanose
summary: A post-stream-entry practitioner tracks recurring jhana/nana cycles, fruitions, and forehead/ear energetic phenomena across sporadic sits, eventually having a sit that Kenneth Folk judges to be second path.
interesting_sentence: "Wonder if this is just a big a&p or could it be 2nd path?" —chuanose
helpful_sentence: "As Kenneth said: As you walk closer to the horizon it still seems equally far away. (scrambled quote)" —PEJN
who_should_read: Practitioners past stream entry who want a candid, intermittent account of jhana cycling and arupa jhana exploration on the way to second path.

author: CulGodfrey
summary: Across roughly a year of scattered sits, logs startling energetic bursts (inner-ear vibration surging out the fingertips) that trigger self-conscious fear, exhaustive stream-of-noting sessions on itching/tingling, a walking-noting practice through a prairie, and heavy financial/loneliness stress managed with metta from a new dharma friend.
interesting_sentence: "It was so startling, I felt totally self-conscious and had to guide myself back to a state of calm with some self-talk, \"You are not a reactor. Whatever arises just notice it.\"" —CulGodfrey
helpful_sentence: "I'm trying to condition myself to tolerate new sensory experiences by using a mantra we share with preschool and kindergarteners, \"You get what you get and you don't throw a fit.\"" —CulGodfrey
who_should_read: Meditators frightened by intense kundalini-like energetic bursts who want relatable coping self-talk and grounding techniques.

author: jeffgrove
key_words: breakup and shadow side of practice
summary: A running community thread where jeffgrove and other posters share Zen poems, quotes, and reflections on daily contemplative topics, including quitting smoking through noting awareness, the Gendlin Focusing technique, chakra-linked emotional patterns, and a candid account of a breakup revealing the 'shadow side' of meditation practice.
interesting_sentence: "You have to receive your demons and see through them." —jeffgrove
helpful_sentence: "Contemplating a mind with anger the body physically reacts (somatic change) with muscle tension, increased breathing, increased heart rate, tightening in the stomach and neck." —jeffgrove
who_should_read: Contemplative practitioners looking for inspirational quotes and reflections on emotional/somatic patterns rather than technical insight-stage reports.

author: 831Daniel
summary: 831Daniel logs months of daily noting-outloud sits marked by persistent unexplained body pain, recurring confusion about the technique, and tentative self-diagnosed cycling between dark night and equanimity, culminating in an attempt at Mahamudra noting.
interesting_sentence: "Seemed close to dying." —831Daniel
helpful_sentence: "The more specific you are labeling, the more you are aware of the sensations of the moment... and the more awake you are becoming!" —betawave
who_should_read: Beginners struggling with noting technique and confusion who want an honest, unfiltered account of grinding through dark night territory.

author: DanielThorson
summary: Meticulous near-daily noting log combining spinal breathing, kasina, and breath concentration with the self-inquiry question "what is aware of this experience," tracking a year-long equanimity plateau, a brief dip into dark-night-like frustration and sleepiness, and mounting crown-centered energetic activity while preparing to return to a Goenka center for stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "Is it possible that I'm passing all the way up to equanimity via spinal breathing and kasina? It seems by the time I start insight I'm already where I consider to be equanimity..." —DanielThorson
helpful_sentence: "Best to stay technique focused (noting at a certain rate, on a certain number of foundations of mindfulness for a certain period of time) rather than trying for things such as 'panoramic focus' or trying to dissolve tension." —mumuwu
who_should_read: Dedicated yogis wanting a granular, sit-by-sit account of plateauing in equanimity and troubleshooting via self-inquiry and letting-go while preparing for stream entry.

author: DanielThorson
summary: Documents daily sits combining breath concentration into apparent first jhana with vipassana noting of vibrations, while secretly practicing Kenneth Folk-style noting at a Goenka center.
interesting_sentence: "there's a difference between rules and ethics" —DanielThorson
helpful_sentence: "2nd Jhana is something like first without the sense of directed effort." —mumuwu
who_should_read: Goenka practitioners curious how noting and jhana concentration can be layered onto a Vipassana retreat schedule.

author: Dan G
summary: A busy father working with mentor Ron Crouch logs months of daily noting practice dominated by intense third-eye pressure/vibration, cycling through restlessness, fear, and family-life stress toward growing equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "It was like I was only seeing the world as a totally awful, dark place. Scary and suffocating." —Dan G
helpful_sentence: "Days with no practice at all are the biggest drag of all to progress. Bigger than aversion, tiredness, and stresses of life." —NickP
who_should_read: Householders juggling family and work who want a candid, mentor-guided log of body-centered A&P and Dark Night phenomenology.

author: Dadriance
summary: A 53-year-old Goenka practitioner of 7-8 years in Nairobi, newly inspired by MCTB, sits twice daily and experiences intense A&P-type kriyas -- spinal tremors, jaw and facial muscle spasms, eye and neck tension -- that gradually subside into a period of sloth, torpor, and muddled concentration.
interesting_sentence: "It was very clear that the body was really operating according to its own program." —Dadriance
helpful_sentence: "I see this illusion of 'I', as the blockage that hinders the flow of some sort of energy that makes its way up the spine, spending time at the chakras." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Goenka-tradition practitioners wanting a detailed account of intense physical kriyas typical of A&P and the dullness that can follow.

author: DerekACameron
summary: A brief log where an hour of noting produces a momentary sense of watching the noter as separate from self, followed by the felt sense of "I" briefly exploding and reassembling.
interesting_sentence: "At this point, it suddenly occurred to me that perhaps 'I' was not as important a person as I had always assumed!" —DerekACameron
helpful_sentence: "Derek, think of noting as a feedback device rather than a tool for the intellect to capture what is going on." —mumuwu
who_should_read: Meditators curious about noting practice's limits and brief witnessing/self-dissolution glimpses.

log: Direct perception mode, jhana, and insight
author: CheleK
summary: Chuck outlines a technique combining whole-body energetic awareness with a relaxation response to dissolve mind-body contraction, redefining "jhana" and "insight" as stillness-and-release rather than the traditional Theravadan sequence.
interesting_sentence: "A shovel digs into the ****. It digs in, picks up the ****, and flings it. There is release. There is stillness." —CheleK
helpful_sentence: "When ever this happens it is important to cycle through the relaxation response. This breaks the sort of mind/body inertia of that tension state which allows you to then ground it more deeply." —CheleK
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in blending jhana-like absorption with body-based insight through a relaxation-and-grounding technique usable off the cushion.

author: AlvaroMDF
summary: A meticulous months-long noting log using First Gear and Shinzen Young's Five Ways, cycling through pleasant tingling and light-show phenomena, frustrating "grit-your-teeth" dukkha sits, and growing equanimity toward unpleasant sensations.
interesting_sentence: "Have you ever experienced monkey mind? Of course, right? What about bats#!t crazy gorilla?" —AlvaroMDF
helpful_sentence: "Even distractions are okay -- in a way they bring us deeper, because when we come out of distraction we are even more settled than before." —betawave
who_should_read: Disciplined daily practitioners wanting a granular, blow-by-blow noting log spanning months of cycling between pleasant absorption and frustrating dry spells.

log: Distrusting as practice (Alejandro's practice notes II)
author: APrioriKreuz
summary: APrioriKreuz (Alejandro) frames deliberate 'distrust' of all self-imbued knowing, equanimity, and attainments as a fresh dis-embedding practice, paired with continuous noting throughout the day.
interesting_sentence: "Knowing is the poison that poisons everything \"I\" know." —APrioriKreuz
helpful_sentence: "Don't ignore anything. That is just identifying it as soemthing the self doesn't like and thus identifying with it. You wanna do the opposite. Dis-identify!! Include the pain in the noting. It all goes into the awakening making machine. Plop it in there and note it." —Nick
who_should_read: Advanced meditators interested in dis-embedding and non-attachment to insight who want a first-person take on cultivated distrust as a meta-technique.

author: Dodge.E.Knees
summary: Detailed blow-by-blow account of moving through the jhanic arc and repeated insight cycles with strong kundalini-like energy, reaching frequent fruitions and NS-style cessations and conjecturing 3rd path.
interesting_sentence: "I feel like I have witness death and re-birth first hand." —Dodge.E.Knees
helpful_sentence: "Using the word \"resting\" is usually a good indicator that you are getting the hang of it, from my perspective. If there's effort involved, that negates \"resting.\"" —giragirasol
who_should_read: Experienced jhana practitioners dealing with kundalini energy who want a granular, real-time account of rapid cycling toward 3rd path.

author: DonLoristo
summary: Early-stage practitioner logs daily samatha/vipassana/walking sits with detailed noting of leg numbness, chest pressure, and light/energy phenomena while preparing for upcoming Vipassana retreats.
interesting_sentence: "images of a black skinned demon sticking it's red tongue into my crown area and a blue skinned being blowing air into and fingering the crown area" —DonLoristo
helpful_sentence: "However, the great thing about doubt is that, of the 5 hindrances, it's the only one you can use against itself." —AlvaroMDF
who_should_read: New practitioners preparing for their first Vipassana retreat who want an honest, granular noting log including struggles with doubt and physical discomfort.

author: dreamrabbit
summary: Fast-onset practitioner cycles rapidly through A&P, Dark Night and Equanimity within days, tentatively claims stream entry (walked back after community pushback), then works through intense kundalini-like head pressure via mantra, pranayama, and self-inquiry.
interesting_sentence: "I used to think people who believed in Kundalini and chakras were delusional New Agers. I am now humbly chastened." —dreamrabbit
helpful_sentence: "If your goal is developmental enlightenment, I would recommend dropping the breath-following as well as any attempt at \"managing the energy phenomena.\"" —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Yogis experiencing fast, disorienting cycling through the nanas alongside kundalini-like energy who want to see how community feedback tempers a premature path claim.

author: dudeitseddy
summary: A teenage practitioner's year-plus journal of detailed noting, body sweeping, and candle-kasina practice marked by intense kundalini-like energy movements and third-eye pressure, cycling repeatedly through A&P, severe Dark Night depression and suicidal ideation, and brief equanimity, while also managing SSRI treatment and counseling.
interesting_sentence: "My experience was that my depression went away entirely when I attained to what I call the tipping point, the 5th stage on my hybrid map (aka MCTB 4th Path)." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "Rather than a mechanical drill forcing rock to break into pieces, successful noting practice is more like a stone skipping over the water repeatedly, gently, lightly, precisely and persistently over and over and over again. If you drill yourself, you will hurt yourself." —BrunoLoff
who_should_read: Young or struggling practitioners navigating severe Dark Night alongside co-occurring depression and anxiety will find a candid model for balancing meditation with therapy and medication.

author: eden212
summary: Describes shifting into '3rd gear' beyond the witness using Kenneth Folk's Direct Mode technique, a 'just relax' mantra for releasing bodily tension, and a Buddho breath concentration practice producing strong first jhana rapture.
interesting_sentence: "I really appreciate the part where Kenneth calls it a deadman switch, and you can hold it for as long as you want peace , all you have to do is pay the toll for the train ride." —eden212
helpful_sentence: "I let awareness simply rest on these tensions, there is nothing to really do but let this awareness naturally rest on these tensions." —eden212
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring Kenneth Folk's Direct Mode who want a first-hand account of surrendering past the witness into expansive, luminous states.

author: Ed76
summary: A KFD noting-practice log tracking progression through early insight stages (access concentration through the 3rd nana) under Kenneth Folk's direct feedback, alongside a parallel Triratna Buddhist sangha practice and a fear-driven pause in noting triggered by online warnings about vipassana's risks.
interesting_sentence: "I think I was making progress, small glimpses of an absence at my core made me feel a little scared, fear took on a life its own and started to look for ideas, and evidence to support it, started investigating the dangers of vipassana." —Ed76
helpful_sentence: "I don't see any reason why you can't integrate Triratna/hardcore dharma... My approach is to enjoy and contribute to the benefits of sangha life - which I really value - and follow my own inspiration as far as personal practice goes." —richardweeden
who_should_read: Meditators curious how Kenneth Folk's nana-by-nana feedback works in practice, especially those trying to balance a traditional Triratna sangha with pragmatic-dharma noting.

author: catsquotl
summary: A father of six documents early, inconsistent noting and walking meditation sits that repeatedly get cut short around the 18-38 minute mark by restlessness, fatigue, or doubt, while planning a first retreat.
interesting_sentence: "Its strange how i see this unfolding, but am not yet able to stop this.." —catsquotl
helpful_sentence: "You aren't trying to control the mind with this practice. I find it most fruitful to let it jump around - that's what it does. Your job is to note what it is jumping to." —MuMuWu
who_should_read: New meditators juggling family obligations who want relatable examples of torpor, restlessness, and inconsistent early noting practice.

author: Eezy
summary: Details an unusual samatha practice of radiating equanimity and luminosity through six directions while cycling between 2nd and 3rd gear, reaching formless jhana-like states, followed by a detailed exchange with another yogi clarifying the difference between the Witness and sublime-abode practice.
interesting_sentence: "Note: upon giving attention to left side of head, a sense of mind and body unexpectedly exploding into total luminosity, similar to rapture with no excitement, wonderful, and the first time this has happened." —Eezy
helpful_sentence: "The Witness is indeed that silent witnessing sense of observer at the back of the head or behind the eyeballs." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Jhana practitioners wanting to see how brahmavihara radiating practice can be merged with Kenneth Folk's witness/gear model to progress through the arupa jhanas.

author: Eric_G
summary: A KFD practitioner post-2nd-path tracks irregular 15-55 minute sits, reporting frequent night-time fruitions and mild cessations, deep jhanic absorption around 7th-8th jhana with a 'beyond the beyondness' experience, scalp/headband sensations, and a debate with another poster over effortful versus effortless awareness.
interesting_sentence: "But yesterday it was like I was noticing something even beyond the beyondness, kind of like if you had a thought in parenthesis and then you noticed there was yet another set of parenthesis around the whole thing." —Eric_G
helpful_sentence: "Have you ever noticed that whatever it is that is intending to be aware and trying to be aware is simply another thing that arises in awareness (whatever that is) and that you have absolutely no power, beyond going to sleep or something like that, to turn awareness off?" —MuMuWu
who_should_read: Practitioners past stream entry who want an honest account of post-path plateaus, jhana exploration, and the ambiguity of mapping subtle ongoing experience.

author: Eric_G
summary: An already-stream-entered practitioner describes inconsistent sitting punctuated by clear jhana 1-7 access, cycling through A&P-to-equanimity territory with occasional fruitions and blips, while also coaching a friend through Mahasi-Kenneth noting.
interesting_sentence: "There is also more of a quality of openness that I associate with psychedelics, kind of a richness and 3 dimensional-ness that is hard to put a finger on." —Eric_G
helpful_sentence: "Your recent advice to me about the details of noting really made a huge difference. In the past week, I've made some serious progress in quieting the incessant chatter and avoiding falling asleep, so I'm finally climbing out of my 2 year rut." —Eric_G
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry practitioners curious how jhana practice and review-stage cycling show up as recurring A&P-to-equanimity fluctuations with occasional fruitions.

author: Eric_G
summary: A KFD forum yogi tracks a multi-year noting practice - including out-loud noting, detailed sense-door noting, eyelid kasina, and formless jhana exploration - through repeated cycles of A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity, self-diagnosing 1st path in April and claiming 2nd path by December, with a high-dose psychedelic experiment triggering a severe secondary dark night along the way.
interesting_sentence: "So now 2 arahats are 51% sure I got a path. Not sure what that adds up to" —Eric_G
helpful_sentence: "Review phase A: start at A&P, go up to Equanimity, blip back to A&P, repeat." —nadavspi
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring detailed noting variations and formless jhana territory who want a granular, multi-year account of cycling through the nanas toward 2nd path.

log: Even's journal: Avoidant personality disorder,most likely at 2nd path.
author: even_
summary: A practitioner with Avoidant Personality Disorder moves from CBT and Autogenic Training into noting practice, cycling through A&P, dark night, and re-observation into a Kenneth-confirmed 2nd path, then explores pureland jhanas and possible Nirodha Samapatti.
interesting_sentence: "It felt like sinking down to the bottom of a 1000 meter deep ocean, staying there without ability to see anything around you." —even_
helpful_sentence: "From here on out, make sure you're not manipulating your experience. Intention is a powerful thing. If you intended to be in a new state, a new one will show up." —awouldbehipster
who_should_read: Practitioners managing personality disorders or chronic depression who want a detailed account of noting practice mapped onto the insight cycles up through 2nd path.

author: mudstick
summary: A newcomer to noting practice cycles through pleasant/unpleasant/neutral states, struggles with candle kasina eye strain, and tries Shikantaza, while receiving concrete technique guidance from mumuwu and Nikolai Halay.
interesting_sentence: "You are the train station, you do not go to meet the trains, you do not follow after the trains, you simply notice the ones that stop or pass by." —mudstick
helpful_sentence: "make a resolution upon sitting to note what arises without looking for anything or manipulating the experience." —mumuwu
who_should_read: New meditators wanting a relatable beginner's log balancing noting with kasina and Shikantaza under mentor feedback.

author: Cliff78
summary: Months of daily four-foundations noting move through dark-night fear, chronic illness, and chest-centered trauma release, followed by chakra-style perspective switching, jhana practice, and a spontaneous cessation-like 'blip' in daily life.
interesting_sentence: "Either everything is me or nothing is. The self/other distinction is nothing more than a bad habit." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "The idea of \"me\" seems(in my experience) to be equal to every other sensation. The idea of \"me\" is the same as an itch, or desire for ice cream." —RevElev
who_should_read: Meditators wanting an unusually detailed, months-long day-by-day noting journal spanning dark-night territory into jhana and a cessation-like event.

author: tomotvos
summary: During a business trip with extended sitting time, a householder practitioner battles intense whole-body shaking and aversion in re-observation, and gradually reduces the shakes by dropping resistance to physical discomfort.
interesting_sentence: "It's actually very predictable and expected. This is what happens as the unstable strata of mind in between 3rd and 4th jhana are developing." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "The more you can accept, the less it will hurt... Allow your focus to become more diffuse. Try being aware of all of the surface of your skin at once." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners struggling with re-observation shaking and aversion who want candid peer support and concrete diffuse-awareness techniques.

author: Gary-Isozerotope
summary: Chronicles managing a persistent itchy skin condition with equanimity and cold showers, practicing Misogi and Buteyko breathing for lung health, and working Shinzen Young's expansion-contraction-gone noting during a phone retreat while tallying weekly Mindfulness Units.
interesting_sentence: "The itching gets very very strong and requires utmost equanimity to refrain from scratching, or taking very hot showers, which put me into a state of unequaled physical bliss." —Gary-Isozerotope
helpful_sentence: "I can have equanimity with pain much more easily than with severe unrelenting itching." —Gary-Isozerotope
who_should_read: Practitioners dealing with chronic physical irritation who want to see mindfulness and pranayama-style breathwork applied to a real bodily hindrance.

author: Gary-Isozerotope
summary: Gary works through Actual Freedom-style apperception practice with teacher Nikolai, tracking a persistent 'radiant core'/flow-of-becoming sensation in his chest and experimenting with HAIETMOBA to approach a PCE.
interesting_sentence: "I want to have a PCE, a real, no-doubt, ass kicking PCE, and see for myself it that convinces me." —Gary-Isozerotope
helpful_sentence: "By discernment I mean seeing in real time what is going on. Seeing the point of contact of a sense object and its corresponding sense door." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring Actual Freedom/apperception methods who want a detailed, dialogic account of chasing a PCE.

author: garyrh
summary: Gary logs detailed sitting reports on itches, whole-body vibrations, and crown/third-eye sensations moving through cyclical A&P-like surges, with Kenneth Folk coaching his noting and concentration directly.
interesting_sentence: "A clear bright itch is what I call the 'kiss of concentration.'" —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "Avoid speculation about where you might be, though; later on, that will be easier to spot. For now, just the facts." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Pre-stream-entry yogis working with noting who want a model of clear phenomenological reporting alongside direct teacher feedback from Kenneth Folk.

author: G3off
summary: Geoff logs a brief early-morning sit where he counted breaths 1-10 and noted itching, temperature contrasts, and tingling at the base of the spine.
interesting_sentence: "Felt tingling at base of spine towards end of session." —G3off
helpful_sentence: "Felt it easier to not get caught up in the contect, to let it pass by. Watched it from a distance." —G3off
who_should_read: Beginners curious about a brief, plainly described body-scan sit with minimal embellishment.

author: Giulio B.
summary: Documents a disciplined restart of choiceless noting and breath-counting practice adapted to chronic back pain (done lying down, sandwiched with stretching), using public 'about to practice' accountability posts and escalating rules to build daily consistency.
interesting_sentence: "This is a completely unavoidable self-attack." —Giulio B.
helpful_sentence: "I have in periods of strong discomfort found that breaking up longer sits with some moving meditation (Tai Chi) does not disturb much." —Pejn
who_should_read: Meditators with chronic pain limiting posture, or anyone needing external accountability structures to sustain a daily sitting habit.

author: GiulioB.
summary: A first-time meditator's day-by-day account of working through the four foundations of mindfulness while lying down due to chronic back pain, then shifting to candle-flame kasina paired with noting aloud under a self-imposed 'Sayadaw 0' Mahasi-style regimen.
interesting_sentence: "Under a clinical point of view, i say of myself that i'm a Strong (i've been labeled with) Avoidant Personality and a Narcissistic Personality." —GiulioB.
helpful_sentence: "One simple thing you might start out with is trying to maintain full-body awareness during your shower, taking a positive and kind attitude towards the practice." —villum
who_should_read: Absolute beginners with physical limitations like back pain looking for an honest, granular account of learning the four foundations of mindfulness.

author: Giulio B.
summary: Giulio logs short evening noting sessions investigating a recurring abdominal pulsing sensation at Kenneth Folk's request, cycling through fear-of-failure, depressive lows, and brief motivational upswings.
interesting_sentence: "i'm going to see the Gates of Hell either tomorrow or saturday (getting prepared already)." —Giulio B.
helpful_sentence: "Become the world's foremost authority on your own pulsing." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Beginners with chronic pain or OCD/depression who want a candid look at inconsistent, short-session noting practice.

author: GiulioB.
summary: GiulioB. logs frequent short concentration-noting sits despite severe chronic-pain-linked depression, culminating in a crisis post that led admins to briefly lock the thread before he resumed with steadier daily practice.
interesting_sentence: "My depression is extremely severe, it actually doesn't pass a day or two in which i spend several hours dreaming to find a gun to shoot myself." —GiulioB.
helpful_sentence: "The trick is to make \"the problem\" into the object. When you think \"this is useless,\" find the associated mind state and note it." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Meditators managing severe depression and chronic pain who want to see how noting practice and community support can coexist with a mental-health crisis.

author: Howard2Clegg
summary: During a self-arranged home retreat, Howard moves from formal out-loud noting through high equanimity into a distinct path moment followed by repeated 'fruition' bliss-wave events, which his teachers Beth and Kenneth confirm as 2nd path, after which he struggles to adapt to a 'not-doing' practice instruction.
interesting_sentence: "Last night my mind was like a cross between New Orleans Mardi Gras and the Amazon rain forest; all kind of crazy sounds and colours but with big hairy spiders eating each other in the shadows; and all of it, very cool." —Howard2Clegg
helpful_sentence: "I now know that this is a problem if you want to get path as insight is the key not absorption." —Howard2Clegg
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry practitioners curious how a teacher-guided second-path confirmation and its 'not-doing' aftermath actually unfold.

author: Howard2Clegg
summary: Howard chronicles roughly six weeks of noting and mind-state practice culminating in an intensive home retreat where a Kenneth Folk-taught eye-posture flickering technique triggers dramatic involuntary body spasms and quivering, alongside migraines, a throat infection, and a brief taste of equanimity amid chronic fatigue syndrome and recurring drowsiness.
interesting_sentence: "Walking is particularly interesting I'm like a cross between a spastic spider and some kind of contemporary ballet dancer." —Howard2Clegg
helpful_sentence: "It appears that sensations are not inherently pleasant or un-pleasant but a characteristic that is added by the 'observer.'" —Howard2Clegg
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about unusual energetic/kundalini-like phenomena and eye-posture concentration techniques during intensive retreat practice, especially those balancing meditation with chronic illness.

author: Howard2Clegg
summary: After a ten-day-to-two-week 'review' period of low motivation and dissolved worry, Howard resumes half-hour jhana sessions noticing transitions between distinct spaciousness states while an inclusive compassion practice assigned by Kenneth spontaneously deepens into stable, self-sustaining metta.
interesting_sentence: "So this time review was a bit like the holiday that I always wanted to have but never quite managed, no matter how exotic the destination; and all in the comfort of my own home." —Howard2Clegg
helpful_sentence: "I was starting to use these sensations as the focus rather than anything physical, this appeared to speed up the transitions." —Howard2Clegg
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring formless jhana absorption and inclusive compassion practice under a teacher's guidance who want a candid account of an integration ('review') period.

author: Howard2Clegg
summary: Howard logs months of twice-to-four-times-daily sits mixing Dzogchen, Mahamudra noting, and a personal 'gears' framework for the nyanas, cycling repeatedly through Dark Night/Re-Ob into sustained Equanimity while managing Chronic Fatigue Syndrome under close mentorship from Kenneth Folk and Beth Resnick-Folk.
interesting_sentence: "In this instance it is like trying to sleep with a brass band playing under your skin." —Howard2Clegg
helpful_sentence: "It's odd and perhaps counter-intuitive, but the deepest freedom comes from not needing anything, including freedom." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners cycling repeatedly through Dark Night and Equanimity post-stream-entry, especially those balancing intensive daily practice with chronic illness, will find Howard's granular technique notes on Dzogchen, direct mode, and Mahamudra instructive.

author: HowardClegg
summary: A student of Kenneth Folk who already claims mid-to-high 2nd path Equanimity logs months of multiple daily sits tracking nyana cycles, then shifts into sustained Mahamudra ('ships in the harbor') and Dzogchen practice, using it to dissolve suffering around migraines, indigestion, insomnia, and family stress.
interesting_sentence: "I was my own, one man football crowd." —HowardClegg
helpful_sentence: "This moment is just as it is, whether it is pleasant, unpleasant, or neutral, deep or shallow, sacred or profane." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners past stream entry who want a granular, technique-rich account of using Mahamudra and Dzogchen to work with chronic pain and everyday suffering.

author: iftah11
summary: A pre-stream-entry yogi in Israel logs near-daily 30-70 minute noting sits over several months, describing vivid A&P sensations (tingly body waves, swollen palms, energy rushes) alternating with prolonged dark-night bouts of fear, anger, and grief, while wrestling with doubt after his local sitting group dismisses stream entry as a goal.
interesting_sentence: "It was as though my body squeezed or something, I felt an urge to open my eyes because the panic was so great." —iftah11
helpful_sentence: "Get interested in how they never stay the same experience even for a second." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Beginning yogis navigating early A&P/dark-night cycling and doubt from unsupportive sitting communities will find a relatable, granular daily record.

author: JYET
summary: A wide-ranging log riding the jhana/nana 'arc,' confirming 2nd path with teacher Ron, then layering in tummo breath-retention practice, shakti energy, fasting, and self-inquiry while cycling through blissful, anxious, and increasingly effortless equanimous states.
interesting_sentence: "It looks like the path I got on last retreat was second and not first." —JYET
helpful_sentence: "Notice the tendency to form an adversarial relationship with thoughts. Just let 'em be." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring energetic practices like tummo and kundalini alongside jhana/nana cycling who want an honest account of confirmed 2nd path and the ambiguity of nirodha samapatti attempts.

author: JYET
summary: A Swedish yogi transitions from jhana practice under Leigh Brasington into Mahasi noting under Ron, then details a two-week Dhammacari retreat with dukkha, head-nicking phenomena, and blackouts, ending with Ron confirming likely stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "So it was quite ironical to sit with the teacher and talk about the dukkha in the morning." —JYET
helpful_sentence: "But you can't 'turn it off'. Some people never have strong energetic effects in meditation, others do. If you do, you might as well learn from traditions that deal productively with it as a supplement to your vipassana." —giragirasol
who_should_read: Concentration-oriented yogis curious how a jhana-heavy background integrates with an intensive Mahasi retreat leading toward stream entry.

author: JackWick
summary: A Goenka-retreat-launched yogi develops a dramatic, spontaneous kriya (kundalini-like spinal twisting and face-planting) alongside noting and kasina practice, cycling through vivid A&P light/pressure phenomena, prolonged dark-night fear, guilt, and depression, and stretches of low equanimity while juggling single motherhood, illness, and shifting allegiances between Goenka, Zen, and Advaita teachers.
interesting_sentence: "my head felt like someone had come along and chopped the top off." —JackWick
helpful_sentence: "Turning the attention directly toward these \"I\" phenomena will weaken and unravel the misperception of self." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Meditators curious about intense spontaneous kriya/kundalini phenomena and an unusually candid, cyclical, months-long account of a new yogi navigating A&P, dark night, and equanimity while raising kids alone.

author: Jaime_Arnold
summary: A new noting practitioner transitioning from Zen describes energy rushes, bodily 'wobble' sensations, and spontaneous grief about mortality arising during twice-daily hour-long sits, with Kenneth Folk framing a subsequent rough sit as entry into harder territory.
interesting_sentence: "I have also had a few overwhelming moments of feeling what i could only call grief in have the deep feeling that 'I', my partner and everyone around me will die" —Jaime_Arnold
helpful_sentence: "Note them all carefully and you will find a kind of happiness that is independent of conditions." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Beginners transitioning from a Zen background to noting practice who want a candid account of early energetic phenomena and doubt.

author: james-ing
summary: A detailed daily noting log cataloguing recurring involuntary body shaking, forehead pressure, drowsiness cycles, and visual flickering as the practitioner works through the early Insight Knowledges toward an anticipated A&P, with close coaching from Kenneth Folk.
interesting_sentence: "GET UP NOW OR YOU'LL DIE FOR SURE!!" —james-ing
helpful_sentence: "Your vipassana noting practice is just like that; you can put any experience in the hopper by noting it and it instantly becomes nuclear fuel to turbocharge your awakening." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners early in noting practice curious about typical pre-A&P phenomenology like shaking, drowsiness, and forehead pressure.

author: jfmatteson
