summary: Chronicles jhana-heavy sits filled with pulsing, itching, and rocking sensations that give way to a frustrating dukkha-nana slump, then a return to surrender that opens into panoramic peacefulness and formless jhanas on the way toward 2nd path.
interesting_sentence: "Insight alert: noting what's there rather than waiting for what you want to be there is more effective!" —jfmatteson
helpful_sentence: "Mind reacts to some sensations with aversion by shrinking away from them and to some with greed by trying to cling to them." —Antero.
who_should_read: Practitioners nearing 2nd path who want a candid, sensation-by-sensation account of jhana cycling and how surrender speeds passage through the dukkha nanas.

author: jfmatteson
summary: A father of a toddler builds a rigorous daily 4-foundations noting and jhana-jumping practice, transcribes detailed noting sessions, and reports a probable stream-entry breakthrough during a two-day weekend retreat.
interesting_sentence: "So, I just decided to give myself permission to attain stream entry. Why not!" —jfmatteson
helpful_sentence: "Then, sit and watch, the way a cat watches a mouse hole, alert for the moment the mouse pops his head out of the hole. By turning toward the thoughts and taking them directly as object, you co-opt these former enemies into allies in your awakening." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Noting practitioners wanting a granular, transcribed model of daily 4-foundations practice building toward a retreat-triggered stream-entry moment.

author: JeanV
summary: Works a breath-counting kasina practice using the bottom of a plain white ceramic plate while wrestling with disproportionate emotional suffering tied to a migraine, later shifting toward including rather than excluding subtle desires for transcendence.
interesting_sentence: "I feel like I'm stuck between worlds." —JeanV
helpful_sentence: "I think I was starting to move a little too much towards wanting a  transcendant experience." —JeanV
who_should_read: Meditators developing concentration foundations who want a candid, brief account of working with physical pain and shifting from striving toward inclusive acceptance.

author: jigmesengye
summary: Chronicles months of Kenneth Folk's five-part noting practice escalating through a 3-week Mahasi retreat and a 32-day TMC retreat where he experiences stream entry (Kenneth's one-word reply "Pop."), then moves into review-stage practice mapping visual/mental "strobing" onto the material and formless jhanas amid persistent qi-like swaying and vibration phenomena.
interesting_sentence: "Pop." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "You never note the three characteristics. Never, ever, ever." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Serious noting practitioners preparing for or reflecting on stream entry who want an unusually granular, retreat-tested account of nana progression, jhana absorption, and integrating chi-kung energetics with vipassana.

author: apperception
summary: Recounts a retreat where the author cycles through A&P, dark night, and equanimity while claiming second path, then documents months of kasina concentration work progressing through the vipassana jhanas up to the formless states.
interesting_sentence: "I felt like I was being eaten by biting green flies." —apperception
helpful_sentence: "I contained that feeling within the space of knowing as well." —apperception
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about detailed retreat phenomenology and systematic kasina/jhana exploration after stream entry.

author: NeverSummer
summary: Systematically maps a daily 'jhanic arc' ascending through the eight jhanas and into newly-discovered Pure Land jhanas, alongside 2nd-gear self-inquiry and Tibetan energy practices, while working through recurring heart-chakra anxiety flare-ups.
interesting_sentence: "There was a sense of a consensual relationship with what I assume to be reality starting to shift and crumble." —NeverSummer
helpful_sentence: "The technique of riding the arc involves going into the 6th jhana, latching onto that knowing element and noticing that is present in the jhanas as you go down from 6 to 1." —mumuwu
who_should_read: Jhana enthusiasts wanting a granular, day-by-day map of the eight jhanas through the Pure Land jhanas and Nirodha Samapatti attempts.

author: jwhooper
summary: Logs marathon multi-hour sits combining metta, mantra, self-inquiry, and kasina work, describing crown-energy vibrations, a nimitta, unexplained blackout-like sits, and a culminating no-self collapse before a bout of Bell's Palsy disrupts his practice.
interesting_sentence: "There is nothing to do, and no one to do it. Seen through, there is nothing left to practice." —jwhooper
helpful_sentence: "The very act of trying to get rid of it simply feeds the process... You have to change the rules." —Ona
who_should_read: Actualism-influenced practitioners exploring PCE-adjacent states, NS attempts, and non-dual inquiry who want a granular day-by-day phenomenological log.

author: johnnyzampano
summary: A brand-new meditator logs a day of walking, breath-noting, and fire kasina sittings (a two-color bowl and a 7" purple paper disc), describing shifting light/shadow effects and a session of deep inhale/exhale noting shortly after a weekend retreat boosted his access concentration.
interesting_sentence: "No notion of time or anything, just start and end, start and end." —johnnyzampano
helpful_sentence: "The trick is to keep the eyes steady. That's a lot of where the power of that meditation comes from. Locking the eyes in position seems to make the mind go into concentration mode." —mumuwu
who_should_read: Beginner meditators curious about starting a daily kasina and breath-noting practice will find a candid first-weeks account and practical concentration tips.

author: jwhooper
summary: A software developer spends well over a year doing daily kasina concentration, noting, and surrender practice, repeatedly probing for Pure Land jhana and Nirodha Samapatti without ever confirming stream entry, cycling between long stretches of boring equanimity and painful emotional dark-night periods.
interesting_sentence: "I am tired of suffering. I will take the end of it by any means, AF, jhanas, a pill, insight, pyramid power ... I just don't care." —jwhooper
helpful_sentence: "Feelings (sensations that you can observe objectively, like heat, cold, tenseness, tingling) as opposed to emotions (things that are called \"fear\" or \"anger\" or \"aversion\"). These two kinds of objects are distinguishable and occur separately, even though we think they're just one thing mainly out of habit." —cmarti
who_should_read: Long-term solo concentration practitioners who want an unvarnished account of cycling between equanimity-boredom and dark night without a clean path attainment.

author: JonB2
summary: Documents a return to daily noting practice after a long hiatus, tracking subtle shifts in body-sensation awareness, recurring fear tied to self-monitoring during public speaking, and a spontaneous slide into self-inquiry.
interesting_sentence: "It's like the insight caused and causes the problem." —JonB2
helpful_sentence: "Also reading a short dharma talk on the 3 characteristics helps enormously." —JonB2
who_should_read: Practitioners restarting a noting practice after a break who want to see how fear and self-monitoring can surface during the Dark Night.

author: foolbutnotforlong
summary: Describes attaining what the author calls 4th path/arahantship through a practice derived from Acariya Maha Boowa's teachings on unified mindfulness-wisdom, followed by weeks of post-awakening laziness, effortless jhana absorption (including a strobing 8th jhana and Pure Land jhanas near the door of Nirodha), and the mind visibly trying to reconstruct a "self" around the new realization.
interesting_sentence: "Being in the 8th jhana brings the 4th path fruitions  within seconds, as an equanimity within the jhana is felt every time one comes in and out of it" —foolbutnotforlong
helpful_sentence: "My shifting to PCE/3rd gear seems to be a simple as turning a switch. Simply putting all my attention in a clear sensate manifestation (a strong color, a crisp sound, a fine but well-defined sensation on the skin, etc)." —foolbutnotforlong
who_should_read: Practitioners near the end of the map curious what post-4th-path/arahantship integration, laziness, and 'self' reconstruction actually feel like day to day.

author: foolbutnotforlong
summary: A practitioner with strong natural concentration recounts an early misinterpreted A&P experience followed by years of depression, then describes rapid jhana attainment, subtle 'popping' sensations, a Pure Land Jhana attempt, and a second vivid A&P experience.
interesting_sentence: "well, if I have reached stream entry, congrats Jorge, but we are continuing to push forward regardless" —foolbutnotforlong
helpful_sentence: "One day during meditation I felt 2 small 'pops' followed by another 2 more, they were barely noticeable, so I did not make a big deal about it." —foolbutnotforlong
who_should_read: Concentration-oriented practitioners curious how jhana attainment, subtle 'pop' phenomena, and misread A&P experiences can intertwine with years of depression.

author: roboto212
summary: Tracks an unexpected A&P event during a massage-like touch, subsequent Dark Night/Equanimity cycling tied closely to a fruitarian diet and water fasts, and repeated coaching from Kenneth Folk and Nikolai to drop path speculation in favor of bare sensation-by-sensation reporting.
interesting_sentence: "I sat up, knew immediately what it was, and a 4 hour long full body orgasm followed, along with a bunch of other conscious things." —roboto212
helpful_sentence: "Notice that there is no speculation, just painfully simple descriptions of what happened in terms of bare phenomena." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Newer yogis prone to premature self-diagnosis who want a model of how experienced teachers redirect reporting toward bare phenomenology.

author: linguos
summary: Details an extended breath-counting sit that dissolved into a dualistic-split 'event horizon' and a black flicker before a strange new A&P wave, followed by a terrifying sleep-paralysis episode and uncertainty about whether he has since settled into Equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "The sort of fear I'd expect to experience before being burnt alive or a conception of Catholic hell." —linguos
helpful_sentence: "I'm confident I've been in equanimity for a bit now... although without, save for a couple times, no obvious signs of dukkha nyanas, only progressive dissolution" —linguos
who_should_read: Yogis puzzling over an ambiguous cessation-like event who want a vivid first-person account to compare against their own experience.

log: Journal entry--no clue where I'm at
author: CulGodfrey
summary: A first practice report following an initial meeting with Kenneth Folk, consisting of an unbroken stream of first-foundation noting (itches, twitches, tingling, brief blissful nothingness, and stray thoughts) with the author unsure which of the 20 strata of mind it corresponds to.
interesting_sentence: "Of the 20 strata of mind, do you know what level you've reached?" —CulGodfrey
helpful_sentence: "This is awesome detail! I'm looking forward to more." —RonCrouch
who_should_read: New meditators curious to see an unfiltered, raw example of first-foundation noting practice as taught by Kenneth Folk.

author: orasis
summary: Orasis (Justin) cycles through kundalini-like kriyas, Mahamudra Noting, and Rigpa/Dzogchen practice, documenting an energetic 3rd-gear peak, recurring dark-night grimacing and jhana-jumping episodes, an anxiety attack that dissolves into a near-cessation 'no one home' state, and a later stretch of near-continuous equanimity disrupted by a painful nerve impingement.
interesting_sentence: "I guess this is what an anxiety attack feels like when there is no one to be anxious." —orasis
helpful_sentence: "Seeing the tension as evidence of the issue at hand rather than as the problem may prove to be a more useful strategy." —mumuwu
who_should_read: Practitioners dealing with intense kundalini-like kriyas and dark-night grimacing who want a detailed account of using Mahamudra Noting and Dzogchen-style resting awareness to navigate cycling insight stages.

author: orasis
summary: Following stream entry work with Kenneth Folk and a weekend Dzogchen retreat, orasis describes a post-awakening settling process marked by a fading Witness-sense, headless peak experiences on walking meditation, energetic kryas tied to residual selfing, and a shift toward accepting suffering and grief as part of ordinary embodied life.
interesting_sentence: "I guess this is what an anxiety attack feels like when there is no one to be anxious." —orasis
helpful_sentence: "Settling on a View was useful because it gives the conceptual mind a simple model of reality so it doesn't need figure things out anymore." —orasis
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry practitioners interested in Dzogchen View and integrating awakening into ordinary emotional life will find a candid, detailed account of that settling process.

author: orasis
summary: Justin (orasis) journals frequent noting and open-awareness sits marked by spontaneous kriyas, eye flickering, and jhana cycling, gradually stabilizing into a near-constant, low-effort 'recognizing awareness' he calls 3rd gear.
interesting_sentence: "The demon is in the cage but it is struggling to reconstitute itself and get out." —orasis
helpful_sentence: "In my experience it's almost like where there *were* unquestioned, deeply held assumptions (that \"I\" am the character in my stories, or \"I\" am a proprioceptive location \"in\" the body, or so on) increasingly these same phenomena, as they are brought to light, don't necessarily stop arising per se as much as they become seen as guesses rather than assumed knowledge, and hence become more translucent and flexible." —jhsaintonge
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about spontaneous kriyas, eye-flickering, and jhana cycling during noting practice, and how such energetic phenomena map onto the A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity territory.

author: orasis
summary: Attempts a solo 30-minute first-jhana session using Kenneth's virtual nose-pinch and coalescing yellow-orange blob technique, repeatedly losing the object to eye/headache tension and intruding thoughts before ending by relocating the blob first.
interesting_sentence: "Felt eye and headache tension trying to hold onto the blob as it sometimes pulsed in and out and sometimes was more stable and just kind of morphed around in shape." —orasis
helpful_sentence: "Lost the blob and decided to end the session by finding the blob first then coming out." —orasis
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring Kenneth Folk's kasina-based jhana instructions who want a candid account of the eye strain and distraction that arise in early concentration work.

author: KeithStrand
summary: Chronicles a year of daily out-loud noting practice blending Mahasi and Goenka techniques, including eye-strobing, energetic surges, and a 10-day Goenka retreat, without ever reaching cessation.
interesting_sentence: "If the mighty \" I \" of Keith is nothing but a pile of inanimate things earth, wind, water , heat, thoughts, mind states, sensations and phenomenon is my concern with self not a pathetic fallacy?" —KeithStrand
helpful_sentence: "I think that it is best to try and master one practice, for a while, rather than go back and forth with each one every day, since that seems to lead to floundering." —Yadid
who_should_read: Yogis curious about integrating Mahasi-style out-loud noting with long-term Goenka retreat practice while navigating motivation dips and craving for stream entry.

author: KeithStrand
summary: A newcomer switching from Goenka sweeping to Kenneth Folk-style noting logs months of first-gear practice, an eye-flickering concentration technique, and recurring energetic kriyas, sensations, and sleep-paralysis episodes without reaching stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "So it seems to be a bit of a slippery game where sometimes i can stand above the fish barrel and look down see the thoughts moving around and other times when i come to i realize that my head was in the fish barrel." —KeithStrand
helpful_sentence: "There is nothing you cannot learn to note; learning to note is waking up. If you cannot note, it's because you are absorbed. Absorbed equals embeddedness. Embeddedness is the opposite of enlightenment." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Beginner noting practitioners switching from Goenka who want a candid, month-by-month account of energetic phenomena and the eye-flicker concentration technique.

author: kennethfolk
summary: Brief, sparse noting-practice entries logging short timed sessions (6-15 minutes) of text noting at home in San Francisco, including one done while listening to Beethoven's 9th Symphony and another during a Lakers radio broadcast.
interesting_sentence: "I am curious as to what the past couple of years have been like for you in terms of your practice and view on practice/awakening." —Bill29ish
helpful_sentence: "Text noting by myself while sitting on the couch in our apartment in San Francisco listing to the Second Movement (Scherzo) of Beethoven's 9th Symphony:" —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Readers curious what raw, unfiltered timestamped noting-session logs look like with minimal narrative or insight commentary.

author: kennethfolk
summary: Kenneth Folk reports a self-experiment in continuously grounding emotional 'proto-blobs' in bodily sensation, going 13+ days without anger, and sparks a long community discussion (with Chris Marti, Naomi/lhamo, mumuwu, and others) about how this 'direct perception mode' relates to post-4th-path stability, jhanic cycling, and the three-speed-transmission model.
interesting_sentence: "It is as though I were taken over by some madness or briefly inhabited by some freaky pod person." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "If you can note, you are not wasting your time." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Meditators who have completed the classic path model and want to explore how emotional-grounding/direct-perception practices interact with post-4th-path stability.

author: kennethfolk
summary: A single terse entry noting the feeling of only just beginning to scratch the surface of what practice makes possible.
interesting_sentence: "I feel as though I'm just beginning to scratch the surface of what is possible." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "I feel as though I'm just beginning to scratch the surface of what is possible." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Readers curious about a brief, unelaborated personal note rather than a detailed practice record.

author: KirbyScarfe
summary: A daily Mahasi-style noting journal tracking a shift from primary-object focus to choiceless noting, documenting recurring tingling/pressure/itching phenomena, cycles of dullness and frustration, and a post-retreat state resembling equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "For me, following an itch in this way became a frequent launching pad from 3rd to 4th nana." —jfmatteson
helpful_sentence: "Our only job is to be there when they happen." —Rob_Mtl
who_should_read: New noting practitioners who want a granular, day-by-day model of pushing through dullness, doubt, and physical sensations toward a more equanimous baseline.

author: Kundun
summary: Kundun progresses through Kenneth Folk's gear system over time, moving from labeling (1st gear) to witness/self-inquiry practice (2nd gear) to an increasingly goalless shikantaza-style 'just sitting' (3rd gear).
interesting_sentence: "It always happened spontaneously and at some point of the phenomenon I got bit frightened and got out of it. Nowadays I don't fear it anymore.." —Kundun
helpful_sentence: "The assignment of negative - neutral-positive is much easier as you don't really have to give that label to 'know' the nature of the phenomena." —Kundun
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about Kenneth Folk's gear system who want a first-person account of shifting from noting into witness practice and eventually goalless shikantaza.

author: IntegralDoc
summary: IntegralDoc (Kyle) takes up kasina concentration practice with an orange bowl on Kenneth Folk's suggestion, reports early jhanic absorption, several nights of jittery kundalini-like insomnia, and a vivid lucid-dream-like episode of his head expanding into a black hole/tunnel.
interesting_sentence: "my skin was crawling and I wanted to tear it off." —IntegralDoc
helpful_sentence: "What I mean by keeping the object in sharp focus is that rather than just 'vegging out' and allowing the kasina to freely move about and do its thing, I bear down on it, very lightly - don't struggle or try to overpower it - but just see if you can make it stay in focus." —n8sense
who_should_read: Concentration practitioners drawn to kasina and jhana work, especially those navigating intense somatic sensations or dark-night-like sleep disturbances.

author: JLaurelC
summary: After a two-week sutta-jhana retreat with Leigh Brasington covering the rupa and arupa jhanas, Laurel spends months riding the witness up and down the jhanic arc through hypnagogic dreamlike states, torpor, and equanimity, ending the log uncertain whether a brief taste of Pure Land jhana (PL1) counts as third path.
interesting_sentence: "Piti without this pleasure, on the other hand, is like sticking one's finger into an electrical outlet (I've felt that; the itches on the skin are an unpleasant manifestation of piti)." —JLaurelC
helpful_sentence: "Make sure you are sticking with the witness throughout - if the mind gets absorbed in the dream content, but eventually comes back to the witness as object, then I think things are probably working as they should." —mumuwu
who_should_read: Post-second-path meditators exploring structured retreat-based jhana training and witness-based jhanic-arc practice will find a candid, technically detailed account of the confusing middle ground before third path.

author: JLaurelC
summary: Newly claiming third path, the author cycles through Pure Land jhanas up to the 8th, does occasional vipassana noting fine vibrations at low equanimity, and navigates bronchitis and steroid tapering while chronic anxiety recedes into a stable 'honeymoon' period.
interesting_sentence: "I've found that steroid use tends to interfere with jhana, wondering if others have had that experience." —JLaurelC
helpful_sentence: "I decided not to push." —JLaurelC
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring Pure Land jhana progression who want an honest account of navigating illness, anxiety relief, and post-path equanimity.

author: JLaurelC
summary: A stream-enterer cycles through 3C's/dukkha-nana territory with noting, jhana, metta and a brief kasina experiment while managing fibromyalgia, insomnia and a driving phobia, culminating in an apparent fruition and confirmed second path around July 4th.
interesting_sentence: "I frequently have a recurrence of fear that awakening will cut me off from the world and turn me into the walking dead." —JLaurelC
helpful_sentence: "This work isn't about antidotes, it's simply about really feeling the poison. The rest just happens from that direct experience." —betawave
who_should_read: Stream-enterers muddling through post-SE review and second-path cycling who want a candid, detailed account of energetic phenomena alongside real-life anxiety, chronic pain, and relational triggers.

author: JLaurelC
summary: An anxious, fibromyalgia-affected academic and mother logs eight months of daily noting, samatha, and jhana practice through repeated A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity cycles, a 9-day IMS retreat, and Neurontin withdrawal, culminating in a January 2012 breakthrough that Kenneth Folk confirms as stream entry, followed by an unexpectedly lonely week of felt self-loss.
interesting_sentence: "It was like turning over the ignition on a Ferrari; all of a sudden the engine roared into gear." —JLaurelC
helpful_sentence: "You'll go from seeing concentration as "focus on a fixed and stable point" to seeing it as "living with a giant, ever-changing panorama". On the way it will seem like you are losing concentration, when actually you are gaining a better kind of concentration." —Rob_Mtl
who_should_read: Anxious, health-challenged householder yogis working the noting path toward stream entry who want a candid, multi-month account of the dukkha ñanas and the disorienting grief that can follow attainment.

log: Letting go into the stream - A second practice journal.
author: johnnyzampano
summary: After a six-week retreat that deepened his prior Stream Entry, the author logs daily sits cycling through sustained 'third gear' equanimity, a brief dark night dip, and structured 1st-3rd Jhana work with his teacher.
interesting_sentence: "Looking into the mirror no one is looking back." —johnnyzampano
helpful_sentence: "In my sits I focus on the breath until concentrated then focus on the piti (1st jhana) and expand it all over the body, sometimes it becomes very strong, shifting to the happiness (2ed jhana) piti fades leaving a pure warm happy feeling that feels much better then piti, letting go that I focus on the calm peacefulness (3rd jhana) which feels like home to me, and I can more easily get locked deep into it." —johnnyzampano
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry yogis wanting a candid, retreat-grounded account of sustaining equanimous 'third gear' awareness alongside early Jhana training.

author: LiamO'Sullivan
summary: A phenomenologically detailed daily noting journal tracking rapid multiple-per-second noting, a 110-minute dry-noting marathon, and a ten-hour travel sit that cycled through fear, disgust, and re-observation into equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "There were also two intriguing mind moments in which there was no discernable experiencer." —LiamO'Sullivan
helpful_sentence: "Watch out if you are noting something negative - aversion, irritation, frustration and so on - in such a way as to effectively disown it or distance yourself from it." —AndyW45
who_should_read: Committed noting practitioners cycling near Equanimity who want a granular phenomenological account of surrender, dukkha ñana breakthroughs, and integrating samatha with rapid noting.

author: limbic
summary: Limbic recounts a ten-day retreat and a later center retreat marked by head and face kriyas, jaw-release work resolving facial numbness, an anxiety attack, and a water-yoga session that revealed hidden shoulder tension.
interesting_sentence: "And I had a head kriya moving my head to the back right, with a pleasant spread of these vibrations around my ear." —limbic
helpful_sentence: "Trying not to zone too close in to the tensions, thereby exacerbating them." —limbic
who_should_read: Practitioners experiencing kundalini-style kriyas and anxiety who want a grounded account of working through energetic phenomena with body awareness.

author: limbicsail
summary: Chronicles a shift from pre-A&P into reliable A&P territory through commuter-train sits, marked by recurring third-eye pressure, bodily vibrations, a spontaneous crying release, and emerging fear and anger alongside the bliss.
interesting_sentence: "I was noting so much sadness and remorse, right next to the pleasant vibratory phenomenas coursing through my body." —limbicsail
helpful_sentence: "The point is to learn to closely observe all the phenomena that happen to come and go in the body and mind, INCLUDING the reaction of liking them or not liking them." —giragirasol
who_should_read: Meditators newly encountering A&P-stage energetic phenomena who want a granular, sit-by-sit account of third-eye pressure, vibrations, and emerging fear and doubt.

author: LocoAustriaco
summary: An Austrian yogi cycles through witness states, tummo, Mahamudra-Noting, and a self-described '8th stage' of bodily depersonalization, then deliberately uses psychiatric medications targeting opiate and 5-HT2a receptors to test and ultimately dampen his own 'enlightenment' states.
interesting_sentence: "Tried to switch off \"enlightenment\" with several medications which adress different brain receptors. Unfortunately successful." —LocoAustriaco
helpful_sentence: "A stage is, by definition, not something you can enter and exit at will. It is, rather, a phase of development that one has either attained or not. A state, on the other hand, is a transitory phenomenon." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Meditators curious about Actualism/witness states, tummo and pure land practices, and the neurochemistry of self-transcendent states will find a uniquely detailed, experiment-driven account.

author: marcpiano
summary: Documents a 9-day Mahasi-style noting retreat that triggers intense kundalini vibrations, bliss, and visionary strobing, followed by a crash into irritability, misery, and aversion to practice back home.
interesting_sentence: "Then suddenly there was awareness of a flash, a very sharp inhalation of air through the nose and the entire body suddenly seemed to jerk and I was instantly awake and out of the meditation." —marcpiano
helpful_sentence: "I discussed this with a dhamma friend who advised this is just what heppens - some insights will stay and some will fade and mindfulness with gradually start to dull as more gross dhammas occupy awareness. The important thing is not to cling to them." —marcpiano
who_should_read: Yogis working the Mahasi noting technique on an intensive retreat who want a moment-by-moment account of A&P into early dark-night phenomenology.

author: mpavoreal
summary: A decade-plus noting practitioner logs daily 30-90 minute sits through relentless IT work stress, migraines, and an SSRI trial, cycling through dark-night-like discouragement while working with Kenneth Folk on the Wheel and visual-strobing techniques toward 1st path.
interesting_sentence: "I never thought I'd devote an hour a day come h or high water for 10+ years to discover that I'm a chronic yogi and that my learning challenges have followed me into meditation practice." —mpavoreal
helpful_sentence: "We aren't noting to get to a place where it's easier to note. We're just noting. There is no second step." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners juggling demanding careers and chronic illness who want a candid, long-form noting journal spanning years of dark-night difficulty.

author: mpavoreal
summary: Detailed daily noting reports moving from scattered technique-switching to sustained 1st-gear noting under Kenneth Folk's guidance, tracking nana speculation, migraines, and insomnia-driven night sits.
interesting_sentence: "It looked like experience could all be objectified from a space of no movement." —mpavoreal
helpful_sentence: "However wonderful formal metta practice is, it has the potential to distract you from noting and thus diffuse some of your hard-won momentum. Keep noting." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Pre-stream-entry noting practitioners who want a granular, honest account of nana speculation complicated by real-world illness and insomnia.

author: mdaf30
summary: A post-4th-path practitioner tracks lingering heart-chakra pain, kundalini circulation, and an emergent inner light while swapping 2nd-gear noting for devotional chanting and reflecting on Kashmir Shaivite tantra.
interesting_sentence: "Things change, and the spiritual path is nothing if not one long mood swing." —mdaf30
helpful_sentence: "It seems that the somewhat labored, forceful concentration I was engaged in was actually causing the nanas to arise; a product of contracted attention itself." —mdaf30
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners past 4th path curious how emotional-energetic work and devotional chanting continue to deepen realization post-awakening.

author: mdaf30
summary: A Tantric Hindu practitioner with a prior kundalini awakening works with Kenneth Folk's gear/jhana model, undergoes two distinct energetic "pop" breakthroughs at the third eye and crown, and wrestles with persistent heart-chakra contraction while debating his own path level.
interesting_sentence: "I once had someone tell me that I was the kind of person who liked to bang his head repeatedly against a wall just to make sure that it was a wall." —mdaf30
helpful_sentence: "Mark, whenever you feel frustrated and helpless in a higher gear, switch to a lower gear. Frustration is the cue that it's time to downshift." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners blending Hindu Tantric/kundalini backgrounds with Buddhist path maps who want to see how a gear/jhana-based diagnostic framework gets applied to intense energetic breakthroughs.

author: meekan
summary: Logs several formal sits, including an 8-hour day of sitting and walking, marked by recurring itching, expansive light-filled states, low mood, and one episode of intense fear that subsides into pleasantness by the end of the sit.
interesting_sentence: "Sitting bull is just a lot of S.B." —meekan
helpful_sentence: "Perhaps on some level still trying to avoid them through accepting" —meekan
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in a candid, unpolished record of a long retreat-style sit including transient fear and light phenomena.

author: meekan
summary: Meekan chronicles months of daily Mahasi-style aloud noting practice — tracking recurring 'monster itches,' strobing visual phenomena, and long stretches of dark-night-like sadness and aversion — while receiving direct coaching from Kenneth Folk on technique and pacing, without ever confirming a path attainment.
interesting_sentence: "Where is that weasel, and when will it go pop?" —meekan
helpful_sentence: "All you have to do is note. There is no second step. The noting is the thing that will set you free." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Yogis doing sustained Mahasi noting practice who want a candid, blow-by-blow account of itch/pain objects, strobing light phenomena, and the discouragement of stalling below A&P.

author: meekan
summary: Logs many long noting sessions (up to two hours) cycling through sadness, chaos, light, and back pain, including a reported 'near miss' total sensory blackout, while remaining uncertain whether he is close to stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "And I may have had a near miss a couple of weeks ago with a total blackout, but what's missing..?" —meekan
helpful_sentence: "path moments just need one moment of 100% mindfulness and usually happen when you are very relaxed and in the transition from daydream/trance back to mindfulness." —betawave
who_should_read: Noting practitioners who want a granular, blow-by-blow diary of long sits cycling through dukkha nanas toward equanimity.

author: Meganfunck
summary: A psychedelic-catalyzed spiritual awakening account covering kundalini-like energetic symptoms, an ayahuasca-triggered call to meditate, a Goenka-style vipassana retreat with an out-of-body episode, and subsequent noting practice amid anxiety, jealousy and disordered-eating urges.
interesting_sentence: "I ended up at this sort of astral bar or something like that and recognized this native american man immediately, I went up to him and conversed with him like I knew him." —Meganfunck
helpful_sentence: "I'd suggest you work on noting and journal your sittings here in conjunction with that." —mumuwu
who_should_read: Meditators curious how psychedelic experiences can trigger classic kundalini/A&P phenomena and segue into formal noting practice.

author: Bill F
summary: Documents the first two days of an intensive somatic Metta technique (per Ajahn Maha Chatchai via Bhante Sujato) that builds concentration on loving-kindness phrases through to jhana and a felt sense of impersonal, pervasive metta.
interesting_sentence: "There is a real sense of wonder, and presence, and openness. Maybe that's love." —Bill F
helpful_sentence: "Once the attention can be consistently sustained upon the jhana resulting from the previous five steps for a period of 20 minutes, move on to next person." —Bill F
who_should_read: Practitioners wanting a concrete, step-by-step somatic metta technique that builds toward jhana.

author: telecaster
summary: A post-stream-entry practitioner repeatedly cultivates on-demand fruitions via breath noting and eye-rolling energy surges up the spine, cycling through equanimity and brief dark-night dips while struggling to access concentration jhanas and tracking alcohol's effect on practice momentum.
interesting_sentence: "So after enlightenment, you are walking around in a cloud of dust, but you are also sharing molecules with the dust cloud." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "don't note and disembed, instead find one of the sensations that is stable and pleasing and focus attention on it." —RonCrouch
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry meditators curious about on-demand fruitions and the awkward early push toward concentration jhanas will find a candid, detailed blow-by-blow account here.

author: telecaster
summary: Telecaster (Mike) describes shifting from goal-seeking to a 'no fixed view' surrender-based vipassana practice, reporting reduced physical pain, a tentative equanimity self-diagnosis, and recurring investigation of a self-generated 'image of me,' interwoven with quotes from Krishnamurti, Bodhidharma, Suzuki Roshi, and Kenneth Folk.
interesting_sentence: "It's kind of like Dorothy seeing the truth about the Wizard of Oz as just a man behind some curtains except there is no wizard back there anywhere - at least not that i've found yet." —telecaster
helpful_sentence: "After building a good deal of momentum in your sitting practice, step back from objects and let the mind remain attentive without settling on anything." —awouldbehipster
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to a devotional, surrender-based approach who want an informal, humor-laced record of investigating the constructed sense of self alongside classic non-dual quotes.

author: telecaster
summary: Telecaster (Mike) logs months of rise/fall/stop noting practice marked by strong energy surges up his spine, a brief kasina experiment with a cereal bowl, and stretches of high equanimity, while Kenneth Folk tracks his progress and eventually speculates he quietly passed first path.
interesting_sentence: "In Mike Monson's case, the most likely scenario is that he got 1st Path (stream entry) a couple of weeks ago, was in the review phase for awhile, and is now in the early, pre-A&P nanas (#s 1-3) of the new cycle, i.e., the cycle that will eventually lead to 2nd Path." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "You want to max out your processing power with massive cognitive load while noting, because when you do you don't have any time to suffer." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Yogis who want a candid, detailed noting-practice diary showing how Kenneth Folk distinguishes equanimity phenomenology from a possible unrecognized stream entry.

author: cloudsfloatby
summary: Near-daily vipassana log by 'cloudsfloatby' (MikeR), a student of Ron Crouch, tracking rapid cycling between re-observation and equanimity, a confirmed second-path fruition after his cat killed baby birds, and subsequent jhana access and subtle repeated cessations.
interesting_sentence: "The story began...'I am...'- but there is no 'I' separate from the totality of experience." —cloudsfloatby
helpful_sentence: "If the first few were like driving over a cliff the subsequent ones were like driving over a garden hose." —cmarti
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry yogis wanting a detailed, near-daily map of cycling through dark night, equanimity, and jhana territory toward second and third path.

author: telecaster
summary: Telecaster (Mike Monson) shifts focus from vipassana noting toward cultivating samatha jhana access, reports repeated fruitions and 'iron skull cap' energy sensations at the crown, and works with Kenneth Folk and Owen Becker to figure out whether he's reached second or third path.
interesting_sentence: "You just have to be the tank and the fish and the aquarium light, all at once." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "Maximize cognitive load through noting. When all of your processing power is used up by the noting, you have nothing left with which to suffer or wander." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Stream-enterers grappling with samatha jhana development and path-identification uncertainty will find Kenneth Folk's and Owen Becker's detailed technical replies especially clarifying.

author: marazi
summary: A 22-year-old college student, 'marazi' (Moshe), reports emerging from three months of dark night into equanimity during a 10-day Goenka retreat, then works out how to blend Goenka-style body scanning with Mahasi noting technique afterward.
interesting_sentence: "This is not your pain, this is the pain of the world, do you wish to hold it?" —marazi
helpful_sentence: "Whether you are sitting like a buddha statue with sick samadi or restless with a wandering mind both of these situations are opportunities for dis-embedding and thus enlightenment." —ClaytonL
who_should_read: New practitioners considering a Goenka retreat who want an honest account of struggling with body-scanning technique before finding equanimity.

author: mumuwu
summary: An extraordinarily detailed multi-year KFD journal tracking kasina and jhana practice, Mahasi-style noting, and eye-flicker/nose-pinch techniques through repeated A&P-to-equanimity cycles and cessations, culminating in path claims confirmed by Kenneth Folk up through a self-reported 4th path, after which the author blends in direct-mode/actualism practice to chase PCEs and dissolve the sense of a separate self.
interesting_sentence: "I am a lone, aging dynamic object instantiated on a universal-size multicomputer. My reference count just reached zero a few millennia ago; soon I will be deleted. Is there any hope?" —mumuwu
helpful_sentence: "If you keep the 'cognitive load' high, which means that you use up most or all of the processing power of your mind by challenging yourself to note accurately and continuously, there is no processing power left for suffering or wandering." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Yogis serious about mapping the physioenergetic/jhana territory in fine-grained detail will find an unusually rigorous, multi-year model of how kasina, noting, and direct-mode/actualism practices interlock across the entire path.

author: PeonyIris
summary: PeonyIris logs a series of 40-45 minute four-foundations noting sits (plus a walking/chanting session at a meditation center) that surface repeated waves of grief, rage, and confusion alongside pleasant body sensations, then shifts into Kenneth Folk-guided "Who am I?" self-inquiry that produces vivid jewel-colored visual fields.
interesting_sentence: "it punches arm through glass." —PeonyIris
helpful_sentence: "The bystander is a 1st Gear practice, meaning that it looks at the objects that arise in the mind." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Meditators using four-foundations noting who want a candid, moment-by-moment account of raw grief and rage surfacing alongside bystander/witness self-inquiry.

author: N.ga
summary: N.ga, a relative beginner, logs breath-counting and sound-based noting practice, describes a two-year-old A&P experience followed by a persistent dark-night-like malaise and waning interest in day-to-day life, and receives extensive community commentary on self-inquiry, ego-hiding, and equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "To use meditation as a way to have more pleasant experiences and less unpleasant experiences is like stepping over dollars to pick up pennies." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "When you sit, keep about 25-30% of your attention on the preferred object, which is usually the breath at the nose or the abdomen." —awouldbehipster
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate meditators stuck in post-A&P dark-night malaise and hobby disengagement who want peer and teacher perspectives on self-inquiry and equanimity.

author: nadavspi
summary: Post-4th-path journal tracking direct-mode emotional grounding, skandha metta, self-inquiry into 'who knows the witness,' and a tummo breath-retention experiment, culminating a year later in a further deepening described as emptiness and loss of agency.
interesting_sentence: "It's like an endless free fall into the abyss." —nadavspi
helpful_sentence: "Emotions are events like chemical releases, neuronal activity, and macroscopic, observable behavior like facial expressions, emotional outbursts, etc. Feelings, on the other hand, are the subjective experience of those emotions." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Post-4th-path practitioners curious about integrating direct-mode emotional grounding, tummo, and non-dual self-inquiry into daily life.

author: nadavspi
summary: A meticulous, near-daily noting journal tracking recurring itching/vibration phenomenology through one-day retreats and claimed attainments of 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and finally a tentative 4th path over about two years.
interesting_sentence: "I'm becoming more convinced that this was 4th path but it's way too soon to be sure." —nadavspi
helpful_sentence: "It's all about the accuracy, the continuity, and the duration of the noting, so it doesn't matter how you sit, whether you are sitting, standing, or walking, or what phenomena arise during the session." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Meditators wanting a rigorous, blow-by-blow noting-based account of progressing through all four paths within roughly two years.

author: Serenamay
summary: Returning to noting practice after a months-long break due to chronic fatigue syndrome, Serenamay does a short overnight retreat, rereads MCTB, and works on speeding up her noting toward Kenneth's suggested one-observation-per-second pace.
interesting_sentence: "During the few months of no meditating I was aware of gradually getting magnetized towards the 'meniscus' of life and losing 'depth' and this felt uncomfortable." —Serenamay
helpful_sentence: "I would just say to myself 'gather' and then I could pull back into centre." —Serenamay
who_should_read: Meditators restarting practice after a hiatus, especially those managing a chronic illness, who want an encouraging account of easing back into noting.

author: NickPi
summary: NickPi logs daily samatha-vipassana sits with detailed jhana markers (3rd-eye tension, throat energy, visual flashing) and out-loud noting recordings, while describing a months-long plateau he identifies as Equanimity both on and off the cushion.
interesting_sentence: "it seems that my laptop shares my EQ" —NickPi
helpful_sentence: "You're exactly right, you can't avoid it, only note it. But that's still worthwhile, even if it doesn't feel like it" —Rob_Mtl
who_should_read: Yogis with strong jhana access who feel stuck in Equanimity and want validation that off-cushion momentum matters as much as on-cushion sits.

author: NickP
summary: Across months of daily jhana-and-noting practice culminating in a 4-day Gaia House retreat, NickP tracks recurring energetic crown/third-eye markers and a series of ambiguous fruition-like 'blips' that his teacher Ron eventually confirms as Stream Entry.
interesting_sentence: "I don't want to die!" —NickP
helpful_sentence: "You'll continue to manifest many of your old patterns for quite some time, well into later paths (and, as they tell me, beyond)." —JLaurelC
who_should_read: Practitioners doing daily jhana-plus-noting practice who want a granular, honest account of the confusing lead-up to a confirmed Stream Entry.

author: NikolaiStephenHalay
summary: Post-4th-path practitioner details a jhana-based 'letting go' method that reliably carries him through the eight jhanas into nirodha samapatti, alongside nama/rupa noting and sustained investigation of the 'I AM' sense as just another arising object.
interesting_sentence: "There's no subject whatsoever in anybody's experience in my current subject to change opinion." —NikolaiStephenHalay
helpful_sentence: "I will always feel a subtle tension in the way that one-pointedness and sense of effort conditions the 1st jhana. I focus on that tension created by those factors. And I allow them to drop away." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Post-4th-path practitioners wanting a granular, jargon-light account of using jhana as a letting-go ladder into nirodha samapatti.

author: multiarms
summary: multiarms opens a new log describing a Zen/Taoist background and a Liberation Unleashed non-dual awakening, then reports 35-40 minute daily sits under Ron Crouch that move from A&P into the misery ñana of Dark Night.
interesting_sentence: "I now have access to 1st and 2nd jhana and can sit for prolonged periods without going insane." —multiarms
helpful_sentence: "I usually can identify the A&P within about 10-15 minutes, and then pass into DN." —multiarms
who_should_read: New KFD noting practitioners with an Advaita/non-dual background curious how that experience interacts with the classic ñana cycle.

author: Nic_M
summary: Transitioning from two years of Samatha practice into noting after MCTB, she reports A&P-style kundalini surges, crown pressure, and inconclusive 'fruitions' during jhana exploration, alongside informal introspection on the imagistic structure of her thoughts.
interesting_sentence: "Welcome to the kundalinibursts, thirdeyepressure, metalskullcap whatthehellishappeningtome club!" —NikolaiStephenHalay
helpful_sentence: "Not sure about the best thing to do here, but at the moment I switch to silent noting, and if I start getting lost in thought I revert to the labelling." —Nic_M
who_should_read: Practitioners moving from concentration-only Samatha into noting who want a candid account of ambiguous kundalini-like A&P phenomena.

author: NigelThompson
summary: A brief opening journal describing 30-90 second noting sessions squeezed into a busy day, including noting physical sensations while carrying his toddler son and a prior evening of mantra and candle concentration.
interesting_sentence: "A door to a door to a door" —NigelThompson
helpful_sentence: "Great resolve to keep using your time even if it's chunked up into small bits. Just like small amounts of money, it adds up!" —JAdamG
who_should_read: Busy parents or beginners curious how to fit very short, frequent noting sessions around a demanding daily schedule.

author: Nicobobo
summary: A pre-path yogi spends months trying to stabilize first jhana via breath-at-the-nostrils concentration, repeatedly unsure if he has reached access concentration or jhana, before shifting back toward freestyle noting practice.
interesting_sentence: "Are aversion, desire, and joy not emotions?" —Russell
helpful_sentence: "Jhana is the mind relaxing into the object of focus... if one expects anything, jhana will not happen." —TravisGM
who_should_read: Anapanasati practitioners stuck on the access-concentration-versus-jhana threshold who want an honest, blow-by-blow account of that cutting edge.

author: NikolaiStephenHalay
summary: A 9-year Goenka practitioner who claims stream entry then a fast move into 2nd path spends months mapping the 8 jhanas and mysterious post-8th absorptions via Kenneth Folk's Witness/jhanic-arc method, riding intense third-eye and kundalini-style energetics through a grueling 2nd-path dark night, before ending on a sustained, unconfirmed non-clinging shift.
interesting_sentence: "My theory is that the \"hole\" where the mind can dip in and out of nibbana is not big enough to stay in, which would be the case for anagamis and above as they have blasted a path, over time, for the energy to flow up the body to the crown without hinderance and thus have a bigger \"hole\" to dip into. Hahaha! I'm just making this stuff up as I go." —NikolaiStephenHalay
helpful_sentence: "The review phase happens with each cycle, including those mini-cycles that happen so frequently for someone who has 2nd Path. That's why the only reliable way to diagnose 3rd Path is the ability to access either nirodha samapatti or one or more of the Pure Land jhanas." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners deep in 2nd path who want a granular, energetically-detailed account of jhana exploration, Witness practice, and the long grind of a 2nd-path dark night.

author: NikolaiStephenHalay
summary: Nikolai cycles through detailed cross-path fruition and jhanic-arc/red-kasina concentration work, claims 4th path mid-thread, then adopts Kenneth Folk's 'direct mode' Actual-Freedom-influenced PCE practice, which triggers a heated community debate over post-4th-path practice.
interesting_sentence: "I even posted 10 minutes after getting 4th path for goodness sake." —NikolaiStephenHalay
helpful_sentence: "The highest standard I know is the end of suffering. Are you suffering in this moment? If the answer is "yes, I am suffering," then you know what to do: put your head down and practice." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about granular cross-path fruition phenomenology and the controversial KFD-community 'direct mode'/PCE experiment will find a candid, technique-rich first-person account here.

author: nyingje
summary: A newly-mentored student recounts her first Skype session with Kenneth Folk and early noting practice marked by intense energetic sensations (pulsing, fluttering eyes, heat, tingling), plus a breakthrough moment of vivid tactile awareness during a walking meditation on a difficult, anger-filled day.
interesting_sentence: "For the \"first time\" I actually felt the air on my hands as I walked around the park." —nyingje
helpful_sentence: "Remember to keep up the noting with gentleness and constancy because the tortoise always wins this race." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: New students beginning formal noting practice who want to see how intense energetic sensations and mood swings show up in the earliest days of mentorship with Kenneth Folk.

author: obobinde
summary: A ten-year on-and-off Mahasi-style noter logs months of daily 45-120 minute sits centered on the rising and falling of the abdomen, wrestling with chronic leg numbness/pain, experimenting with out-loud noting and posture changes on forum advice, while reporting he has never crossed A&P despite a recent 15-day retreat.
interesting_sentence: "It seems i'm a slow learner as i've never crossed A&P, probably because i've haven't been steady enough." —obobinde
helpful_sentence: "For example, are you VERY frustrated, or A LITTLE BIT frustrated? Even just knowing that, you are objectifying that feeling even more, and it becomes more interesting to note, because you see that your sense frustration gets more and less and more and less intense, second after second." —Rob_Mtl
who_should_read: Committed Mahasi-style noting practitioners frustrated by physical pain and a plateaued practice who want concrete tips on out-loud noting, posture fixes, and daily-life carryover.

author: omnipleasant
summary: A father of young children logs over two years of near-daily hour-long noting practice (KFD 1st gear, quadruplets, nose-pinch jhana, self-inquiry, mahamudra listening), documenting recurring kriyas, light fluctuations, and possible cessation blips while cycling repeatedly through A&P, dullness/dark-night periods, and equanimity, never quite settling on whether he has attained stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "You will find that that scariest monsters in your mind can be allies in your own awakening as soon as you note them." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "If you are noting in a way that requires all of your attention, your mind will not wander and you will not suffer." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners juggling family life and daily noting practice who want a granular, honest, multi-year account of kriyas, dullness, and the doubt-laden cycle toward possible stream entry.

log: On the Dharma Bus: Liam's Practice Thread
author: LiamO'Sullivan
summary: A nurse with a Thai Forest and MCTB background logs rapid, accelerating noting sessions performed during train commutes, tracking speed and inclusiveness of noting while preparing for an upcoming two-week Mahasi retreat.
interesting_sentence: "The faster I noted, the top half of my body rocked back and forth more." —LiamO'Sullivan
helpful_sentence: "The effort to use labels based on the four sattipathanas blocked out distracting mindnoise." —LiamO'Sullivan
who_should_read: Practitioners wanting to see how fast, inclusive noting practice can be woven into a commute rather than requiring a quiet cushion.

author: Opera
summary: Works to establish a daily 10-40 minute noting practice while cataloguing eyelid visuals, breath and belly sensations, and one alarming sit where chest pain, heart pounding, panic, nausea and dizziness recur, echoing a past anxiety episode.
interesting_sentence: "the pain and the pounding located at the heart increased in intensity to a level that panicked me" —Opera
helpful_sentence: "Try to notice what happens before and after the thought, that widens the scope of your attention." —PabloP
who_should_read: Beginners trying to establish a consistent daily practice who want a candid, unfiltered account of noting difficulties and anxiety-related side effects during meditation.

author: OwenBecker
summary: Already post-4th-path at the start, Owen Becker rides a self-devised 'jhanic arc' up to the 13th jhana using breath and candle-kasina concentration, experiments with Kenneth Folk's 'direct mode' and PCEs, and by the log's end describes a further shift into an emotionless, memory-scrambled, seemingly Actual-Freedom-like state alongside fellow practitioner Nikolai.
interesting_sentence: "No emotions at all. Figured I'd make that clear." —OwenBecker
helpful_sentence: "If someone were holding your head under water, you would be obsessed with doing what ever it took to get the next breath of air. For solving the problem of suffering, you need to have the same kind of commitment." —OwenBecker
who_should_read: Yogis already past 4th path who want a granular, community-vetted account of jhana/kasina technique, direct-mode/PCE experimentation, and the further shift toward an emotionless, Actual-Freedom-like state.

author: OwenBecker
summary: Daily KFD log tracking Owen from 2nd-path dark-night cycling through repeated 13-jhana arc traversals, nirodha samapatti, and Kenneth-Folk-confirmed anagami (3rd path), culminating in a persistent perceptual shift where the sense of a separate self drops away and chronic fear dissolves.
interesting_sentence: "There is nothing in the universe that isn't pure awareness, even the chump that thinks he's me." —OwenBecker
helpful_sentence: "What helps me is to bring the practice back into my body. Breath with your whole body. Relax into what is." —awouldbehipster
who_should_read: Vipassana/noting practitioners near 2nd-3rd path who want a detailed, mentor-guided account of the jhanic arc, pure land jhanas, and the final shift into a centerless, self-less perspective.

author: PabloP
summary: A KFD First Gear noting practitioner logs roughly five months of near-daily out-loud/silent noting sessions (plus Four Foundations Noting and Kenneth Folk's Aversion Parameter Evaluation), repeatedly cycling through A&P, Dark Night, and Low Equanimity amid vivid chakra-like energy flows, creepy visions, and job/relocation stress, without ever stabilizing past Low Equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "Finally I surrender to the energy, went up through the back and down to the chest where it spread, and a second later the legs ejected energy like missile rockets for a minute." —PabloP
helpful_sentence: "We’ll define aversion as any sense whatsoever that you’d like this moment’s experience to be other than it is." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Noting practitioners who want a granular, phenomenology-rich account of ñana cycling, energetic/chakra sensations, and Kenneth Folk's aversion-tracking technique.

author: PEJN
summary: A near-daily KFD noting practitioner logs months of sits cycling repeatedly through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity, experimenting with posture, TM, gym and walking noting, and deliberate 'resolutions', culminating in an intense energetic event (post #70) that Kenneth Folk flags as possibly stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "Then all of me was sucked up in the head for a few seconds. Pretty dark in there. Conscious all the time." —PEJN
helpful_sentence: "The big insight for me at 4th path (5th stage in Kenneth's new model), was that no phenomenon has a higher status than some other phenomenon." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Noting practitioners curious how TM, samatha, and open-eyes noting compare, and how deliberate intentions ('resolutions') can shape which insight stage arises in a sit.

author: PEJN
summary: A daily jhana- and noting-focused log tracking repeated A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity cycling and multiple fruitions after first path, culminating in a tentative second-path claim confirmed with Kenneth Folk and further cycles pursued via jhanic-arc, arupa jhana, Mahamudra, and Pure Land practice.
interesting_sentence: "It felt like going totally out of control 'Not without a doctor besides me' , I thought!" —PEJN
helpful_sentence: "Recognize that any aimlessness and restlessness that may arise is a result of insufficient surrender. There is nothing for you to do, ever." —villum
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry jhana practitioners curious how repeated A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity cycling and deep jhana/arupa work can play out on the way toward second and third path.

author: PEJN
summary: Chronicles a year of post-2nd-path practice cycling through jhana-arc work, kriyas, chakra sensations, and repeated quit-and-return phases, while experimenting with Mahamudra Noting and Shinzen Young's 'Do Nothing' under guidance from Kenneth Folk and Antero.
interesting_sentence: "There is aversion against looking at the aversion!" —PEJN
helpful_sentence: "There is this quality of unstained knowing that is like a bubble of infinite inner silence that is never affected by the turbulence." —Antero
who_should_read: Post-first-path practitioners curious about integrating jhana work into daily life and a candid account of the on-again-off-again motivation that follows deeper insight cycles.

author: doviende
summary: A complete beginner establishes a twice-daily 20-30 minute breath-concentration practice, reports tingly sensations in his fingers, and is redirected by forum members toward simple noting instead of vibration-chasing.
interesting_sentence: "Found a buzzy / tingly sensation in the fingers and the hand, like a toned-down version of pins-and-needles when your foot goes to sleep." —doviende
helpful_sentence: "If you want to start vipassana, I would begin with simple noting." —Russell
who_should_read: Absolute beginners deciding between concentration and noting practice who want to see real-time forum guidance on getting started.

author: doviende
summary: A brand-new meditator tries walking meditation, breath focus, and early noting practice over his first week, oscillating between good concentration and frustrating mind-wandering.
interesting_sentence: "It seems like there's a certain undescribable thing that I can \"do\" to get more concentrated...hard to explain." —doviende
helpful_sentence: "A wandering mind doesn't mean that your meditation isn't going well. Rather than trying to make it stop, welcome it and continually return to your method, no matter how many times you have to do it." —nadavspi
who_should_read: Brand-new meditators curious what the first week of noting and concentration practice, doubts included, actually feels like.

author: haquan
summary: A longtime non-dual practitioner (haquan/"David") narrates a permanent shift in consciousness at age 18 from mystical fruition experiences, describing dissolved ego boundaries, intense direct empathy, cessation of inner monologue, and ongoing practice of "open-handed magick," with the forum debating whether this constitutes 4th path.
interesting_sentence: "Enlightenment is for pussies in my crowd." —haquan
helpful_sentence: "You don't have to experience everyone's emotions. You don't have to feel the emotions of people walking down the street you don't know." —haquan
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about first-person phenomenology of non-dual, ego-dissolved awareness and how it maps onto 4th path claims.

author: Pirkku
summary: A beginner practicing the lightning rod technique and binary self-referencing/no-self-referencing noting reports a felt sense of self localizing around the eyes and moments of concept-free perception, while limiting practice intensity due to mental health issues.
interesting_sentence: "I was for example walking down the stair just now and my feet looked like alien feet popping up into my field of vision." —Pirkku
helpful_sentence: "The binary noting was very beneficial as it was seen quite clearly and rapidly that self-referencing hurt, quite badly, and not self-ferencing was defenitely not as painful." —Pirkku
who_should_read: Beginners managing mental health limitations who want a candid look at using noting and the lightning rod technique to locate the sense of self.

author: BrunoLoff
summary: A grad student vows to reach arhatship by a fixed deadline, logging months of 4+ hour daily noting practice, kundalini-like spasms and mudras, a painful head-pressure 'centerpoint' obsession, and eventual burnout that forces him to abandon the intensive schedule.
interesting_sentence: "Sit. your. butt. down. and. NOTE." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "If you cannot note, it's because you are absorbed. Absorbed equals embeddedness. Embeddedness is the opposite of enlightenment." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Intensely driven practitioners who want a cautionary, blow-by-blow account of pushing noting practice to the point of diminishing returns.

log: P's practice thread: a new experience...?
author: Privacy_Dude
summary: A lapsed-then-renewed practitioner intensifies anapanasati practice and experiences a brief eyes-open non-dual merging with the room during a sit, followed by effortless extended sitting and a novel 10hz vibration sensation in subsequent sessions.
interesting_sentence: "And then at its peak, for the merest fraction of a second, there wasn't a 'me'." —Privacy_Dude
helpful_sentence: "No matter what is happening, the key is to pay attention to WHAT is happening right now, and not try to make it be any particular way." —giragirasol
who_should_read: Meditators curious about eyes-open non-dual glimpses arising from anapanasati/jhana practice and how to interpret them on the map.

author: putthajana
summary: A Goenka-trained beginner logs near-daily Mahasi-style noting sessions, often on train commutes, tracking persistent painful sensations in the forehead/third-eye region and experimenting with noting speed, aloud versus silent noting, and pairing techniques to build momentum.
interesting_sentence: "It is interesting to see the extent of struggle, anguish and moaning that the mind goes to in order to avoid pain." —putthajana
helpful_sentence: "You should note whatever is arising in the moment...and that includes the mind state of 'looking for' or 'searching' or 'wanting' or 'craving'." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Aspiring stream-enterers wanting a relatable, blow-by-blow account of daily noting practice fit around work and commuting.

author: rachelgraber
summary: A beginning meditator struggles with restlessness, chronic back pain, and unfocused daydreaming during hour-long sits, gradually refining her noting vocabulary with coaching from more experienced forum members.
interesting_sentence: "feeling of consciousness being split in two by a revolving vertical disk (both a visual disk and a sensation)" —rachelgraber
helpful_sentence: "fear, expectation, pressure, aching, pain, aversion, itching, tickling, shifting, calm, silence, rocking, straining, stretching, movement, planning thoughts, tired" —Russell
who_should_read: New meditators struggling with restlessness and vague noting who want concrete examples of simplifying and speeding up their labeling.

author: lhamo
summary: Describes a disciplined six-day silent retreat conducted at home with her husband, culminating in a fruition that Kenneth Folk assessed as Third Path based on newly accessed jhanas above the eighth.
interesting_sentence: "I completed a Path during the retreat, much to my own surprise." —lhamo
helpful_sentence: "This happens when a yogi finishes up what I call \"review phase B,\" in which each sitting involves moving up and down through every jhana previously attained, beginning with the first, and begins a new \"developmental phase,\" where the yogi has to painstaking work her way up through the Ã±anas again." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Householders considering a self-directed retreat who want a real-world account of balancing family logistics with intensive practice.

author: Richard12314
summary: A householder practitioner alternates concentration jhana work (tracking third-eye pressure and a nimitta) with systematic Shinzen Young-style noting in daily life, culminating in a self-identified A&P marked by jewel-toned lights, fine vibrations, and heightened sexual energy.
interesting_sentence: "It's amazing that you can have an argument in your mind with someone you don't know. You date, get married, fight and get divorced in 15 seconds LOL." —Richard12314
helpful_sentence: "The sequence of meanings is more important that the details of sensation, feeling, thought, etc." —jhsaintonge
who_should_read: Practitioners wanting a granular, technique-by-technique account of building jhana concentration alongside daily noting on the way to A&P.

author: ricketybridge
summary: Chronicles the lead-up to a self-guided weekend retreat, alternating jhana practice with pure-awareness noting and reporting body-wide pressure and an intense fear response identified as the fear nana, alongside a struggle with weekend procrastination.
interesting_sentence: "Sometimes I feel like I'm going to die in that moment, when I'm just lying on my bed, even though I know that's ridiculous lol." —ricketybridge
helpful_sentence: "even people who are enlightened still experience personal quirks; enlightenment is not a magic bullet that makes us perfect." —JLaurelC
who_should_read: Practitioners nervous about approaching stream entry who want reassurance that fear-based phenomena and retreat-prep procrastination are normal.

author: Rob_Mtl
summary: A second-path practitioner details his 'Witness' and jhanic-arc practice, describing a recurring 'cartoon image' of self tied to facial muscle tension that he and others identify as the core figure/ground insight, alongside self-inquiry experiments and early nirodha-samapatti sensations.
interesting_sentence: "Gentlemen, that's the whole enchilada as far as advancing through the Theravada paths is concerned." —cmarti
helpful_sentence: "I'd let awareness rest on a sound until the sound ended and completely disappeared from awareness. I'd then let awareness choicelessly come to rest on another sound. Less than a minute of this would reliably induce a fruition." —andymr
who_should_read: Post-second-path practitioners navigating a long equanimity/dark-night plateau who want detailed phenomenology of Witness practice and self-referential insight work.

author: rocketbuddha
summary: Logs near-daily sits cycling from A&P through dark night to equanimity with frequent 'fruitions,' gradually shifting from noting to a deep concentration practice up through a self-reported 13th jhana, culminating in a caffeine detox and a dependent-origination insight he reads as a possible 4th path shift.
interesting_sentence: "so today it finally dawned on me, dependent origination, yes, I get it - somehow then I was able to stay happy all day and night" —rocketbuddha
helpful_sentence: "In my experience the life style changes you mentioned in post #19 are a big part of integrating the insights that come with paths." —WF566163
who_should_read: Yogis interested in blending noting with concentration/jhana practice who want to see review cycles tracked through successive paths.

author: rswalker
summary: A complete-beginner's first log entry describes a home vipassana sit with strobing light and a swirling tunnel vision plus early self-inquiry into the 'me', after which another practitioner shares Kenneth Folk's detailed noting instructions.
interesting_sentence: "By the time you master this technique, you will be an arahat." —NikolaiStephenHalay
helpful_sentence: "Note once per second, aloud, for the duration of your sitting." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Complete beginners just starting formal noting practice who want to see Kenneth Folk's classic noting instructions applied to a first log entry.

author: rocketbuddha
summary: Chronicles six prior ten-day body-scanning Goenka retreats followed by resigning from a job to sit a 20-day retreat where the author attains stream entry via noting on day six and continues cycling through further fruitions and jhana exploration.
interesting_sentence: "It was very bizarre as I zipped by all nanas after misery." —rocketbuddha
helpful_sentence: "I'll add this: at least in my experience, 'consistency' is a bigger factor than 'intensity' in building momentum." —Rob_Mtl
who_should_read: Goenka-retreat veterans curious how switching from body scanning to Kenneth-Folk-style noting can produce stream entry on an extended retreat.

author: Robwynge
key_words: attention on the act of perceiving
summary: A practitioner new to formal noting experiments with Shinzen Young's 'feel/image/talk' and 'body/sight/sound' categories during nostril-focused breath sits, repeatedly slipping into a pleasant dreamy unfocused state, then receives detailed advice on noting the act of perceiving itself to deepen concentration.
interesting_sentence: "The apprehension of (the apprehension of) perception itself." —NikolaiStephenHalay
helpful_sentence: "You could also become aware of the very act of perceiving the breath...it actually works wonders in maintaining the mind in one spot." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Meditators who keep drifting into a pleasant 'dreamy' unfocused state during breath concentration and want a concrete technique for sharpening attention on the act of perceiving.

author: Rod1
summary: Documents months of twice-daily jhana practice combined with noting, itchy-face ñana markers, and a night of depersonalized 'blank self' cycling with repeatable fruitions that the author confirms as stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "my mind was reaching down to find 'me' and there was nothing there. Really weird, just nothing, no evidence - a blank space." —Rod1
helpful_sentence: "Absorption (jhana) is embracing the illusion of solidity, whereas insight is trying to debunk it." —NickP
who_should_read: Jhana-focused practitioners wanting a month-by-month log of how deep concentration in the four jhanas can be paired with noting to trigger and confirm stream entry.

author: Rod1
summary: Motivated by his father's death, a scientist-yogi methodically maps his climb through samatha jhanas 1 through 5, layers in noting practice during a work break, and arranges Skype coaching with Kenneth Folk after his planned retreat falls through.
interesting_sentence: "My Father died a few months ago and through watching him die I resolved that I need to return to Buddha's teachings and (being a scientist) to try it out seriously regarding liberation so that death is not the panic ridden trauma for me that it was for my father." —Rod1
helpful_sentence: "you could split your sits so that you attain up to 4th jhana, then start noting. Or if you sit twice in one day, have one sit where you work on going beyond 4th jhana, and another doing mostly noting." —JLaurelC
who_should_read: Concentration-focused newcomers who want a methodical, jhana-by-jhana progression log paired with an emerging noting practice.

author: RonCrouch
key_words: Adyashanti's The End of Your World
summary: Post-fourth-path journal in which RonCrouch works to establish Kenneth Folk's 'Direct Mode' body-grounding practice, cycles through recurring mini dark nights and disconnection episodes, and launches a teaching website while navigating his wife's confusion at his changing habits.
interesting_sentence: "You are off the ride, now it's time to embrace the free-fall." —OwenBecker
helpful_sentence: "It's finding the body sensations that occur with the emotions and then just resting attention on the body sensation." —RonCrouch
who_should_read: Meditators past fourth path who want a candid account of post-path integration, emotional grounding practice, and the pull toward teaching.

author: RonCrouch
summary: A three-year concentration practitioner switches to noting/vipassana under Kenneth Folk's guidance, methodically tracking his cycles through A&P, Dark Night (Fear, Re-Observation), and Equanimity via detailed sensation reports until confirmed cessations mark stream entry and he begins stirring toward a second insight cycle.
interesting_sentence: "This one is a quick sudden darkness that comes down from the crown area and is a little like a blind is being pulled down and back up in an instant." —RonCrouch
helpful_sentence: "What was it that happened in that discontinuity between 11th nana and 4th? That was a fruition." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Vipassana beginners transitioning from years of concentration practice who want a clear, systematically mapped account of cycling through the nanas to a confirmed stream entry under close teacher guidance.

author: RonCrouch
summary: Chronicles a rapid sequential progression from stream-entry through second, third, and eventually fourth path, marked by kundalini-like energetic surges, systematic exploration of the arupa and Pure Land jhanas, repeated attempts at Nirodha Samapatti, and a growing 24/7 noting/witness practice amid a new baby, a PhD defense, and a cross-country move.
interesting_sentence: "It feels like beginning the practice all over. I started off with the idea that I would be in some super-state beyond all this. Go figure. Here I am." —RonCrouch
helpful_sentence: "This is the opposite of what happens in the 10th nana, Knowledge of Re-observation, where every time you put your attention on an itch it disappears and pops up somewhere else." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners moving through the post-stream-entry paths who want a granular, cycle-by-cycle account of second, third, and fourth path diagnostics like kundalini surges, Pure Land jhanas, and Nirodha Samapatti.

author: Russell
summary: Russell describes a two-week deepening into existential fear during dark-night practice, noting a puzzling near-absence of his usual anger and anxious reactivity even as he anticipates the next difficult cycle.
interesting_sentence: "I (I'm going to regret saying this) almost look forward to a next strong painful/fearful period because I feel that it is when I can really dig in a work with this stuff." —Russell
helpful_sentence: "When anything at all comes up (thoughts, emotions, etc) I seem to be able to just drop them and things get more spacious in a way." —Russell
who_should_read: Meditators moving through dark-night fear who want a brief, honest account of watching anger and anxiety cease to grip as usual.

author: Aquanin
summary: Chronicles Aquanin's (Russell's) practice from confirmed 2nd path through months of jhana work up to the 8th/9th jhana and Pure Land jhanas, witness-based concentration on a '3rd eye' focal point, repeated near-misses at Nirodha Samapatti via a 'sinking' technique, and an eventual self-declared 3rd path after a long Dark Night-like confusion period.
interesting_sentence: "Almost like if you were trying to grab a greased watermelon." —Aquanin
