helpful_sentence: "When you start to sink in, do not push the button, but try to be the button, merge with it." —Antero.
who_should_read: Yogis deep in jhana practice and post-2nd-path review who want a candid, granular account of Pure Land jhanas, Nirodha Samapatti attempts, and the confusing run-up to 3rd path.

author: Aquanin
summary: Post-3rd-path log tracking rapid cycling between Dark Night anxiety/terror and deep Equanimity, frequent on-demand fruitions and Nirodha Samapatti absorptions, self-inquiry into 'who is aware,' and tentative glimpses of a possible 4th-path shift guided by Kenneth Folk and Chris Marti.
interesting_sentence: "It was almost like I was about to barf up my self!" —Aquanin
helpful_sentence: "Whatever problems arise will be related to the dissonance between what is happening and what you think SHOULD be happening." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Post-3rd-path practitioners navigating intense DN/Equanimity swings and self-inquiry toward possible 4th path will find this log's candid detail and teacher pointers valuable.

author: Aquanin
summary: A father of young kids logs near-daily 20-90 minute noting sits guided remotely by teacher Ron Crouch, cycling through A&P, Dark Night (severe off-cushion anxiety, facial grimacing/twitching, jaw and back pain), and Equanimity before a confirmed Stream Entry, then tracks ambiguous near-misses and bliss-wave fruitions while suspecting he has begun a second path.
interesting_sentence: "Is it bad when my wife ask's me if I'm in the Dark Night again?" —Aquanin
helpful_sentence: "So, after all this effort, it's counter-intuitive to stop making an effort, and yet, that's what you have to do, since if you make effort, that just becomes the object you advert to instead of emptiness." —Rob_Mtl
who_should_read: Practitioners working closely with a personal teacher through their first stream entry who want a highly granular, day-by-day account of nana cycling, near-misses, and post-path uncertainty.

author: Sam12S
summary: Restarts serious vipassana practice using a Shinzen Young-influenced Feel/Hear/See noting style, tracking flashing sensations, knee pain, and a stretch of anxious, unmotivated resistance to sitting.
interesting_sentence: "I was meditaing with my girlfriend and a couple of times I felt like standing up and shouting at her, or putting my head in her lap and crying." —Sam12S
helpful_sentence: "Difficult stages of practice can be some of the best times for new progress...but that doesn't mean you have to like it" —AnthonyYeshe
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate noters using Shinzen Young's Feel/Hear/See style who want an honest account of resistance, boredom, and early dark-night-like restlessness.

author: Sam12S
summary: After early coaching from Ron Crouch, Sam builds a choiceless Mahasi noting practice through inconsistent motivation, then gives a detailed day-by-day account of an 8-day retreat marked by knee/back pain, flickering visuals, and dreamlike confusion but no clear insight breakthrough.
interesting_sentence: "Objects feel like bubbles; if I'm not paying attention I'm either missing them entirely or slipping into the bubble and identifying with it." —Sam12S
helpful_sentence: "Allow yourself to settle into that ringing in your ears, or some other sensation or phenomenon. Get concentrated into it and watch it break up into its component parts. This is essential vipassana." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: First-time retreatants who want an honest, unglamorous log of motivation struggles and physical difficulty during an 8-day Mahasi retreat.

log: Sauntering Down The Endless Path - A Practice Journal
author: TommyMcNally
summary: After retracting a claim to 4th path, Tommy cycles through an intense work-triggered Dark Night with paranoid ideation, then details daily practice mixing kasina jhana, breath noting up through the nyanas, Direct Mode disembedding, and binary self-referencing noting.
interesting_sentence: "This was like full-blown psychosis, the sort of crazy breaks Daniel talks about in MCTB, and I was forced to sit and watch my mind manufacture these elaborate plots and conspiracies against me by people in work." —TommyMcNally
helpful_sentence: "the goal is probably better something like 'i wont stop practicing for a single second' with a flavor of relaxed alertness." —beoman
who_should_read: Practitioners recovering from a premature high-path claim who want a candid account of a work-triggered Dark Night episode alongside detailed jhana-noting hybrid technique.

author: Serenamay
summary: A UK-based mother and piano teacher working closely with Kenneth Folk logs daily vipassana and jhanic-arc sits centered on pulse-tracking and an involuntary eye-blinking mudra, cycling through A&P, dark night nanas, and equanimity while chronic fatigue syndrome repeatedly interrupts her practice.
interesting_sentence: "The body was not a body in the usual sense but a body of light, golden light." —Serenamay
helpful_sentence: "It is the false idea that concentration is preferable to not-concentration that is causing you to suffer." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to KFD's mudra/pulse/blinking techniques and jhanic-arc mapping who want a candid account of early insight-cycle work complicated by chronic illness.

author: Serenamay
summary: Serena transcribes handwritten sitting-meditation notes describing resistance dissolving into calm presence, then a brief non-dual 'bystander' state that lost its usual 'energetic presence' and dissipated after an outside noise, in her second posted journal thread on the KFD forum.
interesting_sentence: "There isn't the same sense of an 'energetic presence' with nondual today as I have experience on previous occasions." —Serenamay
helpful_sentence: "There is concurrently frustration and calm presence, though the calm presence is fore grounded." —Serenamay
who_should_read: Newcomers to KFD-style bystander/non-dual awareness practice curious about early, unpolished journal entries and forum onboarding norms.

author: s.elstob
summary: A highly detailed Mahasi-style noting log tracking four nights of kundalini-triggered swiveling and rocking (identified by Kenneth Folk as A&P), followed by months of granular itching/vibration reports as the poster cycles through the dukkha nanas amid his wife's worsening illness, with the community repeatedly helping him place himself on the map.
interesting_sentence: "The motion became more and more pronounced until I was rocking off my knees and pitching forward until I felt I would be toppled off the stool." —s.elstob
helpful_sentence: "In general, it's best not to put mapping labels on things when you're noting the four foundations of mindfulness." —JLaurelC
who_should_read: Noting practitioners wanting a granular, blow-by-blow account of A&P-triggered kundalini and the itching/vibration texture of the dukkha nanas, with real-time community map-reading.

author: Jackinbox
summary: After a 10-day Myanmar retreat and a later intensive Mahasi retreat, the poster develops severe concentration-triggered spasms he believes mark A&P, then experiments with detailed vs. minimal noting styles and briefly touches a jhana-like state while off ADHD medication.
interesting_sentence: "After about 3 days I started to have severe spasms when I would try to push my concentration, force myself to do noting or at the moment of falling asleep." —Jackinbox
helpful_sentence: "Energy flow and similar thoughts to me are just stories that are added on to basic phenomena." —Jackha
who_should_read: Retreat-going meditators managing ADHD or depression who want a frank account of concentration-induced spasms and comparing detailed vs. minimalist noting styles.

author: OwenBecker
summary: OwenBecker describes a custom metta variant that directs loving-kindness sequentially through the five skandhas (form, thought, feeling, volition, consciousness) to calm the emotional body and support progress toward stage 6 permanent emotional grounding in the 7-stage model.
interesting_sentence: "I don't have to waste time trying to figure out how to direct loving kindness to illusory phenomena." —OwenBecker
helpful_sentence: "Finish off the meditation by noticing the change in the feeling tone. If you want to begin grounding and enter direct mode, now is a particularly good time." —OwenBecker
who_should_read: Practitioners familiar with the KFD 7-stage model who want a concrete metta variant for working through stage 6 emotional grounding via the five skandhas.

author: somefatguy
summary: Reports that an intensive practice of the "four right exertions" (deliberately eliminating unwholesome thoughts and cultivating wholesome ones) has removed chronic chest-centered angst and remorse, though the poster's momentum is faltering and insight practice holds no appeal.
interesting_sentence: "I used to feel intense angst/being at the center of my chest, this is now gone, along with remorse, so that's good." —somefatguy
helpful_sentence: "They are an intense practice dedicated to eliminating unwholesome thoughts and giving rise to wholesome ones." —somefatguy
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to effort-based (right exertion) practice as an alternative to insight noting for cutting through chronic angst and remorse.

author: paulsomerville
summary: A newcomer starting a four-sits-a-day First Gear routine asks whether to practice the four foundations of mindfulness separately or combined, and receives advice to start separate then move to random/paired noting.
interesting_sentence: "My personal favourite has always been random noting (whatever takes the centre stage) and noting pairs and triplets (even quadruplets) when I really want to make sure there is no bandwidth left for mind wandering." —Antero.
helpful_sentence: "It is good to concentrate on the separate foundations of mindfulness in the beginning, but once you have some experience on them I would suggest noting all four in a balanced way." —Antero.
who_should_read: Absolute beginners deciding how to structure the four foundations of mindfulness across daily sits.

author: Antero.
summary: Antero describes moving from 6th jhana access through a 'Pure Witness' practice to a centerless 'Spontaneous Presence,' then lays out a four-step method (clarity of the senses, effortlessness, the transparent undercurrent of knowing, and abiding as presence) for recognizing this knowing quality directly.
interesting_sentence: "When the flashlight of attention was eventually turned to the witness itself, the center point vanished and the observer was dissolved." —Antero.
helpful_sentence: "If one tries to keep it using deliberate mindfulness, naturalness is spoiled by turning it into a conceptual state." —Antero.
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to Dzogchen/Mahamudra-style non-dual pointing-out instructions who want concrete steps for recognizing spontaneous, centerless awareness in daily life.

author: Squeee
summary: Squee logs two years of two-hour daily noting practice—including kasina, anapana, noting-aloud coached by Kenneth Folk, and the eye-flickering sweet-spot technique—reporting vibratory 'watcher shaking,' a Kenneth-identified A&P episode, and later a possible fruition-like discontinuity of consciousness.
interesting_sentence: "Squee, stop doing so much. You don't have to do anything or force anything or look for anything. You seem to be having an A&P experience. Just sit there and let it happen." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "Try just noting whatever is centre stage. Wherever the mind falls. And give yourself a second or two in between noting to really note with as much detail as possible." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Dedicated daily practitioners curious about noting-aloud, kasina work, and the phenomenology of A&P vibrations and near-fruition discontinuities.

author: someguy77
summary: Someguy77 chronicles months of daily noting ranging from brief sits to marathon multi-hour sessions, describing persistent crown/forehead tingling, a long plateau in equanimity, a distinct dark-night episode, and debates with fellow posters over retreat-level intensity versus consistent daily practice.
interesting_sentence: "I dreamed that I got stream entry, but it looks like it was just a fairly vivid A+P experience." —someguy77
helpful_sentence: "I believe, just based on my own experience, that consistency is the key, not Olympian meditation periods lasting hours and hours." —cmarti
who_should_read: Meditators stuck in a long equanimity plateau who want reassurance and practical tips on balancing retreat-level intensity with sustainable daily practice.

author: notbrian
summary: Describes an intense kundalini-triggered A&P event followed months later by a discrete stream-entry experience during a walk with his wife, reached after about two weeks of intensive noting under Kenneth Folk's phone guidance.
interesting_sentence: "Then, it was like I lost consciousness entirely for a moment and reality surged back on with seemingly greater intensity." —notbrian
helpful_sentence: "I have also become better at observing the fruition occurring while meditating." —notbrian
who_should_read: Meditators curious about a discrete stream-entry cessation event and the intense noting practice that preceded it.

author: Sugato
key_words: eight jhanas mapped to body parts
summary: Describes cycling through the eight jhanas via an eye-fluttering technique into Pure Land Jhana states, mapping each jhana to specific body locations, and preparing for a 7-day retreat at Muttodaya monastery.
interesting_sentence: "jhanas are simply energy moving from one bodypart to another, (I guess this what Kenneth means by completing the circuit)." —Sugato
helpful_sentence: "Whatever happens for you is exactly the right thing to be happening in this moment. Attainments are just ideas overlaid on reality. Reality comes first." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Concentration-focused meditators curious about a detailed body-mapped phenomenology of the eight jhanas and Pure Land Jhana states.

author: sventhepsychonaut
summary: Tracks a new-to-noting practitioner's shift from Zen-style concentration to Theravada noting, cycling through kasina work, kundalini-like energy, and self-diagnosed A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity episodes over roughly a year including a six-month backpacking break.
interesting_sentence: "Made we wonder wether DN exists with and without psychological content." —sventhepsychonaut
helpful_sentence: "If you can un-link DN from the idea that there is a psychological cause for it, eventually it reveals itself as a cluster of causeless physical sensations." —Rob_Mtl
who_should_read: Beginners transitioning from concentration practices to noting who want an honest, blow-by-blow account of early insight-cycle confusion.

author: TommyMcNally
summary: A Scottish KFD practitioner logs near-daily standing/seated vipassana and samatha sessions in his garden over several months, documenting recurring kriyas and candle-kasina jhana, repeated self-diagnosed Fruitions, a grueling Dark Night stretch, and later work with Kenneth Folk's direct-perception 'gears' technique and Nirodha Samapatti.
interesting_sentence: "In short, I can see this \"Tommy\" guy who, for all intents and purposes, is me but there's no identification with him or those thoughts that I once considered \"mine\" anymore." —TommyMcNally
helpful_sentence: "Notice that 'energy' is a catchall word. It does not directly point to an experience. See if you can note the sensations and/or mental impressions that comprise 'energy.'" —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners who want a blow-by-blow, technique-rich noting log spanning A&P energetics through a rough Dark Night into equanimity and early Nirodha Samapatti experiments.

log: Tales from the Zafu - Seraching for Stream
author: johnnyzampano
summary: Exhaustive daily noting logs dominated by hand-vibration and kasina phenomenology culminate in a 10-day Goenka retreat fruition and a distinct day-31 'gap' during a six-week IMS course that the author tentatively calls Stream Entry, followed by a rocky post-retreat relapse into depression and drinking.
interesting_sentence: "I believe I got Stream Entry on day 31, and will post some of my experiences." —johnnyzampano
helpful_sentence: "The temptation now will be to rest on your laurels, turning all of this into a pleasant memory. Instead, see if you can nurture it like the precious bud that it is." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about granular hand-vibration and kasina phenomenology leading up to a self-doubted stream-entry claim on long retreats.

author: tarverator
summary: A brief reintroduction post from a Shinzen student resuming a systematic practice log after erratic months, referencing a prior 37-day silent retreat and a pending year-long self-verification of probable stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "(Giving it a year to decide for sure, until May 1.)" —tarverator
helpful_sentence: "My formal practice has been somewhat erratic these last few months, but I am feeling inspired to get somewhat more systematic about it all again, so I am starting a practice log here." —tarverator
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about Shinzen's Do Nothing technique and 'Theme Blast' technique-cycling who want a candid post-retreat reintegration check-in.

author: tfarrah
summary: Chronicles a shift from Beth Resnick-Folk's noting instruction into Kenneth Folk's numbered jhana system, tracking day-by-day concentration depth, RLS-related bodily discomfort, and parallel inner-child trauma work following a claimed first-path attainment.
interesting_sentence: "She did some sort of very intense thing where she removed the outer layers of her body, including her ribcage, did something to them or had something done to them, then carefully replaced them." —tfarrah
helpful_sentence: "It's essential to understand that jhana is a place in the mind, a particular frequency, a habitat, as described by a particular set of jhanic factors." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners integrating Kenneth Folk's jhana system with noting practice and trauma-informed inner-child work who want a candid, technically detailed log of that combination.

log: The No-Big-Wow Club (continuation of Turtle Log conversation)
author: betawave
summary: A multi-practitioner survey thread comparing how mind-blowing (or subtle) A&Ps, post-path bliss waves, and review fruitions actually were for a dozen-plus KFD forum members, revealing wide variance from ecstatic ayahuasca-triggered A&Ps to years of barely-noticed cycling.
interesting_sentence: "First: yes!!, a fantastic blissful sense that I could release every single thought and perception like a helium balloon, immense huge immense wonderful immense (temporary!) relief from any and all pain or stress I ever felt." —Rob_Mtl
helpful_sentence: "Mahasi Sayadaw says you should be able to climb from A&P to EQ in 5 acts of noting." —apperception
who_should_read: Meditators wondering whether their own low-drama, low-bliss progress is normal will find reassurance and a wide range of comparison data points here.

author: Hopeful_Seeker
summary: A brand-new solo practitioner's five-day noting experiment produces intense spontaneous kriyas, a video-triggered A&P, and a rapid Dark-Night-like collapse into nicotine craving and despair, prompting him to ask the forum whether unguided practice could really produce insight stages.
interesting_sentence: "It became so intense that it felt as though my upper half was moving through at least a 3 or 4 inch arc." —Hopeful_Seeker
helpful_sentence: "If you can repeatedly release this resistance and just get a brief moment of relief, the mind is training itself how to automatically find its own relief." —orasis
who_should_read: New, teacher-less meditators curious what spontaneous kriyas and rapid A&P/Dark-Night cycling can feel like, and how a community responds to it.

author: thitatto
summary: After years as a self-described chronic dark-night yogi, thitatto restarts daily noting-aloud practice under Kenneth Folk, repeatedly reaching what he calls high Equanimity/4th-jhana states with strong 3rd-eye activity, including one ambiguous 'blip' during a home solo retreat that he isn't sure was an A&P event.
interesting_sentence: "Guess it could have been the A&P Event as well, but I've had like tons of meditation induced manic episodes before, and I'm wallowing in the Dark Night all the time." —thitatto
helpful_sentence: "Bill Hamilton used to say that people sometimes spent years with no progress because, although they believed they were meditating, they were really just jelling out in a pleasant hypnogogic state. Noting cures that." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Long-time dark-night-prone practitioners looking for a realistic account of rebuilding daily practice motivation and touching Equanimity without forcing stream entry.

author: Tim11
summary: An exceptionally granular, months-long concentration/noting log tracking escalating head-pressure, kundalini-like energy surges (including one violent uncontrolled episode), and progressively subtler whole-body vibrational and no-self perceptions, without ever landing on a confirmed nana or path.
interesting_sentence: "...suddenly all hell broke loose - there was violent energy surges, big boiling bubbles, and physical shaking along with very strong upward pressure on the very top of my head." —Tim11
helpful_sentence: "You seem to be getting past the stage that is traditionally called the \"corruption of insight,\" which just refers to the very common tendency of the mind to become attached to the pleasant sensations at the stage of the A&P (Arising and Passing Away of Phenomena)." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Concentration-heavy practitioners wanting an unusually detailed, blow-by-blow account of kundalini-like energetic phenomena and the long plateau of subtle refinement that can precede clearer insight markers.

author: Tina_A
summary: A beginner noting practitioner with ADD and thyroid-related anxiety notes her way through an acute panic episode in public and later reports spontaneous no-self insights (seeing without a seer, tasting without a taster) during ordinary daily activities.
interesting_sentence: "It was seen that there was just seeing, with no seer." —Tina_A
helpful_sentence: "Noting leads to the end of seeking. Thinking leads to thinking." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners with poor concentration or ADHD who want reassurance that a simple noting practice can still work despite distractibility.

author: TommyMcNally
key_words: Down to Earth Dharma video project
summary: A Scotland-based practitioner alternates kasina concentration (including Pure Land jhanas and a suspected nirodha samapatti) with vipassana noting through repeated A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity cycles and multiple fruitions, weathers an SSRI-discontinuation crisis that overlaps with Re-Observation, and settles into a centerless, doerless 'done' feeling he tentatively links to post-4th-path territory.
interesting_sentence: "the truth grabs you by the shoulders and screams in your face telling you to just shut the f*ck up, look at what's happening right there in front of you and pay attention." —TommyMcNally
helpful_sentence: "So long as we're identifying with these things then we are what they are in that moment in time because the self is whatever appears in awareness in that moment." —TommyMcNally
who_should_read: Experienced yogis cycling through repeated Dark Night territory alongside SSRI tapering, or anyone curious about detailed jhana and nana phenomenology from a self-taught, multi-tradition practitioner.

author: current_event
summary: A 14-day self-retreat following Tarin Greco's Reformed Slackers Guide produces vivid A&P phenomenology (starfield formations, forehead vibrations) and easy access to high equanimity but no stream entry, followed by practice notes tracking the post-retreat comedown and a lingering pull back toward path.
interesting_sentence: "next intention: sit longer don't make such a drama of it all, get path" —current_event
helpful_sentence: "I always experience a letdown after a retreat, and a reluctance to sit." —JLaurelC
who_should_read: Retreatants who didn't attain stream entry on a self-retreat and want a realistic picture of post-retreat integration and continued practice.

author: Torsken
summary: An early-stage practitioner tracks a persistent forehead pressure/tingling sensation tied to work-related feelings of inadequacy, alongside modest breath-and-noting practice explicitly aimed at general anxiety reduction rather than formal path attainment.
interesting_sentence: "...sometimes I have felt a sudden sensation on my left temple, as if someone pinched a small hole with a needle there, resulting in a strong feeling of energy shooting out directly out from my head trough this small hole." —Torsken
helpful_sentence: "As to the tingling in the forehead, it *might* help if you concentrated on the breath in the stomach instead of at the nose" —villum
who_should_read: Meditators who notice persistent energetic head sensations tied to psychological stress and want reassurance that anxiety-reduction is a valid starting goal.

log: U Tejaniya, Dis-Embedding, and my first 5 day
author: livinlite
key_words: Don't Look Down on the Defilements
summary: A first five-day retreat log describes an ecstatic, non-dual 'no separate I' opening reached through intensive noting, followed by a persistent dis-embedded, U Tejaniya-influenced awareness practice carried back into ordinary daily life.
interesting_sentence: "...everything stayed disconnected for a while, like a mushroom trip." —livinlite
helpful_sentence: "Another thing you can do is post for others--just honestly try to post things that you have found are helpful to you and might be of benefit to others." —jgroove
who_should_read: Retreatants curious about U Tejaniya-style choiceless awareness, and anyone wrestling with self-consciousness about sharing practice experiences publicly.

author: viktorvnh
summary: A two-month practitioner recovering from depression after a Goenka retreat works through structured four-foundations noting logs, documenting persistent physical pain, technique doubt, and emerging fear-and-shame material.
interesting_sentence: "Two months ago I was deep into a depression, self-loathing, unsecurity, social phobia etc... it wasn't my first depression. I wanted to run away kind of from my life" —viktorvnh
helpful_sentence: "If you allow the sensation to fill your entire horizon, can you find any subcomponents that make the actual sensation? How would you describe those underlying sensations?" —Antero
who_should_read: Newer meditators processing depression alongside a fresh Goenka-retreat foundation who want to see structured four-foundations noting logs and troubleshooting.

author: viktorvnh
summary: Alternates 45 minutes of choiceless noting with 30 minutes of breath-counting concentration while struggling to stay motivated and battling knee pain and self-doubt severe enough to derail his sitting resolutions.
interesting_sentence: "the monsters of self-doubt and wanting to give up have become so strong lately that they have almost overpowered me." —viktorvnh
helpful_sentence: "Have you tried doing the concentration practices before the vipassana, instead of the other way around?" —Russell
who_should_read: Practitioners battling motivation loss and self-doubt who want a candid, unpolished account of restarting a practice journal.

author: TommyMcNally
summary: While noting in triplets and working fruition through all three doors, Tommy experiences a jarring pop-ripping-blackness cessation that other posters recognize as their own third-path 'violent fruition,' after which he shifts focus to kasina practice, the jhanic arc, and attempts at nirodha samapatti.
interesting_sentence: "I used the analogy of two strong magnets suddenly slamming together in the brain then followed by the cessation." —NikolaiStephenHalay
helpful_sentence: "The trick is to remember to note the mind contents such as: \"Oh god that was mind blowing\", or \"Was that path?\" rather than being embedded." —OwenBecker
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about visceral, high-intensity fruition/cessation experiences near third-path territory and how to stay unattached to dramatic phenomenology.

author: WhoisMax
summary: An intensive practitioner logs noting-aloud sessions across standing, sitting, and walking postures totaling roughly six hours a day, describing 'beautiful breath,' flickering eyebrows, and a visual laser-show, while oscillating between focused calm and jumpy technique-hopping.
interesting_sentence: "I turned and started to see colors and then the more i watched a lasershow began." —WhoisMax
helpful_sentence: "a meditation session dominated by unpleasant feeling tones becomes a problem only if we add 'the second arrow.'" —jgroove
who_should_read: Intensive retreatants curious about a high-volume, multi-posture noting log with vivid visual phenomena and frequent technique-switching.

author: Yadid
summary: Yadid returns to daily 3-4 hour sits after a month-long lapse, cycling repeatedly through A&P, harsh 'electric chair' Dark Night vibrations, and expanding Equanimity while preparing for a self-retreat aimed at stream-entry.
interesting_sentence: "So... Stream-entry where the f*** are you?" —Yadid
helpful_sentence: "What generally works for me if I'm going for fruition is shifting into more of a sense of being quietly aware of what's happening, not noting frantically, and watching things unfold without me having to be a part of it." —TommyMcNally
who_should_read: Practitioners restarting a lapsed practice who want a candid, blow-by-blow account of cycling through A&P, harsh Dark Night 'electric chair' sensations, and Equanimity while pushing toward stream-entry.

author: Yadid
summary: Yadid logs near-daily noting practice and repeated cycling between Dark Night and Equanimity across Goenka retreats and home sits, wrestling with craving for stream-entry and cessation while receiving coaching from Kenneth Folk and forum peers.
interesting_sentence: "it is more important to you to get enlightened than anything else" —Yadid
helpful_sentence: "Craving for path is never in the way, Yadid, as long as you note it." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Meditators cycling through equanimity and craving for stream-entry who want a candid, blow-by-blow noting log with peer and teacher feedback.

author: Yadid
key_words: Mahasi retreat with Sayadaw U Vivekananda
summary: Yadid documents four months of a re-ignited daily noting practice woven with weekend day-retreats, a Shinzen Young retreat, and a 9-day Mahasi retreat with Sayadaw U Vivekananda, repeatedly cycling through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity while staying uncertain whether he has attained stream-entry despite Kenneth Folk suggesting he may have.
interesting_sentence: "The Dark Night is a teacher. The lessons are screaming out: SUFFERING - MUST - BE - ATTENDED - TO." —Yadid
helpful_sentence: "If one slows down before the plate, one's speed is very slow upon touching it and one will come to a quick stand still sooner. But if one keeps the fast pace going even after arriving at the plate, there will be no premature slowing down of one's running." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Meditators who cycle in and out of consistent practice and want an honest, day-by-day account of noting through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity while balancing a day job and relationship.

author: yogi_rencarts
summary: Yogi_rencarts recounts moving from Ajahn Brahm breath meditation and Tibetan Ngondro through a severe dark night into vipassana noting, claims stream-entry and later second path via jhana-based fruition, and eventually reports a lasting shift into non-symbolic/nondual awareness he associates with third or fourth path while exploring Actualism.
interesting_sentence: "I used to feel like I was a thing that was in my body looking out at the rest of the word and now everything that used to automatically consider to be me is now just experience arising within a vast field of awareness with the vast field of awareness not at all being me either." —yogi_rencarts
helpful_sentence: "The full realization was like a return to a normal, everyday kind of experience...life goes on, everything is nondual, but nonduality is really really prosaic (has no particular flavor)." —EndInSight
who_should_read: Advanced yogis curious how jhana mastery, cross-tradition practice, and Actualism can combine with the four-path model to describe a lasting nondual shift.

author: yuksaka
summary: Yuksaka delivers highly granular daily noting reports pairing body sensations with mind-states, tracking vibrations, tingling, kasina practice, and migraine-related phenomena while tentatively placing himself around the A&P/dark-night territory.
interesting_sentence: "BTW, I also had a sleep-paralysis this morning where I felt a strong light/sound inside my head." —yuksaka
helpful_sentence: "Are you sure you shouldn't fall asleep?" —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Noting practitioners wanting a model of extremely fine-grained, technical sensation labeling in daily practice reports.

author: jin..lin
key_words: jhana progression across Zen and Vipassana
summary: During a three-month semi-retreat, jin..lin blends Zen sitting with noting technique learned from Kenneth Folk, describing smooth cruises through the four jhanas and formless jhana alongside reflections on the five skandhas.
interesting_sentence: "Then my head snapped back to the left. I always have this reaction." —jin..lin
helpful_sentence: "our daily lives are dominated by unpleasant feelings" —jin..lin
who_should_read: Zen practitioners integrating noting technique who want a concise example of jhana progression and five-skandha reflection.

author: awouldbehipster
summary: A Ch'an practitioner details weeks of hua-tou ('Who is dragging this corpse around?') inquiry alongside KFD jhana-strata cycling through the Suddhavasa territory, describing kundalini-like energetic phenomena, recurring cessation/fruition review phases, and a deepening 'no-dog' Witness practice.
interesting_sentence: "It was as if a veil made of shadow-substance was swiftly pulled away from my being in a downward motion, resulting in a bright, alert, utterly lucid experience of non-dual certainty." —awouldbehipster
helpful_sentence: "what I found helpful was to objectively become aware of the sensation and watch the emotions/memories/thoughts tied to the sensation, but without jumping into it." —mikaelz
who_should_read: Meditators curious about combining Ch'an hua-tou self-inquiry with KFD jhana-mapping will find a detailed, philosophically rich first-person account of blending the two approaches.

author: awouldbehipster
summary: Jackson (awouldbehipster) cycles between samatha jhana practice at the anapana spot, Mahasi-style noting, and surrender-based 'natural wakefulness' non-meditation, detailing jhana factor sequences, repeated fruitions with bliss-wave afterglow, and a self-experiment to prolong fruition absorption via deepened concentration.
interesting_sentence: "Make friends with your own built-in hell and be free." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "The sooner you interrupt the downward spiral of negativity by noting, e.g., “despair, sadness, frustration, grief, hopelessness, anger, bitterness, hatred, rage,” the better." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring jhana-vipassana integration and fruition-absorption experiments who want a candid, community-supported account of surrender-based practice near the anagami stage.

author: awouldbehipster
summary: Cycling between Kenneth Folk's 'gears' (formless 2nd/3rd-gear awareness, jhana, and body-grounded vipassana), the author reports recurring cessations, Dzogchen-style resting in rigpa, and periods of mental dullness, while also coaching a struggling forum member through acute anxiety.
interesting_sentence: "No 'mind' is moving to and fro. The movements are not 'mind'. It's an illusion." —awouldbehipster
helpful_sentence: "Find the cause, and then cultivate dispassion toward the cause until it gives way." —awouldbehipster
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to Dzogchen-influenced non-striving practice and Kenneth Folk's gear-switching approach who want candid reflections on cessation, dullness, and working with anxiety.

author: beoman
summary: A highly technical KFD log combining tai chi energy work, systematic noting, and deliberate jhana cycling that repeatedly reaches Nirodha Samapatti cessation while wrestling with chronic painful head sensations and the pursuit of 4th path.
interesting_sentence: "an image of me appears somewhere in my visual field, along with a light switch, and flicks it back and forth. fruition happens with the flicking" —beoman
helpful_sentence: "Objectification is the entire game, Claudiu... there is no 'I' to be found" —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry practitioners wanting a granular, jhana-heavy account of chasing cessations and unblocking chronic head-based energy pain on the way toward higher path.

author: beoman
summary: An extensive verbal-noting log covering candle-flame kasina practice, repeated flickering/bliss-wave cycles, vivid visual hallucinations, and community pushback against premature claims of fruitions and higher paths.
interesting_sentence: "it looks kind of like they're coming out of a wall, with 2-3 showing at the same time, kind of like this but they aren't skulls." —beoman
helpful_sentence: "I haven't yet heard enough to conclude that you have attained 1st Path, so it's premature to be talking about 3rd (Nirodha Samapatti is only available at 3rd Path)." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Yogis who want an unusually granular, blow-by-blow account of noting practice and candle-flame kasina, plus the ambiguity of interpreting bliss waves and fruitions early on.

author: brianm2
key_words: cycle of losing and regaining motivation
summary: Detailed phenomenological essays on a 'mental association body' overlaid on raw sensation and on the construction of space and self, plus an unusually peaceful, distinctive fruition during a one-week IMS retreat.
interesting_sentence: "It was like a sudden realization accompanied by a freeing feeling like, \"wait a minute-- I don't *have* to do this.\"" —brianm2
helpful_sentence: "Note \"disdain\" and it immediately becomes fuel for your awakening. If you fail to note it, however, it can become a story to live in." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in fine-grained phenomenological analysis of self-construction and dependent origination, and in navigating community feedback and motivation cycles after a retreat fruition.

log: dowsing for steam entry, practice thread
author: nuclei
summary: Nuclei restarts his practice thread under a new username after a quiet summer and recent surgery, describing a sit combining breath counting and open-awareness noting that surfaced fine, settled tension in the jaw and mid-head alongside persistent distracting narratives.
interesting_sentence: "Before, before the summer, I was highly embroiled in a really pessimistic narrative." —nuclei
helpful_sentence: "Big thing in these sits is difficulty with stories, mind going adrift with stories." —nuclei
who_should_read: Yogis returning to practice after a health setback who want a low-key example of restarting sit logs and noting through everyday distractions.

author: EndInSight
summary: A practitioner who reached technical 4th path via forced, ultrafast Mahasi-style noting details years of post-4th practice with Kenneth Folk's direct-mode 'state 6/7' techniques, chases PCEs and near-PCEs through music and synaesthesia work, adopts actualist-style 'affect vs. actual sense-object' analysis, reports crossing into an anagami-like non-affective baseline, and eventually withdraws unsubstantiated attainment claims after community pushback.
interesting_sentence: "What a wonder, to see that the Absolute is nothing more than the play of my senses. Samsara is nirvana." —EndInSight
helpful_sentence: "I've refined the 3rd jhana technique like so: if I notice clear affect in some part of my body, I put my hand on top of the area, and contrast the warmth / coolness / whatever with the affect, until the actual sensation underlying it reveals itself." —EndInSight
who_should_read: Post-4th path practitioners drawn to fine-grained PCE/direct-mode phenomenology and actualism will get a uniquely detailed, if controversial, map of practice beyond technical fourth path.

author: Jackha
summary: After severing both quad tendons in a bicycle accident, Jackha maintains a noting practice through hospitalization and a painful drug reaction, eventually resuming short cushion sits and phone-based ping pong noting during rehab.
interesting_sentence: "At one point I could feel a glue that kept me embedded and wouldn't let go." —Jackha
helpful_sentence: "You don't have to stop doing (whatever it is). You just have to notice that you are doing it. As soon as the fact that you are doing (whatever it is) becomes the new object for investigation, you are doing vipassana in the most efficient way possible." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Meditators facing serious illness, injury, or hospitalization who want a candid, supportive account of maintaining noting practice through severe pain and setbacks.

author: Jackha
key_words: Progress of Insight map vs territory
summary: Jack chronicles roughly two years of daily ping-pong mahamudra noting with a partner, cycling between Kenneth Folk's 'ships in the harbor' listening technique, Shinzen Young's Do Nothing, and Ken McLeod's shamatha-vipassana approach, reporting stretches of effortless open awareness pervading phenomena punctuated by doubt, restlessness, and striving.
interesting_sentence: "It was as if worries, knee pain, etc. were going by as a stream with emptiness holding them all. Nothing could touch 'me'." —Jackha
helpful_sentence: "Most techniques are most effective when you do the same thing for several months, without varying." —giragirasol
who_should_read: Practitioners already comfortable with basic noting who want a granular, multi-year account of refining mahamudra 'listening' and troubleshooting the tension between striving and effortless awareness.

author: Jackha
summary: Describes a rejuvenated daily 60-90 minute practice of breath samatha followed by four-foundations noting, including a repeated episode during pair noting where time slowed and any sense of self disappeared, plus a recurring 30-40 minute wall of aversion/restlessness that forum members identify as dark night territory.
interesting_sentence: "Two times during the last two weeks I entered a space during vipassana meditation where time seemed to slow way down and any sense of self disappeared." —Jackha
helpful_sentence: "the right technique is any technique that keeps you on the cushion during this rough patch." —betawave
who_should_read: Noting practitioners hitting a recurring aversion/restlessness wall who want peer discussion on staying with dark-night-territory sensations.

author: hearsbirds
summary: Detailed body-scanning noting sessions tracking energetic tingling, throbbing, and visual phenomena, followed by two vivid dreams surfacing core wounds around identity tied to her mother and ex-husband.
interesting_sentence: "You just want to hurt me. You can't do this to me. You hate me." —hearsbirds
helpful_sentence: "Believing that awareness is \"me\" happens, as in \"my thoughts\", \"my sensations\", \"my thigh\"." —hearsbirds
who_should_read: Meditators curious about finely detailed phenomenological noting reports of energy sensations and how dream content can surface identity-related material.

author: j0shj0sh
summary: A beginner's noting log tracking progress from mindfulness-of-breathing and kasina concentration through a dream-based A&P event into months of intense Dark Night-style cycling of facial twitching, gagging, and disgust.
interesting_sentence: "My tongue continues to move until I gag, which is simultaenously extremely unpleasant and pleasant." —j0shj0sh
helpful_sentence: "You might want to try some \"pure\" aka anchor-less noting at this point: just note whatever is occurring every second or two, without following the breath." —nadavspi
who_should_read: Noting beginners wanting a blow-by-blow account of moving from concentration practice into A&P and the characteristic bodily cycling of the Dark Night.

author: jgroove
summary: jgroove restarts his practice journal after a post-stream-entry letdown driven by spiritual materialism, logging daily 40-60 minute noting sits and repeatedly taking recurring alcohol cravings as the object of practice.
interesting_sentence: "I remember listening to a podcast in which a researcher pointed out that if you give a bunch of lab rats alcohol, some portion of them will be like, \"So what?\" But there will be some other percentage that will take to the stuff like Homer Simpson." —jgroove
helpful_sentence: "My current working theory is that the next path will open up for me when I'm ready. Being ready means that I need to continue to recognize expectations and wishful thinking, and be able to pay attention to what does happen rather than continuing to look for what I wish to happen." —andymr
who_should_read: Meditators navigating post-stream-entry disillusionment and addictive cravings will find a candid model for turning both into practice objects.

author: jgroove
summary: Chronicles roughly a year of daily noting, 'ships in the harbor' listening, and whole-body-breathing practice, cycling through high equanimity and 'review' phenomena, until Kenneth Folk declares stream entry after Chris Marti coaches him to watch the arising of sense objects via dependent origination.
interesting_sentence: "Translation: it looks to me as though Joel attained stream entry about two weeks ago." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "You can always feel the unpleasant body tension of adding something extra. Whenever you feel it clearly, it automatically releases, just like the Buddha's hot coal simile; you will always drop a hot coal immediately when you see that you are clutching it." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Stream-entry-seeking noting practitioners juggling work and family who want a candid, phenomenologically detailed account of grinding through equanimity into path with teacher coaching.

author: jgroove
summary: Documents an extended, often daily 'noting aloud' practice used to fight chronic sloth and torpor, tracking recurring third-eye pressure, body-rocking kriyas, and brief tastes of equanimity while receiving direct coaching from Kenneth Folk on tanha and 'ships in the harbor' noting.
interesting_sentence: "I drifted off and saw a monster that looked exactly like ET, only it had yellow eyes, long sharp teeth and claws like Wolverine from the X-Men. YIKES!" —jgroove
helpful_sentence: "So the body is the fulcrum and making the connection in realtime is the lever." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Noting practitioners battling chronic sleepiness and dullness who want a granular, technique-by-technique account of how disciplined out-loud noting and Kenneth Folk's direct guidance can move practice forward.

author: lanocheoscura
summary: Recounts an apparent A&P/first-jhana breakthrough during a 15-day Mahasi retreat in Penang three years earlier, then begins one-on-one loud-noting training with Kenneth Folk, including noting practice during a daily train commute.
interesting_sentence: "This felt like watching a bullet bouncing against the walls of a maze with a high speed." —lanocheoscura
helpful_sentence: "I found the instruction interestingly: there is nothing that can disturb you from the practice, for example if there is a loud sound you just say \"hearing\"." —lanocheoscura
who_should_read: Beginners curious what starting one-on-one loud-noting training with a teacher looks like and what an A&P-like jhana experience can feel like.

author: limbicsail
summary: Limbicsail transitions from six months of body-scanning into noting practice, attends a ten-day retreat mixing body-scanning and open-awareness noting after switching on Kenneth's suggestion, passes through dark-night territory, and afterward struggles with a persistent sight/sound perceptual imbalance he calls a 'vortex'.
interesting_sentence: "If I happen to hear something, I'll remember to note sight. Then I'll get into a sight/sound vortex." —limbicsail
helpful_sentence: "You seem to have an established practice from just the one post, but more posts describing your practice in detail focused purely on the phenomenological experiences would be much more helpful in pinpointing where you could use some suggestions." —NikolaiStephenHalay
who_should_read: Meditators curious about shifting from noting to open-awareness practice on retreat and the sensory-door imbalances that can follow.

author: limbicsail
summary: Logs a dense sequence of chair-based noting sits and a home-plus-center retreat dominated by recurring aches (especially in the right femur), vibratory phenomena on the face and forearms, and frequent battles with distraction and restlessness.
interesting_sentence: "It is like the sound of the toilet flushing perpetually with everlasting waste while a mob of knocked up mouseketeers scream at me to turn off the alarm." —limbicsail
helpful_sentence: "Even getting on the cushion some days is victory." —rocketbuddha
who_should_read: Noting practitioners who want a granular, unfiltered record of cycling through bodily pain, vibratory phenomena, and restlessness during an intensive self-directed retreat.

author: limbicsail
summary: A detailed multi-week noting-practice sitting log spanning several retreats (a 10-day Goenka retreat, service time at a meditation center, and a 7-day retreat before Christmas), tracking recurring distraction and morning torpor, a stretch of low-grade dark-night heaviness, and a self-reported shift into equanimity with recurring subtle vibrations on the face and forearms.
interesting_sentence: "The issue is that perhaps I have just been a loathing twenty minute dark night yogi for the past, say three or six months despite a vigorous allotment of practice time." —limbicsail
helpful_sentence: "my new idea about it is that i was just associating an unpleasant physical sensation with a mindstate that may not have actually been there in the beginning." —limbicsail
who_should_read: Noting practitioners on long retreats who want a granular, blow-by-blow account of cycling through distraction, torpor, and dark-night heaviness toward equanimity.

author: mindful1983
summary: Announces a self-diagnosed third-path attainment with crown/base chakra energy and aura perception, but Kenneth Folk challenges the claim for lacking jhana or Nirodha Samapatti evidence and locks the thread.
interesting_sentence: "I now feel like an alien who has a new body that he's trying on and still learning." —mindful1983
helpful_sentence: "The ability to attain either the Pure Land jhanas or Nirodha Samapatti are the only reliable indicators of 3rd Path." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners tempted to self-diagnose advanced path attainment who want to see how KFD teachers evaluate claims against jhana/Nirodha evidence.

author: mindful1983
summary: A free-associative log of chakra-focused sitting, jhana absorption attempts, and reiki/trance practices tracked through recurring 'chi burnout' cycles, auras, and speculation about approaching 3rd path.
interesting_sentence: "i feel gone. i can't find myself when i look for myself... but i still seem to have a pretty big and selfish ego." —mindful1983
helpful_sentence: "Perhaps you could go deep into method B and then switch to practicing method A to find the flaws and such that are still inherent in it (all phenomenon have the three characteristics)." —mumuwu
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to energetic/kundalini phenomenology who want a candid, unfiltered stream-of-consciousness log of late-stage practice.

author: limbicsail
summary: Detailed daily noting-and-concentration practice log spanning several weeks, tracking recurring back pain, kriyas, vibratory A&P-like phenomena, and frequent distraction, culminating in the start of a 9-day retreat.
interesting_sentence: "I've described it before like that green foam stuff which holds a lot of water which you stick flowers into (ha)." —limbicsail
helpful_sentence: "Now instead - I just sit, I sit and I notice." —limbicsail
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about the granular, day-to-day texture of noting practice combined with concentration training, including how to work with recurring body pain and distraction, would find this useful.

author: n8sense
summary: A mainstreamed practice journal tracking kundalini-like energy, eye fluttering, brow-chakra concentration work, and long cycles between dark night misery and equanimity, with ongoing coaching from Kenneth Folk and forum peers.
interesting_sentence: "Moments ago I felt like a fighter jet sitting on the runway, engines revved as high as they can go without exploding, waiting for clearance from the tower to take off." —n8sense
helpful_sentence: "One of the pointers I've used to keep myself on track throughout the years is \"constancy, not heroics.\"" —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about chakra/kundalini phenomenology mapped onto the insight cycle and the value of non-manipulation.

author: pat42
summary: A short, sparse journal describing a rapid cycle of disgust, misery, and sadness followed by a new spacious 'empty' sensation after kasina practice, alongside struggles to precisely label subtle body sensations using triplet noting.
interesting_sentence: "But now i feel like a empty lobster shell." —pat42
helpful_sentence: "3) Note \"triplets\" (body sensations + feeling tone + mind-state), e.g., \"pressure-neutral-investigation; coolness-pleasant-contentment; itching-unpleasant-aversion.\"" —pat42
who_should_read: Newer yogis learning triplet noting who want a candid, unpolished account of the difficulty of labeling subtle sensations.

author: mikesettle
summary: An early journal from a new student of Kenneth Folk practicing seated and daily-life noting of body, thought, and feeling tone, dominated by intense sessions of fear, dread, and aversion.
interesting_sentence: "fear was all over me like a mask and a body suit." —mikesettle
helpful_sentence: "I thought it was all bad. It's not. There is real joy out there, and its made more accessible by the practice that Kenneth is having me do." —mikesettle
who_should_read: Beginners starting formal noting practice who want to know what intense early fear and aversion during sits can feel like.

author: richardweeden
summary: An 11-year meditator new to KFD logs sit-by-sit reports mapping vipassana ñanas onto jhanas via self-inquiry ('what knows this?'), including one sit with a surge of fear and suicidal thoughts that resolves into panoramic equanimity and a likely fruition.
interesting_sentence: "the problem of suggestibility - I've been reading about these stages with enthusiasm and interest for a couple of weeks - and boing! here they are." —richardweeden
helpful_sentence: "If you can report the bare sensate experience without the speculation and do that for long enough, it'll will become clear where you are at to both yourself and others." —mumuwu
who_should_read: Experienced concentration-and-insight practitioners who want granular, jhana-by-jhana descriptions of ñana progression and self-inquiry-triggered fruitions.

author: richardweeden
summary: A 28-day solo UK retreat report describing progression through pure land jhanas and intense 3rd-eye activation that culminated in a spontaneous, non-manipulated dissolution of self read as 4th path, followed by renewed interest in tantra and deity yoga.
interesting_sentence: "There was only surrender, of nothing to nothing." —richardweeden
helpful_sentence: "There's some sort of morphic field resonance with a liberated teacher." —jhsaintonge
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners curious about pure land jhana phenomenology and a spontaneous, non-manipulated route to 4th path on extended solo retreat.

author: r0ny
summary: A newcomer from an advaita background blends breath concentration with noting, reporting long sits, cycling mostly through dukkha nanas, stretches of high equanimity, and bouts of health-anxiety over ordinary body sensations.
interesting_sentence: "at times i will get afraid as if i am loosing my identity , i am going die kind of thing,and i open my eyes to overcome it etc" —r0ny
helpful_sentence: "As you describe more detail about your experience during your practice, people will be able to give you better guidance / advice." —malt
who_should_read: Newer practitioners combining noting and concentration practice who want an honest account of early cycling and health-related anxiety.

author: solxyz
summary: A busy, irregular practitioner tracks repeated small fruitions, cycling between reobservation and equanimity, recurring cessations noticed upon waking from naps, and jhana practice complicated by chronic digestive pain.
interesting_sentence: "There is a sense that because I have nothing to lose, everything is wonderful and beautiful in its own way." —solxyz
helpful_sentence: "Give as much raw data as possible and try not to interpret too much." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about subtle post-path cycling and self-diagnosing fruitions amid chronic physical pain and a busy schedule.

author: sparqi
summary: Chronicles months of hara-anchored noting and kasina samatha culminating in a smooth jhanic traversal, access to immaterial jhanas, and a possible fruition event that the author and Kenneth Folk tentatively identify as a path attainment.
interesting_sentence: "The head as a fifth limb feels like a tightly curled fist." —sparqi
helpful_sentence: "It takes awhile to develop the necessary psychological calus's just like with bricklaying or filling out income tax forms or whatever." —triplethink
who_should_read: Practitioners working with kasina/jhana concentration and hara-based noting who want a granular account of self-image dissolution leading toward a fruition event.

author: sparqi
summary: sparqi documents beginning formal kasina-based samatha practice under Kenneth Folk's guidance, tracking visual phenomena ('chicken herding,' inner light shows, disk stabilization) alongside intermittent Mahasi-style noting, while also managing a cracked rib from illness, and reports the first stabilized, bright, steady kasina image as fruit of the practice.
interesting_sentence: "my chicken herd degenerated to arguing and fighting with philosopher chicken insisting on providing a loud commentary." —sparqi
helpful_sentence: "Let your mind naturally widen the field of awareness as you deepen into concentration... All you have to do is stop pretending to be in charge." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Complete beginners to kasina samatha who want a detailed, methodical log of early concentration phenomena and a teacher's real-time troubleshooting.

author: sparqi
summary: Chronicles months of intensive kasina and feeling-object samatha combined with free-style noting, working through the four jhanas and rapidly cycling A&P/dark-night nanas alongside vivid kundalini-like energetic releases and third-eye investigations, with Kenneth confirming fourth-jhana access.
interesting_sentence: "Spoke to Kenneth yesterday, he confirmed that I seem to have access to 4thJhana, so Im peaking at equanimity." —sparqi
helpful_sentence: "2nd jhana. Mind soaks into object. Corresponds to A&P. Deep joy. Gently rising energy. Room appears brighter, even with eyes closed. Steep and go deeper." —sparqi
who_should_read: Concentration-focused practitioners interested in kasina/jhana work combined with kundalini-like energetics and rapid nana cycling.

author: stercuji
summary: Short present-moment journal entries noting breath, body scanning, "don't know" mind, and the arising and passing of anxiety, tension, and sadness during simple sitting.
interesting_sentence: "immersion and beauty in the ordinary, mind grasping for more, relaxing into don't know" —stercuji
helpful_sentence: "The stomach sensation, the anxiety label, the worry activity, noted and dissolves." —stercuji
who_should_read: Beginners curious about simple noting practice that blends breath awareness, body scanning, and 'don't know' mind with passing anxiety.

author: telecaster
summary: A KFD forum member logs a series of 40-60 minute rising-falling-stopping noting sits marked by spine energy surges that twist the neck, brief rapture, and an encounter with chronic throat/chest disgust and pain, before a pain-pill relapse dulls sensitivity and a subsequent clean sit brings renewed joy.
interesting_sentence: "It doesnt feel like vipassana to me for some reason." —telecaster
helpful_sentence: "I start with noting the breath and then my basic goal is to not miss anything that is happening -- sensations, thoughts, feelings, etc." —telecaster
who_should_read: Practitioners refining an intuitive noting technique who want a candid, blow-by-blow account of cycling between rapture, energetic surges, and low-grade discomfort within an equanimity phase.

author: throughline
summary: Two-week solo-then-group retreat journal detailing Mahasi-style noting and choiceless awareness of the whole-body field, an explicit 'A&P-like phase,' dark-night-style misery and aversion, and stabilizing access to equanimity with cessation-adjacent drop-outs at the passing away of sensations.
interesting_sentence: "The thing that really blew me away was the A&P-like phase." —throughline
helpful_sentence: "...suggested that I could temper down the search for the three characteristics -- more like they could present themselves if I was in a position to be receptive." —throughline
who_should_read: Serious retreatants wanting a granular, multi-week Mahasi-noting journal that maps body-field sensations onto A&P, dark-night, and equanimity phenomenology.

author: kacchapa
summary: A years-long householder practice log built around candle-kasina concentration and out-loud noting under Kenneth Folk's mentorship, repeatedly cycling through A&P, dark night, and equanimity while contending with migraines/CSD, family psychiatric history, and work demands, without ever confirming a path attainment.
interesting_sentence: "Just some more pleasant colors with some sense of fear in the background... but seemed clear there is no answer to speculation, but to keep practicing." —kacchapa
helpful_sentence: "I believe the way to handle E is to not care about back-sliding into dark night, which is inevitable and even if you dont, you will experience the DN in later paths so who cares?" —Yadid
who_should_read: Householder meditators juggling chronic illness and family psychiatric history who want an honest, granular account of cycling through the insight stages without a confirmed path attainment.

author: yadidb
summary: An intensive noting-and-kasina practice log spanning multiple Goenka, Shaila Catherine, Christopher Titmuss, and Mahasi-tradition retreats, in which the author repeatedly crosses the A&P and cycles through dark night and equanimity under close mentorship from Kenneth Folk, without yet attaining stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "Mr. Goenka, you are mistaken. The 7th and 8th jhanas are not lost. They are part of the collective treasure of humankind and they can be uncovered by anyone who is willing to look carefully at their own experience." —kennethfolk
helpful_sentence: "Go ahead and go to sleep, but be the witness who watches the body/mind go to sleep. Make a resolution to keep part of the mind alert while the rest of the mind nods out." —kennethfolk
who_should_read: Practitioners preparing for insight retreats who want a blow-by-blow account of cycling through A&P, dark night, and equanimity with real-time teacher feedback from Kenneth Folk.

author: finding-oneself ♤
summary: First 10-day Goenka retreat log describing a shift from breath-focused concentration to the sweeping method and noting practice, self-assessed near misses at stream entry and high equanimity, followed by late-retreat burnout and a lighter, less striving post-retreat practice.
interesting_sentence: "I know this stuff works because subjectively, the day I got out of retreat was twice as long as the day before retreat." —finding-oneself ♤
helpful_sentence: "Go as slow or as fast as you need to notice reality as clearly as possible." —Dream Walker
who_should_read: Practitioners preparing for a first Goenka retreat who want an honest account of chasing stream entry, adjusting noting technique, and reaching equanimity without idealizing the outcome.

author: Hibiscus Kid
summary: Comments and quoted log excerpts track Hibiscus Kid's noting practice through drowsy, doubt-filled sits and a shift away from a teacher, with the DhO community offering encouragement and reflections on motivation and mindfulness.
interesting_sentence: "Dad, you are now in the Hell realm and in there is fire and very hot, go back to breathing" —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "If we force the mind to be mindful, it's a matter of will. If we learn to prefer mindfulness, then it becomes natural and easy." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners struggling with doubt, drowsiness, and motivation swings who want to see supportive community engagement around a stalled noting practice.

author: A Dietrich Ringle
summary: Brief journal entries describing fire kasina jhana practice, an A&P-like event experienced 'without the am,' and reflections on using death itself (clear light yoga) as a vehicle toward further attainment after claiming third path.
interesting_sentence: "*Crosses fingers that he doesn't go psychotic." —A Dietrich Ringle
helpful_sentence: "I read somewhere that the angami has overcome the fear of death, and that an arhat adds overcoming the fear of birth." —A Dietrich Ringle
who_should_read: Third-path practitioners curious about fire kasina jhana practice and using terminal moments as a vehicle toward fourth path.

author: Kev in
key_words: Alice Miller Drama of the Gifted Child
summary: A new DhO poster describes escaping compulsive striving for parental approval by reliably entering access concentration, coinciding with a second A&P event.
interesting_sentence: "The truth is, no matter how much uncontrollable thought is flowing through my head, I'm able to predictably enter 'access concentration' and remove my slavery to thought." —Kev in
helpful_sentence: "The need to gain their parents' approval is something which is imprinted deeply in children when the parents use them to meet their own emotional needs and expectations, rather than accepting them for how they are." —George S
who_should_read: Newcomers wrestling with the pressure of parental approval who want a brief account of finding relief via reliable access concentration.

author: bluefields
summary: A college student commits to a year of daily nostril-focused shamatha concentration practice, aiming to reduce anxiety and internet habits while gradually extending sit length per Wallace and Culadasa's guidance.
interesting_sentence: "I got inspired to do this after watching this video of a guy practicing table tennis for a year." —bluefields
helpful_sentence: "It would be better to meditate 20 minutes a day consistently for a year, rather than 30 minutes for 2 weeks then intermittent practice." —mysterie
who_should_read: Beginners wanting a structured, teacher-informed plan for building a sustainable daily concentration practice from scratch.

author: Timmy Davis
summary: A 20-year-old chronicles three Mahasi-style retreats at Satipanya where overstriving and fear of loss dominate, an ambiguous 20-minute experience of selfless sensations, and an eventual switch from noting to TWIM's 6Rs and metta practice.
interesting_sentence: "There is no one home, yet engaging action continues." —Ryan J
helpful_sentence: "Meditation is perverse in that trying hard/forcing seems to work not so well compared to lettting go and letting things flow a bit more" —Eva Nie
who_should_read: Beginner meditators struggling with overstriving and retreat-induced anxiety who want to see how balancing effort with surrender and metta can help.

author: A Dietrich Ringle
summary: A short, informal log describing use of the What Three Words geolocation app as a grounding/coping mechanism to manage anxiety triggered by watching the Olympics, with no formal meditation technique or sit described.
interesting_sentence: "Whenever I watch the Olympics I get a desire to be homeless." —A Dietrich Ringle
helpful_sentence: "If I map my house it can be relieving and absorbing, potentially leading to shamatha." —A Dietrich Ringle
who_should_read: Readers curious about improvised anxiety-grounding techniques tied to media triggers rather than classic insight-meditation phenomenology.

author: A K D
summary: A daily log of short-to-hour-long sits mixing open awareness, Michael Taft guided meditations, breath shamatha, noting, guru yoga, and fire kasina, mostly contending with work stress, drowsiness, craving, and low motivation rather than reporting clear insight-stage breakthroughs.
interesting_sentence: "Why are you practicing Kid?! From what I'm reading in your posts there is not much \"right desire\" to get to know what Dukkha really is!" —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "You are fighting it, Kid! Minimum 45 minutes, open eyes, noting aloud! You know the drill, you know that you know! Stop tiptoeing and hit the nail straight in its head!" —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Meditators who struggle to maintain formal practice around a demanding job will recognize the recurring battle with drowsiness, craving, and low motivation described here.

author: A K D
summary: Near-daily log alternating Brahmavihara practice with 'dropping the ball' open-awareness sits and emerging self-inquiry, tracking recurring anxiety, doubt, and drowsiness before closing on an ambiguous flash-and-jolt experience the author is unsure qualifies as a cessation.
interesting_sentence: "Almost 40 minutes into the sit, out of nowhere, there was a white flash in the head: an image like a spiderweb or concentric rings that form when a rock is thrown into water." —A K D
helpful_sentence: "Let the flavor of release be your guide." —A K D
who_should_read: Practitioners transitioning from noting into open-awareness and self-inquiry who want a candid, granular account of motivation swings, doubt, and drowsiness between sits.

author: A K D
summary: A multi-year, sporadically updated log tracking a shift from noting to open-awareness and self-inquiry practice, with recurring cycles of low motivation and desire-for-deliverance frustration, later experiments with Fire Kasina, Shikantaza, and Western ceremonial magick, and reflections on an SSRI taper, a breakup, and a relocation to Germany.
interesting_sentence: "It's as if there is a ghost/God/Universe looking through my eyes at an appearance and the appearance, which is also the ghost/God/Universe, is aware that it is being looked at and is somehow reflecting the same awareness back at me." —A K D
helpful_sentence: "Ask yourself what you can do today to make the conditions in the immediate future conducive to the behavior you want." —Martin V
who_should_read: Meditators struggling to sustain a consistent open-awareness practice amid work, relationship, and life-transition stress will find a relatable, low-drama account of motivation cycling and desire-for-deliverance territory.

author: Sam
summary: Restarts formal practice after a years-long hiatus using candle-flame kasina, logging short daily sits and troubleshooting the wandering/blinking after-image dot with help from experienced kasina practitioners.
interesting_sentence: "The dot is a sign you're doing well, it's not the goal." —David Kyle Spencer
helpful_sentence: "Instead focus your eyes on one point of the darkness and the light will stay still, it just wont be in the center of your vision." —Michial N
who_should_read: Beginners restarting a concentration practice after time away who want practical, step-by-step candle-kasina troubleshooting.

author: Ross A K
summary: Practices actualism's HAIETMOBA inquiry while walking and driving alongside classical jhana concentration, repeatedly touching PCE/excellence-experience states and working through money-related fear and anxiety via naivety and surrender.
interesting_sentence: "I drive way better in the flow of perfection." —Ross A K
helpful_sentence: "just do whatever causes the fear to come up, then sit with the fear until it fades... then do what causes the fear to come up, then sit with the fear until it fades... etc." —Beoman Claudiu Dragon Emu Fire Golem
who_should_read: Practitioners curious how actualism's PCE-focused inquiry can blend with classical jhana practice woven into daily activities like walking and driving.

author: Adam L
summary: Logs months of near-daily 30-60 minute samatha-to-noting sits under a private teacher, tracking energetic twitching, absorbed equanimous states, and a few unexplained momentary 'blank outs' while preparing for a first 10-day Goenka retreat.
interesting_sentence: "Perhaps I've regressed from Equanimity to dark night territory again." —Adam L
helpful_sentence: "Noting is quite useful to see one's own patterns of habitual thinking/feeling, and noting can show just how much the mind is chatting away in every direction, diffusing its energy everywhere like fireworks." —katy steger
who_should_read: Practitioners preparing for a first Goenka retreat who want a granular, teacher-guided week-by-week account of early concentration and insight phenomena.

author: finding-oneself ♤
summary: In an opening entry, the author strings together a series of synchronicities involving blue jays, cardinals, Alice in Wonderland, and The Matrix, reframing his path as a personal 'religion' aimed at reconnecting with his deceased brother Eric.
interesting_sentence: "I dub blue jays, birds of awakening." —finding-oneself ♤
helpful_sentence: "A part of me feels lost. It may never fully come back but the wounds can heal, and I can have a deeper connection with Eric again." —finding-oneself ♤
who_should_read: Readers curious about synchronicity-driven, meaning-making spiritual narratives intertwined with grief, rather than formal meditation technique.

log: Adventures in Deconstruction: A Practice Thread
author: Tommy M
summary: Tommy M describes a sustained baseline shift into panoramic, non-dual, affect-free perception, works through a Dzogchen five-skandhas visualization and beginning tummo practice, and navigates a job loss, house repossession, and divorce while forming a new relationship with fellow DhO member Steph S.
interesting_sentence: "There was a massive shift in baseline around two months ago, roughly, which left no sense of location, affective emotions or self-referential thought in its wake." —Tommy M
helpful_sentence: "I'd suggest just trying to experience as much of the breath as possible, but also look at what implies physical sensations of rising and falling 'cause, in my experience so far, that distinction breaks down leaving just a sense of movement rather than of breathing." —Tommy M
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in Tibetan Dzogchen/Mahamudra approaches to non-dual awareness and how sustained perceptual shifts interact with major life stress.

log: Aeon's log: DN & EQ to Stream Entry
author: Aeon
summary: Aeon uses third-jhana techniques to dissolve chronic dark-night misery into equanimity, combines EMDR trauma therapy with insight meditation and TWIM/Pure Land mantra practice, and finds practice deepens dramatically after a period of celibacy and Taoist 'cooldraw' energy work.
interesting_sentence: "This DN stuff feels like emotional waterboarding." —Aeon
helpful_sentence: "Giving up and just letting things be, without trying to fix and without trying to avoid, is the only way through." —shargrol
who_should_read: Meditators with trauma or addiction histories looking for concrete jhana- and EMDR-based strategies for working through a stubborn dark night.

author: George S
summary: A self-diagnosed narcissist works through NPD, intense kundalini/piti energetics, and repeated nighttime depersonalization episodes while dating his stream entry, 2nd, and 3rd path fruitions and later describing (while formally disclaiming) a collapse of personal perspective during a COVID-19 quarantine, alongside detailed troubleshooting of stuck piti blocking access to hard jhana.
interesting_sentence: "Nibbana is not an experience, it is simply the abscence of the (assumed) experience of being someone." —George S
helpful_sentence: "To state the obvious, fear never goes away. It can be seen through, and that's what happens as your mind acclimatizes to it." —Chris M
who_should_read: Meditators curious how narcissism, kundalini, and DP/DR-like depersonalization intersect with path claims, or who want a candid forum debate distinguishing psychiatric dissociation from anatta insight.

author: George S
summary: Working through jhana factors via the 16-step Anapanasati sutta, Buteyko breathing, and piti-sukha energetics, the author cycles through A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity while managing kundalini-like facial pain and chronic fatigue syndrome, ultimately retracting an earlier premature claim of high attainment as narcissistic self-diagnosis.
interesting_sentence: "I'm reasonably confident that I am either pre-SE, post-3rd or post-4th." —George S
helpful_sentence: "JUST SEE IF YOU CAN MAKE IT TO THE END OF THE CURRENT BREATH WITHOUT GETTING DISTRACTED." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners balancing home practice with family life and chronic illness who want a detailed, technique-heavy account of jhana/samatha-vipassana cross-training and a candid cautionary tale about premature attainment claims.

author: George S
summary: Practicing Ajahn Brahm-style anapanasati and reduced/Buteyko breathing, George cycles through piti-sukha jhana bliss, kundalini-like tension release in the head, throat and chest, and repeated Dark Night-to-Equanimity swings, while using 6 realms and 5 elements frameworks plus Shinzen's craving equations to work through family conflict with his in-laws, chronic fatigue, and anger at his son.
interesting_sentence: "It reminds me of watching someone dying when increasingly long periods pass between breaths and just when you think surely they must be dead by now there's another breath, and it goes on for hours like that. It's kind of comforting to think this is what dying might be like and it could be pleasurable." —George S
helpful_sentence: "Craving takes you beyond what is and into the domain of time and fear of not thriving." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners working with Ajahn Brahm-style jhana and reduced breathing who want a candid, long-form account of cycling through piti-sukha bliss, kundalini-like tension release, and Dark Night/Equanimity swings alongside real-world family stress.

author: George S
summary: Returning to Mahasi-style noting after a year off, George S works intensively through Ken McLeod's Five Elements/Six Realms practice from Wake Up To Your Life, tracing narcissistic reaction patterns and releasing kundalini-type energetic tension while cycling through equanimity and dark night toward increasingly frequent nondual glimpses.
