interesting_sentence: "She smiled and after a while said 'I know what it is - a pony'." —George S
helpful_sentence: "One trick which seems to work is instead of focusing on the sensation itself, focus on the resistance or clinging to the sensation." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners integrating psychological and trauma work with insight practice who want a candid, humor-laced account of elements-and-realms emotional processing alongside advancing nondual insight.

author: George S
summary: George S blends intensive anapanasati/jhana practice — breath counting to 100+, kasina work, and Ajahn Brahm-inspired concentration technique — with deep inner-child, shame, and narcissism processing triggered by a chronic fatigue relapse, culminating in a self-described 'technical 4th path' realization.
interesting_sentence: "I don't believe one can be partially pregnant. This is like that. It's all or nothing, baby." —Chris M
helpful_sentence: "I have learned from repeated experience that, in that unremarkable period, the real concentration is building, completely off-screen." —Martin
who_should_read: Practitioners looking to integrate deep psychological/trauma work (narcissism, shame, inner-child healing) with concentration practice will find this log's candid blending of the two especially clarifying.

author: George S
summary: George S recounts a long stretch of high-dose THC-assisted meditation trip reports exploring jhana, elemental/alchemical visualizations, and a chaotic racially-themed rebirth breakdown, interwoven with forum pushback on his motivations for teaching and unresolved shame/narcissism work.
interesting_sentence: "The big insight turned that completely on its head - recognizing it’s never going to happen, there is no such thing as a permanent mental state - this current experience, WHATEVER IT IS, is already it, always was and always will be." —George S
helpful_sentence: "Clearly there’s a certain amount of unavoidable pain (first arrow) that comes from just being alive - fatigue, sickness, accidents, misfortunes, aging, death - but you can avoid stabbing yourself with the second arrow (the mental suffering of trying to control what you can’t ultimately control)." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about high-dose psychedelic-assisted jhana practice and the messy psychological unraveling that can accompany claims of advanced attainment will find this log's unfiltered honesty instructive and cautionary.

author: George S
summary: George S charts an intense year of kundalini-driven energetic sensations moving through the chakras, recurring depersonalization/anatta episodes, and tentative fruition-like experiences, while working through childhood-rooted anxiety and self-forgiveness alongside soft-jhana breath practice.
interesting_sentence: "Then the amusing thought arose that I might well be the most enlightened asshole I know." —George S
helpful_sentence: "The gentler approach is to have the intention to stay on the breath, allow for both success and failure to happen, and when failure eventually happens (which of course it will, that's built into the practice, no big deal) --- then the imporant part of practice happens: noting what was so seductive to the mind." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners grappling with intense kundalini/jhana energetics alongside anxiety, depersonalization, and family strain will find a raw, detailed companion account of cycling through the nanas.

author: Akko !
summary: A second-path yogi cycling through jhana practice, metta, and noting describes cannabis-enhanced concentration sessions, a vivid 'Indra' vision merging past lives, and navigating an international move while wrestling with emotional shadow material and kundalini-like energy.
interesting_sentence: "Always remember the four noble truths: shit's fucked, fuck me, fuck this, go fuck yourself" —Akko !
helpful_sentence: "The practice is be aware, lose awareness, then be aware again. It's ok to suck at this." —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Second-path practitioners interested in jhana-vipassana integration, cannabis-assisted concentration, and detailed A&P/dark-night phenomenology during major life transitions.

log: Alan's [enter catchy title here] Meditation Notes
author: Alan Smithee
summary: A stream-entry-seeking teacher logs years of near-daily body-scanning and noting sits (with detailed technique breakdowns and hour tallies), several Goenka retreats including a vividly narrated 3-day course, and repeated restarts of practice after burnout from teaching, marriage, and parenthood.
interesting_sentence: "I jerked off a total of two times... I'm a terrible Buddhist." —Alan Smithee
helpful_sentence: "You're not trying to pry away the condition of being embedded, you're trying to see the sense of intention that leads to the embedding, and then dropping that." —Mind over easy
who_should_read: Yogis wanting a highly systematic, technique-by-technique breakdown of scanning and noting on the road to stream entry, especially those balancing intensive practice with a demanding career.

author: Alex N
summary: A rigorously analytical Goenka vipassana practitioner logs a structured 3-4 hour/day routine, detailed body-scan phenomenology (heart and spine EMF oscillations, phosphene absorption states), EEG self-research, and reflections on pain endurance drawn from adhiṭṭhāna retreat sits.
interesting_sentence: "I feel an invisible hand gently squeeze my windpipe. This is the first time I've ever felt choked from within." —Alex N
helpful_sentence: "Perceive body sensation, observe change, and starve off your concepts." —Alex N
who_should_read: Data-minded Goenka vipassana practitioners interested in rigorous body-scan phenomenology, EEG self-experimentation, and pain-endurance practice on long retreats.

author: Fatal Hubris
summary: A TMI- and jhana-focused practitioner claims stream entry via a vivid 'cinema of my life' vision after five hours of jhana practice, reports daily fruitions and reobservation cycling, and is preparing a three-week fire kasina retreat while placing himself on the verge of second path.
interesting_sentence: "I was sitting in a cinema watching the movie of my life and then looked around and saw the cinema filled with me, all the other me's turned to look at me and it all collapsed." —Fatal Hubris
helpful_sentence: "Everyone goes at their own pace..." —Fatal Hubris
who_should_read: Concentration-heavy TMI/jhana practitioners curious how a psychedelic-triggered A&P can accelerate into stream entry and rapid post-SE cycling.

author: finding-oneself ♤
summary: An intermittent practitioner since 2011 mixes noting, do-nothing, and Loch Kelly-style non-conceptual glimpses across cushion sits, gym workouts, and a 5-MeO-DMT trip, repeatedly claiming brief equanimity states that a fellow poster (Bahiya Baby) challenges as under-evidenced jargon.
interesting_sentence: "Equanimity is like your dearest childhood memory, a memory so rich with sentiment that it couldn't have ever really happened." —Bahiya Baby
helpful_sentence: "I would suggest sticking to ONE technique for at least 6 months of daily 45-60 minutes meditation. Buy into it completely!" —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Practitioners juggling irregular schedules and life stress who want an honest look at how community pushback tests claims of reaching equanimity outside retreat conditions.

author: Alex H
summary: A new-to-DhO practitioner shifts from TMI's nostril-focused concentration (which produced chronic pressure) to Rob Burbea/Ajahn Geoff-style full-body breath and Mahasi noting, adding a 20-minute metta warm-up inspired by TWIM before settling into an hour of comfortable, low-key piti.
interesting_sentence: "My concern with my noting practice is that I just twist it to focus on feeling body sensations because that's pleasurable." —Alex H
helpful_sentence: "Instead of coming to a sit with an attitude of nailing your concentration to the object, come with the attitude how much play can I have." —Alex H
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate meditators frustrated with TMI's nostril-focus who want a concrete example of switching to full-body awareness and daily-life noting.

author: finding-oneself
summary: Over roughly a year and a half, Alex journals an on-again-off-again practice mixing noting, LED kasina, and Loch Kelly/Angelo DiLullo-style non-dual 'natural meditation' with microdosed 5-MeO-DMT and DMT, repeatedly reaching Equanimity while working to quit a long-standing kratom addiction, but never crosses into stream entry before the log is frozen for length.
interesting_sentence: "Btw if you see Angelo Dillulo kill Angelo Dillulo. If you see Daniel Ingram kill Daniel Ingram. If you see the Buddha kill the Buddha." —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "Reobservation is sort of like a gate keeper that only lets worthy meditators through the gate. You have to let your egotism die and become very simple." —shargrol
who_should_read: Meditators cycling repeatedly between Dark Night and Equanimity pre-stream-entry, especially those blending vipassana with psychedelics or non-dual teachers like Angelo DiLullo and Loch Kelly, will find a candid, unfiltered companion account here.

author: Alexandra F
summary: Six consecutive days of intensive lying-down sensory noting and self-inquiry produce a rapid arc from rage and existential terror at the dissolving sense of self to a rapture-filled 'shooting into boundless space' experience and a settled insight that every sensation is fair practice material.
interesting_sentence: "It was like I could feel my soul sneering, like, 'you thought that would last?'" —Alexandra F
helpful_sentence: "I realized that thinking itself is not a hindrance -- it's thinking about that is a hindrance." —Alexandra F
who_should_read: Meditators curious about the raw, day-by-day phenomenology of early insight practice, from self-dissolution panic to sudden rapture and boundless-space openings.

author: Alex N
summary: Details a refined Goenka-style vipassana body-scan practice, including a self-developed technique for distinguishing interoceptive body sensation from proprioceptive spatial awareness within a daily 3-4 hour sitting routine.
interesting_sentence: "So, take the meditation instruction seriously: perceive body sensation, observe change, and starve off your concepts." —Alex N
helpful_sentence: "Concentrate on individual points of sensation, not on lines, areas, or volumes." —Alex N
who_should_read: Goenka-tradition vipassana practitioners wanting nuanced, technical tips for refining the body-scan technique.

author: Alice S
summary: A sleep-apnea/hypothyroid-diagnosed labor-and-delivery nurse builds a daily practice around a phone teacher's guidance—handwashing mindfulness, stickK commitment contracts, body-scan noting—cycling through headless-practice glimpses and anger-sensation mapping, and self-assesses a match to the stages of insight and first three fetters after a week-long retreat while preparing for a 10-day self-directed retreat.
interesting_sentence: "After this retreat, there was 7% less Alice." —Alice S
helpful_sentence: "Start with the minimum amount of time you are certain you can do every day, without fail." —Alice S
who_should_read: Working professionals with health complications seeking a model for teacher-guided, off-cushion mindfulness anchors (handwashing, commitment devices) building toward a self-recognized stream-entry milestone.

author: V C
summary: A beginner returning to practice closely investigates nostril-breath and chest sensations, notices thoughts fusing with and masking bodily sensation, and experiments with focusing on a flickering visual field, while veteran posters encourage him to try Mahasi-style fast/full noting.
interesting_sentence: "the center of it would flicker like a movie screen, and at the same time it seemed like I was moving through a much more panoramic vision than usual" —V C
helpful_sentence: "You can't think thoughts when you are fast noting thoughs, sensations, etc." —Sam Gentile
who_should_read: Beginner meditators exploring breath-based body awareness who want an unpolished, session-by-session look at early noting practice and community feedback.

author: Andrius Mikalauskas
summary: In two brief weekly updates, the author sits with a diffuse field of bodily sensations and restlessness, finding that relaxing into rather than investigating the field makes it feel more luminous and quiet.
interesting_sentence: "Like from a space emerges expanding field, and behind it there are my uninteded intetions, agitations, restlesness." —Andrius Mikalauskas
helpful_sentence: "So what i do is try to see through it and relax, there is nothing i can do anyway." —Andrius Mikalauskas
who_should_read: New meditators curious about a minimalist, field-awareness approach to sitting with restlessness and diffuse body sensation.

author: Tara ☔️
summary: A practitioner with childhood trauma, PTSD, and ADHD uses fire kasina and sensory-grounding concentration practice to reconnect with dissociated emotion, reports an ego-death experience and a self- and teacher-assessed equanimity, then is hospitalized for a manic/psychotic episode before stabilizing with medication and therapy.
interesting_sentence: "I just thought the pylon at the end of my garden was matreiya but let's keep that between ourselves." —Tara ☔️
helpful_sentence: "It's typically a more intense experience of the emotion, but it passes through the system much quicker and leaves less residue." —George S
who_should_read: Meditators with trauma histories or ADHD wanting a candid account of how concentration practice can intersect with dissociation, mania, and psychiatric crisis.

log: An honest attempt at fruition / stream-entry
author: Alexander Tesfamichael
summary: A Mahasi-style noting practitioner logs rapid arising-and-passing sensations in fine detail and experiments with Shinzen Young's 'note gone' technique, decomposing complex thoughts into raw sensory components while working toward a first fruition/stream-entry experience.
interesting_sentence: "Like walking through a doorway, but your shirt gets caught on the door handle." —Alexander Tesfamichael
helpful_sentence: "the basic idea is to take complex mind objects (e.g. thoughts about scooters) and decompose them into more simple mind objects (sounds, words, images), noting how they are always changing, never satisfactory, and can't be identified with a sense of self." —George S
who_should_read: Noting practitioners working toward a first fruition who want granular, technique-level detail on 'note gone' and rapid sensation decomposition.

author: Andreas Thef
summary: Documents Andreas' months-long cycling through intense facial kriyas, energetic head/neck movements, and shifting Dark Night/Equanimity states while noting his way from Cause & Effect/Three Characteristics toward what he tentatively concludes is 1st path fruition.
interesting_sentence: "There were times when I almost wished for some totally weird and scary multiple years long Kundalini ordeal." —Andreas Thef
helpful_sentence: "there is fear, I am aware of the fear, if I'm aware of it, I'm not afraid but rather I'm aware of fear." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners puzzling over whether intense physical kriyas signal A&P or Dark Night will find a detailed body-based case study mapped against the Progress of Insight.

author: Kuru
summary: A short log centered on fire-kasina candle-gazing sessions tracking the green afterimage dot, interspersed with a report of cycling out of a rough patch back into equanimity after prior Dark-Night bleedthrough into daily life.
interesting_sentence: "Like all beings are here intertwined and trapped in Indra's Jewelled Net of interdependent Hell together, feeding off of one another." —Kuru
helpful_sentence: "You can almost feel that flickering sensation of the dot correlate with a flickering sensation on the skin or somewhere in the sensorial field that might not really be the body but its 'there' somewhere." —Kuru
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about candle/fire kasina phenomenology and its interplay with A&P-Equanimity cycling would benefit most.

author: Andrew B
summary: Chronicles a DhO member's parallel commitment to joining the A.'.A.'. Thelemic magical order (Gems from the Equinox, Raja Yoga, the Resh ritual) alongside a modest daily Culadasa TMI breath practice, with a year of prior therapy credited for stabilizing both pursuits.
interesting_sentence: "As a matter of fact, if it weren't for the occult, I probably never would have gotten so deep into Buddhism, and if it weren't for getting deep into Buddhism, I wouldn't have found my way back to the occult!" —Andrew B
helpful_sentence: "Consider setting a regular update interval, such as 1x week, allowing yourself to post more if you wish, but not less." —Noah D
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about blending Thelemic ceremonial magick with a beginner-level Buddhist meditation practice will find a candid account of joining an occult initiatory order.

author: Andrew Mayer
summary: A meticulous near-daily noting log tracking Andrew Mayer's progression from scattered breath-focus through 'gapless practice,' self-inquiry into a 'strange attractor' sense of self, and stable equanimous absorption states, guided extensively by mentor Nikolai's RAIN-based feedback.
interesting_sentence: "As I understand it, the Strange Attractor is a center that doesn't exist." —Andrew Mayer
helpful_sentence: "This act of recognition and acceptance (via specific neutrality) coupled perhaps with becoming curious as to what is arising, how and why, will allow the mind to cease its habitual reaction to such shapes, colours and movements of mind." —Nikolai
who_should_read: Serious noters wanting a blow-by-blow daily record of concentration/insight practice paired with expert mentor feedback on the RAIN technique.

author: Andrew B
summary: Chronicles early, inconsistent sits combining statue-still concentration (which produced a spontaneous jhana-like absorption with silence and body-wide relaxation), third-eye/nadi focus, centering prayer, and freeform noting.
interesting_sentence: "When I moved my awareness to it, it started to slowly fade out, and then go silent. But when that happened, it was like the whole room became full of silence." —Andrew B
helpful_sentence: "I found this to reorient my approach to practice in an interesting way. It turned it into something fun again, instead of some grim obligation." —Andrew B
who_should_read: Beginner practitioners curious how stillness-based concentration and third-eye focus can spontaneously trigger jhana-like absorption before formal insight practice begins.

author: a my les
summary: Describes a series of content-heavy fruitions across multiple sense doors during a five-day home retreat, followed by heavy cannabis use that dimmed clarity and a subsequent Buddhist Geeks retreat that produced extended A&P states.
interesting_sentence: "watching a stream of thoughts become hands pulling a rope, the thought 'what if they just let go?' arises and the last three hands fall away, and so does everything, for a moment" —a my les
helpful_sentence: "I've also found that the 'act as if you are enlightened' to be quite useful for sila and daily life" —a my les
who_should_read: Yogis curious how content-heavy fruition phenomenology interacts with real-life complications like cannabis use and career doubt.

author: a my les
summary: Recounts a year of cycling between A&P and deepening Equanimity via a shamatha/do-nothing/noting sequence, an unusually vivid loving-awareness experience, and a frightening encounter with occult content that bled into practice.
interesting_sentence: "I became paranoid that there was a dark edge to the loving awareness I had felt earlier that week." —a my les
helpful_sentence: "Breaking it down into sensations can cause it to disperse or at least make you realize your mind is just applying an arbitrary label to a small part of a broad experience field." —George S
who_should_read: Meditators cycling between A&P and Equanimity who want a candid account of blending noting, do-nothing, and loving-kindness practice amid anxiety and low motivation.

author: Angel C
summary: Two weeks into daily 30-minute concentration practice, cycles through periods of deep calm and frustration while approaching but not reaching the first jhana, including piti lasting hours and a brief fear-of-dying episode.
interesting_sentence: "I felt like I was about to get even more concentrated, but that would mean to lose some control over myself somehow, so I resisted it." —Angel C
helpful_sentence: "I'm pretty happy about today's practice, as I reached a state of concentration new to me, my mind was pretty clear for what I perceived as a long time, I felt very peaceful." —Angel C
who_should_read: Absolute beginners to concentration practice curious what early piti and near-jhana access states feel like.

author: Auntie Antei
summary: A near-daily log of multiple silent 'Do Nothing'/surrender sits per day paired with a Zhan zhuang and ba duan jin qigong routine, tracking a recurring 'sine wave' of consciousness dipping below and above a threshold of thought, occasional possible nirodha-samadhi-like gaps, and the author's deliberation between entering an indefinite home retreat and starting a family with a new partner while touring as a musician.
interesting_sentence: "Stream entry wasn't my salvation. Things can still be very, very rough. Sotopannas less happy than an average Joe are a dime a dozen." —John L
helpful_sentence: "Rather, it's about letting go of our efforts to be any particular way. It's about seeing how we don't direct experience, and instead, experience happens at us." —John L
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to a soft, effortless 'Do Nothing' surrender-based style who want to see how disciplined daily sitting is sustained through touring, noise, fatigue, and a major relationship decision.

author: Auntie Antei
key_words: car accident and brain surgery recovery
summary: Following a three-month retreat, Antei logs twice-daily sits combining no-effort/letting-go awareness practice with zhan zhuang and ba duan jin qigong, wrestling with weeks of recurring torpor and, after a serious car accident and brain surgery, gradually rebuilding sitting volume during recovery.
interesting_sentence: "Last night I decided to drive home through an ice storm and predictably enough got into a wreck and now my car is totaled and I’m in the hospital recovering from brain surgery." —Auntie Antei
helpful_sentence: "Noting is basically dropping practice provided you're noting phenomena at a rate of at least 1 thing a second and not getting caught up on specific things." —Jure K
who_should_read: Practitioners managing daily torpor and dullness, or balancing intensive sitting practice with an unexpected health setback, will find this granular day-by-day surrender practice log useful.

log: Antei's Three-Month Solo Retreat Practice Log Winter 2024-25
author: Auntie Antei
summary: Recaps a practice history from MIDL and Zen breath/koan training through jhana-focused solo retreats to a three-month vipassana retreat at a monastery, then asks for help posting the full write-up online after hitting length limits on the forum and Reddit.
interesting_sentence: "In winter of 2023-24 I did a solo retreat for a month and had some kind of jhana breakthrough although I haven't developed the practice to where it's very clear and I'm far from adept." —Auntie Antei
helpful_sentence: "The instructions were to just sit and let everything come and go on its own, notice any effort to control and drop it." —Auntie Antei
who_should_read: Practitioners curious how a Zen/breath-counting background transitions into jhana-focused solo retreat practice under private teacher guidance.

author: Antonius Block
summary: A newly serious noting practitioner logs near-daily 40-135 minute sits dominated by intense body vibrations and jaw/face tension, tentatively identifying his own progress through A&P, Dark Night phases (fear, misery, disgust) and early Equanimity, including one extended post-sit state of unusually sustained moment-to-moment mindfulness.
interesting_sentence: "For a short time my whole vision (with eyes open) flickered, I don't think I've had that before." —Antonius Block
helpful_sentence: "I have a feeling that it doesn't really matter, I should just keep going and keep up the journal and try to not stress too much about progress." —Antonius Block
who_should_read: Meditators wanting a granular, session-by-session model of tracking vibratory phenomenology and dark-night emotions through careful noting.

log: As best as I can recall - feedback welcome
author: Delicate Monster
summary: Recounts progressing through three Goenka 10-day retreats and a switch to noting practice, describing vibratory 'flickering' sensations, sustained equanimity, and an apparent cessation event that sparks debate over which path it represents.
interesting_sentence: "I am Arahat too! I had cessation this morning, immediately I remembered that I already obtained first path from a dream I had as a child, then third path came in the mail along with some other junk." —Delicate Monster
helpful_sentence: "Path itself is utterly unmistakeable; much as it's been said a million times before, 'you'll know when it happens' really is the easiest and least outcome-seeking/goal-pursuing way to put it." —Tommy M
who_should_read: Meditators curious how a naturally insight-inclined mind maps onto the Progress of Insight, especially around ambiguous cessation and post-cessation identity questions, will find this exchange useful.

author: Asaf M
summary: Documents daily noting practice combining breath-based access concentration with novel techniques like anchoring on dukkha at nanosecond timescales and widening time perspective to work with discomfort and craving.
interesting_sentence: "Seems like nibaana is right here at femtolevel timescale, the quantum level." —Asaf M
helpful_sentence: "Widening the timescale leads to acceptance. 'I am going to be with this for the long haul, might as well get comfortable with it' Or conversely, leads to disenchantement 'I dont feel like obessing over this thing forever'" —Asaf M
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in creative noting variations—like anchoring on dukkha and manipulating perceived timescale—to work with discomfort and craving in daily-life practice.

author: Ashley K
summary: A stay-at-home mother new to dry insight practice logs about six months of daily noting (drawing on MCTB2, Manual of Insight, and MIDL meditation), cycling through A&P energetic phenomena, dark-night fear, irritation and grief, and settling into a stable, low-effort equanimity marked by recurring eyelid 'light shows' and champagne-bubble body sensations, all without ever going on retreat.
interesting_sentence: "It's like I'm afraid of cessation." —Ashley K
helpful_sentence: "One thing that can help in the midst of a can't-be-stopped mental drama is just raise a finger of one hand." —shargrol
who_should_read: Busy parents and working professionals wanting a candid, retreat-free model of daily noting practice steadily progressing through the classic insight stages.

author: Ashley K
summary: Documents roughly nine months of frequent noting practice (typically 30-90 minute sits) cycling between deep equanimity states and irritable, restless dark-night-like patches, marked by a self-developed 'getting meta' investigation technique, late-added brahmavihara/metta intentions, and extensive shargrol-guided troubleshooting of walking meditation, mapping, and effort.
interesting_sentence: "My heart is done, yo, it can't take the rollercoaster ride of pain anymore." —Ashley K
helpful_sentence: "When you find perfect no effort, that's the same thing as the mind dropping itself into nibbana." —shargrol
who_should_read: Noting practitioners juggling work and parenting who want a candid, month-by-month model of deepening equanimity through 'getting meta' investigation and expert DhO mentoring.

author: Ashley K
summary: Chronicles roughly five months of near-daily noting practice cycling repeatedly between reobservation/dark-night episodes (family conflict, work stress, an ADHD diagnosis) and stretches of stable equanimity marked by absorption, spaciousness, and sharply reduced reactivity, with no cessation or path claimed.
interesting_sentence: "I feel destroyed, defeated. I give up, for real this time. I'm not going to let my fighting ego get up again, ever again." —Ashley K
helpful_sentence: "The real trick to EQ is to get as curious about the EQ stuff as we did when investigating dark night stuff." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners cycling between reobservation and equanimity who want a candid, detailed account of how life stress and mood interact with formal noting practice.

author: Ashley K
summary: After a year-long hiatus for inner work on anxiety and overfunctioning, Ashley resumes daily noting practice and logs detailed sits featuring mind-screen light phenomena, energy sensations, recurring irritation and dark-night-like distress, a brief centerless/vantage-point-loss experience, and pulsing 'near miss' moments that shargrol flags as possibly cessation-adjacent.
interesting_sentence: "You might be 100% you had a cessation, just keep sitting and be curious about what happens next." —shargrol
helpful_sentence: "Anything that seems off (ill will, fear, doubt, uncertainty, vagueness. resistance) can be 'noted' --- and it instantly becomes fuel and not a problem." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners returning to noting practice after a break who want a candid, detail-rich account of cycling through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity phenomena alongside expert DhO guidance.

author: elizabeth
summary: A DhO member with decades of prior magickal and Tai Chi practice shares a year of poetry and dharma-book excerpts alongside brief first-person notes on fire-kasina concentration, jhana, and at least one cessation-like 'blip' that led to a lasting perceptual shift she frames as post-path review.
interesting_sentence: "I was surprised when something happened that seemed to fit Daniel's description of a cessation and a shift in perception. It was good but also unexpectedly odd." —elizabeth
helpful_sentence: "Off cushion, I focused on staying with whatever was happening at that moment without pushing it away or trying to hold on." —elizabeth
who_should_read: Practitioners combining concentration objects like fire kasina with insight who want a poetic, post-cessation reflection on 'silent running mode' and integrating awakening into ordinary life.

log: Auntie Antei’s Log of Destiny - Practice Log 3
author: Auntie Antei
key_words: dip below the axis of consciousness
summary: A meticulous multi-month solo house-retreat log tracking daily zhan zhuang qigong and hours-long silent sits marked by recurring 'dips below the axis of consciousness,' persistent mental music, and a teacher-guided practice of effortless surrender amid constant neighborhood noise.
interesting_sentence: "I don’t want to comfortably rest in comfort, I want to comfortably rest in discomfort." —Auntie Antei
helpful_sentence: "This is the metaphor of 'dropping the hot coal', when we actually feel the burning from the hot coal, the hand automatically drops it, but if we're distracting ourself from feeling it, we hold onto it for a long time." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners on long solo retreats who want a granular, honest model of sustained do-nothing/surrender-based practice and how to work with distraction, physical discomfort, and remote teacher feedback over months.

log: Auntie Antei’s Practice Log 4 — A Log Near a Stream
author: Auntie Antei
summary: Chronicles roughly five months of an intensive home retreat (often 10-19 hours/day) built around an effortless 'notice and let go' practice under a personal teacher (K), tracking recurring 'dip' phenomena, sukha/piti sensations, and repeated cycling between equanimity and dark-night backsliding without ever claiming a path.
interesting_sentence: "It’s as if my dreaming self experienced a cessation and already lives without the sense of an observer and I’m just waiting for my waking self to catch up." —Auntie Antei
helpful_sentence: "I'll say that again: watching how attention moves itself from object to object, seeing the seeking mind." —shargrol
who_should_read: Long-retreat yogis drawn to an effortless, surrender-based (rather than noting-based) approach who want a granular, months-long account of cycling through equanimity and dark-night backsliding.

log: Auntie Antei’s Practice Log 5 - Effortless Trusting Surrender
author: Auntie Antei
summary: Logs months of multiple daily sits on a solo home retreat tracking piti/sukha, dips, and a persistent dreamlike quality of experience, combining chanting, shavayatra relaxation, and self-inquiry, while shargrol and Papa Che Dusko map her fluctuations onto Reobservation, desire for deliverance, and early Equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "It’s like having a friend who talks a lot. They’re your friend and care about your well being but it’s so peaceful when they’re not around!" —Auntie Antei
helpful_sentence: "But also notice that \"noticing and letting go\" is actually aversion --- more specifically, the \"letting go\" aspect is aversion." —shargrol
who_should_read: Long-retreat samatha practitioners curious how subtle daily fluctuations of piti, dips, and mood get mapped onto the classic stages of insight by experienced forum mentors.

author: Aviva HaMakom
summary: Chronicles an ADHD-diagnosed psychotherapist's dramatic kundalini-driven A&P triggered on a 14-day Pa-Auk-style samatha retreat, followed by months of cycling energetic Dark Night symptoms, dietary experimentation, and ceremonial magick practice.
interesting_sentence: "I AM THE GREAT GOD PAN, AND YOU BELONG TO ME! I COMMAND YOU TO WORSHIP ME!" —Aviva HaMakom
helpful_sentence: "If rapture (piti) is too strong I focus on bliss (sukha) which calms things down." —George S
who_should_read: ADHD or neurodivergent meditators and anyone experiencing intense kundalini/energetic phenomena who want a detailed, medically-literate account of navigating A&P and Dark Night safely.

author: Jure K
summary: Jure K restarts his log while working through fear rooted in PTSD and medication tapering, cycling repeatedly between reobservation and equanimity with intermittent A&P surges and cessation-like fruitions, and taking up Papa Che Dusko's self-inquiry prompt 'who is experiencing this?' alongside noting and jhana absorption.
interesting_sentence: "There are no birds to be killed! But what wants to kill is a better question." —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "You simply let expereince arise during the sit --- whatever comes up --- and your job is to note one sensation or emotion or thought about 10 times a minute, one note with each outbreath." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners navigating PTSD, medication tapering, and formal noting practice together will find a candid, cycle-by-cycle account of self-inquiry and reob-to-equanimity dynamics useful.

author: J Bird
summary: Returning to Vipassana after a decade away, J Bird combines jhana-first sits, homemade Dependent-Origination inquiry, and dantian/qi work, weathers a mental-health crisis during a home retreat, and settles into a persistent, effortless nondual 'awake awareness' informed by Loch Kelly and Ken McLeod pointers.
interesting_sentence: "I really thought I was stable and clear eyed, but I was just floating above a whirlpool of attachments." —J Bird
helpful_sentence: "Making note of what it's like when things fall appart, when things are not under our control, and when they suck is the heart of the path!" —Martin V
who_should_read: Experienced yogis returning to practice after a long break who want a candid, month-by-month account of nondual pointing-out practice, retreat setbacks, and a genuine mental-health crisis intertwined with deepening insight.

author: Bagpuss The Gnome
summary: A Goenka-trained practitioner troubleshoots worsening skull-tightening headaches triggered by jhana practice, eventually finding relief by switching to Shinzen Young's sound-based 'Gone' noting and later his contraction/expansion Focus on Flow technique.
interesting_sentence: "...i am pulled over the \"event horizon\" and either my brain scrunches or i pop out in jhana or fall back to re-ob or something." —tom moylan
helpful_sentence: "For tonglen, breathe in with the intention to take on the tension-pain of the world, and breathe out with the intention to offer something good, perhaps your power of concentration." —fivebells
who_should_read: Concentration-heavy meditators battling physical pain or headaches from intense practice, looking for peer-tested workarounds.

author: Bagpuss The Gnome
summary: Chronicles a series of U Ba Khin-style body-scanning and anapanasati retreats where the author cycles repeatedly through Equanimity and Dark Night, exploring chakra-like jhana sensations, third-eye absorption, and extended 'boredom' phases in high equanimity while weighing whether to switch to noting practice.
interesting_sentence: "By the time the bell goes my bodily sensations are just a mass of streaming champaign bubbles so fine they almost defy observation." —Bagpuss The Gnome
helpful_sentence: "If there is no dissatisfaction in equanimity, there is no going further." —katy steger
who_should_read: Yogis working with U Ba Khin-style body-scanning who want a granular account of cycling through equanimity and dark night phenomena while weighing noting as a complementary technique.

author: Bagpuss The Gnome
summary: Sitting twice or three times daily using a 'just do nothing' and pleasure-as-focus approach, the author describes a vibrational, nana-linked four-stage jhana process, spontaneous nimitta arising and dissolving, sudden energetic drops into new spaces, and significant paraspinal/glute pain alongside fear of 'losing it'.
interesting_sentence: "Mist coalesces into nimitta, nimitta dissolves as it’s focused on, a new one appears (only brighter) and I dissolve that one." —Bagpuss The Gnome
helpful_sentence: "Instead of worrying about what comes after what and how it should be I just lie down, take a deep breath and sink into it." —Bagpuss The Gnome
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring nimitta-based jhana practice and body-centered letting-go techniques who want a candid account of pleasure, energy, and fear in early jhana work.

author: Bahiya Baby
summary: Resuming practice after a musical hiatus, Bahiya investigates the falling-away of self-referential attention through dependent-origination and Mahamudra-flavored practice, works through sensual desire and repressed anger, and reports the sense of agency dissolving toward what he calls 'centerlessness.'
interesting_sentence: "It's like the self is a hologram made to seem real by attention but when attention flickers the self flickers." —Bahiya Baby
helpful_sentence: "Sometimes it takes 'no practice' practices, or nonmeditation, for things to fully untangle." —shargrol
who_should_read: Post-third-path practitioners investigating agency and centerlessness who want to see the conceit/restlessness fetters discussed in granular, lived detail.

log: Bahiya 3: Put these foolish ambitions to rest
author: Bahiya Baby
summary: Describes a shift toward centerless awareness via a 'no meditation' investigation of the three characteristics, a forum anger outburst over confrontational real-world behavior followed by a self-imposed hiatus, and later reframes lingering restlessness, conceit, and desire-for-immaterial-rebirth fetters through Adi Da-influenced language about 'unfelt pain' and 'work-making' amid a long debate about whether anything lies 'underneath' experience.
interesting_sentence: "I'm not trying to say that I'm a vampire, I'm not... I'm a werewolf." —Bahiya Baby
helpful_sentence: "Dukka is your teacher." —shargrol
who_should_read: Anagami-stage practitioners working through anger and residual fetters who want a raw, community-debated account of 'no meditation' practice and its psychological fallout.

log: Bahiya 4 or: How I learned to stop worrying and love 4th path
author: Bahiya Baby
summary: Chronicles a shift into panoramic peripheral awareness and spontaneous heart-openings, works through the conceit and restlessness fetters using shargrol's five-elements/Dakini framework, then spirals into an anger-driven forum blow-up, a threatened departure, and a community reconciliation around Sangha and metta practice.
interesting_sentence: "I am going to be the most miserable Arhat the world has ever had the misfortune of enduring." —Bahiya Baby
helpful_sentence: "It is exhausting to do 5 element practice, but it leads to a lot of insight and resiliency." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners working through the final fetters (conceit, restlessness, ignorance) post-3rd-path who want a raw, unfiltered account paired with a detailed five-elements fetter-clearing method.

author: Bahiya Baby
summary: A long, literary log tracing shifts from seeing sensations as empty via sevenfold reasoning, through a tentatively claimed-then-retracted third-path fruition, deep 6th-8th jhana and agencyless walking-around states, an MDMA-assisted no-self experience, and a heart-centered guru-yoga-influenced practice.
interesting_sentence: "My entire administrative faculty have gone to the Bahamas and I'm not sure there'll be jobs for them when they get back." —Bahiya Baby
helpful_sentence: "No Distraction 10-15 minutes: This is a very inclusive "no distraction" where "the mind that knows" can't be distracted." —shargrol
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners exploring post-third-path agencylessness and non-dual heart practice who want an unusually literary, non-technical account.

author: Baka Baka
summary: A highly discursive, philosophically dense log from a software engineer working through post-stream-entry insight cycles amplified by psychedelics (ayahuasca, weed, a novel stimulant), culminating in a vivid 'Indra' visionary fruition the author interprets as evidence of second path.
interesting_sentence: "Always remember the four noble truths: shit's fucked, fuck me, fuck this, go fuck yourself" —Baka Baka
helpful_sentence: "The trickiness of the god realms is that they look a lot like awakening but bring with them a lot of greed, superiority and indifference, often in very fine barely noticeable detail." —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Analytically-minded practitioners curious how psychedelics, computer-science metaphors, and post-SE insight cycles intersect, especially those questioning how to validate path claims without a teacher.

author: ANDRE BARROS
summary: A Brazilian software developer logs near-daily hour-long mental-noting sits from March to May 2024, mostly wrestling with recurring dullness/torpor and leg pain, reporting one clear A&P-flavored session and several equanimity-like sits, while forum veterans coach him on phenomenological description and technique.
interesting_sentence: "The A&P is sex !! The dark night is changing a shitty diaper !!" —Bahiya Baby
helpful_sentence: "Physical sensations are always in the present moment, so if you are mindful of a sensation you are back in the present moment." —shargrol
who_should_read: Early noting practitioners who struggle with chronic dullness/torpor will find a candid, blow-by-blow model of how consistent daily logging and community feedback can sharpen practice.

author: ANDRE BARROS
summary: A Brazilian yogi logs near-daily hour-long noting/vipassana sits marked by recurring dullness, mini-dreams, and diffuse-attention phases mapped against the vipassana jhanas, receiving detailed ongoing coaching from shargrol and others on emotional tonality, non-resistance, and balancing effort with relaxation as he cycles through the Dark Night toward brief tastes of equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "It was like playing the Kazoo out of sync because the music started to become fast and complex." —ANDRE BARROS
helpful_sentence: "So if you allow your body to drop into dullness, with no shame, worries, or fears... I'll bet it transforms into something closer to the third vipassina jhana or even full-on third jhana." —shargrol
who_should_read: Dark-night yogis stuck in chronic dullness and diffuse attention who want a candid, day-by-day account of learning to stop fighting it, plus a mentor's detailed real-time coaching.

author: ANDRE BARROS
summary: A near-daily hour-long noting practice log tracking cycles through dullness/re-observation (confusion, lucid dreams, memory loss, leg pain) toward low and then high equanimity, with heavy feedback from forum regulars on letting go of control.
interesting_sentence: "Then I thought, 'To hell with it. If I'm losing control, let me lose it.'" —ANDRE BARROS
helpful_sentence: "The wisdom mind is vast and can easly 'hold' any experience, even difficulty, doubt, confusion, daydreams, trances, etc." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners currently cycling through re-observation and equanimity who want a granular, day-by-day model of how dullness, daydreaming, and letting-go interplay in noting practice.

author: ANDRE BARROS
summary: Andre logs roughly two months of daily hour-plus noting sits centered on dissecting a recurring 'dullness' phase of trance-like lucid dreams, leg pain, and monotony, eventually distinguishing it from the Dark Night and touching brief equanimity, absorption-like colored lights, and glimpses of a 'pure awareness' watching-view.
interesting_sentence: "It was always something strange, like trying to use a screwdriver to open a gate." —ANDRE BARROS
helpful_sentence: "The watching/knowing view is that in-between state that flashes for a moment when you notice that you are aware/watching/knowing itself." —shargrol
who_should_read: Meditators stuck battling persistent dullness or trance states during noting practice who want a granular, day-by-day account of working through it with community mentorship.

author: ANDRE BARROS
summary: Daily hour-long sittings, often paired with brief walking meditation, cycle through phases of control, lack-of-control 'lucid dreams,' and Dark Night discomfort under close guidance from forum mentor Shargrol, eventually settling into a sustained, lighter Equanimity with reduced craving for progress.
interesting_sentence: "So, if I read this correctly, you've swapped one fixation (desire) for another! Robbing Peter to pay Paul" —Chris M
helpful_sentence: "In mindful equanimity, you are aware of the texture, feeling, tone, and there is a sense of curiousity and intimacy with the experience." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners cycling through Dark Night into Equanimity who want a granular, day-by-day account of forum-mentored practice and how to work with lucid-dream-like fantasies and re-observation confusion.

author: ANDRE BARROS
summary: Andre describes recurring dreamlike, semi-lucid states arising near the end of his noting-based sits, works through subject-object non-duality questions with shargrol and kettu on the path to stream-entry, and reports having moved through a three-year Dark Night phase in 2024 while now shifting focus from formal sitting to character work in daily life.
interesting_sentence: "Just forget about Stream Entry! There is no chance you will ever get it!" —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "Don't just chill in EQ or union, instead ask 'what is the mind that knows EQ? what is the mind that knows union?'" —shargrol
who_should_read: Meditators nearing stream-entry who experience jhana-adjacent lucid dream states or are struggling to stay motivated through a long post-Dark-Night plateau.

author: Bart
summary: Opens a jhana-focused log built around Leigh Brasington's 'Right Concentration', starting with a structured, gradually-increasing-duration breath practice and reporting a first 10-minute sit where the breath dissolved before access concentration was established.
interesting_sentence: "Breath almost completely dissapeared without having developed enough concentration (that's why I wanted to use 'sound of silence', I remember now)." —Bart
helpful_sentence: "What I've noticed is that it's completely unhelpful to force yourself to do concentration meditation for extended periods of time, when you are very scatterbrained to begin with or when you haven't meditated much the past week." —Bart
who_should_read: Beginner concentration practitioners starting a structured jhana practice with Brasington's method who want to see a bare-bones first daily log.

author: Paul Quinn
summary: Outlines a first-year sutta-based practice plan emphasizing a moral foundation, cultivating wholesome states, and twice-daily 40-60 minute breath concentration sits with monthly progress summaries.
interesting_sentence: "Develop a solid foundation in morality." —Paul Quinn
helpful_sentence: "Develop concentration by paying attention to the breath." —Paul Quinn
who_should_read: New practitioners looking for a simple sutta-based structure combining morality, breath concentration, and monthly self-review to start a practice routine.

author: Tashi Tharpa
summary: A veteran TMI practitioner logs months of near-daily breath-counting and freestyle noting sits, tracking Culadasa's concentration stages, recurring hypnogogic/vibratory phenomena, and childhood-rooted patterns behind his 'head-centered' practice.
interesting_sentence: "The body seemed like an aggregate, a formation, suddenly suspended in the space." —Tashi Tharpa
helpful_sentence: "If you just left your attention on the object, you would have already found the pleasant qualities, because a state of real concentration is itself pleasurable." —spatial
who_should_read: TMI practitioners wanting a granular, multi-month account of working through the concentration stages amid recurring dullness and hypnogogia.

author: Ben V
summary: Benoit chronicles years of Mahasi-style noting and later one-on-one jhana coaching with Kenneth Folk, describing recurring facial/head vibrations and 'observer-collapse' states while never reaching stream-entry despite multiple solo retreats, and working through depression and his dog's death.
interesting_sentence: "Here's the big insight then: Insight is dukkha!" —Ben V
helpful_sentence: "Basically nibbana is in the spaces but the mind doesn't know how to find it. Eventually it will grab onto nibbana and that's stream entry." —shargrol
who_should_read: Noting practitioners curious about long-term equanimity plateaus, jhana exploration with Kenneth Folk, and practicing through grief and depression.

log: Ben's Meditation Log and Origin Story :)
author: Ben H
summary: A 25-year-old new to formal practice recounts a psychedelic-triggered mystical experience that drew him to Buddhism and describes his first weeks of Sirimangalo/TMI-style noting and walking meditation while coping with poor health.
interesting_sentence: "my entire vision was engulfed by an overlayed image on reality that was identical to a painting by Alex Grey called Net of Being" —Ben H
helpful_sentence: "I need to see clearly what makes me suffer and why. But suffering is hard to track down—even in meditation—because the moment I notice it, it disappears." —Ben H
who_should_read: Newcomers to meditation, especially those who arrived via a psychedelic mystical experience, looking for a relatable origin story.

author: Ben Sulsky
summary: Ben Sulsky logs roughly a year of intensive daily jhana-and-noting practice, moving from dark-night territory through a stream-entry-like review cycle into equanimity-stage 1st/2nd path work, with detailed shamatha jhana 1-8 experiments and reflections after his daughter's birth.
interesting_sentence: "It's shocking people do it without the benefit of the maps and without noting, I basically can't imagine making much progress off retreat without these tools." —Ben Sulsky
helpful_sentence: "The result is I've mostly just been sitting and seeing what happens, figuring that if there's an intention to investigate the 3Cs then the brain will sort itself out eventually." —Ben Sulsky
who_should_read: Serious home-practice yogis wanting an unusually technical, self-skeptical account of jhana work and post-stream-entry equanimity.

author: Bill T
summary: Bill restarts his practice log after a hiatus, describing a mood and gratitude boost from combining yoga with meditation and committing to less evening screen time plus 20 minutes of morning meditation.
interesting_sentence: "I'm struck by how we live better than Kings and Queens these days, but most of us are so unappreciative and miserable." —Bill T
helpful_sentence: "Make room in the mornings for at least 20 minutes of meditation." —Bill T
who_should_read: Practitioners looking for a relatable, low-key account of recommitting to a daily meditation and yoga routine after a lapse.

author: bill of the wandering mind
summary: A long-time noting practitioner switches to Bhante Vimalaramsi's 6R/Metta (TWIM) method, tracking strong loving-kindness states, kriyas, and repeated cycling through dark-night-like instability.
interesting_sentence: "Thats a heck of a drug right there." —bill of the wandering mind
helpful_sentence: "the 'release' step is a twofold process, the first is to let the distraction be, the second is to stop giving it your attention and then go on the the relax phase of the 6R process." —bill of the wandering mind
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about TWIM/6R-Metta as an alternative to noting for working with dark-night-style instability.

author: Bill T
summary: A long-time Goenka Vipassana practitioner describes a kundalini-charged A&P event in January 2020, then documents months of fire kasina, mantra, and jhana practice (cycling through 1st-5th jhana) alongside recurring A&P-like energy surges, brief death-like sensations, and a gradual settling into what he interprets as Equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "...got into a pretty good concentration then WHOOSH, a huge rushing orgasmic 'kundalini' type energy going through my body from bottom to top." —Bill T
helpful_sentence: "I would suggest to really decide which technique to pursue and STICK with IT for at least 6 months and give a good try before swapping." —Che Guebuddha
who_should_read: Vipassana practitioners curious how layering in fire kasina, mantra, and jhana work reshapes an A&P integration and eventual settling into equanimity.

author: Bill T
summary: A jhana-focused practitioner logs near-daily fire-kasina and Brasington-style concentration sits during UK lockdown, describing progressively deeper absorption up to hints of the 5th jhana along with one intense session marked by twitches, pins-and-needles, and kundalini-like energy.
interesting_sentence: "It's such a different state of consciousness, it's almost like default mode ignores it on purpose." —Bill T
helpful_sentence: "It's very interesting - adding a kasina to any sit is like cranking up the concentration, dropping down the dreamy-ness." —Bill T
who_should_read: Concentration-focused meditators interested in Leigh Brasington-style jhana and fire kasina practice will find a relatable day-to-day account of deepening absorption amid a busy work and family life.

author: Antonius Block
summary: Describes intense sits, often extending well past the timer, marked by fast full-body vibrations, high-pitched sound, bright lights, and noting of the three characteristics, plus a brief detour into questioning practice via Actual Freedom philosophy.
interesting_sentence: "I actually heard/felt some kind of "cracking" which seemed to come from my nose/sinuses/forehead." —Antonius Block
helpful_sentence: "I tried to be aware of the sensations' impermanence (which is very apparent from the flickerings), non-satisfactory character (this is less obvious - sensations don't really satify or not satisfy, they just are, although some are more pleasant than others), and non-self character (easily seen: if I ask where I am it is clear I am not in the sensations, not anywhere really)." —Antonius Block
who_should_read: Vipassana practitioners curious about full-body vibrational A&P phenomenology and how it intersects with Actual Freedom's critique of spiritual attainment.

author: Bob J Turner
summary: A day-by-day account of single-pointed breath-at-the-nose concentration practice tracking nimitta formation, piti, kriyas and the nana sound over several months, including a brief dark-night-to-low-equanimity cycle and forum guidance to stop rotating techniques.
interesting_sentence: "I’m not certain what happened next but I suddenly took a golf swing (I don’t play golf and don’t watch it on TV) to the left from the waist up." —Bob J Turner
helpful_sentence: "For high levels of concentration, I have found the best results come from actually treating pleasure as a distraction." —Martin V
who_should_read: Yogis pursuing TMI-style breath/jhana concentration who want a granular, honest day-by-day account of nimitta and piti development.

author: Bob No No
summary: An eclectic daily log mixing Clarifying the Natural State concentration exercises, tummo, tonglen and guided nature-of-mind/inquiry sessions, marked by strong physical kriyas and an emotionally raw revisiting of a childhood memory involving his mother.
interesting_sentence: "It was me as a kid, standing in front of my mother as she removed her robe and showed her naken body to me." —Bob No No
helpful_sentence: "Use the pain as knowledge, and bring back some pleasure in the practice when the insights of the 3 characteristics start cutting a blade too deep." —Richard Zen
who_should_read: Newer practitioners exploring eclectic energetic and non-dual practices who want an honest look at how childhood trauma surfaces in meditation.

author: BobsUncle
summary: After years of inconsistent TMI practice, switches to Shinzen's See/Hear/Feel and Mahasi-style noting and dedicates a Covid furlough year to daily practice aimed at stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "Practice, practice, practice, and Bob's your uncle!" —Tim Farrington
helpful_sentence: "An advantage of labeling it is that it gives a task to the verbal parts of the mind, so they are less likely to start telling stories that can distract us." —Martin
who_should_read: Beginners transitioning from TMI-style concentration to Mahasi noting, especially those balancing practice with physical limitations.

author: Boris F
summary: A richly detailed, multi-retreat log cycling through no-self investigation, brahmavihara and five-elements work, jhana cultivation, several cessations, and deep trauma-processing insights around a lifelong 'need to understand.'
interesting_sentence: "I really don't like that state, even though on some level it's all totally fine, but I also don't like how paradox it is, although I would never want to trade it back for how it used to be... *argh!*" —Boris F
helpful_sentence: "To practice samatha is completely different. You simply rest your hand lightly on the pillow case and leave it there. If it moves, follow it. You don't care what is inside; you don't want to know." —Kenneth Folk
who_should_read: Experienced practitioners wanting a granular, psychologically-informed account of post-stream-entry cycling, no-self investigation, and integrating trauma work with insight practice.

author: Brandon Whited
summary: A frustrated, goal-oriented meditator restarts his log with a 120-day commitment to body scanning and daoist standing meditation while soliciting community advice on which single technique (vipassana, fire kasina, or Dzogchen) to commit to, receiving cautions against overstriving and attainment-chasing.
interesting_sentence: "Who can I talk to... who'll help me decide on which practice to devote myself to until it fucking works?" —Brandon Whited
helpful_sentence: "Purely future-oriented goals are at best mostly worthless and at worst very dangerous." —Josef C
who_should_read: Meditators feeling adrift among competing techniques and teachers who want reassurance and concrete advice on committing to one practice.

author: Brandon Whited
summary: Documents a shift from daily 45-minute noting sessions to 60-minute fire kasina practice—complete with murk phenomenology of swirling purple/black/yellow visuals—while battling video-game addiction, procrastination, and experimenting with ayahuasca alongside meditation in pursuit of stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "I've changed the passwords to something random on all my games/gaming services. I won't touch them for at least a fucking year." —Brandon Whited
helpful_sentence: "So walking meditation is a very simple exercise of learning to both pay attention, but also learning to drop the clinging to any given sensation, urge, emotion, or thought." —shargrol
who_should_read: Beginners fighting procrastination, video-game compulsion, and daily-practice consistency who want a candid, unfiltered account of starting noting practice and later fire kasina.

author: Brandon Dayton
summary: Recaps a multi-year arc from a 2019 A&P through dukkha-ñāṇa convulsions and sleepiness plateaus into a jhana-and-equanimity-focused 'Mahayana vipassana' practice guided by teacher Abre, culminating in a partial MAPLE retreat attempt and ongoing self-inquiry via Sheng Yen's Method of No Method.
interesting_sentence: "The space suddenly became many spaces nested in one another and it was as if I was zooming in (or out?) through these nested heavenly realms." —Brandon Dayton
helpful_sentence: "Think of deep sea divers pausing in their ascent, to let the nitrogen out of their blood and avoid getting 'the bends.'" —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: Long-term insight meditators integrating jhana, equanimity and dark-night material who want a model for pacing intensive practice alongside family life and teacher support.

author: Brandon Dayton
summary: An extensive pre-stream-entry log tracking repeated cycling through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity via alternating noting, concentration/piti work, and a later teacher-taught body-sound-mind-awareness 'Mahayana loop' practice, with recurring terror around infinity/death and heavy sleepiness episodes.
interesting_sentence: "And there is this horror of being trapped in the crushing fact of neverending existence." —Brandon Dayton
helpful_sentence: "The only thing I ever needed from the Maps is the heads up on the possibility of the Dark Night after having some nice concentrated blissful meditation thinking thats all to it and that bliss is the goal." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Pre-stream-entry practitioners cycling repeatedly through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity who want a richly detailed, multi-month account combining noting, concentration, and teacher-guided practice.

author: Brandon Mather
summary: A newcomer blends Robert Adams-style self-enquiry, centering prayer, and breath counting before adding fire kasina and TWIM metta, working through a nihilistic/suicidal-ideation episode and reporting sharply reduced pornography and gaming compulsions.
interesting_sentence: "Maybe i'll kill myself, maybe I won't, it doesn't matter." —Brandon Mather
helpful_sentence: "This is classic of the nana (knowledge) of Mind & Body (stage 1 of the Progress of Insight - see MCTB and/or Practical Insight Meditation)" —George S
who_should_read: Beginners with addiction or depression histories curious how combining self-enquiry, prayer, and TWIM/metta can shift compulsive behavior and surface Dark-Night-like episodes.

author: Brodie
summary: A systematic Unified-Mindfulness practitioner cycles through see/hear/feel circuits and tinnitus-based jhana attempts, reports a fast A&P-through-near-Dark-Night arc on an 8-day Shinzen Young retreat intensified by LSD, and settles into Equanimity with a first taste of no-self.
interesting_sentence: "I think this is my first real taste of no-self without psychedelics." —Brodie
helpful_sentence: "So, if at some point you feel you are no longer making progress despite trying several different techniques in your toolbox, maybe consider that you could be in the DN and try reading advice related to that." —Danny S
who_should_read: Systematic Unified Mindfulness practitioners curious how combining Shinzen's circuits with Burbea-style jhana/energy-body work plays out through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity.

author: Brooklyn
summary: A near-daily, TMI-guided journal moving from breath concentration through months of spontaneous kundalini-like body twisting and shaking into reliable access concentration and glimpses of first jhana, punctuated by a meeting with Culadasa and a one-day Goenka retreat.
interesting_sentence: "This is how posesed person must look like." —Brooklyn
helpful_sentence: "When the breath changes due to unconsious precesses (even though it suits your consious processes), \"you\" did not do it, so don't intrefere. Just notice that it has changed and keep observing everything passively and objectively, letting the breath continues as is." —Brooklyn
who_should_read: TMI practitioners curious about kundalini-like energetic release phenomena and the gradual path from breath concentration to access concentration and early jhana.

author: Bruno Loff
summary: Chronicles a self-organized 12-hour-a-day home retreat in Portugal that triggers an escalating kundalini-like energetic imbalance—tracked with a Galvanic Skin Response biofeedback device—forcing a temporary pause in practice amid anxiety and IBS symptoms.
interesting_sentence: "At the very extreme of the process (at least the most extreme I have personally experienced it), it feels a bit like being raped by the universe." —Bruno Loff
helpful_sentence: "When I incline into calmness that way, the sensations tend to slowly settle, yet leaving the area more alive and open than it was before the release." —Bruno Loff
who_should_read: Meditators facing kundalini-like energetic overwhelm during intensive retreat who want a technical, instrumented account of managing the anxiety it triggers.

author: Bubby Soup
summary: A dream-and-waking blended log mixing fire kasina and mantra work with lucid-dreaming experiments and a return to classic noting vipassana, culminating in a strong anatta-flavored near-fruition during a 'letting the mind wander' exercise.
interesting_sentence: "There was this kind of satisfying unwordable knowing forming, but it gave way to a kind of fear and desire combo, something gripped and held and another yearned and grasped and so no fruition." —Bubby Soup
helpful_sentence: "His typical guided meditation structure is move the body -> build concentration -> main meditation (usually some nondual/vipashyana/inquiry practice)." —Kailin T
who_should_read: Goenka-trained vipassana practitioners curious about blending fire kasina, lucid-dream work, and noting who want an honest account of a near-fruition anatta experience.

author: bud
summary: A weekly noting-practice log tracking a grinding cycle of A&P body shaking and prolonged dark-night dissolution/reobservation (fear, disgust, gagging, boredom) with only brief tastes of low equanimity, including a 7-hour partial-day home retreat.
interesting_sentence: "I felt pathetic and useless. I wanted it all to just fucking end so bad." —bud
helpful_sentence: "If fantasy/low concentration is still an issue, then consider not eating after noon." —katy steger
who_should_read: Noting practitioners deep in dark-night dissolution/reobservation cycles who want reassurance and concrete markers for what brief low equanimity feels like.

log: Calmly going forward- Andrew's practice notes
author: Andrew
summary: A year-long actualism (Actual Freedom Trust) log built around the HAIETMOBA question and a daily 'smiling practice' experiment, mixing real-world confrontation reports and philosophical wrangling over AFT theory amid pushback from a moderator about whether the posts constitute genuine practice.
interesting_sentence: "self-moderated deletion. too many threads polluted by 'me', even if it is my own." —Andrew
helpful_sentence: "in general, it sounds to me like you are ranting and venting rather than caring and contemplating." —Trent
who_should_read: Actualism/AFT practitioners wrestling with how much to blend Buddhist insight practice with Richard's method, who want a warts-and-all record of resistance, self-criticism, and forum debate.

author: Tomer Boyarski
summary: A loosely structured collection of brief, contented daily-life reflections and retreat musings (Ajahn Brahm vs. Shinzen) rather than a detailed record of formal sitting practice.
interesting_sentence: "No one is walking on the non-existing path between nowhere and nowhere. But he is really walking!" —Tomer Boyarski
helpful_sentence: "compassion = Love + sadness compersion = Love + happiness" —Tomer Boyarski
who_should_read: Readers wanting light, casual snapshots of everyday contentment and gratitude rather than deep insight-stage analysis.

author: Caro
summary: A detailed multi-month log moving from first noting practice under teacher Ron Crouch through repeated A&P-like energy cycles and a dark-night-like collapse requiring a break and psychotherapy, to a black-curtain fruition experienced as a likely 2nd path and a return to jhana- and metta-focused practice.
interesting_sentence: "Suddenly, it felt like something black passed over my field of vision (although I had my eyes closed) and awareness." —Caro
helpful_sentence: "What does help is, if I manage to subtly change my inner orientation towards acceptance and just being with whatever is." —Caro
who_should_read: Practitioners cycling through post-stream-entry dark-night territory who want an honest, phenomenologically detailed account of noting, jhana, and integrating psychotherapy with insight practice.

author: Cedric
summary: An extended, highly interactive noting-practice log tracking a multi-year journey through repeated Dark Night/Equanimity cycling, a 10-day Goenka retreat, and a month-long TMC retreat under Sayadaw U Pandita, without yet reaching stream entry by the log's end.
interesting_sentence: "I won't relent. I am hot on the trail of the housebuilder!" —Cedric
helpful_sentence: "Actually, the practice can be boiled down to \"just keep noting\" and literally just that." —Jigme Sengye
who_should_read: Noting practitioners preparing for retreat who want a candid, blow-by-blow account of cycling between Dark Night and Equanimity plus practical retreat logistics advice.

author: Charlie B
summary: A raw, emotionally intense log chronicling a prolonged, multi-year Dark Night marked by numbness, fear cycling, and anger at a former Zen teacher's dismissal, interwoven with paleo-vs-vegetarian diet debates and glimmers of approaching equanimity by the final entries.
interesting_sentence: "I guess a good metaphor would be if you cut down the jungle to grow a garden... its going to be muddy, full of dead animals... a complete mess." —Charlie B
helpful_sentence: "I now base my practice off a few key principals such as \"The path can be summarized in one word: relaxation\" and \"Feeling the pain fully is purifying it\" and \"Don't go into stories, stay with the present moment\"." —Charlie B
who_should_read: Yogis stuck in a long, emotionally brutal Dark Night who want a visceral, unfiltered account of numbness and fear cycling and the slow approach toward equanimity.

author: Charlotte
summary: Working through The Mind Illuminated stages 5-7, Charlotte reports strong piti and brief first/second jhana touches that she suspects mark an A&P event, followed by a caffeine-detox-linked dip into dullness and mundane sits.
interesting_sentence: "Ironically I have meditated more today than any day this week, chasing the pleasurable piti." —Charlotte
helpful_sentence: "I think I have been doing a little too much clinging and chasing what was, rather than being with what is." —Charlotte
who_should_read: TMI practitioners in the concentration stages who want a candid account of piti, dullness, and caffeine's effect on practice.

author: Chris André
summary: A former Thai forest monk with 15 years of practice describes attaining stream entry, then working through a persistent bodily 'self-contraction' via vipassana and open awareness while combining formal noting practice with gestalt psychotherapy exercises as he pursues 2nd path.
interesting_sentence: "Last post is the only time in 15 years of meditating that I have ever felt as if the process was completed." —Chris André
helpful_sentence: "Just learn the same lessons, apply the same advice to the hard stages, avoid the standard traps, investigate the three characteristics, work to learn to navigate the stages, be present to what is happening, incorporate more and more of the senses into the practice along with space, balance the seven factors, that sort of thing." —Daniel M Ingram
who_should_read: Stream-enterers curious what the transition toward 2nd path feels like, especially those interested in blending psychotherapy with insight practice, will get a detailed phenomenological account.

author: Chris André
summary: Chris André cycles through repeated 'this is it' experiences he initially frames as possible 4th path, combines noting-aloud vipassana with jhana exploration up through the formless jhanas and gestalt-therapy self-expression techniques, works through a childhood core wound and grief, quits alcohol for three months, and ultimately settles on claiming only 2nd path.
interesting_sentence: "I probably had that a thousand or more times over 6 years as I cycled through something like 27 or so path cycles post 2nd path, and their review phases would be so impressive, so right-feeling, particularly during Review phases when I would get into Equanimity, but they didn't last, didn't hold up." —Daniel M Ingram
helpful_sentence: "Lagging behind is an illusion. Everything is happening now. However, until you realize that whatever sensations that imply \"lagging behind\" are actually occurring now, they might be able to fool you into believing that such a thing is possible. Thus, investigate carefully the sensations that imply \"lagging behind\", as you will realize they are always immediate and that they themselves are the thing happening, and they are as valid a thing to happen now as anything else from a vipassana point of view." —Daniel M Ingram
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry practitioners navigating repeated 'am I done yet' experiences would benefit from this candid account of misreading Equanimity/Review highs as higher paths and integrating therapy with practice.

author: Chris André
summary: A post-stream-entry practitioner blends Qi-energy breath meditation, Mahasi-style noting (progressing to noting aloud), and Shikantaza while processing ayahuasca ceremonies and gestalt therapy, working through cycles of manic obsession and dark-night material to establish a stable daily sit.
interesting_sentence: "So bringing this new willingness to stay with my difficult shit into an ayahuasca journey, woah! that was really something." —Chris André
helpful_sentence: "There is a cognative side (the thoughts within the dark night) and the visceral side (the little tensions)." —shargrol
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry practitioners cycling through obsessive patterns and dark-night material who want a candid account of integrating psychedelics and therapy with daily vipassana.

author: Christian
summary: A returning TMI practitioner logs a month of 10-30 minute sits, battling morning dullness while experimenting with breath concentration, 'who am I' self-inquiry, and noticing piti-like tingling sensations.
interesting_sentence: "Somehow get an image of a tube (=awareness) in which sensations arise, but otherwise can't really \"get\" anything." —Christian
helpful_sentence: "It's funny how sometimes you think you're not getting anywhere in a given meditation sit, but over several sits, you're actually moving slowly forward." —Christian
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate TMI practitioners wanting an honest, unpolished log of early concentration-stage struggles with dullness.

author: Colin
summary: Colin logs daily 20-30 minute noting sessions alternating with breath concentration, tracking vibratory sensations, spatial 'blips,' and cycling dullness/restlessness that he interprets as the middle dukkha nanas after transitioning away from a TMI-based practice.
interesting_sentence: "The experience was much like when walking down stairs and missing the last step; there was a jarring, unexpected falling sensation before the spatial orientation stabilized again." —Colin
helpful_sentence: "The ultimate objective is to really dig into what those are, where they come from and how they're constructed." —Chris M
who_should_read: Practitioners transitioning from concentration-based (TMI) practice to noting who want a granular, honest account of dark-night-like dullness and restlessness.

author: Life's Playground
summary: A lifestyle- and business-focused practitioner logs brief daily concentration sits (often 5-30 minutes) combined with Wim Hof breathing, kriya yoga, float tanks, and hypnosis, framed mainly around productivity, journaling habits, and material success rather than classical insight territory.
interesting_sentence: "It's like I'm in a video game and I see the main quest being laid out, but I often get sucked into side missions, and avoid completing the main quest." —Life's Playground
helpful_sentence: "Honestly I think you have your hopes set too high for meditation. Have you tried therapy?" —George S
who_should_read: Readers curious how concentration meditation gets folded into a hustle-culture self-improvement routine (Wim Hof, float tanks, hypnosis, outcome journaling) rather than classical insight practice.

author: Chris G
summary: Chris G logs months of daily 40-60 minute breath-concentration sits following Culadasa's Ten Stages, tracking his progress through stages 2-6 via 'continuity of attention,' positive-reinforcement noting, and occasional unusual visionary experiences, while wrestling with doubt about balancing practice with work.
interesting_sentence: "Last night: strange experience with the spirit of an animal (a gorilla) wanting to incarnate itself into my body." —Chris G
helpful_sentence: "So long as attention dwells on the meditation object longer than on any one distraction, the meditation object is perceived as being the primary object of attention." —Culadasa
who_should_read: Concentration practitioners following Culadasa's Ten Stages who want a candid, stage-by-stage account of continuity of attention, effort-vs-ease tradeoffs, and unusual imagery that can arise along the way.

log: Cultivating Diligence, Coughing up Phlegm and Listening to the Heater
author: Mindspace M
summary: Sit-by-sit notes cycling between dark night and equanimity, including a golden-light head-opening experience and a possible-but-later-doubted stream entry marked by hearing three bleeps mid-sit.
interesting_sentence: "felt the top of my head open up and golden light stream in." —Mindspace M
helpful_sentence: "A stable mind is able to solve one problem at a time without getting swamped by a whole pile of things." —Not Tao
who_should_read: Practitioners cycling between dark night and equanimity who want a candid, dated account of daily practice and second-guessing a possible stream-entry moment.

author: Emil Jensen
summary: Tracks a transition from weeks of dukkha-nana aversion and restlessness into a stable low-Equanimity state, marked by spontaneous laughing/crying releases, a vivid lucid dream of meditating on the moon, and a sleep-paralysis episode.
