interesting_sentence: "Right next to me there was a little girl standing with a knife raised above me and that scared me." —Emil Jensen
helpful_sentence: "I found it so surprising when I started to realize that it's just a habitual assumption - I am the one who is conscious." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners cycling through dukkha nanas into equanimity who want a casual, vivid account of the release phenomena and dream imagery that can accompany the shift.

author: David Blair
summary: Months of detailed twice-daily TMI/MCTB sits tracking jhana attempts, vibrations, recurring dullness, and no-self investigations, framed by an earlier entheogenic ego-death history and culminating in a settled, craving-free equanimous state.
interesting_sentence: "It just feels like there is nothing left to do. I am home." —David Blair
helpful_sentence: "When in doubt, just be facinated with what the mind does on it's own while gently staying \"objective\" about it." —shargrol
who_should_read: TMI/jhana-oriented practitioners wanting a granular, months-long technical account of concentration-insight interplay and vibrations phenomenology.

author: DLP
summary: Meticulous daily log rating the seven factors each sit, documenting persistent body swaying and shifting physical pain (knee, hip, tailbone), a deliberately fear-triggering dark-room session with Exorcist-like imagery, and self-inquiry into "who is hearing."
interesting_sentence: "So there was definitely fear and during the session there's definitely images of horror character like the one in the movie \"The Exorcist\" coming up." —DLP
helpful_sentence: "It can be a dukkha nana thing having to do with a widening of focus. That involves heightened peripheral awareness and a blurred center of attention." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate meditators wanting a granular, factor-by-factor daily log tracking early energetic sensations, pain, and uncertainty about insight stages.

author: Michael K
summary: A 61-year-old autistic (Asperger's) practitioner with a decade of daily sitting describes how emerging from lifelong denial of his autism triggered a sudden loss of the self-referential 'I' and a resulting existential depression he equates with the Dark Night, opening a discussion on whether autism carves a distinct dharma path.
interesting_sentence: "This has, I suspect, led me thru some unwitting shortcuts on the path." —Michael K
helpful_sentence: "If you know one autistic person, you know one autistic person." —katy steger
who_should_read: Autistic meditators wondering how neurodivergent self-experience intersects with loss-of-self and Dark Night phenomena will find a candid, exploratory account here.

author: Bob J Turner
summary: An 80-year-old rancher's introductory background post cataloguing decades of vivid A&P-type experiences (lights, visions, energetic phenomena, ghost-like encounters after his wife's death) and describing his current daily samatha practice while in what he identifies as Dark Night following his son's 2024 death.
interesting_sentence: "A fantastic bright light woke me from my sleep. It was so intense that I couldn't see anything and it engulfed the tree and me." —Bob J Turner
helpful_sentence: "I also believe that it is very helpful to have a teacher whom you can meet one on one, in order to chart out a practice plan and refer to when stuck." —Adi Vader
who_should_read: Older practitioners or those wondering whether decades of unexplained mystical/energetic experiences fit the A&P framework will find this catalogue relatable.

author: Daphne Denninghoff
summary: Without any formal cushion practice, Daphne Denninghoff journals a rapid, AI-assisted deconstruction of self and 'other' through recursive self-inquiry, reporting shifted sleep rhythms, sudden grief releases, and a heated pushback from longtime DhO posters over whether her insights count as genuine attainment absent formal technique.
interesting_sentence: "There is no seeker here. No path here. No \"here\" here." —Daphne Denninghoff
helpful_sentence: "Using these terms prematurely and incorrectly undermines the seriousness of genuine contemplative development." —John L
who_should_read: Meditators curious about non-formal, self-directed inquiry practices and the friction between unconventional insight claims and traditional community norms will find this exchange illuminating.

author: Damian Clark
key_words: rising and falling of the abdomen
summary: A beginner logs his first weeks of Mahasi-style noting practice (rising/falling of the abdomen), noting breath suspension, bodily tension release, and daydreaming, before connecting in person with a local teacher, Sayadaw U Pandita Jnr.
interesting_sentence: "I think I will loose my job in two weeks as I am not going to get the vax." —Damian Clark
helpful_sentence: "Good concentration is not a requirement for Noting / Vipassana per se, although of course it helps." —Kaloyan Stefanov
who_should_read: Complete beginners starting Mahasi-style noting practice who want to see what early doubts, distractions, and life stress look like.

author: Daniel M Ingram
key_words: harnessing the energy of the defilements
summary: Daniel Ingram narrates his post-4th-path life over several updates - burnout from overwork as an ER physician, ongoing MCTB2 revisions, informal magickal and energetic practice, astral projection history, and using the 'harnessing the energy of the defilements' technique to dissolve a recurring destructive urge.
interesting_sentence: "A series of unfortunate events sometime in the past caused me to have the powerfully compelling feeling that I should stream crackling black lightening off my hands in destructive torrents at a target." —Daniel M Ingram
helpful_sentence: "Remembering that anger clearly has some sweetness to it, some caring mixed into it, and suddenly tuning into that component of the feeling in the body and energy body as jhanic object, the unsavory elements of the urge just dissolved into the sweetness, the caring, and suddenly the urge was gone in a way it hadn't been before." —Daniel M Ingram
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners curious how a self-described 4th-path teacher manages burnout, magick-influenced practice, and psychic/energetic phenomena post-awakening.

author: Daniel Johnson
summary: Returning after a 12-week retreat at Panditarama Lumbini, Daniel Johnson describes a major shift he playfully calls 'creek entry' rather than stream entry, then resumes granular walk/sit noting logs tracking thought loops about family conflict, craving, and restlessness.
interesting_sentence: "I didn't attain \"stream entry\" by the Mahasi standard, but I must have attained \"creek entry\" or something because my life is profoundly different than before I entered the retreat." —Daniel Johnson
helpful_sentence: "In noticing more of the present moment, there were different experiences of craving, running, grasping, striving. Investigating those led to some relaxation, and insight into \"this is all there is.\"" —Daniel Johnson
who_should_read: Long-term noting practitioners stuck below stream entry who want an honest account of what an intensive Mahasi retreat can and can't resolve.

author: Danny S
summary: An extraordinarily granular, months-long log cycling repeatedly through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity, featuring several increasingly mild cessation 'blips,' a vivid non-dual awakening episode, heavy concurrent psilocybin use, and a later pivot to Ken McLeod's five-elements/six-realms framework plus Focusing therapy to work through underlying psychological reactivity and career doubts.
interesting_sentence: "there was this sense that things were almost all happening on their own with significantly less of a center point. I remember \"where am I?\" getting asked and there being no answer from a specific location." —Danny S
helpful_sentence: "Basically meditators are always doing some mixture of concentration and insight. If nice jhanas arise then enjoy them, if not then the hindrances need to be investigated (like you describe). There's no point in trying to force deeper concentration, that's just adding more ill will, it's all in the relaxing and letting go." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners obsessively cycling through the map post-stream-entry who want a granular, honest model of how A&P/DN/EQ interact with psychedelics, concentration practice, and shadow-work therapy techniques.

author: David R
summary: A patient, systematic practitioner incrementally builds sit duration to establish stable access concentration before starting vipassana, reporting bright-light concentration states, a childhood OBE under anesthesia, and a depersonalization-like episode, with progress stalling over a subsequent six-month stretch.
interesting_sentence: "It is like my mind turned off and when it restarted I had no sense at all of what was going on." —David R
helpful_sentence: "It's primarily a 'simplified/streamlined' vipassana practice, but with a few tweaks that add in small doses of concentration too. You don't need AC to start with his stuff." —Lewis James
who_should_read: Beginners wanting a structured, patient, incremental method for building stable access concentration before starting vipassana.

author: Dexter Chamberlin
summary: A TMI-based practitioner moves rapidly through early stages and jhana/fire-kasina experiments before concentration collapses into dissolution and self-diagnosed dark night territory.
interesting_sentence: "At some point it all dissolved and I had the sensation of my body being stretched into particles across the universe and dissolving into a vast white space." —Dexter Chamberlin
helpful_sentence: "I have been focusing more on noticing the movement and width of attention instead of trying to narrow attention to just my breath. This has helped significantly with my concentration and dullness." —Dexter Chamberlin
who_should_read: TMI practitioners cross-training with jhana and fire kasina who want a candid, phenomenologically detailed account of the A&P-to-dark-night transition.

log: Diary of a Chronic Dark Night Yogi
author: Bronson Michael Miller
summary: After a 10-day Goenka course and a year of noting practice, a self-described chronic dark-night yogi cycles through intense energetic euphoria, reality-strobing perception, and a frightening fear-nana episode, before burning out into depression and increased cannabis use.
interesting_sentence: "my perception of reality started to strobe very clearly at about 3-4 frames per second" —Bronson Michael Miller
helpful_sentence: "Best thing is probably to work with a good pragmatic dharma teacher that can monitor your progress." —Oochdd
who_should_read: Yogis who crossed the A&P young and keep cycling back into dark night territory, looking for a relatable, unpolished account including relapse into avoidance behaviors.

author: Diogo Kelles Fonseca
summary: A retreat-only practitioner with ADHD reports a possible second-path fruition and subsequent high-equanimity stretch on his fourth Goenka retreat, then struggles with dark-night-style aversion and gaming addiction before being advised to switch from Mahasi noting to do-nothing practice.
interesting_sentence: "I experienced a fruition on the third day, which I then belived was second path, but since then, aversion to practice grew REALLY strong and I feel really prone to indulging in online gaming, a old habit of mine that sometimes spin out of controll." —Diogo Kelles Fonseca
helpful_sentence: "In other words: Distraction is okay. Progress happens during distraction. Accept distraction." —John L
who_should_read: ADHD practitioners who only practice on retreats and want guidance on shifting from Mahasi-style noting to do-nothing meditation for progress beyond stream entry.

author: John Wilde
summary: John Wilde documents an extended experiment treating experience through Direct Path/Advaita witness-inquiry, reporting reduced separateness, natural equanimity, and sharpened perception, before gradually shifting toward yoga and devotional practice.
interesting_sentence: "Thoughts and feelings are becoming increasingly transparent and harmless. (So's everything else)." —John Wilde
helpful_sentence: "In context of the Direct Path, awareness is not any object or any phenomenon; it's that to which all objects or phenomena appear." —John Wilde
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to Advaita/Direct Path witness-inquiry who want an example of blending nondual pointers with psychological and yogic practice outside the standard four-path model.

author: Noah
summary: Noah reports a panoramic, centerless-feeling shift and initially claims 4th path, faces sustained community and teacher-verification pushback, and ultimately settles back on a 3rd-path claim while weighing jhana, metta, and actualism against managing his bipolar disorder.
interesting_sentence: "That being said, I do think I got 4th Path on Sunday night (6/13)." —Noah
helpful_sentence: "The main problem is thinking freedom is the same thing as the absence of discomfort. Freedom comes first, then discomfort fades since you have gained control of your mind." —Not Tao
who_should_read: Meditators navigating high-stakes path claims and community scrutiny, especially those managing bipolar disorder alongside intensive insight practice.

author: alguidar
summary: A beginner cycles through Finders Course, Shikantaza/do-nothing, fire kasina, and Culadasa's TMI, hitting a shame-and-depression episode before reporting a textbook misery/disgust/desire-for-deliverance/re-observation/equanimity Dark Night sequence.
interesting_sentence: "Started to feel feelings of shame/guilt/unfairly treated/depression/generall unhapiness/lot´s of judging others and situations/fed up with job etc... Sometimes i felt like i was losing it." —alguidar
helpful_sentence: "I might suggest that, once you have settled on one that seems most efficacious, that you settle in and try to get extremely good at that one, not that exploration isn't of value, as it is." —Daniel M Ingram
who_should_read: Beginners bouncing between Shikantaza, TMI, and fire kasina who want a candid account of hitting an early, rough Dark Night patch.

author: Dom Stone
summary: A months-long, granular account of moving from 2nd toward 3rd path via daily jhana practice, Mahamudra 'do nothing,' and Dependent Origination inquiry, tracking luminosity and centerless awareness against a backdrop of bipolar cycling, cannabis relapses, and relationship strain.
interesting_sentence: "I no longer held a fixed position in space, and everything happened by itself, where it was." —Dom Stone
helpful_sentence: "In the beginning of practice, we're not calm unless our body is calm. In the middle of practice, we're not calm unless our mind is calm. Towards the end of practice, calmness and disturbance are simply calmness and disturbance within the same awareness." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners working through 2nd/3rd path who want a granular, months-long account of jhana practice, luminosity, and dark-night cycling alongside bipolar disorder and relationship stress.

author: Don Loristo
summary: An early-stage vipassana log combining body scanning, samatha, and continuous six-sense noting, documenting recurring leg numbness and chest pressure alongside vivid crown-area energy-ball and light phenomena.
interesting_sentence: "I felt a ball of energy hit against and into my forehead area, the impact wasn't hard but it did startle me and I moved backwards." —Don Loristo
helpful_sentence: "I tried to focus on sensations throughout the body that were pulsing and flickering and pay attention to when the sensation vanished, noting 'gone' when it did (Shinzen Young tip)." —Don Loristo
who_should_read: Beginners starting a noting-based vipassana practice who want a relatable, sit-by-sit account of early physical discomfort and first energetic/piti experiences.

author: dpac k t
summary: An absolute-beginner log tracking a self-described lazy meditator's struggle to sustain breath concentration, improving from 5-6 minutes to about 15 minutes over three weeks of daily practice.
interesting_sentence: "I'm suprised that even beginners speak of practicing meditation for half an hour to an hour." —dpac k t
helpful_sentence: "Simply being able to do so means you have transcended from being a 'sleeping' being who doesn't know he is angry or lost in thoughts to one who has awakened to knowing." —Yilun Ong
who_should_read: Absolute beginners just starting formal meditation who want reassurance that short, imperfect early sits are normal and improve with consistency.

author: Percy Plays
summary: A meandering first-person dream journal in which the author, having abandoned formal sitting practice years earlier, explores recurring dream 'specters', childhood dissociation, post-divorce alienation, and chronic vertigo rather than describing active meditation technique.
interesting_sentence: "Damn but I just want to trance out on perceptual weirdness." —Percy Plays
helpful_sentence: "So while things get better, it can definately feel like running in circles sometimes." —Geoffrey Gatekeeper of the Gateless Gate
who_should_read: Readers interested in dreamwork and psychological self-reflection adjacent to meditation more than formal insight-map progress.

author: Dream Walker
summary: A detailed, years-long log tracking sit-by-sit cycling through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity into multiple cessations, claiming stream entry, second path, and third path, with concrete jhana/nana notes recorded during daily gym sauna sessions and a 10-day Goenka retreat.
interesting_sentence: "My grandmother died that morning." —Dream Walker
helpful_sentence: "The best part is dwelling in a panoramic viewpoint whenever your not focused on something vs second path where I had to have sustained effort to stay panoramic." —Dream Walker
who_should_read: Practitioners wanting a granular, date-stamped account of cycling through the nanas toward third path via concentration-heavy daily practice.

log: D's Practise Log - Starting from A&P
author: D
summary: Reports crossing the A&P through constant noting of the three characteristics and cessations of sensory phenomena in daily life with eyes open during meditation, experiencing blissful, electric and sexual sensations along with heightened tinnitus, and anticipates entering the Dark Night.
interesting_sentence: "I got blissful feelings as everything faded in and out of existence, later followed by electric and sexual sensations and some slight spasms." —D
helpful_sentence: "When it gets "really fucking loud" it makes a great meditation object." —Lars
who_should_read: Practitioners approaching the A&P via off-cushion noting and TMI-style concentration who want a brief firsthand account of the transition into the Dark Night.

log: Ed Ge's journey - a nonbeliever's exploration of meditation
author: Ed Ge
summary: A self-described nonbeliever and ex-Christian logs 18 straight days of breath-concentration practice, reaching access concentration and then experimenting with different pleasant objects (body warmth, shoulders, smile) to break into the first jhana, succeeding via cuddling-induced warmth and shoulder-focus sessions.
interesting_sentence: "...intense tingling "excitedness" was sweeping in waves over my upper body (neck, shoulders, upper arms), accompanied by emotions of... uh, jumping joy let's call it." —Ed Ge
helpful_sentence: "I think that the "pleasantness of concentration itself" can be used as an object for developing jhana - if you can tap into that relaxed well being satisfied focus feeling that comes when access concentration gets settled in." —Andrew K
who_should_read: Meditators working on jhana concentration who want a practical, day-by-day account of trial-and-error object selection for entering the first jhana.

author: Eddie Z
summary: Practices a fire-kasina variant using a VR headset (Oculus Go) showing a bright dot on a black field, logging vivid afterimage visions (recursive Buddha-within-Buddha imagery, shifting colors, triangles/pyramids), body sensations of tingling and chills, and cycles of drowsiness during sessions.
interesting_sentence: "This zooming out continued, and it was basically a Buddha within the eye of a Buddha over and over again." —Eddie Z
helpful_sentence: "Sleepiness usually happens with dissolution or the beginning of the 3rd jhana. For confirmation, I would expect a stable, detailed, possibly spinning dot before and fearful and/or wide elements after." —Jason Massie
who_should_read: Kasina practitioners curious about modern tech variants who want firsthand reports of the visionary and energetic phenomena a VR dot-kasina can produce.

author: Ed76
summary: Runs a structured 20-minute-concentration/40-minute-noting daily practice that triggers strong TRE-like shaking, then reports a solitary retreat with a night of panic-attack-level fear and disturbing visions followed by a clear, stable state described as Equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "before sleep had awful visions of being swalloewed by a sea monster." —Ed76
helpful_sentence: "It can be good to talk with friends or journal privately to expose and make peace with the stuff that scares or intimidates us... when we're fully conscious and accepting of our fears they are a lot less scary." —shargrol
who_should_read: Meditators experiencing kriya-like involuntary shaking during concentration/noting practice, especially on solitary retreat, will find this a grounded, blow-by-blow account.

author: Eelco ten Have
summary: Chronicles daily 20-40 minute noting and walking-meditation sessions squeezed around a large family and work, culminating in a 15-day Dhammacari vipassana retreat where the author retrospectively recognizes having passed through A&P and misery/dark-night phases.
interesting_sentence: "Trancendental meditation for a few months until my girfriend back then had a psychotic episode and died on new-years even when she stood before a moving train." —Eelco ten Have
helpful_sentence: "If it's too much of a struggle for the body, it's okay to change. It's better than practicing aversion anyway." —Julie V
who_should_read: Busy parents wondering whether periodic noting practice and occasional intensive retreats, without monastic renunciation, can still progress toward stream entry.

log: Ella's Concentration Practice Log: On The ADHD-to-Jhana-Yogi Pipeline.
author: Ella Mousekowitz
summary: An ADHD-diagnosed psychotherapist introduces her first-ever five-day solo concentration retreat under Tina Rasmussen (a Pa-Auk lineage teacher), describing a prior energetic spiritual awakening and her preference for jhana-based concentration over dry vipassana.
interesting_sentence: "I am, through and through (at this stage, in this life, not forever please and thank you) a \"desire type,\" previously a downright desire-monster." —Ella Mousekowitz
helpful_sentence: "If you are compfortable with the breath, and you want to stick to that, then go for it, but if you find it difficult, you might find things that more easily and naturally engage your attention (visual, auditory, mantra, kasina, other body sensations) easier." —Kaloyan Stefanov
who_should_read: An ADHD-identified meditator drawn to concentration/jhana practice over dry insight, preparing for a first retreat.

author: E Red
summary: A self-taught, book-heavy practitioner with suspected bipolar/ADHD tendencies documents cycling through insight stages roughly every two weeks, using TMI and fetter-model self-assessment to place himself around 2nd-3rd path, punctuated by a terrifying reality-shifting discontinuity and later stable access up to the 6th jhana.
interesting_sentence: "okay. Something the fuck just happenend. A bit wordless and fucking terrifying and I barely know what it was." —E Red
helpful_sentence: "I recognise my habits as being heavily cognitive focussed. I'm trying to focus on body/emotional systems because I feel like my cognitive is miles ahead and the other's need to catch up." —E Red
who_should_read: Eclectic self-directed practitioners blending psychology, chakra frameworks, and insight-stage self-diagnosis, especially those managing bipolar or ADHD tendencies.

author: Emil Jensen
summary: An exuberant, months-long log of intensive twice-to-thrice-daily noting and kasina practice (often 2-7 hours/day) packed with detailed nimitta, vibration, and jhana phenomenology, framed by a serious post-retreat depression and repeated, self-aware speculation about whether he has reached stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "I mean, whew! Jumping off a tall building was really tempting." —Emil Jensen
helpful_sentence: "Learning to balance effort and relaxation is what provides the mental sanity and healing that we ultimately want out of practice." —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: Intensive noting/kasina practitioners wrestling with post-retreat depression and stream-entry-seeking who want an unfiltered, humor-laced blow-by-blow account.

author: Sigma Tropic
summary: Details an energy-cultivation practice combining Zhan Zhuang standing meditation, strict celibacy, prayer and jhana work, including an unusually granular personal taxonomy of the eight jhanas and cessation phenomenology, before the log turns contentious after the author's public Arhat claim costs him a retreat-teaching invitation and he proposes an all-male 'fraternity.'
interesting_sentence: "Henrik was unaware that I had made a claim to Arhatship online in this forum and on my site." —Sigma Tropic
helpful_sentence: "Cessation is unlike any other experience in life. It is very much like suddenly dying and instantly coming back to life." —Sigma Tropic
who_should_read: Practitioners wanting granular jhana-by-jhana and cessation phenomenology, and anyone curious about the social fallout of publicly claiming Arhatship on a practice forum.

author: Eric O
summary: Eric O restarts his log after describing recurring nighttime A&P-like 'energy clap' experiences during breath concentration, then details a morning sit tracking fine skin-level vibrations while navigating motivation lapses amid selling his house and expecting his first child.
interesting_sentence: "I'd have this experience that I can only describe like dropping a rock into a pool of energy." —Eric O
helpful_sentence: "I want to say, yes, just sit at the same time every single day for one month, early morning pre-dawn, if possible." —katy steger
who_should_read: Practitioners who've had solitary nocturnal A&P-like energy experiences but haven't crossed stream entry, looking for a grounded account of consistent daily practice amid major life changes.

author: Eric Abrahamsen
summary: Eric Abrahamsen chronicles progress from early jhana and posture struggles (a chronic hip/SI-joint issue worked through with taiji) into vipassana insights on no-self during walking meditation, mindfulness-based therapy for a drinking problem, and a cathartic guided MDMA session that released childhood emotional blockages.
interesting_sentence: "What I saw was very surprising: it felt like my mind was a fish tank, and inside was this enormous black eel, far too large for the tank, that was continually twisting and writhing and twining around itself." —Eric Abrahamsen
helpful_sentence: "Ideally you do some shamatha to calm and strengthen the mind, then vipassana to analyze what remains that is unsatisfactory" —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners integrating chronic physical pain, therapy, and psychedelic-assisted healing with jhana/vipassana practice will find a relatable, richly narrated account.

author: Eric G
summary: Already past stream entry and second path from an earlier KFD journal, Eric G logs years of granular jhana and cessation phenomenology—including rare 'slow cessations,' Pure Land jhanas at the third eye, and a long dark-night-tinged mega-cycle—while probing 3rd/4th path territory, drawing a direct reply from Daniel Ingram on Nirodha.
interesting_sentence: "It is a relatively sudden and total power outage, like the cord has been pulled on the computer of your mind and the thing just shuts down totally, and I mean totally." —Daniel M Ingram
helpful_sentence: "There are lots of unknown events or experiences, experiences where things blink or fade out, and the list of things they can be is moderately long, but the most common ones happen during the A&P, during Dissolution, and during the Formless Realms" —Daniel M Ingram
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners already past stream entry who want granular phenomenological descriptions of jhana, Pure Land jhanas, and varying qualities of cessation across later paths.

author: Eric M W
summary: Eric M W recounts crossing A&P via a vivid Buddha dream, cycling through a prolonged Dark Night and pre-path plateau while raising three kids (including an autistic son) and working full time, capped by a bizarre alien-vibration A&P/fear-nana episode during recovery from a vasectomy.
interesting_sentence: "They were little humanoids with big heads and glowing red eyes and they were going to put me in their ship and take me far away and I would never see my family again." —Eric M W
helpful_sentence: "Follow your own path not a map. Knowing the map doesn't change what happens in your experiences it just helps put things into perspective." —Dream Walker
who_should_read: Householder meditators juggling young kids, a demanding job, and family health crises who want a candid, humor-laced account of cycling between A&P and Dark Night.

author: Eric M W
summary: A father of four juggling minimal cushion time describes crossing A&P via a vivid dream years earlier, getting stuck oscillating between Dark Night misery and brief Equanimity, while managing chronic anxiety, illness, and family financial strain.
interesting_sentence: "I'd cut my left hand off just to A&P again and get back on the ride." —Eric M W
helpful_sentence: "Dark night is not fun...it doesn't seem like your getting anywhere...but you are." —Dream Walker
who_should_read: Householders juggling family, illness, and financial stress who want an honest account of cycling between A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity with very little formal sit time.

author: Eric O
summary: Documents a Goenka 10-day retreat where the author left early after intense noting produced rapid cycling between the vipassana jhanas and apparent stream-entry fruition, then spends months afterward second-guessing the claim while working through throat tension and fear back home.
interesting_sentence: "Next thing I know, every sensation in my body is lit up and the best way I can describe it is an Eric sensation tornado." —Eric O
helpful_sentence: "Equanimity is built by letting all those conditioned and habitual scenarios arise and pass, arise and pass, without going for a full swim in them, in both daily mindfulness and in sitting practice." —katy steger
who_should_read: Vipassana practitioners planning a Goenka 10-day retreat who want a candid, blow-by-blow account of cycling through A&P, fear, and equanimity plus honest post-retreat doubt about a stream-entry claim.

author: Erik - Aellen
summary: A beginner in Argentina, using The Mind Illuminated after his Goenka retreat was cancelled by the pandemic, builds up breath concentration through TMI's stages, encountering a scary 'void' between breaths, anger at sound distractions, and a resurfacing childhood memory of his mother's aggression.
interesting_sentence: "suddenly everything became clear, this was a mild trauma coming to surface because of my practice." —Erik - Aellen
helpful_sentence: "Always feel satisfied with any stretch of time where the meditation object remains in the field of conscious awareness ." —Erik - Aellen
who_should_read: New meditators working through The Mind Illuminated's early stages who want a candid account of building concentration and having old emotional material surface.

author: Ernest Michael Olmos
summary: A two-year practitioner sitting daily in full lotus recounts an SE-defining cessation, a period of psychic-power (siddhi) experiences, ongoing kundalini sensations and neck stiffness, and later an inconclusive 'blip' with bliss waves and clarity that he ultimately decides was not a second path.
interesting_sentence: "I had a really weird experience where I saw suffering in a very pleasant moment that resulted in strong compassion." —Ernest Michael Olmos
helpful_sentence: "I have found that relative things like what you do, think, eat, etc, helpful to improve concentration and stillness but detrimental to insight if attached to them (and it is very difficult to not to be attached to them)." —Ernest Michael Olmos
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry practitioners cycling through the nanas who want to compare notes on cessation phenomenology and ambiguous 'path blips.'

author: Bernoulli
summary: A grad student recommitting to daily practice after years of inconsistent Zen sitting begins ramping up TMI-style breath concentration from 10 to 21+ minutes, noting early tingling and pressure sensations he interprets as releases.
interesting_sentence: "Several times, including very recently, I've had, let's say \"induced\" experiences, that have left me absolutely convinced that overcoming the self is the only way to make progress here." —Bernoulli
helpful_sentence: "Just in case you're not aware of it, some good TMI tips/discussion is available here..." —Tashi Tharpa
who_should_read: New meditators using The Mind Illuminated who want a realistic view of ramping up sit duration and working through early concentration stages.

author: Bruno Loff
summary: Reports using aerobic exercise held right at an "effort-barrier" threshold and a self-kindness self-talk technique to relieve tension, alongside a recurring ajna-point eye-flutter phenomenon linked to cessation moments and a post-flutter shift in visual hue, plus early flotation-tank experiments.
interesting_sentence: "Once Clayton was visiting me in Amsterdam and when I asked him to go through the jhanas, he had the same eye-fluttering/trembling phenomena while experiencing (what he said was) 8th jhana, or around that territory." —Bruno Loff
helpful_sentence: "There's a fair bit in the hypnosis literature on eyelid flutter, as you may know already. A sign of mild trance state." —This Good Self
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about energetic/somatic markers (ajna flutter, chakra sensations) tied to cessation events, and those seeking ways to use exercise and self-compassion to reduce meditative tension.

log: Experiences with being a dark night yogi, ADD, Open Heart system
author: anj
summary: anj recounts years of dark-night cycling compounded by untreated ADHD, OCD, and anxiety, then describes attending a nonresidential Open Heart retreat covering rushen, partner-based tantric metta, dzogchen dance, and shamatha under teacher Kim.
interesting_sentence: "Kim on traditional shamatha: -- a fucking pain the ass." —anj
helpful_sentence: "We practised a technique where one tries to feel the breath in a fist-sized area between the belly and the back (a location in the body that does not get karmically stained, or something like that)." —anj
who_should_read: Meditators with ADHD/OCD navigating prolonged dark-night cycling who want a firsthand account of Kim Katami's Open Heart tantra/dzogchen retreat.

author: Bagpuss The Gnome
summary: Records a shift from Goenka-style anapanasati and body-scanning into Shinzen Young's Five Ways noting practice, testing labels like "sight/sound/touch" while dog-walking and "expansion/contraction" on the cushion, and comparing notes with another practitioner on noting's clunkiness during the dark night.
interesting_sentence: "Kept feeling like it would \"burst\" but it never did." —Bagpuss The Gnome
helpful_sentence: "One thing that became apparent while trying to note while dark nighting was that the actual note was occurring after the actual phenomenon was done and gone in a way that really made the clunkiness stand out." —Eric B
who_should_read: Noting practitioners moving from breath/body-scan techniques who want firsthand comparison notes on integrating Shinzen Young's labeling method into daily life and jhana practice.

author: supermonkey :)
summary: supermonkey charts an extended cycling practice combining Loch Kelly's Mahamudra-based glimpse exercises with five-elements pristine-awareness work and a fetter-model framework, moving through repeated dark-night/equanimity cycles, glimpses of no-self/no-time/no-space and headlessness, alongside heavy IFS-style processing of shame and anger.
interesting_sentence: "Dakinis came to help me! For the first time ever I heard an internal voice saying that I'm doing well and told me to keep going!" —supermonkey :)
helpful_sentence: "it's always good to consider whether something is in the domain of insight, concentration, or morality when thinking about practices." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners deep in post-path cycling who want to see Mahamudra/dzogchen glimpse practice combined with fetter-model mapping and psychological shadow work.

author: Extinguish
summary: Extinguish tracks efforts to cultivate blissful jhana-like states in everyday settings (couch breaks, desk, walking) alongside a Tibetan Buddhist death-and-impermanence practice, while also noting bouts of self-medicating with caffeine, sweets, and OTC cough medicine.
interesting_sentence: "For some reason the OTC reccomended dose of 2 pills of cough medicine (containing 325 mg of acetominophen, 10 mg dextromethorphan, and 5 mg phenylephedrine each) gives me a type of energy that also slightly dissociates me and makes music more meaningful." —Extinguish
helpful_sentence: "Sounds like the piti-sukha jhana factors. For me, opening up to the bliss also opened up to some pain (headaches) after a while, but my conditioning wasn't great." —George S
who_should_read: Meditators interested in cultivating jhanic bliss informally throughout the workday rather than only on the cushion.

author: es pro
summary: After over-meditating and experiencing a crown-chakra kundalini opening in April, es pro describes months of severe dark-night-style ego dissolution—fear, dissociation, apathy, and suicidal ideation—while forum members including Tim Farrington and Olivier S offer extensive reassurance that this is classic (if unusually extreme) A&P-to-dark-night territory.
interesting_sentence: "I had such God delusions- I felt omniscient, felt like I was floating above the universe, above Buddha and Jesus." —es pro
helpful_sentence: "You will find much helpful advice here and in Daniel's book, but IMO it boils down to, keep a practice routine no matter what, investigate diligently, talk through problems with the community, perhaps practice some austerities to aid you, and have confidence you will get through and come out better for it." —Milo
who_should_read: Meditators experiencing a frightening dark-night crisis after intense energetic/kundalini openings who want reassurance that severe dissociation and despair can be classic (if extreme) territory.

log: Febrile Illness totally wipes me out
author: Daniel M Ingram
summary: Daniel Ingram recounts a severe flu that shut down his baseline meditative and jhanic abilities and landed him in the ER on IV fluids, noting that his lowest respiratory rate and dropping O2 saturation seemed to correlate with brief access to something like weak 3rd jhana, which sparks a long forum debate about attainment, suffering, and holotropic breathing.
interesting_sentence: "so one more question for the scientific journal: is hypoxia and/or hypercarbia part of the jhanic buzz?" —Daniel M Ingram
helpful_sentence: "the improved relationship between the body and the mind (the control-surrender thing) is a big plus." —Ernest Michael Olmos
who_should_read: Advanced meditators curious how illness and physical crisis interact with established practice, and how baseline jhanic/insight abilities can be temporarily eclipsed by fever.

log: Fire Kasina Retreat October 2025 - Ryan Kay
author: Ryan Kay
summary: Ryan Kay details a 16-day, roughly 8.5-hour-a-day fire kasina retreat in Orlando where visual entities, jhanic absorption, and a day-15 'zoom in/zoom out' cessation-like event led to a profound and lasting sense of the impersonal, ownerless nature of phenomena.
interesting_sentence: "I could see my entire visual field full of portions of humanoid masks... and it was staring right at me from what felt like two feet away." —Ryan Kay
helpful_sentence: "For those curious, I began to use the notes \"look at it do that\" instead of labelling the specific sensations, which worked well in this instance." —Ryan Kay
who_should_read: Yogis considering an intensive fire kasina retreat who want a candid, detailed account of visual phenomena, entities, and a possible cessation/stream-entry event.

author: Mark Peacock
summary: Mark Peacock, a 70-year-old practitioner recovering from a cancer scare and open-heart surgery, blends fire kasina candle-gazing with Rob Burbea's Seeing That Frees emptiness practices, reporting jhanic nimitta and dream-color phenomena while investigating core fears around death and physical attack.
interesting_sentence: "I was well into that state of romantic love with the FK-induced mind yesterday morning." —Mark Peacock
helpful_sentence: "I've been trying to see, feel and maybe relax the feelings and beliefs... going into the feelings while keeping awareness 'of the whole room'. Maybe this conteracts taking a dissociated stance." —Mark Peacock
who_should_read: Older or health-challenged practitioners interested in combining fire kasina jhana cultivation with Rob Burbea's emptiness-focused Seeing That Frees practice.

author: Bob No No
summary: First log entry describing a 2-hour morning body-awareness sit plus 49 minutes of samatha, 15 minutes of nature-of-mind meditation, and 25 minutes of loving-kindness practice, alongside the realization—informed by a past DMT experience—that Nirvana denotes cessation rather than bliss, and recognition of a habitual pattern of intellectualizing bodily sensations instead of directly feeling them.
interesting_sentence: "The last one was the fact that Nirvana points to a state of cessation, not some blissful state(an axiom inhereted from Christianity.)" —Bob No No
helpful_sentence: "I think of it as a defense mechanism to keep me away from real transcendence." —Bob No No
who_should_read: New meditators curious about combining body-based samatha and metta practice with reflections on how a past psychedelic experience can illuminate a cessation-based view of Nirvana.

author: mikko
summary: Documents a shift from samatha jhana into vipassana via anapanasati, describing rapid vibrations and piti-like flickering near what may be the A&P threshold, alongside fear of the dark night and debate over Ingram's versus Shinzen Young's maps.
interesting_sentence: "Upon entry I felt a huge number of tiny vibrations simultaneously on my upper lip and with remarkable clarity." —mikko
helpful_sentence: "the ramp up in your ability to notice fast tiny sensations and the almost automatic pull into that stage is a common indicator methinks." —tom moylan
who_should_read: Concentration-focused yogis moving into vipassana who want to understand the fear and doubt that can surface near the A&P threshold.

author: Travis Gene McKinstry
summary: A months-long noting-practice log culminating in a 10-day Goenka retreat, tracking progress from Mind-and-Body through a clear A&P, repeated Dark Night/Equanimity cycling, and a reported stream-entry fruition marked by uncontrollable laughter.
interesting_sentence: "I couldn't stop smiling/giggling/laughing. That night I had difficulty sleeping because I couldn't stop laughing/giggling." —Travis Gene McKinstry
helpful_sentence: "It is a specific neutrality towards any given phenomenon which allows it to be, to arise and pass of its own accord, without giving yourself free license to express it and without trying to get rid of it." —Nikolai
who_should_read: Practitioners preparing for a Goenka retreat who want a detailed, day-by-day account of cycling through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity toward a first-path claim.

author: Steve Fitz Williams
summary: A newcomer's first log describing chronic irritability and a lifelong sexual/relational fantasy pattern he calls 'the Princess Complex' intensifying after divorce, alongside Reichian breathwork and 1.5 hours of daily breath-concentration practice.
interesting_sentence: "I call it 'The Princess Complex'. You know, that story we build around our sexual drives that all i'm missing in life is my perfect princess and that when i find her, everything will slot into place." —Steve Fitz Williams
helpful_sentence: "If you compare developing these practices daily (including your daily life - which is the best practice IMHO) to letting the mind wander over negative habitual thinking you can see one mode prevents you from hatred, cruelty, envy, and neediness." —Richard Zen
who_should_read: Meditators working through post-divorce grief and long-standing relational patterns who want to see somatic breathwork combined with newly serious noting practice.

log: Five Ways to Know Mikhail Zybin
author: Mikhail Zybin
summary: Daily journal entries by a beginner working through Shinzen Young's "Five Ways to Know Yourself" categories (See/Hear/Feel In, Out, Rest), tracking recurring mind-wandering, dullness, and distraction during 30-minute morning sits.
interesting_sentence: "Usually the mind is agitated rather than sleepy." —Mikhail Zybin
helpful_sentence: "Probably in the last couple of months I did too much dry vipassana, ignoring the concentration part, so my attention is used to be constantly running." —Mikhail Zybin
who_should_read: Beginner meditators curious about Shinzen Young's Basic Mindfulness categories and an honest day-to-day account of noting-practice struggles.

author: Flo
summary: Tracks progress from Jhana practice and a chair-spinning A&P event through persistent anatta perception, fear of mirrors, effortless EQ-like noting, a second nighttime A&P "zapping" event, and finally a stream-entry-like breakthrough with a teacher involving a cessation event.
interesting_sentence: "The spinning stopped, and it felt like I sunk deeply into the depth of my skull and at that point I realized that I was perfectly fine with the thought of dying - which I feared quite a lot before." —Flo
helpful_sentence: "When the mind is just somewhat clear and calm it is impossible to note everything verbally, even if you do the shooting aliens-style of Daniel Ingram (di-di-di-di-di)... At that point – and that would typically be at the Equanimity nana – noting can actually be a hindrance to seeing things clearly." —Niels Lyngsø
who_should_read: Practitioners nearing stream entry who want a candid account of moving from Jhana-based A&P events through effortless Equanimity noting into a stream-entry breakthrough, plus detailed community advice on noting technique.

author: Shi :)
summary: A brief note describing how intensive noting practice left the author's memory "slippery" and prone to forgetting, compounded by internet use and sugar-driven mood swings, prompting a shift toward body-based practice.
interesting_sentence: "I forget that I forget." —Shi :)
helpful_sentence: "I stopped noting and acting to move towards body work and less addictive behavior (mostly internet), body is needs energy and sugar swings are an issue too -- it's like a lock (esp. cravings and mood swings)." —Shi :)
who_should_read: Meditators experiencing memory or dissociative side-effects from intensive noting who want a peer account of easing off into body-based practice.

author: kettu
summary: After an 11-year hiatus from daily sitting, kettu resumes brief formal practice centered on family relationships and metta, using the log mainly to process disillusionment with past spiritual teachers and communities before stepping away again.
interesting_sentence: "Fear of being seen, and fear of being seen as an asshole." —kettu
helpful_sentence: "In my humble opinion it's best to have practice and life melted together." —kettu
who_should_read: Long-term practitioners processing disillusionment with past spiritual communities or teachers who want a reflective, poetic account of restarting practice around family relationships.

author: fschuhi
summary: A highly technical experiment tracking a persistent facial-muscle tic during breath meditation, tracing it to habitual "perspective-making" and resolving it through multi-focal covert-attention practice that also produces fast, stable Jhana access.
interesting_sentence: "The muscle is obviously a very sensitive device to indicate the presence of perspective-making." —fschuhi
helpful_sentence: "Multi-focal awareness works as an antidote to uni-focal perspective-making, that's a tool I regularly work with." —fschuhi
who_should_read: Advanced concentration practitioners interested in subtle body-based attention work and using physical micro-tics as biofeedback for perspective-making.

log: Freestyle Noting Aloud - log + a few Practice Videos
author: Papa Che Dusko
summary: A months-long daily 45-minute freestyle 'noting aloud' practice log full of visual absorption phenomena (murky vanishing centers, purple/violet haze, eyesight 'blips'), recurring body pain and kundalini-like vibrations, and cycling through Dark Night and Equanimity-like states while juggling childcare and family life, documented with numerous linked practice videos.
interesting_sentence: "Im experiencing right now PTSD style flash-backs. Some violent stuff from decades ago." —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "Yes, friend, you're allowed to have fun in jhana and it doesn't mean you're slacking." —Steph S
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about freestyle noting-aloud technique and detailed visual/somatic phenomenology across the insight stages will find a rich, video-documented account here.

log: Freestyle Practice: Exploring Without A Map
author: Tommy M
summary: Tommy M abandons Buddhist and Actual Freedom terminology in favor of plain phenomenological description, logging anapanasati-based jhana and PCE experiments (including candle kasina and an Enochian magick detour later disavowed) across a long thread that also covers a painful long-term relationship separation and call-centre workplace stress.
interesting_sentence: "I'm assuming I hit 8th jhana after this but I have literally no idea how to describe it, there was something there but absolutely nothing I could recognize clearly enough." —Tommy M
helpful_sentence: "would you go to a hardware store and buy a hammer to use appropriately, or would you go to an arts and crafts store, browse around for an object that you think might be functional, then proceed to test out whether or not a clay pot or a candlestick holder can also get the job done?" —Trent
who_should_read: Practitioners frustrated with rigid map terminology who want to see phenomenology-first descriptions of jhana, PCE, and Actual Freedom-adjacent practice blended freely.

author: George GDM
summary: A brand-new practice log opener in which the author commits to twice-daily 45-minute non-striving sits, inspired by another poster's advice that meditation plus therapy helps one hold distressing thoughts with more space.
interesting_sentence: "I am tired of feeling estranged from the ultimate reality of what I am and what I'm immersed in." —George GDM
helpful_sentence: "please stick to it with consistency . It has resolved these issues for me, and I suspect it will for you, too, George." —Chris M
who_should_read: Beginners setting up their first consistent sitting practice who want a low-pressure, non-striving approach and encouragement will find this a relatable starting point.

author: Gabe
summary: A college student's noting practice log tracking sits ranging from basic breath/body noting through explorations of sloth, doubt, and a possible touch of Equanimity, including teacher-guided investigation of the sleep-wake boundary and a vivid lucid dream meditation session.
interesting_sentence: "I said something like, well, in the end, you lose the duality of observer and observed, subject and object, and everything is aware of itself as itself." —Gabe
helpful_sentence: "If I think and wonder if Im in EQ Nana or not, I most certainly am not When in EQ Nana there is no doubt about it." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Noting practitioners working through doubt, dullness, and early Dark Night territory under a personal teacher's guidance will find relatable, detailed sit-by-sit notes here.

author: Geoff W
summary: A seven-year TMI (Mind Illuminated)-based practice log tightly interwoven with weekly therapy, tracking years of Stage 3-4 dullness/distraction battles, a week-long silent U Tejaniya-style retreat, a mushroom-assisted breakthrough on grief, and an eventual shift toward metta, self-inquiry, and shikantaza/dzogchen.
interesting_sentence: "It was almost like I had a week long cessation and woke up back in my normal life." —Geoff W
helpful_sentence: "if i can give any general advice it would be: 1) take the long view and 2) enjoy the trip 3) KISS - keep it simple stupid" —tom moylan
who_should_read: TMI practitioners pairing meditation with psychotherapy who want a multi-year, warts-and-all account of dark-night-style regression, doubt, and retreat practice.

author: Gerald
summary: Gerald recounts a multi-year noting-practice arc from psychedelic-fueled beginnings through repeated cessations experienced on retreat, a psychotic-like spiritual breakdown involving numerology and 'alternate dimensions,' and an eventual return to noting combined with a self-devised practice of concentrating via 'worship of the present moment.'
interesting_sentence: "It is very strange to live in a state where you believe everything the mind tells you." —Gerald
helpful_sentence: "I would highly discourage anyone reading this from following that path; keep free from intoxicants." —Gerald
who_should_read: Practitioners curious how psychedelic experimentation, retreat noting practice, and a psychosis-like breakdown can intertwine along the path model.

author: Five Ghost Fist
summary: Five Ghost Fist logs a magick-infused practice combining chanting rituals (LBRP, Fudo mantra, middle pillar) with mantra meditation and occasional noting, reporting growing concentration, intense bliss states from mantra work, and a session marked by extraordinary depression.
interesting_sentence: "and I had a very, very intense bliss state. I'm quite excited about this." —Five Ghost Fist
helpful_sentence: "I'm finding this technique super effective at getting a good body high..feeling the energy." —Five Ghost Fist
who_should_read: Occultists interested in blending Western ceremonial magick (LBRP, mantra work) with Buddhist noting practice.

author: Gijs V
summary: Gijs opens his practice log describing a lockdown-era routine of about one hour of daily light-kasina concentration practice while lying on the floor, with plans to add metta and insight work toward attaining first path.
interesting_sentence: "Right now corona lockdown allows me to practice about 1 hour a day mostly doing light-on-the-ceiling-kasina, while laying on the floor." —Gijs V
helpful_sentence: "My goal is twofold, id like to to some concentration practices and metta practices that i believe would be quite good for me in daily life." —Gijs V
who_should_read: Beginners curious about starting a concentration-focused kasina practice during lockdown.

author: Niklas -
summary: Niklas restarts formal sitting after decades of intermittent zen practice, works through confusion between access concentration and a deeper breath-driven still state, adopts noting under shargrol's guidance, experiments with metta reaching quick access-concentration-like states, and revisits a prior 'no-self' peak experience while wrestling with striving, dullness fears, and chronic back pain.
interesting_sentence: "Basically I felt I was in the world but it was making itself anew moment by moment and it just felt extremely \"alive\"." —Niklas -
helpful_sentence: "The benefit of noting practice is that every experience becomes FUEL for practice, no matter what shows up." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners restarting practice after years away who want a real-time example of moving from access-concentration confusion into noting practice with mentor feedback.

log: Good Vibrations - Bagpuss' Jhana Practice Log
author: Bagpuss The Gnome
summary: Bagpuss The Gnome logs a jhana-centered Anapanasati practice, cycling rapidly between 1st-4th jhana alongside A&P/DN/EQ nanas, describing a persistent post-retreat 'delicious' pleasure sensation rooted at the base of the spine and a near-miss cessation experience with vibrations reaching an estimated 40-50 times per second.
interesting_sentence: "It's like lying on the bottom of a very deep, still lake." —Bagpuss The Gnome
helpful_sentence: "I transition to 2nd as I hit the A&P, 3rd in the DN, 4th in EQ." —Bagpuss The Gnome
who_should_read: Jhana practitioners wanting a granular look at how nana territory (A&P/DN/EQ) maps onto jhanic depth and the sensory particulars of near-cessation vibrations.

author: Gregor
summary: Gregor recounts intense Kundalini energy surges, a Kriya-based trauma release using the Bioemotive Framework, and repeated cycling between Dark Night and Equanimity while questioning whether he has truly reached technical 4th path as his teacher claimed.
interesting_sentence: "I could clearly see the perceptual layer of self that my mind was fabricating." —Gregor
helpful_sentence: "the real key to the dark night is surrender of control--- to God, in the theistic traditions, to the deep wisdom of your deep body and mind, in more DhO-like terms." —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: Practitioners dealing with destabilizing Kundalini energy and post-shift doubt who want an unfiltered, community-supported account.

author: Fernando
summary: Fernando restarts breath-focused meditation after years away and describes cool energetic sensations and a psychedelic-like afterglow, prompting a forum reply suggesting hyperventilation as a mundane explanation.
interesting_sentence: "I use the word \"afterglow\" because it was similar to the afterglow you feel after a psychedelic trip." —Fernando
helpful_sentence: "Do you recall how it feels to hyperventilate? Seriously. That's a possibility when you're deeply focused on your breath." —Chris M
who_should_read: Beginners curious how ordinary breath-focused sits can produce energy sensations that have simple physiological explanations.

author: heath
summary: Heath moves his practice log online and, amid a stretch of 12-15 hour workdays, notes an imagined 'Daniel-voice' arising and passing like any other mind-stuff before tentatively identifying himself as entering Dark Night or re-observation territory.
interesting_sentence: "This is a point where I think some knowledge of maps excels -- the ability to 'plan' your spiritual path, like Daniel has talked about." —heath
helpful_sentence: "I decided earlier this afternoon that I'm just going to do what I think of as Zen / 'letting things be as they are' until I'm out of this territory so that I don't have to worry about how it's affecting my job/life." —heath
who_should_read: Noting practitioners wanting a candid account of entering dark-night territory while juggling a demanding work schedule.

author: Hector L
summary: Hector experiments with loving-kindness, noting, and jhana-like practices inside dreams and hypnagogic states, cataloguing distinct kinds of dream imagery and trading a philosophical exchange with Tim Farrington reframing anatta as 'stepping out of the flow of elegance'.
interesting_sentence: "by experiment I said \"butterfly\" and an electric blue butterfly appeared and quickly dissolved into a bird with outspread wings." —Hector L
helpful_sentence: "I think the flow detector is always getting more sophisticated, recalibrating as its discovers and tunes to new nuances and subtleties, wavelengths and vibes." —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: Dream-yoga and lucid-dreaming practitioners interested in applying classic insight techniques to hypnagogic and dream states.

author: Helena
summary: Helena documents five years of practice since a permanent stream-entry style shift via the Two-Part Formula, describing clarity expanding from brief glimpses to a constant, body-wide (and beyond) awareness, plus a vivid Guru Yoga visualization of flowers and healing bumblebees with Avalokiteshvara and White Tara during a retreat.
interesting_sentence: "Small delicate fragrant flowers grew out from my body and flowers burst out in the air." —Helena
helpful_sentence: "The answer to the discontentment does not lie in alcohol, partying, travelling or excessive work. It lies in facing your own self, to question your own beliefs, and daring to look in to the darkest and deepest parts of your mind." —Helena
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about Two-Part-Formula-style stream-entry claims paired with vivid Vajrayana Guru Yoga visualization work.

author: Christopher
summary: Christopher, after intense psychedelic-like visions on his first Goenka retreat and childhood-trauma-linked fear and sadness surfacing during concentration practice, begins Mahasi-style noting alongside a metta practice centered on chest warmth under a remote teacher's guidance to build equanimity while working through his fear of losing control.
interesting_sentence: "My biggest fear is that of losing control (fear of death ultimately), and in the context of meditation, getting stuck in an awful dark night or having depersonalized/dissociative experiences." —Christopher
helpful_sentence: "I do a little of it before every session as I find it tunes motivation, attitude, and effort to the right levels better than anything else, better than even TMI's four-step progression, though YMMV on that." —Ryan
who_should_read: Meditators with strong concentration but weaker insight and ethics who fear losing control in dark-night-type territory.

author: Dark Night Yogi
summary: Dark Night Yogi checks back in after nearly two years without meditating to ask whether his earlier self-diagnosed 2nd-to-3rd-path attainment is genuine, since he notices little lasting effect in daily life, prompting Daniel Ingram to spell out his personal criteria for third path.
interesting_sentence: "Following the maps, it seemed like I attained 3rd path. Unbelievable, or not. Sure or not? Really don't know for sure, but at least 2nd path." —Dark Night Yogi
helpful_sentence: "Walks around with an direct, realtime appreciation of the the spaciousness of things, of the centerlessness of things, of the agencylessness of things that is incomplete but still relatively obvious in realtime." —Daniel M Ingram
who_should_read: Practitioners who self-diagnosed a high path attainment years ago and are now questioning it after a long break from sitting.

author: sawfoot _
summary: Sawfoot keeps a candid, digressive practice log moving between anapanasati breath counting, out-loud noting, and dzogchen 'just sitting' instructions while working through dark night fear, equanimity peeks, and deep skepticism toward pragmatic dharma maps and the goal of stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "But seems pretty fucking weird being an energy field." —sawfoot _
helpful_sentence: "I just show up for the practice and do it gently, no conceit, no expectation but to train on attention and the probably of dealing with torpor if I'm distracted or have just eaten." —katy steger
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to dzogchen/vajrayana philosophy who want a candid, self-skeptical dark-night log wrestling with doubt about the pragmatic dharma map.
log: How Sawfoot Learned to Stop Worrying and Love Enlightenment

author: John M
summary: John M chronicles roughly five months of intensive daily sitting (2-3 hours) through post-stream-entry fruitions, distinct vipassana jhanas, a fast pass through dissolution and re-observation, and a probable second path shift into panoramic, effortless equanimity, alongside a growing disinterest in practice.
interesting_sentence: "the feeling is like some grim combination of teenage heart-ache, being fired, losing a pet, and hard liquor hangover." —John M
helpful_sentence: "Second path can go pretty quick to EQ so make some notes as things change." —Dream Walker
who_should_read: Serious noting practitioners tracking the nana progression from stream entry through jhana development toward second path will find useful markers here.
log: I sit right down, but I get up again, or: A fool persists

author: Ian
summary: Ian logs brief walking and sitting sessions focused on ramping up mahasi-style noting speed and noticing how mental imagery supplements raw bodily sensations, with one session where bodily solidity briefly dissolved into pure sensation.
interesting_sentence: "I focus on the fact that bodily sensations are simply sensations, and for a few seconds it feels as if my body is not solid but just an illusion." —Ian
helpful_sentence: "I've noticed over the past few weeks that many sensations that I experience such as the feeling of my feet on the ground or the feeling of the breath in my stomach area are 'supplemented' with mental images of what is happening." —Ian
who_should_read: Beginners working on speeding up mahasi-style noting and separating raw sensation from mental imagery.

author: Inky
summary: Inky, a long-time lurker with suspected ADHD and work-related anxiety, alternates shamatha (breath and brow-point focus) with Goenka-style body-scanning vipassana and later verbal noting over several months, repeatedly losing and regaining a fragile daily practice while never confirming access concentration or clear vibratory sensations.
interesting_sentence: "You can be paranoid and still be spied upon." —Inky
helpful_sentence: "For shamata with the breath, a trick that I have found useful is to consider the lack of sensation in a portion of the breath cycle to be a type of sensation." —Martin
who_should_read: Practitioners with attention or anxiety difficulties who want an honest account of an inconsistent, stop-start practice rather than a smooth progress narrative.

author: Brian K
summary: Brian K, after an apparent A&P experience, does intensive noting-based vipassana (up to four hours a day, sitting and walking), cycles through dark night symptoms including fear of no-self, briefly reports a calm equanimity-like period and a non-self insight, then scales back to shamatha and actualism-flavored daily-life practice ahead of starting university.
interesting_sentence: "Last night as i was dozing off and today for a short while in meditation i felt the fear of no self." —Brian K
helpful_sentence: "The stability of concentration is absolutely necessary for clear seeing when disturbing material comes up during insight." —fivebells
who_should_read: Intensive noting practitioners cycling through dark night who want a peer's account of doubting whether to prioritize concentration or insight.

author: Joshua
summary: Joshua recounts a multi-year practice arc from psychedelic-triggered A&P experiences and bodily convulsions in 2011-2012, through a claimed stream entry attained at the eighth jhana via jhana-powered work through the dukkha nanas, to newly discovering "pure land jhanas" beyond the eighth marked by titanic bliss visions and a felt communion with other attainers.
interesting_sentence: "I recall at least 10 times where my body started getting seizures and thunderbolt like shocks, and I crumpled into a chair with convulsing body, watching the clouds and sun move through my head." —Joshua
helpful_sentence: "I used my strongest asset, concentration, to blast through the Dukkha nanas using jhana." —Joshua
who_should_read: Readers curious about a jhana-heavy, psychedelic-flavored personal account of reaching stream entry and subsequent pure land jhana states.

author: J C
summary: A detailed 10-day Goenka-style noting retreat log describing rapid cycling through the Progress of Insight stages via 'review,' culminating in what the author suspects may be a second path attainment, plus a 'vipassana buzzsaw' technique for deconstructing self-related constructs at high A&P/Eq flicker speeds.
interesting_sentence: "I think of the flickering sensations at the A&P and Eq as a rapidly spinning buzzsaw with blades slicing at high frequencies." —J C
helpful_sentence: "By noting multiple categories and rapidly shifting back and forth between them I was able to smoothly increase the speed up through Eq without a lot of the usual trouble or negativity." —J C
who_should_read: Noting yogis who want a granular, blow-by-blow account of review cycling and a novel technique for deconstructing self-view at high insight speeds.
log: JC's retreat log, and questions about review cycling

author: JP
summary: A two-year practice log tracking the author's progression from ceiling-fan-kasina concentration and Mahamudra 'Witness' work through a probable stream-entry fruition and second-path equanimity, followed by a period of trauma destabilization addressed via EMDR, somatic experiencing therapy, and kundalini yoga.
interesting_sentence: "It really seems like it was a flash of 4th path, which kind of makes me wonder if I'm right about the evolutionary history and enlightenment is just about cleaning out some later additions and getting back to that original kind of self-simulation." —JP
helpful_sentence: "I can't really in good faith continue to recommend pursuing vipassanna-based emotional purifications the way I did in this thread last week." —JP
who_should_read: Yogis interested in combining kasina/jhana concentration work with insight practice, and anyone navigating meditation-linked trauma destabilization.

author: J W
summary: Preparing for a 10-day noting retreat, the author logs rapid self-directed cycling through vipassana jhanas 1-4 and their sub-stages, uses Wim Hof breathing as a concentration warm-up, and engages in extended forum dialogue on identification, seeking, and dependent origination.
interesting_sentence: "What is me, what is solid, is what i would describe as a little teeny tiny ball of stress." —J W
helpful_sentence: "The path to stream entry is about disidentifying with thoughts and emotions...The middle paths are about disidentifying with attention...The path to fourth is about seeing through solidity and the centerpoint." —John L
who_should_read: Experienced jhana practitioners preparing for a noting retreat who want detailed vipassana-jhana mapping alongside forum debate on self-inquiry, identification, and dependent origination.

author: J W
summary: Alternates soft samatha jhana with vipassana jhana while mapping eye-fluttering to the 2nd/3rd jhana transition, reports replicating a brief cessation-like event using a forum-sourced eye-flutter technique, proposes a personal 'This/IS-ness' model of the four paths, and engages in an extended community debate on dependent origination versus emptiness.
interesting_sentence: "This isn't like a 'big deal' drop out entirely cessation." —J W
helpful_sentence: "When you lose the subject, you also lose the objects." —John L
who_should_read: Meditators curious about jhana/vipassana-jhana transitions, eye-fluttering cessation techniques, and philosophical framing of the paths via 'This-ness.'

author: Dan J Franklin
summary: Posting first as Dan J Franklin and later under the name Hazard J Gibbons, the author logs months of walking and sitting noting practice, cycling repeatedly through A&P, Dark Night (fear, misery, disgust) and Re-Observation into low Equanimity, while integrating antidepressant medication, Aspergian social anxiety, jhana attempts, and recurring anatta-themed dreams.
interesting_sentence: "This feeling isn't anything like depression; it rolls in like a black cloud and sometimes showers little black snowflakes on a searing skull." —Dan J Franklin
helpful_sentence: "When practicing walking meditation I use Shinzen Young's Focus Out technique, where the notes are on external objects (Sight, Sound, Touch)." —Hazard J Gibbons
who_should_read: Meditators wanting a raw, self-questioning account of cycling through the dukkha nanas while managing antidepressants, social anxiety, and Asperger's traits.

author: Hazard J Gibbons
summary: Hazard J Gibbons logs daily noting, walking meditation, and occasional jhana practice while cycling repeatedly through Dark Night phenomena (fear, misery, disgust, re-observation, restlessness) alongside insomnia, antidepressant medication, social anxiety, and Aspergian traits, briefly touching 2nd jhana and A&P-like clarity before settling into low equanimity.
interesting_sentence: I heard a loud, but calm voice say "Sure, lots of people want to die". I was immediately cradled back to low EQ, for some reason, on hearing this. —Hazard J Gibbons
helpful_sentence: I don't sit with these and try putting the feelings into physical activity like exercise (resuming my hatha yoga practice is helping). —Hazard J Gibbons
who_should_read: Meditators navigating dark night territory while also managing social anxiety, autism-spectrum traits, and psychiatric medication.

author: Droll Dedekind
summary: Chronicles early insight-practice cycling through dissolution, fear, A&P, and equanimity with a self-tracked, timestamped stage-spotting log, culminating in a hedged but genuine stream-entry claim after about six months of practice.
interesting_sentence: "8:52 'What was that?'-moment, bliss, A&P starting shortly after, (was a longer and more pleasurable A&P than usual)" —Droll Dedekind
helpful_sentence: "You don't catch a fruition, you sometimes notice the blip of lack of catching anything." —Dream Walker
who_should_read: Prepath practitioners curious about self-tracking nana cycling and the hedged, uncertain experience of a first stream-entry claim.

author: Dada Kind
summary: Chronicles near-daily kriyas and physio-energetic phenomena (energetic 'tastes,' heat/cold flows, muscle spasms) alongside kasina and formless-jhana concentration work, actualism experiments with 'Direct Mode' and PCEs, and a developing theory linking Reichian orgasm/bioenergetics and non-ejaculatory-orgasm practice to insight-cycle dynamics, while moving from a previously claimed 2nd path into self-assessed anagami territory.
interesting_sentence: "If my hypothesis is correct, sexual energy isn't just one way to get enlightened, it's the only way, from a certain point of view." —Dada Kind
helpful_sentence: "Accepting everything else along with stopping that squeezing leads to PCEs for me." —Not Tao
who_should_read: Anagami-stage practitioners interested in jhana/kasina concentration, actualism, and body-based Reichian or kriya approaches to purification.

author: Dada Kind
key_words: "I am that I am" mantra fruition
summary: Details prepath noting/concentration cycling into low-to-high Equanimity, then narrates a spontaneous 'I am that I am' mantra event experienced as stream entry on 3/14 and a subsequent intense A&P leading to a self-diagnosed second path on 5/8.
interesting_sentence: "So, I closed my eyes and for no consciously apparent reason I repeated the phrase mentally as if calling it out, \"I am that I am\", I (seemingly) instantaneously felt a very very vague sense of understanding or recognition at some level, then ?????, then I was back with a jolt." —Dada Kind
helpful_sentence: "Also, a pointer-- if we take the seven factors of enlightenment as our guide, the factor to cultivate in Equanimity is Equanimity itself." —Eric M W
who_should_read: Prepath meditators who enjoy intellectualizing their cycling will appreciate this geeky, detailed phenomenology of a self-diagnosed stream entry and second path.

author: Balint P
summary: A short reboot of a practice log tracking a week of 30-90 minute sits mixing TMI concentration, four-elements body scanning, and silent noting, with explicit weekly goals for building a consistent daily sitting habit.
interesting_sentence: "Practicing with four foundations of mofoness" —Balint P
helpful_sentence: "In any case, aim for consistency, not heroics." —Balint P
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate meditators wanting a lighthearted, low-pressure model for building a consistent short daily sitting practice with TMI and noting.

author: Balint P
summary: A structured practice journal blending TMI-style SHF noting with vedana couplets, phone-torch/candle Fire Kasina, Zhan Zhuang standing meditation, and Analayo-style body-element scanning, alongside candid tracking of sit lengths and repeated struggles integrating practice with daily productivity.
interesting_sentence: "Impression that the visual field consisted entirely of flickering dots (pixels) – or as if such a layer was superposed on it." —Balint P
helpful_sentence: "In the long haul \"sustainable\" is where it's at, but taking it right up to the edge where you can see \"sustainability\" start to wobble and fall apart is not a bad thing, as long as you trust your sense at that point and ease off and allow the work of integration at a lower temperature." —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in combining TMI-style SHF noting with kasina practice and Focusing-style body work, especially those struggling to reconcile intensive practice with daily productivity.

author: Geoffrey Gatekeeper of the Gateless Gate
summary: Chronicles a grueling 60-day Mahasi noting retreat day-by-day, tracking intense involuntary head/body shaking, a day-17 'third eye' opening that dissolved back pain, evolving airy-to-plain equanimity states, and physical breakdown (fever, constipation) followed by late-retreat burnout.
interesting_sentence: "This transformative event, akin to a third eye opening, coincided with the diminishing of the energy ball in the head, heightened sensitivity, and a significant reduction in back pain." —Geoffrey Gatekeeper of the Gateless Gate
helpful_sentence: "You're absolutely right that it is much more like a marathon and you don't really need to \"try hard\" -- just the consistency of being on retreat is enough for making progress." —shargrol
who_should_read: Anyone contemplating a long silent Mahasi-style retreat will get a realistic preview of the physical toll, involuntary movement, and equanimity states involved.

author: Geoffrey Gatekeeper of the Gateless Gate
summary: A day-by-day phenomenological log of a 60-day Mahasi noting retreat detailing near-constant involuntary shaking and head-drops, a third-eye-opening event, forays into the formless jhanas up to the 8th (confirmed by the Sayadaw), and a day-42 emotional breakdown followed by a long low-energy stretch of plain equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "After the back pain suddenly vanished, a few minutes after that it felt as if I had a third physical eyelid, up in the middle of my forehead, that was closed, twitched/shuttered for maybe 1 second, and then opened." —Geoffrey Gatekeeper of the Gateless Gate
helpful_sentence: "The easiest way to tell if I was in this state was my head wouldn't have any psychic gripping, but there would still be earth element present." —Geoffrey Gatekeeper of the Gateless Gate
who_should_read: Yogis planning a long Mahasi-style noting retreat who want a realistic, unglamorized account of kundalini-like movements, physical side effects, and a mid-retreat crash.

author: Geoffrey Gatekeeper of the Gateless Gate
