summary: A monastery resident's detailed log tracks persistent head-pressure/energy phenomena, hypnagogic imagery triggered by binaural beats, multi-day retreat sits with titan-realm intensity, and a sustained investigation of emptiness and dependent origination via Rob Burbea's Seeing That Frees.
interesting_sentence: "It's also clear I was spinning my wheels and getting into some spiritual materialism...But no, just let go and be free." —Geoffrey Gatekeeper of the Gateless Gate
helpful_sentence: "If I look carefuly, in a broad focus way, for holding or tension, I can generally see it and relax it." —Martin
who_should_read: Meditators interested in detailed energetic phenomena (head pressure, hypnagogic imagery) during extended equanimity practice paired with rigorous emptiness/dependent-origination theory on long monastic retreat.

author: Geoffrey Gatekeeper of the Gateless Gate
summary: Documents an extended arc from stabilized equanimity through persistent head-pressure investigation, multiple psychedelic experiences (DMT, 5-MeO/bufo), a three-week Burbea-influenced retreat, and a self-confirmed stream-entry claim with early second-path cycling by the log's end.
interesting_sentence: "Okay, I am fairly confident this is stream entry at this point." —Geoffrey Gatekeeper of the Gateless Gate
helpful_sentence: "SE is a natural result of mindfulness. The mind wants to let go of clinging and avoiding and over-reacting." —shargrol
who_should_read: Yogis interested in the phenomenology of persistent head-pressure/energetic work, psychedelic-assisted insight, and the ambiguous process of self-diagnosing stream entry.

author: Geoffrey Gatekeeper of the Gateless Gate
summary: A stream-of-consciousness, koan-driven log where Geoffrey works 'there's something there' as a personal koan, cycles through intense jhana states (J2 'fireworks' collapsing into J4), attends a weeklong jhana retreat with Angelo Dilulo, and processes his father's terminal decline and death alongside his own engagement.
interesting_sentence: "Enlightenment broke my brain (just like the Zen people said)." —Geoffrey Gatekeeper of the Gateless Gate
helpful_sentence: "It's also important to hold in mind that every path event could just be an A&P event, just to keep a certain degree of modesty and humility." —shargrol
who_should_read: Meditators who enjoy a humorous, non-map-bound account of jhana retreats, energetic 'kundalini'-type phenomena, and processing a parent's death alongside intense practice.

author: Jure K
summary: A months-long noting log in which the yogi battles severe fatigue and dullness through loud, fast noting, works through repeated craving/aversion cycles flagged by George S as A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity territory, and gradually learns to drop effort and control in favor of surrender.
interesting_sentence: "The emotion is like the cowboy that sits in the corner of a bar in darkness with his wide brim hat." —Jure K
helpful_sentence: "If you practice for 45 minutes then note aloud matter of fact experience on a rate of 1-5 sensations a second." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Yogis struggling with heavy sleepiness and fatigue during formal sits who want a real-time model of pushing through with loud noting and community coaching.

author: Jure K
summary: Documents months of nightly 30-60+ minute noting sits at varying speeds (0-8 sensations/sec), working through fatigue, fear, disgust and restlessness while also unpacking childhood-rooted perfectionism and a rekindled affair with a married ex-girlfriend that gets processed both on and off the cushion.
interesting_sentence: "This reminds me of what Daniel m Ingram says about (I forgot what it was) but this makes an A&P look like dry toast." —Jure K
helpful_sentence: "Why not do the noting aloud (or whisper aloud)? It really is a good way of staying in the game and if you do it 1-3 sensations a second there will be enough disembeding to get you going for the duration of your sit." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Practitioners struggling with noting fatigue and resistance who want a candid model of pairing consistent technique with real-life relationship and family trauma work.

author: Jure K
summary: Cycles rapidly through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity while noting 1-5, reporting possible cessations while welding, jhana-like absorptions, and a self-diagnosis of borderline personality disorder tied to childhood abandonment fears and a breakup.
interesting_sentence: "So after seeing my therapist and doing some research I think I may have borderline personality disorder." —Jure K
helpful_sentence: "Once you can go through that whole A&P -> DN -> EQ cycle in a single sit then it really puts the off-cushion cycling into a lighter perspective." —George S
who_should_read: Meditators combining therapy and noting while working through attachment trauma and rapid A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity cycling will find this raw, unfiltered account relatable.

author: Jure K
summary: Chronicles months of daily 40-90 minute sits mixing jhana exploration, noting, and Tonglen, working through recurring fear/misery/disgust cycles, an OCD relapse, and a childhood shame memory, while managing medication and a past hospitalization from over-practicing.
interesting_sentence: "Then I recovered and tried again, this time I went way overboard and had to go hospital because I was that shaken and pretty much lifeless." —Jure K
helpful_sentence: "Feelings don't really need to be explained, they just need to be felt (i.e. experienced in the body). When they are fully felt then they are released and there is no need for the story any more." —George S
who_should_read: Meditators with a trauma/OCD history who want a candid account of pairing jhana practice with dark-night fear and psychiatric care.

author: Jure K
summary: Returning to daily 45-minute noting sits after starting therapy and medication, the author cycles through dark-night resistance, PTSD-linked family triggers, and shame around sex before settling into a carefree equanimity described as matching shargrol's low-to-high EQ account.
interesting_sentence: "We're literally at the mercy of an imaginary image!!" —Jure K
helpful_sentence: "The more you sink your teeth into DN the better seat you get in the EQ, so to speak." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Meditators managing trauma and medication alongside intensive noting practice who want a candid look at cycling through dark night resistance into equanimity.

author: Jure K
summary: Jure K (posting under the name George) logs a stop-start shamatha/noting practice through classic dark-night territory, working intensively with anger, guilt, and family trauma (including a brother's suicidal ideation) with heavy community support.
interesting_sentence: "I pictured my mum standing at the doorway looking at my lifeless cold body, crying and I felt guilt!" —Jure K
helpful_sentence: "Bringing awareness to aversion is the practice! Instead of trying to push through aversion (which leads to spacing out and fantasising), try exploring it instead." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners in early dark-night territory dealing with anger and family trauma who want a candid peer account paired with detailed community mentorship.

author: Gerry V
summary: Describes shifting from formal noting to bare 'noticing' practice, tracking recurring boredom behind the eyes, restlessness, pronounced body sensations like heat/cold-sweats, and reduced anxiety compared to when he first started posting.
interesting_sentence: "Another thing I've been noticing is that I'll get quite hot even during cold which makes me cold sweat which is very unpleasant." —Gerry V
helpful_sentence: "I notice the 3 characteristics of what I notice by asking questions. Is this permanent? Is this me if \"I\" am noticing it?" —Gerry V
who_should_read: Beginners curious about the difference between active noting and bare noticing will see a simple first-hand comparison of the two.

log: Gerry's Noting practice log aiming for Stream Entry
author: Gerry V
summary: A months-long noting-practice log that cycles repeatedly between Dark Night/Re-Observation and Equanimity, troubleshooting narrow-focus versus soft/peripheral attention and the difference between 'noticing suffering' and 'focusing on dispassion,' without yet reaching Stream Entry.
interesting_sentence: "I've had some adrenaline-y feeling come up at points at just how quiet and open things get, I guess I'm just so used to harsh sensations that when I get to that point in my meditation, I get scared, like I have nothing to hold onto." —Gerry V
helpful_sentence: "Once you are solidly in High EQ you don't really 'do' anything...The meditation does you at that point and things kinda just happen and sensations present themselves." —Dream Walker
who_should_read: Yogis stuck cycling between Dark Night and Equanimity who want a candid, technique-level account of narrow versus soft attention and dispassion practice.

author: Henry H
summary: Henry starts a log of his U Ba Khin/Goenka-style vipassana practice, aiming for twice-daily one-hour sits but falling short due to sila lapses (occasional alcohol, internet addiction), poor concentration and sleep, and health problems, then updates a few months later describing a switch from informal practice back to timed sessions via the Dhamma.org app with modest improvement.
interesting_sentence: "The latter seems to function as an intoxicant for me." —Henry H
helpful_sentence: "One hour, twice daily is a great goal! It's worth it." —Alex N
who_should_read: Goenka/U Ba Khin practitioners who want to see an honest, low-drama account of struggling to balance sila, internet habits, health, and a twice-daily sitting goal.

author: Henry Fellows Moss
summary: Henry documents daily 30-45 minute noting sessions across all six sense doors alongside closed-eye 'murk' concentration modeled loosely on fire kasina, tracking recurring sensation-thought sequences, involuntary body spasms and spinal energy surges, a self-diagnosed note-prediction problem, and TMI-style dullness, while forum members offer encouragement, energy-work context, and advice to investigate rather than theorize.
interesting_sentence: "To my delight, images in the style of the jigsaw puzzle I have been working and on swam before my sight and faded, leaving the darkness of my eyelids in their wake, which would then give way to various mental images again; and this would happen alternatim." —Henry Fellows Moss
helpful_sentence: "A lot of the time \"problems\" in meditation come from our sense of self/witness/observer/controller/do-er becoming less strong, less clear. Which is exactly the point of meditation (!) but it does feel weird when we first notice it." —shargrol
who_should_read: Insight practitioners curious about fine-grained six-sense-door noting mechanics and closed-eye murk/fire-kasina style concentration paired with a supportive but occasionally blunt forum community.

author: Irakli Gorgadze
summary: Working through TMI stages 4-5 in twice-daily 30-minute sits, the author battles persistent subtle dullness centered on intense right-thumb sensations amid family dysfunction, then shifts late in the month to multi-hour Strong Determination Sittings that surface insights about learning versus enduring pain.
interesting_sentence: "Though the longer time passes, the worse my siblings' personality become and more co-dependent they become on their abuser, it is disgusting to see them being tarnished like this, but I can't control them." —Irakli Gorgadze
helpful_sentence: "Knowing that progress is not in enduring but in learning, and suffering vanishes while learning and not while enduring, makes the mind focus on learning." —Irakli Gorgadze
who_should_read: TMI practitioners troubleshooting stage 4-5 subtle dullness who want a firsthand account of transitioning into Strong Determination Sitting for pain endurance training.

author: Irakli Gorgadze
summary: Daily TMI-style shamatha log tracking systematic work with subtle dullness via finger and body scanning, piti cultivation, and steady progression from stage 4 toward stage 5 across a month of 30-60 minute sits.
interesting_sentence: "Is breath energy, breath sensations, tingling and piti the same? Or similar? I could feel how breath energy turned into piti as it got intense." —Irakli Gorgadze
helpful_sentence: "The basic progression in anapanasati (mindfulness of breathing) is kaya (body, physical sensations) -> piti (energetic tingling/vibrations) -> sukha (emotions bliss/pleasure/happiness) -> citta (mind)." —George S
who_should_read: TMI practitioners working through stages 4-5 who want a granular, day-by-day account of systematically overcoming subtle dullness.

author: Irakli Gorgadze
summary: A granular, near-daily TMI-style shamatha log tracking six 20-30 minute sits per day, cataloguing specific dullness antidotes, emerging piti and energy-body sensations, and body-scanning experiments while working toward access concentration.
interesting_sentence: "I'd rather have no boundaries, be bullied and be treated as trash than fight for healthy boundaries and worry all the time in a MAP session." —Irakli Gorgadze
helpful_sentence: "Samatha is practicing how to keep the telescope/microscope still, vipassana is looking through it." —Niels Lyngsø
who_should_read: TMI practitioners stuck on dullness in early concentration stages who want a play-by-play of what specific antidotes actually worked for someone else.

author: Irakli Gorgadze
summary: Daily journal meticulously tracking investigation of dullness and distraction through TMI-style breath concentration, counting, and noting practices, while processing ongoing family bullying/abuse and a recovered memory of childhood sexual assault.
interesting_sentence: "Remembered past trauma, when at night when I was sleeping, I was sexually assaulted by my classmate, he was hitting my butt while acting like he was fu*king me, we were clothed and no penetration happened." —Irakli Gorgadze
helpful_sentence: "Once dullness is no longer resisted but given attention and investigated, then it tends to pass." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners working through TMI-style dullness and distraction problems who want granular, technique-level troubleshooting notes, especially while under family-related stress.

author: Irakli Gorgadze
summary: Logs a handful of September and November sits alternating Strong Determination Sitting/Do Nothing with TMI-style concentration, tracking recurring dullness and lurching, and reflects on a 3-month lapse from TMI caused by illness and escapist habits like manga, anime, and PMO.
interesting_sentence: "In these 3 months, I could have mastered at least the 5th stage." —Irakli Gorgadze
helpful_sentence: "It is much better to approach practice by developing consistent habits rather than being too obsessive about it and trying to do more than 6 hours a day (Which I can't seem to pull off, especially for TMI practice)." —Irakli Gorgadze
who_should_read: Meditators struggling with chronic dullness and consistency who want a candid comparison of Do Nothing versus TMI-style concentration practice.

author: Jeyan "Jey" Burns-Oorjitham
summary: Documents roughly a year and a half of near-daily jhana practice using The Mind Illuminated's luminous jhanas, breath-following, and a 'Who is...?' self-inquiry, reporting increasingly frequent 'shudder-to-silence' cessation events and a multi-day home retreat while cycling through the three characteristics, dukkha ñanas, and equanimity toward a stated goal of fourth path.
interesting_sentence: "It's over. The quest, the search has ended. There's nothing left." —Jeyan "Jey" Burns-Oorjitham
helpful_sentence: "Culadasa said in meditation and insight audio #1 that sitting is like training wheels and awareness + attention = sati in daily life is riding the bike" —Jeyan "Jey" Burns-Oorjitham
who_should_read: Practitioners following The Mind Illuminated's jhana progression who want a granular, day-by-day record of luminous jhana and cessation phenomenology on the path toward higher stages.

author: Macky Ben-Jonah
summary: A beginner meditator new to noting practice moves from Shinzen's See-Hear-Feel style through Shargrol's structured noting toward a self-diagnosed 3C/dark-night territory, reporting eye strain, spontaneous joy consistent with early A&P, and persistent difficulty balancing noting practice with studying.
interesting_sentence: "I remember when I first broke the A&P memrane- (it was actually a membrane)- I was doing TMI body breathing practice- trying very hard to put every single cell of my brain on the subtlest of breath sensations in my body." —Sigma Tropic
helpful_sentence: "When it comes to crossing the A&P, my impression is that you can almost never use 'too much' energy and effort." —Danny S
who_should_read: Beginner noters curious about early ñana territory (3C, A&P, dukkha nanas) and how peer feedback can help diagnose stuck points before stream entry.

author: Jacob Hermansen
summary: A Danish TMI practitioner logs about six weeks of 40-90 minute daily samatha-then-noting sits following a hard 10-day vipassana retreat, describing persistent dullness, vivid energy/chi and vibratory body sensations, and inner-child trauma work centered on childhood fear of his father.
interesting_sentence: "My usual tension in the throat transformed at one point into a green/blue soft mass that squirmed and moved, a bit like a lump of slime." —Jacob Hermansen
helpful_sentence: "Mind that there's no rule that says 'one note = one word,' and noting 'subtle thought', 'subtle image', 'thought and image' can be a really good clarifying exercise." —Akko !
who_should_read: Meditators integrating TMI-style jhana/samatha with vipassana who want an honest account of post-retreat dark-night territory intertwined with trauma-processing and inner-child work.

author: Jade Lee
summary: A middle-school teacher with a history of hospitalization for anxiety and depression transitions from Headspace-guided meditation to noting practice, reporting a striking lucid-dream noting experience and a stretch of increased irritability and judgmental reactivity toward family during the newborn/pandemic period.
interesting_sentence: "I was able to note, walking, weight, laughter, fear, comprehension and sounds. I was asleep for maybe a minute and then I came to and had to stop the session to try and grasp what had just happened." —Jade Lee
helpful_sentence: "This gets right to some basic 'street smarts' of meditation, the 'hold your mud' rule of thumb, also known as 'look out for leakage, off the mat.'" —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: New noters coming from app-based mindfulness like Headspace who want reassurance and concrete guidance on early noting technique and how family-life stress can surface during practice.

author: Jai B
summary: A newcomer with a trauma history and four years of prior Feldenkrais training logs short reclining unguided sits tracking breath and body tension, describing a significant somatic release episode in his core and ongoing efforts to note nicotine cravings rather than act on them.
interesting_sentence: "I felt it; something deep in my core grabbed, and then let go. I was so excited!! I thought the rest of me would just kind of follow, but it didn't." —Jai B
helpful_sentence: "Given the background you described, it may also be useful to explore trauma-informed approaches to mindfulness." —Kailin T
who_should_read: Beginners with trauma histories or chronic tension who want a model of using reclining posture and body-focused attention while working through nicotine cravings.

author: Logan G
summary: Returning to daily 20-90 minute breath and jhana practice after a hiatus, Logan cycles through dark-night-like fear, disgust, and desire for deliverance before settling into equanimity-tinged investigation of dullness, aversion, and a dissolving inside/outside self boundary tied to his identification with productivity.
interesting_sentence: "The active dullness almost seems like a defense mechanism, like a hedgehog curling up." —Logan G
helpful_sentence: "It seems hard to know exactly what is going on in the realm of the hazy and nebulous but there still is some sort of clear knowing there." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Experienced yogis moving through dark-night-to-equanimity territory who want detailed introspective techniques for investigating dullness, aversion, and the sense of self.

author: Jake Frankfurt Middenhall
summary: Jake intensifies fire kasina practice to 5-13 hours daily aiming for stream entry, cycling through kundalini-like energy, a prolonged dark night, and self-assessed equanimity, while experiencing sleep problems and psychosis-like symptoms during solo home retreats.
interesting_sentence: "I also had glimpses of this white fluid material which Daniel calls \"The Molten Gold\"." —Jake Frankfurt Middenhall
helpful_sentence: "An attitude of openness to fear, disgust, pain, irritation, anger, dullness, whatever else comes up is something good to cultivate, as, if you are progressing well, you will be having a lot more DNs, and some of these might end up being a bit more rough than others." —Kaloyan Stefanov
who_should_read: Intensive fire-kasina practitioners curious how heavy-dose home retreats interact with dark night symptoms and sleep disruption.

log: Jano's practice log - first 100 days after restarting
author: Jano Pavuk
summary: After restarting formal TMI practice in March 2019, Jano reflects on 100 days of daily breath-focused sitting—working through posture pain, hyper-attentional tendencies, and intellectual over-analysis—while completing a 7-day vipassana retreat with Christopher Titmuss and a 2-day Vajrayana identification retreat.
interesting_sentence: "Very likely the consquence of my hyper anxiety, and hyper active amygdala that has been with me since my childhood." —Jano Pavuk
helpful_sentence: "I understand now that I actually need to put in less effort into focus. Make my effort lighter so I don't switch into hyper-focus." —Jano Pavuk
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate TMI practitioners wanting a candid, foundational account of restarting practice and overcoming hyper-focus and posture pain.

author: Sanath Sambamoorthi
summary: Sanath Sambamoorthi and practice partner Mark Smith log months of daily fire-kasina and jhana work—chasing the fifth jhana, tracking recurring kriyas and shaking, and alternating into breath-based insight practice—while navigating dullness, distraction, and life stress in Tokyo.
interesting_sentence: "At one point there were at least a hundred or more shooting star like objects shooting through and around the nimitta of to a 45 degree angle towards me." —Sanath Sambamoorthi
helpful_sentence: "Clinging to the meditation object, despite intense phenomena, shaking, etc, is the rep to build the unified mind and pliancy." —Sanath Sambamoorthi
who_should_read: Concentration-focused practitioners interested in detailed fire-kasina and samatha-jhana phenomenology, including nimitta development and kriyas.

author: Jared N
summary: Returning to daily TMI-style breath and body-scan practice after a January 2022 dissolution experience and hospitalization, Jared works through months of anger, self-loathing, and intense kriyas, culminating in a self-run 3-day home retreat with vivid no-self and suchness experiences.
interesting_sentence: "It's not me . It's not me . It's not me . It never was, and it never will be." —Jared N
helpful_sentence: "With this kind of jerky stuff or kriyas, what I've found most helpful is looking for the ways I am resisting it by tensing in the body." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners recovering from a difficult dissolution or dark-night experience who want an honest, detailed account of returning to daily practice and working with kriyas and hindrances.

log: Jose's second log. Is this progress?
author: Jose C
summary: After a Goenka-course A&P experience, describes switching from body scanning to TMI-style breath concentration and struggling to stay with recurring emotionally-charged distractions during 1.5-2 hour daily sits, while noting a few unusually absorbed ~20-minute states resembling first jhana.
interesting_sentence: "My attention was in the breath like a dog to a bone, and any distraction were noted but woulnd't take my attention for more than a second." —Jose C
helpful_sentence: "Where attention goes, it magnifies whatever it looks at." —Lotus Vu
who_should_read: Beginner meditators following TMI who want to see how early concentration struggles and emotional resistance play out after an A&P experience.

author: Martin V
summary: Reflects on continuously noticing vedana in ordinary activities like slicing cheese after an unexpected, medically inexplicable recovery from heart failure, describing a panoramic, non-judgmental awareness of feeling-tone in everything he does, thinks, and perceives.
interesting_sentence: "That's where I find myself at the moment: in a beautiful, undulating vedana landscape." —Martin V
helpful_sentence: "This is an ignorance shield; an automatic backend decision to ignore the matter based on its vedana pattern." —Martin V
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in off-cushion vedana practice and how equanimity can manifest as panoramic, non-judgmental awareness woven into daily life.

author: Jason
summary: A long noting-practice journal tracking cycles from A&P through Dark Night and Equanimity to an identified fruition and stream entry, then continuing on through apparent second and third path with jhana work, Nirodha Samapatti experiments, and Mahamudra training.
interesting_sentence: "I shook just a little, blacked out for a millisecond -hardly interrupting some distracting thought- and then bliss wave." —Jason
helpful_sentence: "Anything that can be observed cannot be that which is observing." —Tommy M
who_should_read: Practitioners wanting a granular, cycle-by-cycle account of noting practice progressing through stream entry into second and third path with detailed jhana and Nirodha experimentation.

author: Jazz Muzak
summary: Describes easily entering 4th jhana as a launchpad for vipassana, investigating a persistent head/chest 'reference point' or watcher, and wrestling with frustration and possible emotional repression while informally teaching dharma online.
interesting_sentence: "the light off my lamp turned into a dove at one point, which was quite beautiful." —Jazz Muzak
helpful_sentence: "I was instructed by Michael Taft to work with the impermanence of a single reference point and really follow it closely as it vanishes, to see the vanishing." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
who_should_read: Meditators grappling with a persistent sense of a 'watcher' or reference point who want a candid account of investigating self-referential perception through jhana-based vipassana.

author: Jean B
summary: Chronicles a new father's daily anapanasati and mantra concentration practice as he repeatedly battles torpor and distraction while edging toward but never quite reaching first jhana, later blending noting practice with Kabbalistic magick and sigil work.
interesting_sentence: "18/03/2014 - So close to first jhana on a toilet seat" —Jean B
helpful_sentence: "So to go into meditative training, one is asking the the directing brain to sort of go get a massage and not tell the masseuse what to do." —katy steger
who_should_read: New or intermediate meditators balancing family life who want a relatable, blow-by-blow account of working through torpor toward first jhana using breath and mantra concentration.

author: Jeff Wright
summary: A beginner documents daily Metta/TWIM sits using Bhante Vimalaramsi's 6R technique, tracking joy/bliss escalation, chronic sit-bone and neck pain, and family stress while building toward a 9-day online home retreat with Dhamma Sukha.
interesting_sentence: "I discerned a very distinct, fluttering vibration (maybe 10-20 Hz) in my hearing that sounded/felt like a helicopter in the distance." —Jeff Wright
helpful_sentence: "Bhante V. talks about viewing the hindrances (which in this case are obviously Restlessness) as friends who are teaching you where your cravings lie." —Jeff Wright
who_should_read: New TWIM/Metta practitioners who want a granular, honest account of daily 6R practice alongside the frustrations of chronic pain and a hectic family schedule.

author: Jehanne S Peacock
summary: A multi-year log that begins with classic noting/candle-kasina cycling through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity, then shifts into tantric Open Heart Yoga guru-yoga practice tracked via the Bhumi model, culminating years later in stable baseline clarity and teaching meditation to others.
interesting_sentence: "I remember I was flying upwards, my arms wide open like I was flying, looking up, there were these sparkles around me in black space, kind of like fireworks that are about to explode, and I was thinking: 'FUCK YEAAH THIS IS IT!!!', thinking about A&P type of thing." —Jehanne S Peacock
helpful_sentence: "Jhanic stuff like this can be an interesting object for investigation: find out what the cues, the sensations are which indicate that there is 'space' in front of you. Which cues are telling you about the extent, the direction, the volume?" —Florian
who_should_read: Yogis interested in blending Ingram-style insight maps with Vajrayana-flavored subtle-body practice tracked over many years.

author: Jenny
summary: Details a likely stream-entry cessation on August 8, 2014 during a work lunchtime sit watching 'formations' arise and pass, followed by weeks of jhana-absorption afterglow, magick experimentation, and cycling that others read as movement into second path.
interesting_sentence: "During those few moments, 'I' was dislocated into everything around me, with nothing on 'my' side, no center, no sense of within-body or location-grounding." —Jenny
helpful_sentence: "Don't judge what happens, just judge whether you are making time for practice every day, at least whatever your minimum quality sit is." —x x
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about a detailed first-person account of a spontaneous stream-entry-like cessation and its psychological aftermath, especially those pairing insight practice with energy work.

author: Jim Smith
summary: A householder describes a repeatable relaxation-induced 'transition' he comes to identify as cessation, then builds an extended, non-traditional theory of practice centered on physical relaxation, mindfulness, and surrender to reduce dukkha in daily life.
interesting_sentence: "There is no self in the nerve cells, there is no self in the nerve impulses, there is only a self in the virtual reality the brain serves to our conscious mind." —Jim Smith
helpful_sentence: "For the effects on the body, physical relaxation is effective. For the effects on the mind mindfulness is effective." —Jim Smith
who_should_read: Householders wanting a logic-driven, non-doctrinaire relaxation-plus-mindfulness framework for reducing dukkha without retreat-level concentration practice.

author: Papa Che Dusko
summary: Papa Che Dusko chronicles months of daily 30-45 minute sits and forest walks using Kenneth Folk's 'Look how it's...' technique interwoven with noting, cycling through intense dark-night symptoms (skull pressure, insect-crawling skin, nada sound, grief over his mother-in-law's death) alongside absorption into Jhana and a jokingly self-aware claim of 4th path.
interesting_sentence: "Fuck this shit, it's not a fucking movie. It's real life where the bullies win, the ones we love get sick and age and die and rot away, and the fight in the field of the black birds goes on and on. Look how you grieve this. Look how fucking impossible it is to accept it. There's your fucking koan, man." —Tim Farrington
helpful_sentence: "It's looking at the experiencer of this experience. To whom is this happening?" —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in Kenneth Folk's 'Look how it's...' technique who want a raw, humor-laced account of cycling through dark-night symptoms while juggling family life and grief.

author: Jens
summary: A 26-year-old medical student begins a structured TMI-based Anapanasati practice of 20-30 minute sits, working through TMI stages 2-3, shortly after achieving sobriety from alcohol, with a stated long-term goal of stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "I cut down dramatically in the past half year, and I went completely dry just before new year's eve." —Jens
helpful_sentence: "be ready to be surprised and believe that with positive effort the path will reveal itself and the knots will untangle with sustained efforts." —tom moylan
who_should_read: Beginners juggling a demanding schedule who want a model for starting a TMI-based practice while working through anxiety, depression, and early sobriety.

author: Brian K
summary: Brian K logs months of twice-daily hour-long breath-concentration sits chasing access to first jhana, wrestling with restlessness, posture pain, and inconsistent focus while experimenting with noting and an energy-balancing technique.
interesting_sentence: "Twice today I had an interesting experience, while meditating, and not necessarily while I was extremely focused, well, without being too graphic I noticed I strange tingly feeling at the tip of my sex organ..." —Brian K
helpful_sentence: "Just keep returning attention to the breath each time it falls away, and that practice alone will lead you to stability." —fivebells
who_should_read: Meditators building shamatha concentration toward first jhana who want relatable, warts-and-all troubleshooting of restlessness, posture, and consistency.

log: Jim Jam's Log #1 - The Man Rigging the Table
author: Jim Jam
summary: A combat veteran recounts his path from A&P in 2019 through stream entry (2021), second path attained by sitting roughly seven hours a day during a combat deployment (2022-23), and a vivid cessation-like 'GRRR' fruition he identifies as third path, alongside jhana practice, Dakini visualization, and body-centered trauma work.
interesting_sentence: "I watched a young man die and told myself later that same day that somehow the Dharma would make me feel better about it." —Jim Jam
helpful_sentence: "Being an Anagami is all about learning to look but not stare." —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Practitioners navigating post-third-path integration, trauma processing, and jhana-emptiness practice who want a frank account from a combat veteran blending pragmatic dharma with Vajrayana elements.

author: John P
summary: John P logs several months of daily body-scan and breath-concentration practice augmented with tai chi and energy work, tracking procrastination, energetic phenomena, and a clear passage through Fear/Misery and Re-observation into what he identifies as an early Equanimity while resolving to attain stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "I cried a little because of his misery, later I was reading some zen stories and other things and cried a little other times, for some reason it was really easy to cry, but it wasn't a sorrowful crying and each time I cried it did not last long" —John P
helpful_sentence: "once you are able to cast many simple resolutions during the day, you will find that just because of having done it you ability to follow the resolutions has increased dramatically." —M N
who_should_read: Meditators tracking their own progress through the dark night nanas who want a granular day-by-day example of body-scan practice, energetic phenomena, and the shift toward equanimity.

author: Jim Smith
summary: Describes a personal 'relaxing meditation' technique that moves through soft jhanas 5-8 into a cessation-like 'transition' followed by an afterglow, framed through neuroscience (default-mode vs experiential network) and compared against MBSR, metta, and maps from Shinzen Young, Kenneth Folk, and Daniel Ingram.
interesting_sentence: "The self that doesn't exist is the self that is right all the time, the self that can control every situation, the self that always wins, the self that everyone loves, the self that has the most money, power, status, the self that can fix every problem, etc etc." —Jim Smith
helpful_sentence: "Mindful of letting go means you are aware of a feeling like forgiveness or generosity or metta or compassion or sukkha, where if something unpleasant happened you would not be upset by it." —Jim Smith
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in a neuroscience-flavored, relaxation-based route to jhana and cessation who enjoy comparing dharma maps (Ingram, Shinzen Young, Kenneth Folk) side by side.

log: "Look how its practicing" Papa Che Log
author: Papa Che Dusko
summary: Chronicles a period of intense bodily agony, nicotine cravings, and family stress worked through with the 'Look How It's' walking inquiry and shargrol's Tonglen, brief forays into the 6 Realms and 5 Elements, Kenneth Folk's Listening Mahamudra, and a vivid tantric-style visualization of beheading a 'demon-me' figure.
interesting_sentence: "He keeps banging without Vaseline and laughing out loud and dangling that carrot on a stick." —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "The Mahamudra angle is less about reaching the emptiness and more about bringing the ease of the emptiness back into the form, to see form from what might be called a nondual perspective." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
who_should_read: Yogis juggling family and body stress who want a candid, technique-rich account blending Tonglen, 6 Realms/5 Elements, and Kenneth Folk's Listening Mahamudra.

author: Mindspace M
summary: Recounts a multi-year on-and-off relationship with meditation, from Mindfulness in Plain English and MCTB-inspired jhana attempts to a self-directed ego-dissolution experiment, culminating in an A&P-like breakthrough during a 4-AcO-DMT trip that triggered a materialist turn and a serious commitment to practice.
interesting_sentence: "We had tied ourself in a knot and there was nothing outside of ourselves to untie it." —Mindspace M
helpful_sentence: "One small observation I remember from this early practice was how, when I'd manage to stop paying attention to the everyday thoughts going around my head, my mind would take the opportunity to bring up more seductive and interesting thoughts (philosophical musings, grandiose plans, etc)." —Mindspace M
who_should_read: People curious how a psychedelic experience layered onto a dabbling meditation background can precipitate an A&P-like breakthrough and finally motivate serious practice.

log: My practice log - Meditation & working with anxiety [Elijah Smith] [MIGRATE
author: Elijah Smith
summary: Documents a year-long, anxiety-driven practice combining choiceless awareness, noting, and shikantaza, including destabilizing effects from concentration practice and a brief centerless, self-dissolving experience the author tentatively calls first path before partly retracting the claim the next morning.
interesting_sentence: "Sometimes I will even say \"do your worst\" or \"let it cut me\" and feelings that I used to get stuck with will loosen or even dissolve." —Elijah Smith
helpful_sentence: "They both seemed to agree that concentration tended to derepress psychological content, which is often destabilizing." —Elijah Smith
who_should_read: Meditators using practice to manage anxiety who want an honest account of destabilization risk from concentration practice and DN/EQ cycling.

author: Jim Smith
summary: A collection of theoretical essays, daily mantras, and AI-assisted (Gemini) explorations of identity view, dependent origination, and the deconstruction of the observer, punctuated by a detailed technical debate with fellow practitioner John L over the focus-distraction cycle.
interesting_sentence: "I am wondering if the way meditation and mindfulness is taught is deliberately inefficient to protect people from a sudden psychological shock that could damage mental health." —Jim Smith
helpful_sentence: "One needs to target the observer directly, by either (1) repeatedly relaxing the sense of observing and controlling, or (2) continuing to investigate, but including the sensations of the controller/observer (if any) in one's investigation, and aiming to see how the focus-distraction cycle is uncontrollable and does itself (i.e., is not-self), to the point that the focus-distraction cycle disappears—which requires an incrementally looser grip on experience, culminating in no gripping or focusing at all." —John L
who_should_read: Meditators who enjoy dissecting mindfulness theory (identity view, dependent origination, the observer) rather than reading blow-by-blow retreat reports.

log: Looking for Advice - Dealing with Voidness and Head Pressure
author: J W
summary: J W describes recurring forehead/head pressure that transforms into a black-hole-like 'Voidness' pull during deep meditation, and gathers extensive crowd-sourced advice on whether to lean into it or ground the energy lower in the body.
interesting_sentence: "Fear of the void vanishes into the void. The void loves you, bra." —terry
helpful_sentence: "in my experience, the Void presents itself precisely when we are not trying to do anything except practice the technique." —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: Meditators experiencing persistent head pressure or a pulling 'void' sensation who want a range of first-hand coping strategies from grounding to noting to acceptance.

author: Yilun Ong
summary: A Theravada monk documents an intense, unsolicited Kundalini awakening—whole-body vibrations, visuals, and mania—while practicing choiceless awareness, briefly claiming intellectual stream entry before refocusing on morality (sila) over pragmatic attainment-chasing.
interesting_sentence: "She can perform brain surgery without anaesthesia and me screaming..." —Yilun Ong
helpful_sentence: "It's important to understand that there is nothing \"wrong\" or \"insightful\" about K symptoms, it's just the body rewiring itself." —shargrol
who_should_read: Monastics or lay practitioners dealing with intense, unbidden Kundalini phenomena who want a raw, real-time account of riding it out with community support rather than suppression.

log: My practice log - Meditation & working with anxiety
author: Elijah Smith
summary: Elijah Smith tracks nearly two years of daily practice—cycling through concentration, noting, and choiceless awareness—as he works through psychedelic-triggered anxiety, repeated A&P and dark-night episodes, and a transient 'centerless' first-path-like experience.
interesting_sentence: "Things feel different now, my mental space is very light and I feel centerless, I am pretty sure this is first path." —Elijah Smith
helpful_sentence: "The sensation of tension or pressure in your head is indicative of an increase in the level of concentration. Use that sensation wisely to let you know when your mind has become concentrated so that you can use that state to examine (contemplate) the content of your mind in an effort to see things clearly." —Ian And
who_should_read: Meditators whose anxiety or psychedelic-triggered distress intensified with practice, looking for a granular, technique-by-technique account of cycling through A&P and dark night.

author: Jim Smith
summary: Lays out a highly idiosyncratic, self-devised practice combining relaxation exercises, breath-counting with a half-smile, mudras, and mantra to deactivate stress and the default mode network, while repeatedly arguing that stream entry is an arbitrary rather than necessary milestone.
interesting_sentence: "I think this means that the stream entry is an arbitrary and false milestone." —Jim Smith
helpful_sentence: "Anyway the point is that it is entirely possible to have fear, anger, lust, misery, etc. arise and not have the mind get locked into trance." —shargrol
who_should_read: Householder meditators skeptical of intensive noting practice who want a relaxation-and-happiness-based alternative framed in nervous-system terms.

author: Low Quality Qualia
summary: A returning meditator restarts a Culadasa/TMI-based practice after a pandemic-driven hiatus, logging short daily sits, recurring anger and self-hatred triggered by old memories, and body tension that dissipates concentration in a semi-access-concentration state.
interesting_sentence: "A howl into the void. A scream into the abyss." —Low Quality Qualia
helpful_sentence: "So rather than blowing up a balloon, it is more like operating a bicycle pump, with the periods of relaxation being like the return stroke during which the pressure in the cylinder is low." —Martin
who_should_read: Beginners restarting a TMI-based concentration practice after a break, especially those wrestling with access-concentration plateaus and self-critical reactivity.

author: mind less
summary: A parent with limited sit time integrates samatha and Mahasi-style noting into one practice, reaching A&P nearly every sit and Equanimity often, cycling through multiple samatha jhanas within EQ, while debating jhana-nana correspondences with other posters.
interesting_sentence: "I realized (in EQ) to a new degree that what I take for the outer world is an illusion, and that other people are a part of that illusion. I felt lonely and scared, but it subsided when noting it." —mind less
helpful_sentence: "Just drop them like hot stones." —Fitter Stoke
who_should_read: Vipassana practitioners curious how samatha jhanas map onto the nanas, especially parents fitting concentrated practice into limited daily sits.

author: Nick O
summary: Recounts a two-week Mahasi-style retreat at Tathagata Meditation Center in which chronic knee pain, A&P light phenomena, and dissolution give way to two distinct cessations, leaving a centerless, higher-definition sense field and a recognized daily afternoon restlessness pattern.
interesting_sentence: "yes, yes Nicolas. Precious moment, Nicolas" —Nick O
helpful_sentence: "In a sense, insight work makes suffering more intense but it provides the skills and a relationship with senses to step out of the way of it." —Nick O
who_should_read: Retreatants who want a blow-by-blow account of a full Mahasi-noting insight cycle and cessation, including how physical pain and old fears surface as insight fuel.

author: John L
summary: A law student and self-described 'mature third path' anagami documents a year of surrender-based practice - a solo dark retreat, extensive dependent-origination investigation with the sangha, and a two-week Thich Nhat Hanh monastery retreat culminating in a sustained 'no problemness' realization he links to fourth path, before cycling back into ordinary doubt and inconsistent practice.
interesting_sentence: "The world is not illusory. It's the same old world. The self is not illusory. It's the same old self." —John L
helpful_sentence: "Sensation is just the positive, negative, or neutral tone of the sensation. The \"desire\" is the urge that follows a 1/20th of a second later." —shargrol
who_should_read: Anagamis and post-third-path practitioners wrestling with surrender, dependent origination, and the ambiguity of non-dual/fourth-path territory will find a rare, granular first-person map here.

author: Lukas Lassooy
summary: A former military-depression sufferer turned insight-noting beginner tracks his early nana progression under George S and Tim Farrington's mentorship, then pivots toward Loch Kelly-style non-dual 'awake awareness' practice while consulting Frank Yang and Kenneth Folk.
interesting_sentence: "Started crying and laughing and realised there was never even a Lukas in the first place." —Lukas Lassooy
helpful_sentence: "The great thing about noting practice is that it doesn't matter what you are doing ... so long as you are noting it!" —George S
who_should_read: Newcomers to noting practice who want a grounded peer example of moving from a spontaneous not-self insight into structured Mahasi-style noting, then experimenting with non-dual glimpsing.

author: Mohan Ratnam
summary: A meticulous, near-daily noting and body-scanning practice log by a UK-based Goenka-trained yogi who, despite persistent doubt, physical pain, and dullness, never reaches a clear stream-entry claim even after attending -- and prematurely leaving on day 11 -- an intensive Dhammacari retreat.
interesting_sentence: "I've kind of given up on ever getting stream entry. I'm just doing the meditation." —Mohan Ratnam
helpful_sentence: "Time on the cushion >plus intelligent investigation of the causes of suffering< is very good practice." —shargrol
who_should_read: Beginning-to-intermediate vipassana practitioners struggling with doubt, dullness, and slow progress will find reassurance in this patient, unglamorous, blow-by-blow noting log and shargrol's steady coaching.

author: Nick O
summary: A solar installer and musician charts a multi-year progression from Culadasa's TMI concentration work through a destabilizing first Satipatthana retreat (mistaking A&P for Equanimity), a toroid-shaped 'suffering door' cessation, and eventual centerless post-second-path territory guided by a joy-centered Thai Forest teacher.
interesting_sentence: "It did the thing. Two nights ago. The cosmic shutter close and open thing Such, such empty. This is going to take some getting used to." —Nick O
helpful_sentence: "Anger is usually a protective mechanism that is often overblown, but at the core there is usual a very true sense of injustice or wounding." —shargrol
who_should_read: TMI and vipassana practitioners curious how jhana-based concentration, retreat mishaps, and later a joy-centered teacher's counsel combine on the road through second and third path territory.

author: John L
summary: Cycles through jhana, self-inquiry, Roger-Thisdell-style solidity/centerpoint investigation, and Ken McLeod's five-elements practice while studying for the bar exam, debating Kenneth Folk's three-gears model and tallying flashes of centerlessness dozens of times per sit.
interesting_sentence: "Like if two sheaves of reeds were to stand leaning against one another. If you take away one of them, well..." —John L
helpful_sentence: "The point of knowing nanas is the same reason as elements -- so that you have a framework to make distinctions and objectifications that you wouldn't realize otherwise... basically like training wheels on a bike... but ultimately even dharma frameworks are just frameworks, not ultimate truth." —shargrol
who_should_read: Advanced post-stream-entry practitioners interested in phenomenological self-inquiry, jhana-insight correspondences, and comparative technique frameworks like three gears, five elements, and centerpoint work.

author: Mac Max
summary: A twenty-day fire-kasina retreat log tracing rapid A&P-to-cessation cycling, vivid nimitta visuals, an out-of-body kundalini episode, and a major emotional breakthrough around lifelong self-hatred and dissociation, framed against a decade of Goenka retreats and transformative psychedelic trips.
interesting_sentence: "I lifted up 2 meters over the bed where I was laying, and hung there as the energy felt as if it was going to kill me, got pretty scared but stayed with it." —Mac Max
helpful_sentence: "I don't believe that there are only variations on one progression, and so I don't believe that having a certain attainment means that you have mastered the path that any individual yogi will take." —Martin
who_should_read: Practitioners with a psychedelic-informed background curious how repeated kasina-retreat cessations interact with deep trauma processing and dissociation work.

author: Mr Pixel
summary: Chronicles a Portland Zen practitioner's push to quit nicotine, alcohol, and pot while cycling through dark night and equanimity across several short retreats, using a 30-minute mindfulness-check timer, noting, breath meditation, and metta, and ultimately being told by a psychiatrist he likely isn't bipolar after all.
interesting_sentence: "The psych professionals have all been wrong." —Mr Pixel
helpful_sentence: "AHA --- be very very wary of the idea of \"getting through the dark night FASTER\". That's the ego sneaking into the game. What you want to focus on is \"learning from the dark night BETTER\"." —shargrol
who_should_read: Meditators balancing addiction recovery and mental-health uncertainty (bipolar vs. dark night) who want a candid, consistency-focused practice journal.

author: George S
summary: George S recounts a decades-long arc of kundalini episodes, cessations, and charnel-ground/nondual practices culminating in a sudden realization that all experience is equally 'nibbana' once craving for it to be different drops away, sparking a heated forum debate over whether such insight can be taught as 'nothing to do.'
interesting_sentence: "THE ANSWER hit me like a ten tonne truck: no single experience – however deep or meaningful – is more or less privileged than any other." —George S
helpful_sentence: "There is flow without clinging, intimacy without clinging, emptiness without dispare or a sense of loss..." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about the 'nothing to do' vs effortful-path debate who want a detailed first-person account of a craving-relinquishment realization following years of kundalini and nondual work.

author: J W
summary: J W restarts a daily jhana-based practice, tracking J1-J4 transitions, 'skiptracing' into strobing high-EQ states and odd hypnagogic dream-fragments, while managing insomnia medication and repeated forum pushback to describe raw sensation instead of Pali jargon.
interesting_sentence: "Then, I went into hyperspeed, and it felt like for a few seconds, like maybe 3 or 4 seconds, i was just Matrix uploading information into my brain, like it was almost comical what this felt like." —J W
helpful_sentence: "Do not bog your self down with these uncertainties and insecurities and instead NOTE these uncertainties and these insecurities and note their feeling tone and so on and on." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Jhana-focused practitioners wanting to track fine-grained J3/J4 transitions and 'nada sound' phenomena, and who want a candid example of forum feedback steering someone away from jargon-heavy self-diagnosis.

author: J W
summary: A first-time poster tracks kundalini-like facial tingling and A&P/Dark Night cycling, completes a self-directed 3-day home retreat, and works through whether his childhood and recent 'ego death' experiences constitute Stream Entry.
interesting_sentence: "DN R Us, should be the name of this operation. Hang in there, amigo, it will get worse soon." —Tim Farrington
helpful_sentence: "The key is to just stay with whatever comes up. If i'm sleepy I know I'm sleepy, If i'm happy I know I'm happy and so on type deal." —Dustin
who_should_read: Newer practitioners curious what an early, energetically intense DhO practice log looks like, including a first home retreat and grappling with whether powerful experiences equal Stream Entry.

author: Milo
summary: Milo systematically maps jhanas one through eight with nimitta and piti/sukkha detail, investigates a possible cessation/blackout event involving his dog, and runs granular vipassana experiments deconstructing touch and sound into oscillating mental-object 'ping-ponging.'
interesting_sentence: "Suddenly I get the sense that my hand has blinked out of existance for a moment or two, and this is strange because I am not in any kind of state." —Milo
helpful_sentence: "Cessation is the complete and utter cessation of experience. There is nothing 'there'. Literally nothing. at. all." —Chris M
who_should_read: Concentration-focused practitioners wanting granular, jhana-by-jhana phenomenology and a worked example of investigating whether an unusual blackout event was a genuine cessation.

author: Mumukshu mindfully musing
summary: A Goenka-tradition practitioner logs short daily Anapana-Vipassana-Metta sessions used to manage work stress, panic attacks, and an abandonment-trauma 'knot' felt in the chest and gut.
interesting_sentence: "It feels like a slow hum of twisted trauma, very intangible yet very real which everytime I would pass through would leave me with a feeling of sinking vulnerability." —Mumukshu mindfully musing
helpful_sentence: "dhyan is not about finding sense in sensations but just observing them... if there is no sensation per se to observe then one needs to just take note of the fact that at this stage my mind is too scattered and stressed to be able to grasp these subtle sensations." —Mumukshu mindfully musing
who_should_read: Practitioners managing anxiety and trauma through daily Goenka-style Anapana/Vipassana/Metta who want a relatable, beginner-friendly account of meditation as an emotional-regulation tool.

author: J W
summary: Reports steady practice of 1-5 hours a day (mixing tantric visualizations with Wim Hof breathing), voices discomfort with false attainment claims and "Master Posers," and departs for a month-long solo self-retreat while remaining deliberately agnostic about whether he is 1st or 2nd path.
interesting_sentence: "I think the more relevant question to my practice right now is “Was that emptiness?”" —J W
helpful_sentence: "It’s funny how so often we become part of the problem that we work against." —J W
who_should_read: Practitioners wrestling with how to talk honestly about ambiguous path attainments without slipping into hype or false claims.

author: Mind over easy
summary: Chronicles a multi-week return to daily practice with detailed jhana-by-jhana and nana-by-nana sit reports, culminating in a spontaneous "golden palace" vision the author interprets as a fruition marking 2nd path, framed throughout by a fetters-model discussion of craving and radical surrender.
interesting_sentence: "I see what I for some reason know as ‘the golden palace’. It is extremely shocking, and it pulses and vanishes, and I absolutely come to my senses, like fiercely waking up from a dream." —Mind over easy
helpful_sentence: "Yeah one way to look at the path is a gradual extension of the insight of no controller/doer... Finally the insight extends to the search itself, at which point it collapses under its own weight and we end up simply being here with whatever is going on, because where else could we be!" —George S
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry practitioners working toward 2nd path who want a granular, jhana-by-jhana account of nana cycling and fruition phenomenology.

author: J J
summary: Describes attaining apparently effortless jhana access through a self-devised "24/7 existential" vigilance practice, and finds that solving koans dissolves anxious tension in a way that willpower-driven striving/noting practice does not.
interesting_sentence: "If someone born in a dissolving aeon is born ignorant and later attains awakening, does that mean someone who's born in an aeon of expansion is born enlightened only to attain ignorance?" —J J
helpful_sentence: "Any AF or insight practice should reduce the willpower costs when you are doing things because there is less draining from preferences." —Richard Zen
who_should_read: Meditators curious about blending Actual Freedom-style effortless practice with koan work and informal 24/7 mindfulness.

author: Jeyan "Jey" Burns-Oorjitham
summary: Daily terse entries tracking sits combining TMI stage-9 samatha-without-object, nimitta/luminous-jhana practice, and repeated spontaneous gaps/phala, alongside an off-cushion emphasis on forgiveness and surrender.
interesting_sentence: "Mind rides ñanas like surfers ride waves" —Jeyan "Jey" Burns-Oorjitham
helpful_sentence: "Leaning back a bit in sitting posture putting more weight on hips and less weight on knees has led to Less numbness Less stiffness or soreness Clearer, more vivid sitting" —Jeyan "Jey" Burns-Oorjitham
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about combining TMI-style samatha-without-object with nimitta/jhana work and forgiveness-focused daily-life practice.

author: John L
summary: Cycles repeatedly through the insight stages, reporting several cessations and a growing trust in surrender over control.
interesting_sentence: "...reality stopped and restarted on October 17, 2023." —John L
helpful_sentence: "It's really about knowing the moment as it is, however it is." —Kenneth Folk
who_should_read: Practitioners cycling post-stream-entry who want a candid account of surrender-based practice.

author: Macky Ben-Jonah
summary: A beginner's short daily 20-30 minute noting sits (using Shargrol's structured noting plus TMI's six preparations) mostly report distraction and dullness with little notable phenomena, while the yogi asks the board for consistency and technique advice.
interesting_sentence: "You realize that's all fabrication, right?" —Chris M
helpful_sentence: "the best way to gain more momentum is simply more time on the cushion" —Danny S
who_should_read: New meditators who want a relatable, unpolished account of struggling with distraction and consistency before any insight-stage phenomena appear.

author: SeTyR ZeN
summary: Sits 30 minutes watching the breath every other day, practices continuous vipassana/noting through the day, and uses Green Tara visualization metta when angry, but the log is dominated by shared Robina Courtin talks, translated Tibetan verse, and other tangential links rather than detailed practice narration.
interesting_sentence: "i do a specific metta accompagnied with visualization : \"Green Tara\" , it works for me" —SeTyR ZeN
helpful_sentence: "i sit still for 30 mins every other day watching the breath only, longer or more often for brief burst periods when i 'm in a certain restless state period (generaly around new moon and full moon)" —SeTyR ZeN
who_should_read: Eclectic Mahayana-leaning practitioners who want teacher/talk recommendations and a light breath-plus-metta routine rather than a phenomenologically detailed log.

author: Jason
summary: A beginner TMI practitioner logs early struggles with dullness, drowsiness, and doubt while working through Stage 1-2 concentration practice following a difficult Goenka retreat.
interesting_sentence: "It's like I'm perpetually trying to find the right angle or perspective from which to observe the breath." —Jason
helpful_sentence: "I recognized this thought fairly quickly and gently let it go." —Jason
who_should_read: Beginner TMI practitioners working through Stage 1-2 concentration who want a candid account of drowsiness and doubt struggles.

author: J W
summary: Chronicles years of practice blending nondual awareness, breath work, and songwriting, tracking self-diagnosed insomnia-triggered insight cycles, a Seroquel prescription for sleep, and a possible fruition event during a 10-day solo retreat in Koyasan.
interesting_sentence: "i have discovered bodhicitta." —J W
helpful_sentence: "If I'm writing music and getting overwhelmed, I go sit for 20 minutes, or vice versa, and then come back to the original activity." —J W
who_should_read: Practitioners integrating nondual awareness practice with creative work who want a candid, meandering account of insomnia, bipolar spectrum experience, and mapping mood cycles to insight stages.

author: A B
summary: A visual artist doing intensive daily Shinzen Young-style noting practice, often 10+ hours a day, works through resurfacing incest memories and severe chronic neuropathy, reporting expanding windows of no-self and a five-minute experience of 'blackest black nothingness.'
interesting_sentence: "This morning I sat down to meditate and there was about a five-minute window of just the blackest black nothingness that is everything." —A B
helpful_sentence: "In order to have a normal life, you are going to have to go for an extraordinary one." —A B
who_should_read: Trauma survivors and intensive noting practitioners seeking a raw account of using heavy practice volume to process incest trauma alongside chronic illness.

author: dullmeditator
summary: A frustrated, multi-year practitioner struggles with drowsiness and a compulsive 'search for the next thing to note' while trying to adopt fast, one-per-second noting, and gets blunt community pushback about attempting an unsupervised 9-day retreat.
interesting_sentence: "Sorry to be very blunt about this, but I do not think you should go on retreat." —shargrol
helpful_sentence: "It is totally and completely fine to get lost 1000 times during a sit, as long as you come back 1001 times." —shargrol
who_should_read: Intermediate meditators wrestling with noting speed, drowsiness, and retreat-readiness will benefit from the detailed troubleshooting exchange here.

author: Jason Snyder
summary: An infrequent, candid log cycling through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity via noting, Tolle-style acceptance, and self-inquiry ("where am I"), including a debated possible stream-entry discontinuity that the author later doubts.
interesting_sentence: "It was just like reality was something, then something else discontinuous, without anything in between." —Jason Snyder
helpful_sentence: "What you note is what you aren't stuck to. What you don't note is what you are stuck to." —Richard Zen
who_should_read: Practitioners uncertain whether they've had stream entry who want a candid account of cycling through A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity with self-inquiry and noting.

author: Jo Jo
summary: A decades-spanning log of body-centered practice through Soto and Rinzai lineages, describing intense kundalini-style shaking, chronic tension release, and two historical A&P openings, culminating in a retreat experience of deep hip/pelvis release and quietude.
interesting_sentence: "I sat there like Queen of Kings in total rest and quietude." —Jo Jo
helpful_sentence: "Try to passively yet precisely observe your feelings while you allow your body to shake and jerk as much as it likes." —Dada Kind
who_should_read: Practitioners dealing with strong kundalini-style shaking and chronic body tension who want a body-centered, non-technical account of working through it.

author: Maisam Abbas
summary: A near-daily concentration-focused log of breath counting and Mahasi-style noting during work breaks, tracking gradual progress into access concentration and vivid piti sensations ("static showers") while experimenting with diet and fasting.
interesting_sentence: "It felt like I was holding a big fuzzy ball of static in both my hands and I couldn't feel my hands at all, just the big ball of static." —Maisam Abbas
helpful_sentence: "Unless you actually need to sleep, drowsiness/torpor is usually because you are trying too hard ... When this happens, try to relax and stop pushing the mind, let it rest naturally." —George S
who_should_read: Concentration-focused practitioners wanting a detailed account of building access concentration and jhanic piti/bliss through breath work and light noting.

log: My Practice Log: Meditation, Magick, & Miscellaneous
author: Ryan Jaidsedha Burton
summary: A samatha-heavy log combining Dhammakaya crystal-sphere visualization and Dzogchen awareness-of-awareness practice, framed through B. Alan Wallace's attentional stages, with reflections on a 90-day Thailand retreat and the death of the author's father.
interesting_sentence: "I text messaged a friend yesterday saying that a year ago today was my fathers funeral. When I wrote this text I had actually said that today one year ago was my funeral." —Ryan Jaidsedha Burton
helpful_sentence: "If you just observe things in that way ... insight into the 3C is basically unavoidable." —M N
who_should_read: Samatha-oriented practitioners curious how Dhammakaya visualization and Dzogchen awareness practices map onto Alan Wallace's attentional stages.

log: Jordi's Blog - Going back to meditation
author: Jordi
summary: Returning to meditation after 18 months away, Jordi fights gaming/phone addiction and apathy with a locked time-safe device and nature walks, then rebuilds a breath-and-impermanence sitting practice that produces nimitta, itching piti, and a sense of unified stillness.
interesting_sentence: "The sensation is a desire to just disappear into nothingness." —Jordi
helpful_sentence: "You can even take the thought \"there is something wrong with me\" and just sit with that and really get a sense for what it feels like." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners struggling with gaming/phone addiction and low motivation who want a candid account of rebuilding a meditation habit through structured digital detox.

log: Kailin season 2, "dunno what I'm doing, but stuff seems to be happening"
author: Kailin T
summary: A technically dense, months-long log cycling through jhana concentration, self-inquiry ('who is seeing?'), kasina work, and Shinzen Young's noting techniques, tracking oscillations between Equanimity and Dark Night around a tentative Stream Entry claim from late 2024.
interesting_sentence: "So, I don't know what I'm doing, but stuff seems to be happening." —Kailin T
helpful_sentence: "When we are in EQ, everything feels fine and not very meditate-y, so we tend to start making mundane life plans and forget that we're still meditating." —shargrol
who_should_read: Serious pragmatic-dharma practitioners wanting granular, technique-level detail on jhana, self-inquiry, and Shinzen-style noting while navigating post-SE Equanimity/Dark Night cycling.

author: Not two
summary: Over roughly a year, 'Not two' cycles through samatha/jhana with a distinctive sense-consciousness-to-chakra mapping, Wim Hof breathing, and fire kasina, culminating in a cessation-like 'blip' triggered by directly embracing subconscious fear and a subsequent centerless, no-witness state.
interesting_sentence: "I gave the fear what it wanted - huge searing shocks, thick purifying blasts of spirit." —Not two
helpful_sentence: "You can sometimes get a glimpse of what's up there, but you have to walk the path - traverse it all. There are no helicopters." —Chris M
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners interested in mapping jhana/samatha states onto chakra-like sense-consciousness models and in a detailed first-person account of a cessation event triggered by directly embracing core fear.

author: Andrest T
summary: Daily 45-minute noting sits cycling between 4th Jhana equanimity, third-eye 'fruitions,' and recurring Dark Night material, including a temporary days-long centerless opening after an ayahuasca retreat that repeatedly collapses back into identity-driven suffering.
interesting_sentence: "Not sure if there is such a thing as a temporary opening of the 4th Path but the system not being stable enough to land completely on it, so you only get a glimpse before you are pointed back to there being more work to do?" —Andrest T
helpful_sentence: "It's good to allow the diffusion of attention through the dark night. Allow it to be less speedy." —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Post-second-path practitioners cycling between Dark Night relapse and Equanimity who want a candid, technique-focused account of using noting and surrender to work through identity-deconstruction material.

log: Jose's log, am I in the right path?
author: Jose C
summary: A beginner describes a breakthrough during a 10-day Goenka retreat, then a mixed daily practice of breath focus, body scanning, and noting that has stalled amid morning sloth, prompting doubt about whether to switch from MCTB-style practice to TMI.
interesting_sentence: "I saw both negative (anxiety) and positive (happiness-realization) in my body at the same time, with some colours, I was crying as it was very relieving." —Jose C
helpful_sentence: "One thing I'll add... When there is confusion and a desire to change approaches, it's worth looking more deeply at why there is confusion and why we desire a change." —shargrol
who_should_read: Beginner meditators questioning which system to commit to after an initial breakthrough experience on a Goenka retreat.

author: Kailin T
summary: A months-long, highly detailed jhana/samadhi-focused log spanning a Rob Burbea-style retreat, fire kasina visuals, tonglen and emotion work, and recovery from a high-control spiritual group, with several fruition-like cessation experiences noted but left unconfirmed.
interesting_sentence: "Holy fuck. I don't know what this has turned into. I thought I was meditating." —Kailin T
helpful_sentence: "Practise running up and down and in and out of jhanas, in different positions and locations and mindstates, over and over, until you can do a jhana at the drop of a breath." —Kailin T
who_should_read: Concentration-focused practitioners wanting a granular, months-long fire kasina/jhana log that also honestly tracks emotional processing and recovery from a high-control spiritual group.

author: Marek Mark
summary: After crossing early insight stages via anapanasati three years ago and stopping due to severe panic attacks, the author resumes practice and gets direct technique troubleshooting on noting versus anapanasati from Daniel Ingram.
interesting_sentence: "I ran from my house few times, because the fear was so strong and i wanted to be with people in the street." —Marek Mark
helpful_sentence: "Two suggestions: 1) give much more attention to the objects: if the noting is making it hard to see them, then give the object more of the total mind-time 2) avoid hypnotic or mantra-like noting: only note when you perceived the object, and not if you didn't." —Daniel M Ingram
who_should_read: Practitioners who stalled after a fear-driven dark night and want direct, practical technique advice on switching between anapanasati and noting.

author: Evangeline A K McDowell
summary: Two very short, third-person-narrated entries describe brief vipassana-then-moon-gazing shamatha sessions and a wordless staring exercise with a cat framed as a telepathy/magick experiment.
interesting_sentence: "The tiniest pixel of reality is modified to a purpurish color in the bottom of the left of his view." —Evangeline A K McDowell
helpful_sentence: "10 minutes of vipassa, just to get the ball rolling, untense from the day." —Evangeline A K McDowell
who_should_read: Readers curious about a very short, idiosyncratic log blending basic shamatha/vipassana with esoteric 'magick' framing.

author: Joshua Michael Foxworth
summary: A beginner establishes a daily breath-counting sit habit, gradually increasing duration from 10 to 20+ minutes while working through distraction, leg numbness, and a persistent throat/chest energy sensation.
interesting_sentence: "I had a strange experience one day where it felt like something snapped while I was observing the breath and then I had a feely of waves of something going over me." —Joshua Michael Foxworth
helpful_sentence: "A lot of people have gotten far with daily life practice (not on the cushion)." —Alex
who_should_read: Complete beginners building a first sitting habit who want a candid week-by-week account of overcoming distraction and physical discomfort.

author: Lars
summary: An experienced kasina/jhana practitioner logs vivid visual phenomena across the jhanic arc while investigating dependent origination and successfully tapering a 30-year cannabis habit.
interesting_sentence: "It's like seeing a spark, freaking out and trying to get rid of it by throwing gasoline on it, causing a fire." —Lars
helpful_sentence: "Spotting and renouncing that initial impulse is quite a big milestone, although not specifically on the maps, that I am aware of." —Not two
who_should_read: Kasina/jhana practitioners interested in vivid visual phenomenology, dependent-origination investigation, and honest accounts of quitting long-term cannabis use during practice.

author: Marzanne Vorster
summary: A newcomer to noting practice logs detailed sensation-by-sensation observations, tracing self-referential "me-mechanism" thoughts and past psychedelic-triggered insight cycles while navigating core beliefs of unworthiness.
interesting_sentence: "Every single sensation, whether heard or felt is followed by an image, then a thought or knowing that it's eathier 'mine/me' or 'not mine/me'." —Marzanne Vorster
helpful_sentence: "If you sit with the urge to swallow without actually swallowing, it feels uncomfortable at first but you can separate the involuntary reflex from the voluntary action." —George S
who_should_read: New noting practitioners curious how psychedelic experiences can map onto insight stages and want a granular, emotionally honest account of early sits.

author: Travis McKinstry
summary: A returning noting practitioner cycles rapidly through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity, eventually declaring stream entry after a spontaneous walking-to-the-car fruition-like event, then completes a month-long Mahasi retreat.
interesting_sentence: "I was overcome with a variety of sensations; tremendous bliss, a huge sense of relief like I've never known, incredible peace and a great sense of insight." —Travis McKinstry
helpful_sentence: "That's Dark Night all right. That resistance and anxiety is suffering right there so just note it and try to look directly at it." —J C
who_should_read: Practitioners questioning whether they've hit stream entry who want a candid, uncertain, real-time account of self-diagnosis and community feedback.

author: Lars
summary: Lars documents a years-long post-A&P journey through repeated Dark Night/Equanimity cycles, marked by intense kundalini-like body-vibration syncing, an evolving fire-kasina-like visual practice triggered by a retinal eye injury, deep jhana exploration up to 7th/8th, a mushroom trip, and navigating progressive vision loss from sarcoidosis while treating his retail job as meditation practice.
interesting_sentence: "Now isn't a thing any more than the past and future are. Silly meditator, go sit some more." —Lars
helpful_sentence: "You can be in a state of misery or a state of ease and the mind >knows< this. What is the nature of this knowing?" —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in fire-kasina/jhana visual phenomena and how serious physical illness intersects with practice will find these detailed, honest field notes valuable.

author: Mathias
key_words: Mahasi retreat with Sayadaw U Vivekananda
summary: Mathias, who attained first path on a Goenka retreat and completed a second insight cycle via a solo home retreat, chronicles daily 90-minute sits mixing samatha, vipassana, and 'do nothing' practice while wrestling with subtle skeptical doubt about method and persistent neck/shoulder pain ahead of a planned two-week Mahasi retreat.
interesting_sentence: "I had some thoughts about turning crazy, like the sense of self would be stuck or something." —Mathias
helpful_sentence: "It's good to remember that it Does Not Matter weather we see the 3C's of the ‘I have a dilemma/uncertainty’ or the Doubt thought/sensations, as direct Noting/Noticing of ANY arising will lead to Insight." —Che Guebuddha
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry meditators questioning whether analytically 'hacking' technique undermines simple noting will relate to Mathias's doubt while cycling through a second insight round.

log: Journey to Stream Entry - Meditation Log
author: Charlie Wigton
summary: In his very first post, newcomer Charlie describes deliberately eating fast food to observe its effect on his mind during meditation, noting immediate mental fog and heaviness, and states his intent to use post-meal body-awareness to motivate healthier eating.
interesting_sentence: "I intentionally decided to eat some leftover food that my family had from a burger place to see how it would effect my body." —Charlie Wigton
helpful_sentence: "you can also try to just listen for 15 minutes, with open or closed eyes." —Misha -
who_should_read: Absolute beginners curious about simple post-meal mindfulness experiments will recognize this first tentative log entry.
