author: Monti (female)
summary: A grieving first-time poster describes hearing her deceased father clearly speak her name during a guided meditation featuring birdsong, several months after beginning to meditate following his death.
interesting_sentence: "I then heard my father voice as if standing right in front of me, he said 'hello Montisue' my given name." —Monti (female)
helpful_sentence: "From the perspective of meditation maps, this article by Daniel may help explain what stage you might have experienced." —Noah D
who_should_read: Grieving meditators curious whether vivid sensory experiences of a deceased loved one during meditation are a recognized phenomenon will find this account and the moderator's response useful.

log: Could you review this homebrew scanning method?
author: Pål
summary: Describes a self-devised, affirmation-paced full-body breath technique inspired by Thanissaro Bhikkhu and the Anapanasati/Vitakkasanthana suttas, reporting spontaneous spasms and arm movements while seeking feedback on whether it aligns with traditional jhana instructions.
interesting_sentence: "Pretty often this leads to my arms raising upwards quickly, sometimes to shoulder height, sometimes even above my head, and if I let the hands meet above head then the clap becomes really hard, it almost hurts sometimes." —Pål
helpful_sentence: "That awareness that suddenly appears is meditation itself." —Not Tao
who_should_read: Sutta-focused meditators experimenting with idiosyncratic breath-based techniques who want peer feedback on aligning practice with traditional jhana instructions.

log: Julie's practice log and place to ask questions
author: Julie V
summary: Working toward third path over several years, Julie logs near-daily Mahasi-style sits anchored on the rising and falling of the abdomen, blends noting with metta, wrestles with dark-night misery and remorse over past wrongdoing, and searches unsuccessfully for a trustworthy teacher in Thailand.
interesting_sentence: "After fear, comes misery. I'm actually quite miserable today when I'm by myself ... so that was the summary of the day." —Julie V
helpful_sentence: "It's easier if you let those perceptions arise and pass away and also to have beliefs challenged." —Richard Zen
who_should_read: Practitioners navigating dark-night territory while juggling a demanding job and searching for a trustworthy teacher abroad will find her candid day-to-day noting log relatable.

author: Logan G
key_words: six realms (Wake Up to Your Life)
summary: Extensive months-long log cycling repeatedly through the dukkha ñāṇas via metta/compassion/joy/equanimity radiation, elements and six-realms work, Gendlin's focusing technique on emotional knots, and self-inquiry, reporting dozens of cessations across the suffering, no-self, and impermanence doors alongside deepening insight into anatta and the constructed nature of self.
interesting_sentence: "Its like I've been the snake trying to eat its own tail this whole time, and now I've actually latched onto it and I have had the terrible realization that I'm actually devouring myself." —Logan G
helpful_sentence: "It might be interesting to ask - who is it that suffers, really?" —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners deep into post-stream-entry territory wanting a granular model of pairing emotional/elements work with formal cessation-door investigation.

author: Matt Jon Rousseau
summary: A newer Mahasi-noting practitioner logs building up to 2+ hour daily sits, reporting persistent daydreaming, occasional glimpses of mind-and-body/no-self territory, unsettling 'creepy' sensations, and a burnout dip before considering a retreat at IMS.
interesting_sentence: "Why am I getting creepy feelings,during meditation and after?. I feel kind of like I am entering a room I shouldn't be in." —Matt Jon Rousseau
helpful_sentence: "The trick is to come up with \"categories of thought\" that you can note." —shargrol
who_should_read: New Mahasi-style noting practitioners wanting an honest, warts-and-all account of daily practice building from scratch amid daydreaming and early resistance.

author: Logan G
summary: Post-stream-entry log tracking jhana 1-7 laddering, repeated dukkha-nana cycles, and several no-self and impermanence cessations while investigating the spatial construction of a separate self.
interesting_sentence: "At some point it had the sense that it just a failed to arise at all for a moment, then had a big charge of energy and a sense of a space without space somehow." —Logan G
helpful_sentence: "It has something to do with recognizing that the mind's craving for experience is ... well, simply the mind's craving for experience." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners deep in post-stream-entry practice who want a granular account of jhana-nana interplay and spatial/no-self investigation techniques.

author: junglist
summary: A year-plus daily home-practice log moving through Mahasi-style noting, taikiken standing meditation and fire kasina, with recurring vibrations, a near-miss cessation, and shargrol confirming the author has settled solidly into Equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "Bloomin’ ’Enry, that’s a whole load of spiritual whackoness, opening chakras and microcosmic orbits? Wonder if I could do something more useful with my life." —junglist
helpful_sentence: "Concentration just means you are able to note what is happening, you can describe what is occuring. But often times we say there is a lack of concentration when all that is occuring is dullness, confusion, irritation, boredom, discomfort, distraction, lack of interest, daydreams, etc." —shargrol
who_should_read: Home practitioners balancing family and work who want a granular, teacher-annotated account of noting practice progressing from A&P into Equanimity.

author: Matt W
summary: A self-taught beginner with ADHD experiments with breath-counting concentration across several postures, reporting brief flashes of immersion and about ten cumulative seconds of access concentration.
interesting_sentence: "Several times I told myself that I would try focusing on metta, but found myself immediately averse and confused in a way that caused the thought that I probably don't want myself to be happy (or want myself to want myself to be happy, or something.)" —Matt W
helpful_sentence: "Visualize my sensations as objects, rather than as caused by objects." —Matt W
who_should_read: Beginner meditators with ADHD looking for a candid, unpolished account of the first days of concentration practice.

log: My location on the maps i.e. Investigating my reality
author: Peter Plan
summary: A 27-year-old ACEM/breath-concentration practitioner works through soft third-jhana states and an A&P-versus-dark-night dilemma while balancing an electrical engineering diploma and starting psychiatric therapy.
interesting_sentence: "In another night, I noticed a soft feeling of fear. It got stronger and I had visions of a medieval ship in a brutal storm on the atlantic." —Peter Plan
helpful_sentence: "I noticed how every moment I have a sensation of me realizing something I did wrong was past me. So that I couldn't possibly be angry about my(present)self in any moment." —Peter Plan
who_should_read: Practitioners juggling major life commitments who want an honest account of trying to distinguish A&P energetics from dark night territory using jhana and ACEM concentration.

author: Lanakila Pukana La Iesu
summary: A Goenka-tradition vipassana practitioner logs over a year of near-daily sitting through a friend's family bereavement, sudden housing loss, and recurring panic attacks, reporting frequent bhanga nana and a growing conviction of stream entry with a possible second path.
interesting_sentence: "I can see why the pull back into the field of suffering is personified as Mara; at times it is hard for me to imagine having to be so diligent to resist something which isn't a personally involved consciousness." —Lanakila Pukana La Iesu
helpful_sentence: "noting is to describe your experience moment by moment, and it really helps in getting the paths to note as quickly as possible." —J C
who_should_read: Householders juggling demanding jobs and personal crises who want to see how a steady Goenka vipassana practice can sustain emotional stability through loss and upheaval.

author: Logan G
summary: Logan logs near-daily hour-to-90-minute sits cycling through jhanas, dukkha nanas, and numerous cessations while investigating a felt 'point behind the eyes,' culminating in a detailed account of what he believes is second path.
interesting_sentence: "Holy shit! It was like I'd been sitting in the center of a dark stadium trying to coax a fire to life and suddenly someone turned on all of the floodlights." —Logan G
helpful_sentence: "I've noticed that when I have a cessation, the first thing that comes back is the raw sensation, which is basically weightless, and then the aversion to it returns very quickly after (maybe 1/4 of a second or so after), which is where the weight is." —Logan G
who_should_read: Jhana-capable practitioners deep in post-stream-entry practice who want granular phenomenological detail on investigating the sense of self located behind the eyes.

log: Max's dharma rambles and practice log
author: Maxwell Pollack
summary: About a year into practice, Max journals Shinzen-style purification and soft-jhana techniques, an overzealous 'trigger practice' phase that crashed into anxiety and depression, and extended philosophical debates on emptiness and self-improvement with shargrol.
interesting_sentence: "Claiming an attainment of 'had a great time playing pickleball today.'" —Maxwell Pollack
helpful_sentence: "Emptiness is more like 'vivid freedom' than nothingness or meaninglessness." —shargrol
who_should_read: Newer practitioners drawn to Shinzen-style techniques and philosophical exploration who want a cautionary account of overdoing intense 'trigger practice.'

author: Elijah Smith
summary: Elijah restarts his log after a psychedelic-triggered dark night, describing noting-aloud practice, two brief A&P crossings and one taste of equanimity, while increasingly stuck in dark-night fear and vivid somatic symptoms like an 'ants on skin' desire-for-deliverance sensation.
interesting_sentence: "My last session was interesting because I had a very strong sensation of ants crawling on my skin after noting about thirty minutes... Apparently this is a symptom of desire for deliverance." —Elijah Smith
helpful_sentence: "When a person seeks relief from stress (dukkha) in the mere practice of meditation itself, they are cutting themselves short of attaining the goal of Dhamma practice." —Ian And
who_should_read: Intellectually skeptical practitioners battling persistent dark-night anxiety who want validation that doubt and slow progress are normal, plus concrete noting-technique tips.

author: Kelly Gordon Weeks
summary: A parent of two cycles repeatedly through dark night 'black hole' phenomenology and long stretches of equanimity via daily shamatha jhanas and dietyless compassion visualization, without a cessation in over a year and a half.
interesting_sentence: "She said it was like \"sleeping with a corpse\"." —Kaloyan Stefanov
helpful_sentence: "More cessations might not necessarily do it for you at this point. It can become just another form of craving." —George S
who_should_read: Busy parents cycling through dark night and equanimity without cessations who want a candid, doubt-filled account of higher-path uncertainty.

author: Logan G
key_words: Wake Up to Your Life realms
summary: Blends concentration jhanas with TWIM-style radiating metta/compassion/joy/equanimity and the 6R method to move through a depressive dark night into repeated cessations and what he tentatively identifies as stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "It felt like a big eye blinked, but the eye was made of the space in which consiousness existed." —Logan G
helpful_sentence: "You could almost say that self and craving/aversion and suffering are just different ways of describing the same thing!" —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners combining concentration jhanas with TWIM-style metta and insight who want a granular account of moving from a depressive dark night into stream entry.

log: Meditation Log | Zen Poetry, Magick, Projects
author: Zen Poetry
summary: Documents kasina and jhana concentration practice (earth/fire kasina, Nimitta signs) alongside a switch to Zazen, lucid-dreaming exploration, and a three-month psychiatric hospitalization during which the author continued informal 'Magick' practice.
interesting_sentence: "I met a person who reached a level of Nothingness (open eyes) so high the Material World 'Froze' for her." —Zen Poetry
helpful_sentence: "When excited about developing stronger concentration, let it develop naturally. Blocking the excitement because 'it's not part of the meditation object' will diminish attention." —Zen Poetry
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in kasina/jhana concentration techniques and syncretic 'magick' practice who want a candid account blending meditation with a psychiatric hospitalization experience.

log: My recent practice and life updates
author: Adam
summary: Reports steady progress with noting-based bare awareness and occasional spontaneous joy while deliberately pushing his comfort zone socially after a period of exaggerated dark-night isolation.
interesting_sentence: "...more just general proliferating nonsense such as wondering about what that squirrel over there is thinking." —Adam
helpful_sentence: "Noting 'thinking' whenever I got lost definitely has added stability to my practice, where previously I might have gotten stuck for minutes, hours, or even days or weeks in some narrative." —Adam
who_should_read: Practitioners recovering from an intense dark-night phase who want a candid update on balancing solitary noting practice with re-engaging socially.

author: Kemono Z
summary: A computer programmer's detailed TMI-based noting and Qi Gong practice log punctuated by intense IFS parts-work sessions that surface childhood trauma, crying catharsis, kundalini-like vibrations, and heart palpitations following an earlier Goenka retreat.
interesting_sentence: "Dust is settling down, but two years ago I would not be this calm, or calm at all… nor believe most of this." —Kemono Z
helpful_sentence: "Try to keep your practice as simple as possible - just keep returning to the breath and don't pay much attention to all the other stuff." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners integrating IFS/parts work with formal noting practice who want a candid account of emotional purging and kundalini-like phenomena following a Goenka retreat.

author: Lorenzo G
summary: A brief single-session log describing 15 minutes of candle-gazing concentration practice that approached but didn't fully lock into access concentration, followed by attention to pleasant bodily sensations.
interesting_sentence: "As it started to wane, I focused on the pleasant sensations in my body, and practiced watching and building those." —Lorenzo G
helpful_sentence: "This type of focus has a big physical aspect to it, and the eyes are open, so it makes it easier to see when the mind has wandered." —Lorenzo G
who_should_read: Meditators curious about basic candle-gazing concentration technique looking for a short, unembellished session report.

log: Nathan's Practice Log: Live from the Pacific Crest Trail
author: Nathan Frank
summary: A hiker preparing to begin a five-month Pacific Crest Trail thru-hike commits to three days of Mahasi Sayadaw-style abdominal rising-and-falling noting practice as his baseline technique for the trip.
interesting_sentence: "Feelings aren't facts. Keep practicing regardless of them, good or bad, and more will be revealed." —Nathan Frank
helpful_sentence: "It was recently suggested to me that since uncertainty is a hindrance to concentration, to focus on and start from a position of what I am certain of." —Nathan Frank
who_should_read: Meditators curious how someone integrates formal noting practice into a long-distance wilderness thru-hike would enjoy this early-stage log.

author: Kevin C
summary: Kevin begins a Shinzen Young-style practice log combining Just Note Gone, Do Nothing, and Nurture Positive, encountering intense shame and disgust during noting and a rising line of energy up the center of his body during his mantra practice.
interesting_sentence: "A metaphor might be psycho-logical rumble strips on the highway of mindfulness." —Tarver
helpful_sentence: "Just make sure that you don't get too absorbed in concentration states doing Shikatanza (just sitting/do nothing)." —Richard Zen
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in Shinzen Young's Basic Mindfulness system who want concrete descriptions of noting variants like the 'Not' label and figure-ground reversals.

log: Lorenzo Log July 8 - Second time reaching Access Concentration!
author: Lorenzo G
summary: Lorenzo describes a short sit combining a candle-gazing warmup and a finger-touch concentration exercise, reporting about a minute of what he identifies as access concentration for only the second time.
interesting_sentence: "it felt like my energy flowed out of my eyeballs into the object." —Lorenzo G
helpful_sentence: "I eventually was tuned in to the sensation so well that there was a noticable shift in my focus, and it felt like the object was being drawn towards me in terms of effort." —Lorenzo G
who_should_read: Beginners curious what the first noticeable shift into access concentration feels like using a simple tactile focus object.

log: Meditation advise for a stuck fellow.
author: Peter de Waal
summary: Peter de Waal chronicles a year-plus Mahasi-noting practice arc, from an initial piti-driven 'string of beads' A&P event through dark-night fading and fear, a 5-day vipassana retreat marked by fear and physical pain, and later equanimity-like sits with spontaneous unity and non-duality experiences.
interesting_sentence: "It’s not a chair! It’s seeing the form, the color, feeling cold, feeling texture, feeling the hardness. The mental concept chair is just a cheap knockoff!" —Peter de Waal
helpful_sentence: "So you can see that clear seeing (vipassina) and tranquility (samatha) are not two widely different things, but rather mutual supportive approaches to becoming intimately aware of the mind-body." —shargrol
who_should_read: Mahasi-style noting practitioners who want a granular, months-long account of moving through A&P into dark-night fear and pain and toward equanimity, including a residential retreat.

author: Nath Eris
summary: Nath Eris keeps a highly granular multi-month log of a teacher-assigned metta-plus-noting routine, alongside twice-weekly zen sangha sits, tracking piti, kriyas, and shifting mantra experiments, though reporting mostly recurring distraction and procrastination rather than clear insight-stage progress.
interesting_sentence: "I like to use the mental image of a statue that isn't going to get up anyway, against restlessness." —Nath Eris
helpful_sentence: "99.9% of meditation is relaxation and that means relaxation of our dependency on cerebral gymnastics." —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in fine-grained sense-door noting combined with metta, and in patient documentation of piti, kriyas, and daily motivation struggles over several months.

author: Noah D
summary: A theoretical post reconciling Vipassana, Mahamudra, and 10-Fetter progress models into one map, plus a discussion of using imaginative/law-of-attraction techniques like Spiritual Mind Treatment to accelerate insight.
interesting_sentence: "The opening of the thought door is synonymous with Technical 4th Path, and produces a feeling of “doneness” because it naturally solves every investigation in the process of investigating itself." —Noah D
helpful_sentence: "Getting past self-defeating storylines via active methods is probably more effective than doing only choiceless awareness on these insecurities." —Noah D
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in comparative theorizing across Mahamudra, MCTB, and Fetter-model maps of the path.

author: Nick Lande
summary: Daily diary of metta and fire-kasina practice during a COVID quarantine in China, describing kasina afterimages, brief euphoric/vibratory episodes, and a rainbow-colored vision, while cautiously avoiding "dry insight" after a body-shattering vibration experience on a prior Goenka retreat.
interesting_sentence: "I've noticed this before and I proved it one more time today: jerking off before meditation isn't gonna help you in any way xD" —Nick Lande
helpful_sentence: "I know that both belong to the samadhi type and can help in acquiring concentration needed in the later stages of the insight practices." —Nick Lande
who_should_read: Beginner concentration practitioners curious about combining kasina and metta who want a candid, cautious account of unexpected energetic phenomena.

author: acordemos
summary: An 11-day home retreat log applying Shinzen Young's "See Hear Feel" spaciousness technique and "Do Nothing" practice, detailing repeated fruitions, dissolving sense of a controller across sight/sound/body, an out-of-body episode, and reflections on approaching 4th path.
interesting_sentence: "4th path is the moment of dropping that canoe." —acordemos '
helpful_sentence: "I would focus on the feeling bad - that's typically a judgement (thought) NOT the actual emotion (physical sensations)." —George S
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners near 3rd/4th path who want a granular, technique-based account of dissolving the sense of a controlling self using Shinzen Young's methods.

author: Stephan
summary: Daily jhana-based practice log alternating breath concentration, Mahasi-style noting, and do-nothing technique, tracking movement through jhanas 1-4, recognition of A&P sensations, and questions about fabricated versus unfabricated awareness near Equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "I tried to follow the pulses/zoomed out and zoomed in, but it behaves a bit like a cloud which is stuck in a mountain region." —Stephan
helpful_sentence: "To be honest, it makes no sense to me that your are both tackling A&P and EQ. If you were at EQ, you wouldn't being posting questions about A&P." —Pepe ·
who_should_read: Jhana-focused practitioners who want a granular day-by-day log of shifting between fabricated and unfabricated noting near the A&P/Equanimity boundary.

author: Lorenzo G
summary: Early-stage breath concentration log tracking restlessness and drowsiness before a spontaneous entry into access concentration, followed by depersonalization-like body sensations and vivid emotional/energetic phenomena.
interesting_sentence: "I was sensing my body, but didn't feel that I \"owned\" my body." —Lorenzo G
helpful_sentence: "of course, awareness DOES arise \"on its own\", but it can be inhibited, and there can be structure to it, thought-patterns." —Misha -
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate concentration practitioners curious about the transition into access concentration and early energetic/depersonalization phenomena.

author: Noah
summary: A sprawling, highly conceptual log where a self-described third-pather with bipolar disorder theorizes extensively about the insight maps, Actual Freedom, magick, and 'Mental Science' while doing daily-life noting instead of formal sitting and working with a teacher toward fourth path.
interesting_sentence: "I have a disability, which is that mind discovering something and exploring something." —Noah
helpful_sentence: "So concentration meditation is directly influencing and turning down/off this mechanism by watching it without indulging it in any way." —Not Tao
who_should_read: Intellectually-inclined practitioners who want to see how theorizing, mental health struggles, and fringe practices like Actual Freedom and magick get integrated with the standard insight maps.

author: Nick P
summary: Tracks detailed jhana and vipassana sits centered on visual third-eye energy phenomena and access concentration, moving from A&P through Equanimity toward a planned retreat, and later (after migrating to KFD) reports stream entry in 2013 followed by a decade of intermittent practice.
interesting_sentence: "I am sitting there meditating and everything seems very immediate, no big deal, I am just sitting there." —Nick P
helpful_sentence: "A good trick is: before pressing Publish, select all the text and cut it, then paste it back in and hit publish." —Martin
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in granular jhana/energy phenomenology who want a candid multi-year account spanning A&P, stream entry, and later cycling.

author: Adam
summary: Chronicles months of experimenting with Kenneth Folk's affect-grounding 'direct mode,' actualist PCE/no-focus practice, and wide visual-field awareness, reporting sustained pleasure states, energetic gravitational shifts, and a lasting baseline change, while explicitly denying any path attainment.
interesting_sentence: "It has started to seem like I am actually living in a different world." —Adam
helpful_sentence: "The more you try, the more affect you generate; so it's helpful to match the effort you exert with the effort that is needed to keep your attention working in the right way." —End in Sight
who_should_read: Actualism/direct-mode practitioners curious about grounding affect and PCE work, and anyone wanting a warts-and-all account of technique-hopping and self-doubt.

author: ham
summary: A granular, sit-by-sit log using Shinzen Young-style noting and 'do nothing' technique that tracks cycles of intense physical pain (notably a multi-day shoulder/neck episode), dullness, restlessness, and long stretches of equanimous 'very ok' acceptance.
interesting_sentence: "it felt like a giant was trying to give me a shoulder massage (with those giant hands!), but the giant didn't take into account that I was just a human -- so it hurt 'for real.'" —ham
helpful_sentence: "if you see its arising, you should be able to trace the cause; if you see its end, how it ends lies the insight." —Yilun Ong
who_should_read: Meditators wanting an honest, detailed sit-by-sit log of cycling through pain, dullness, and equanimity using noting and do-nothing techniques.

author: Louise Lecouffe
summary: Logs regular jhana-focused sits combining social Zoom noting games (Feel Out, zen relay, Jhanic Noting) with Do Nothing practice, tracking self-rated mindfulness/energy/equanimity factors and progress through the four jhanas and sub-jhanas.
interesting_sentence: "Aha... 1st jhana, subjhana 4. Yay! Exciting to explore more" —Louise Lecouffe
helpful_sentence: "I highly recommend from time to time putting aside noting and just diving into the body... Noticing the interplay of tension, attention and relaxation is really good practice and a sort of fractal of the whole journey." —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in structured jhana practice within a social/online meditation community using noting games and Feel Out techniques.

author: Noah D
summary: A long, philosophically dense series of posts theorizing wisdom, ethics, and samadhi through a qualia-based framework, alongside a daily July practice log of off-cushion inquiry into time, space, personhood, and vedana, plus critiques of the Mahasi method and perennialist interpretations of awakening.
interesting_sentence: "The goal is not to eliminate or see through the illusion of time, but instead to know with perfect clarity how it is constructed, by detaching its components, lining them up, extracting any sensation heirarchy and then perception occurs." —Noah D
helpful_sentence: "1) follow a subtle sensation like feeling and notice it's impersonal nature ---2) juxtapose two coarse sensation patterns, the most obvious said being mind and body > are they equal? ---3) follow a coarse sensation like the body and notice it's impersonal nature ---4) just gentle ongoing mindfulness" —Noah D
who_should_read: Practitioners who enjoy bridging sutra scholarship with granular direct investigation of time, space, self, and causality will find rich material here.

author: Victor
summary: A discursive exploration of Bon Dzogchen teachings under Tenzin Wangyal, weighing skepticism about Rainbow Body mythology against the practical value of Five Elements, Tibetan Yoga, and Sound Healing practices, ending with a pivot toward Les Fehmi's dogma-free "Open Focus" approach.
interesting_sentence: "It's a huge mistake to believe that successful path completion is about developing some sort of comic-book-hero superpowers, especially as the Buddha was absolutely clear that completion is all about attaining the cessation of suffering, which is the only reliable marker." —Victor
helpful_sentence: "I'd strongly suggest that they also study The Guru Papers: Masks of Authoritarian Power by Joel Kramer and Diana Alstad... it's valuable food for thought and it might save you considerable grief." —Victor
who_should_read: Eclectic Vajrayana-curious practitioners weighing which Bon/Dzogchen practices are practically useful versus which rest on unexamined dogma.

author: alas b
summary: A meticulous daily log of breath-at-the-nostrils concentration practice combining Mindfulness in Plain English's counting/connecting method, TWIM's 6 Rs, and Burbea's whole-body-breath instructions, documenting persistent posture pain, occasional pleasant tingling and point-of-light phenomena, and a brief experience of a centerless field of perception.
interesting_sentence: "For a few minutes, the tone of the experience felt quite ominous - think complex minor chord on the bass notes of a church organ - then that tone went away." —alas b
helpful_sentence: "When you cannot feel the breath there, keep the attention on the lack of sensation... The lack of sensation is the sensation." —Martin V
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate concentration practitioners wanting a granular, day-by-day account of breath-meditation troubleshooting and technique-mixing.

author: Matt
summary: An introductory practice thread recounting a 10-day Goenka retreat with vivid kundalini-like spinal rushes, full-body tingling, sexual fantasies, paranoia, and a brief total blackout of awareness, followed by post-retreat body-scanning practice, a shift to Mahasi-style noting, and a self-described settling into a low-grade equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "All awareness and sensation except a very slight tingling instantly clicked off, then things came back on." —Matt
helpful_sentence: "What you describe towards the end of the course with your extreme irritibility does sound like it could be re-ob, and what you describe on day 11 could be EQ." —Teague
who_should_read: Goenka-retreat alumni curious about kundalini-like energetic phenomena and how to integrate retreat gains into an inconsistent home practice.

author: Noah
summary: Noah journals his transition into Actualism practice shortly after claiming technical 4th path, working through Actual Freedom concepts like HAIETMOBA and sensuousness while managing bipolar-related anxiety and a cross-country move.
interesting_sentence: "There is the physical sense of utter openness, like a huge, soft bubble that is connected, grounding me to my environment." —Noah
helpful_sentence: "Really you *are* a feeling-being, and you in your entirety must go in order to become actually free." —Beoman Claudiu Dragon Emu Fire Golem
who_should_read: Practitioners curious how someone claiming technical 4th path integrates Actualism practice while managing bipolar disorder.

author: Victor
summary: Victor opens a branch thread to discuss Les Fehmi's Open Focus attention-training method and its accompanying book/audio exercises, and Patrick replies describing reaching a deep state within 20 minutes that took days to reach on a Vipassana retreat.
interesting_sentence: "For me, after 20 minutes or so I consistently reach the state that took me 3 or 4 days to reach in Vipassana retreat." —Patrick
helpful_sentence: "There's also a wealth of free material provided by Les and others online, however the book would probably provide all that's necessary." —Victor
who_should_read: Meditators curious about Les Fehmi's Open Focus attentional training as an alternative or complement to Vipassana practice.

author: Misha -
summary: Misha, a self-taught 20-year-old practitioner, describes months of intense unguided sitting (including 11-12 hour sessions), visual dissolution/cessation phenomenology, recurring psychic-power experiences, and self-locates his practice as somewhere in the middle of third path while recounting a childhood marked by autism, ADHD, addiction, and depression.
interesting_sentence: "with quite a lot of certainty, somewhere in the middle of path 3, definitely not 4." —Misha -
helpful_sentence: "If you'd like to try to figure out where you're at on the MCTB maps — which is not everyone's cup of tea — would you say more about your cessation experience? And your experience of the ñanas." —John L
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in unusually intense self-directed practice and unusual psychic-phenomenology reports alongside a neurodivergent, trauma-marked personal history.

log: Matthew Sexton's zero'th path practice log
author: Matt
summary: Matt (Matthew Sexton) logs detailed nightly sits combining a cell-phone-LED kasina warm-up, breath/body noting, and self-inquiry into the watcher, reporting a dramatic 'big-body' jhana-like expansion and repeatedly asking the board to help him locate his experiences on the equanimity/dark-night map.
interesting_sentence: "It was as if my body had dramatically expanded, I felt like I occupied a body 10x normal size." —Matt
helpful_sentence: "Whenever a shift/change of state happens try to notice the signpost of what happened right before and right after... as you become aware of the signs they can be good indicators of where you are at." —Dream Walker
who_should_read: Post-retreat Mahasi/noting practitioners wanting detailed sit-by-sit phenomenology and community help mapping jhana and equanimity signposts.

author: Noah D
summary: A multi-year (2013–2021) monthly-update log tracing intensive off-cushion Mahasi-style noting through stream entry and successive technical paths (confirmed by teacher Ron Crouch as Technical 4th Path in July 2015), followed by an Actualism detour, therapeutic 'perfect parent' visualization work, and later Tibetan tantra/tummo practice, all while managing lifelong bipolar disorder.
interesting_sentence: "I took this as a message from the Universe: Welcome to the dark night, bitch." —Noah D
helpful_sentence: "In retrospect, I can see how valuable that advice was; the active approach of noting was a necessary “disembedding” mechanism for me, at this time." —Noah D
who_should_read: Practitioners managing serious mental illness alongside intensive off-cushion noting who want a candid, long-arc account of progressing through the technical paths into later tantric/psychotherapeutic integration work.

author: Mike H
summary: A self-taught practitioner without retreat experience describes cultivating jhanas 1-4 per Ayya Khema and Leigh Brasington's instructions, then attempting vipassana within or just after the jhanic state, while reporting an unclear oscillation between dissolution, fear, misery/reobservation, and calmer equanimity-like periods.
interesting_sentence: "It might seem bold to claim jhanas without retreat experience, but then again, this is DHO" —Mike H
helpful_sentence: "In terms of insight, I will usually finish a sitting by trying to do vipassana within a jhana state or just coming out of one." —Mike H
who_should_read: Self-taught concentration practitioners curious about combining jhana work with insight investigation without access to formal retreats.

author: Bart Castelijns
summary: A beginner documents daily formal and off-cushion noting practice, working through confusion about correct technique before attending an introductory two-week Vipassana retreat at a Dhammacari center and describing subsequent nighttime 'in the body' sensations.
interesting_sentence: "It seems the deeper I'm in the noting, the more instant the recognizing of a thought" —Bart Castelijns
helpful_sentence: "in principal you only note things other than the breath if these are distracting you from noting the breath." —Bart Castelijns
who_should_read: New noting practitioners wanting a relatable account of early technique confusion before and during a first intensive retreat.

author: housecrow
summary: A beginner describes a recurring 'breathing bag' concentration state deepening into vivid circulatory-system awareness and first jhana, then a period of depression, insomnia and Dark-Night-like misery triggered partly by re-encountering past abuse and conflict with a mentor.
interesting_sentence: "His severed head was caught in the gears and his body was already completely ground up." —housecrow
helpful_sentence: "your descriptions of some of your concentration states are almost textbook examples of \"access concentration\" as well as \"dropping through the jhanas\"." —tom moylan
who_should_read: Beginner meditators dealing with depression or past trauma who want an honest account of how concentration gains can be derailed by dark-night-like emotional turbulence.

author: ma
summary: A returning meditator restarts basic concentration practice while working a low-wage job, reports subtle light/energy experiences echoing childhood episodes and a past Goenka retreat, and receives casual lifestyle advice (nature walks, diet) rather than formal dharma instruction.
interesting_sentence: "I was sitting on the edge, seeing subtle lights and closed eyed visuals and went over." —ma
helpful_sentence: "If you think you might soon hit a depressive episode just remember that the opposite of depression isn't happiness, its vitality." —Mista Tibbs
who_should_read: Beginners restarting a lapsed practice amid social anxiety and low motivation who want a relatable, informal peer-support exchange rather than technical guidance.

author: Noah D
summary: A long-running, highly conceptual sandbox log in which Noah D describes cycling through nondual/centerless perceptual shifts, kundalini-like energy phenomena, deity-yoga visions (Vajrakilaya, Amma-related experiences), and repeated self-diagnosed 'technical' path attainments, interwoven with extensive theorizing on dharma-teacher economics, bipolar disorder, sexuality/masculinity work, and psychotherapy-based shadow integration.
interesting_sentence: "I think probably almost no one on the DhO or in pragmatic dharma has even the 1st stage of it." —Noah D
helpful_sentence: "Awareness is bigger than Attention. Awareness can hold attention, but attention can't hold awareness." —shargrol
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners interested in how post-path integration, psychotherapy, and energetic/magickal practices intersect with ongoing self-diagnosis of attainment levels.

author: Mike Smirnoff
summary: A beginner meditator anchors attention on breath sensation at the upper lip while investigating 'who is it that experiences?', prompting an internal dialogue with a felt sense of 'I' and a forum discussion contrasting vipassana no-self teaching with nondual approaches.
interesting_sentence: "Me: I, are you permanent. I: Yes, I last for ever. Me: How do you know. I: Coz I just do. Hahaha." —Mike Smirnoff
helpful_sentence: "You cold try to observe what happens strictly on a sensate level." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
who_should_read: Beginners curious about self-inquiry practice and how forum mentors gently guide a newcomer without imposing a single tradition.

author: cian
summary: A pure-concentration practice log tracking hour-long breath-focus sits that produce deep trance states, spontaneous kriyas releasing chronic neck tension, sleep paralysis, and lucid dreams, alongside candid notes on distraction, laziness, and the gap between sensitivity and sustained concentration.
interesting_sentence: "By the end of the hour when my alarm went and I opened my eyes, the trance was deep enough that my sense of balance couldn't tell me which direction was up or down, and though I seemed to be somehow magically remaining upright, I thought it best to shake it out and have a lie down lest I fall over." —cian
helpful_sentence: "Don't forget about the other factors of enlightenment too! Joy and Relaxation can be particularly important when pursuing absorption." —Noah
who_should_read: Yogis curious about a pure samatha/concentration experiment and its physical side-effects like kriyas, trance, and altered sleep phenomenology.

author: johnwatcher
summary: A Brazilian college student systematically works through jhanas, breath noting, and candle-kasina concentration following MCTB, documenting a clear A&P event, dukkha-ñana struggles, and a sustained push toward stream entry via daily-life noting and a planned self-retreat.
interesting_sentence: "It was like I had front row seats to the creation of the self, and I could watch it all happen in slow motion, each part of the process." —Bill F
helpful_sentence: "If wide focus is happening, notice that. If narrow focus is happening, notice that. If \"bad practice\" is happening, notice that." —Florian
who_should_read: Vipassana practitioners working toward stream entry who want a granular, MCTB-style day-by-day nana log plus thoughtful peer feedback.

author: Melissa
summary: A 33-year-old beginner combining breath meditation with systematic noting logs escalating pulsations, involuntary shaking, and flickering sensations that shift from likely A&P energy into a rough, irritable phase she suspects may be the Dark Night.
interesting_sentence: "3 days ago, i experienced quite dramatic pulsations/throbbing all over my body, at one point my hand and head shakes involuntarily as though I was having a seizure." —Melissa
helpful_sentence: "Ingram keeps repeating one word through the ENTIRE book of his - Acceptance, of all matter of fact phenomena arising in This moment." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: New meditators without access to a teacher who want a relatable account of noting practice intensifying into strong energetic phenomena and early Dark Night symptoms.

author: Martin
summary: Martin describes daily jhana practice (1st-4th, sometimes up to 5th) paired with self/not-self investigation, a flashing-in-and-out-of-existence event and rising body heat that others read as A&P and possible cessation, an increasingly stable 'no push-pull' equanimity, and a long forum debate over whether he has already attained stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "It's like a long walk south without a map or a destination. The scenery changes and it's warmer but I don't know more than that." —Martin
helpful_sentence: "What I found helpful when I had good access to jhanas was to allow myself to cook up in them and then gently sense for residual resistance, tension, ill will, avoidance, etc." —shargrol
who_should_read: Jhana practitioners curious how sustained concentration attainments interact with self/not-self investigation and the question of whether stream entry can occur without a clearly noticed fruition.

log: My Insight Meditation Practice Log [John Power] [MIGRATE]My Insight Meditat
author: John Power
summary: John Power logs progress through several Mahasi-style noting retreats, including a black-out experience with rapture and bliss (likely A&P), repeated spontaneous breath-stoppage episodes, a dream-like dissolution phase, an experiment with candle-flame concentration, and a tentative self-assessment of Low Equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "No sensations or awareness in the black out. After it happened I realised that I had been away and felt much rapture and joy." —John Power
helpful_sentence: "The more you concentrate or focus on an object, the more energy you use. This makes the practice difficult and tiring. Your mindfulness may actually slacken." —John Power
who_should_read: Mahasi noting practitioners on multi-retreat trajectories who want a candid account of over-effort burnout, breath-cessation phenomena, and uncertainty about map placement.

author: Adam
summary: Adam shifts his practice to cittanupassana (contemplating mental states) after a doubt episode dissolves effortlessly, then spends the log cycling through actualism-influenced inquiry into resentment, approval-seeking, and dark-night-like emotional storms, reporting intermittent PCEs and moments of selfless 'wonder' while wrestling with whether to pursue Actual Freedom Trust methods.
interesting_sentence: "Just came back from an hour long night walk. I don't think I've ever been so consistently with little to no 'selfing' for such a long period of time." —Adam
helpful_sentence: "His teachings made me realize what a good attitude towards meditation is and that because of this good attitude you can see clearer." —John Power
who_should_read: Meditators drawn to actualism or Byron Katie-style inquiry who want a granular, emotionally honest record of cycling through resentment, approval-seeking, and brief pure-consciousness experiences.

author: melozone
summary: melozone (G Z) starts a brand-new practice log after an intense week of meditation, and regular poster Linda "Polly Ester" Ö welcomes them, guessing from their description that the session touched second and third jhana.
interesting_sentence: "After spending some time lurking and feeling inspired by the community of meditators here, I decided I should start a practice log after some pretty intense meditation experiences this week." —melozone
helpful_sentence: "I found that it was a valuable tool for several purposes: processing what had happened in the practice, keeping a record... and receiving valuable feedback." —Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
who_should_read: New forum members starting their first practice log who want to see a warm, encouraging community response to an early jhana-adjacent experience.

author: Martin
summary: A long-running, highly detailed log tracking jhana practice, nimitta and fire-kasina experiments, a deepening panoramic/nondual mode of perception with pronounced non-agency, and equanimity maintained through a real-life heart attack.
interesting_sentence: "Woke up nondual this morning. Just sounds knowing themselves." —Martin
helpful_sentence: "There is a useful practice for cultivating and learning to accept these experiences and that is to say to yourself \"look at it...\", as in \"look at it shuffle cards\", and then keep watching the non-agency happen." —shargrol
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners interested in detailed phenomenology of sustained nonduality, non-agency, and how deep equanimity holds up under real physical crisis.

author: John Power
summary: Daily Mahasi-style noting log describing a painful first retreat, an A&P-like blackout on a second retreat, repeated breath-stopping episodes, and a tentative self-placement in low equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "No sensations or awareness in the black out. After it happened I realised that I had been away and felt much rapture and joy." —John Power
helpful_sentence: "The more you concentrate or focus on an object, the more energy you use. This makes the practice difficult and tiring." —John Power
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate noting practitioners wanting an honest, blow-by-blow account of retreat pain, an A&P blackout, and early equanimity signs.

author: Not two
summary: A martial-artist-turned-meditator works through Culadasa's TMI stages, cycles through A&P-like kundalini surges and dark-night patches, investigates dental pain and dependent origination in daily life, and briefly touches an unresolved cessation-like void before backing off.
interesting_sentence: "I had an experience before sleep (posted elsewhere) of a sudden shutting of the sense doors, total cessation of the breathing reflex, and a strong pull into a void-like textured blackness." —Not two
helpful_sentence: "If you don't know what you're looking for how will you konw when you've found it?" —Chris M
who_should_read: TMI practitioners curious how concentration-stage work interacts with spontaneous A&P/dark-night cycling and near-cessation events.

author: jsn
summary: A software engineer with narcolepsy tracks years of Mahasi-style choiceless awareness noting, detailing long dark-night/equanimity cycles, bizarre hypnagogic 'unknowing' states, half-day home retreats, and an eventual settling into effortless daily practice without a confirmed fruition.
interesting_sentence: "For example, I might suddenly have a very strong, very visceral feeling that my old red leather jacket, the one I can visually remember lying on my round wooden table in our second floor dining room, is a very natural expression of my commitment to my crime-fighting vigilante career." —jsn
helpful_sentence: "Stream entry is ultimately about deep relaxation and a loose and free mind, rather than a tight, problem solving mind." —shargrol
who_should_read: Long-term noting practitioners dealing with a medical condition (like narcolepsy) who want a granular, honest account of dark-night/equanimity cycling without a clean stream-entry narrative.

author: Jake
summary: A beginner alternates short candle-flame kasina sessions with noting practice, tracking recurring involuntary body 'crumples,' frustration from a 10-day Goenka retreat, and a late nighttime sit where the body's sensations briefly vanished amid rapid vibrations.
interesting_sentence: "my body completely disappeared. It felt like the sensations of my my body were peeled away." —Jake
helpful_sentence: "More clear we see/understand that all these thoughts emotions are impermanent&arise without controll&cause suffering (3 Characteristics) it's easier to not get involved them." —Noting Monkey
who_should_read: New meditators wanting a relatable, unpolished first-months log combining kasina and noting, including early arising-and-passing-type phenomena.

author: Martin V
summary: While recovering from a heart attack and covid, Martin practices jhana and works with the five aggregates, reporting the collapse of the 'witness' posture into a quiet, effortless anatta seeing that colors both illness and daily life.
interesting_sentence: "I used to hope that I could be free not to care what other people thought, now I see that there is a much sweeter freedom in not caring what I think." —Martin V
helpful_sentence: "A sustained perception of anatta developed on the pancha skandhas will release the releaser and soften the softners." —Adi Vader
who_should_read: Long-term practitioners navigating serious illness who want a detailed account of applying anatta and jhana practice to health crises and the dissolution of the witness.

author: Brian K
summary: After his first Goenka retreat, Brian describes despondency and a shaky return to noting practice, struggling with procrastination and a destabilizing sense that his personality is just a passing concept.
interesting_sentence: "It all comes and goes (my mental states) and it's like, I am that, but I am also not that, which means I both am and am not that, and neither am nor not that." —Brian K
helpful_sentence: "Even in the worst parts of the dukkha ñanas, there are pleasant sensations." —Jigme Sengye
who_should_read: Recent retreatants struggling with post-retreat despondency and procrastination who want peer support rather than map-focused analysis.

author: David Edwards
summary: A newer practitioner logs a vision of his deceased parents, a floating observer-state on public transport, and a ten-day-plus shift of reduced suffering and heightened compassion that he tentatively identifies as Stream Entry.
interesting_sentence: "I felt and exuded total compassion toward my wife and children and everything generally." —David Edwards
helpful_sentence: "In a non-meditation, recovery-oriented side note, meetings becoming annoying is sometimes (not always and not for all people) a sign of the addictive mind looking for a way to wriggle free, so make sure you have that on your radar." —Martin V
who_should_read: Newer MCTB-influenced practitioners curious how informal noting and concentration practice can produce a sustained shift they suspect is Stream Entry.

author: Kailin T
summary: A meticulous multi-month log cycling through fire kasina, freestyle vipassana, and self-inquiry into agency and dark-night psychological content, culminating in a 13-day vajrayana empowerment retreat with sustained deity visualization practice.
interesting_sentence: "Who is this joker who thinks he's running my life anyway?" —Kailin T
helpful_sentence: "I think of this very much in a 'what fires together wires together' sense, where one is rewiring the trauma by overriding the past emotional charge with love/equanimity." —Ryan Kay
who_should_read: Technically-minded second-path-ish practitioners integrating vipassana with tantric or imaginal practice and psychological/IFS work who want granular phenomenological notes.

author: Mettafore
summary: A daily log of Gil Fronsdal-style Metta phrase practice moving through Karuna and Mudita, tracking recurring head pressure, lust relapses, and past depressive dark nights, culminating in an aborted Pa-Auk retreat in Myanmar and a strong bliss burst during breath meditation.
interesting_sentence: "Kind of like, the knight has to defeat the dragons before rescuing the princess." —Mettafore
helpful_sentence: "First it stopped bothering me, then it melted (broke down) into different kinds vibratory sensations." —Marcus
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to phrase-based Brahmavihara practice who want an honest, granular account of how metta surfaces underlying depression, lust, and energetic obstacles.

author: Magada Rtinguindin
summary: A former Soto Zen ordinand describes an intensive body-scanning satipatthana practice that produced an A&P-like opening of profound peace followed by years of dissociation, hallucinations, nightmares, and fear that he only later recognized (via Ingram's book) as the Dark Night, and now asks the forum how to resume practice.
interesting_sentence: "It felt that I could see so clearly through that deception that I had to act really strongly to look normal and act like my old self." —Magada Rtinguindin
helpful_sentence: "Thinking still occurs in 1st jhana, two of the factors of jhana are applied thinking, vitakka, and sustained thinking, vicara, but these both stop when you enter 2nd jhana." —Tommy M
who_should_read: Practitioners who burned out on unsupervised intensive practice and are trying to re-orient themselves using the map after years of unexplained fear and dissociation.

author: Stuart Charles Law
summary: An Australian yogi with schizoaffective disorder and serious heart disease logs years of intensive 4-to-6-hour daily samatha/jhana practice, cycling through hard jhana, dark night, and sustained high equanimity, before reporting a Boxing Day cessation experience he identifies as stream entry alongside a kundalini awakening.
interesting_sentence: "Don't know, not sure.......then....\"Blip\"......void.......felt a bit of vertigo for a fraction of a second. Feeling of ......what was..No....this is a discontinuity..........this is Stream Entry!" —Stuie Law
helpful_sentence: "Once mastered with the abiltiy to call up, maintain and cease at will, it is just a matter of letting go of the grosser aspects of each jhana to move to the next jhan on up." —Nikolai
who_should_read: Practitioners managing serious mental illness who want a raw, high-volume samatha/jhana log culminating in a candid stream-entry and kundalini-awakening claim.

author: Demoxenos
summary: A self-described beginner narrates a wide-ranging experimental practice spanning fire kasina, Ken McLeod's six-realms meditation, do-nothing sits, TMI breath concentration, and dakini/elemental terror work, punctuated by out-of-body sensations, hypnagogic trance states, and ayahuasca-retreat memories, without ever settling on where he sits on the insight map.
interesting_sentence: "It occurred to me that maybe this grid, or this ground, was the vast spacious awareness I've been looking for." —Demoxenos
helpful_sentence: "It's easy to underestimate walking meditation, but it really does do something special that sitting meditation doesn't do." —shargrol
who_should_read: Meditators who enjoy richly narrated cross-tradition experimentation and want a candid, uncertain account of pre-stream-entry practice full of vivid phenomenology.

author: Babs _
summary: A tantra/dzogchen teacher recounts a three-year burnout that resolved coincident with what he calls opening his 11th 'bhumi,' lays out his own Open Heart Bhumi Model for rating attainment (including of other teachers), and gets into a heated, personal dispute with a critic over his authority to teach and rate others.
interesting_sentence: "My health got almost back to normal in few days after the 1st of Nowember 2016, when hitting the 11th bhumi, which is the first mahasiddha bhumi." —Babs _
helpful_sentence: "So, the essential point of \"sit still without moving\" is to stop doing external movements, to open and relax all the joints and muscles but then allow subtle movement in your physical and energetic body." —Babs _
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to Tibetan tantra and dzogchen frameworks who want to see both esoteric bhumi-mapping teachings and the community friction that strong attainment claims provoke.

author: Mind Body Spirit
summary: A near-beginner describes a single outdoor sit where sustained breath focus eased into a spacious trance-like state with diminished body sense, drifting visualizations, and hyper-vivid perception on opening the eyes.
interesting_sentence: "it felt as if my mind was in some large space & I felt still very awake." —Mind Body Spirit
helpful_sentence: "Some things I did that might've helped: cold air on the face, very slow breathing." —Mind Body Spirit
who_should_read: Beginner meditators curious about early concentration-related trance and visual phenomena that can arise during simple breath practice.

author: Martin
summary: Detailed log of daily jhana practice cycling rapidly through first-to-fourth (and sometimes fifth) jhana, including a recurring 'dukkha-jhana' state where absorption and pervasive dukkha run in parallel, metta-based concentration that eliminates papañca, and later investigative sits aimed at stream entry under a personal teacher's guidance.
interesting_sentence: "I feel like a guy in the cockpit of an alien spaceship, who presses buttons and pulls levers and feels the ship moving in response, and so knows that the buttons and levers do things, but who can in no way be said to be flying the ship." —Martin
helpful_sentence: "The only possible *action* is to let go of aversion to the crappy and preference for the pleasant." —George S
who_should_read: Jhana-focused practitioners wanting a granular, technically detailed account of cycling through the four jhanas while investigating self and dukkha within absorption.

author: Papa Che Dusko
summary: Explores Five Elements and Dakini visualization practice from Ken McLeod's WUTYL (including a death-meditation on falling from a height tied to war-trauma imagery), reports Dark Night-flavored bodily misery, and later documents an intense multi-week nicotine withdrawal alongside health scares (COVID test, high blood pressure, a throat scope revealing Candida).
interesting_sentence: "I even had a guy bleed out on my hands as I was not clearly thinking to maybe rip his clothes open and find the hit spot to stop that bleeding!" —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "The 5 elements are 5 types of experience which tend to make us react unconsciously --- we 'grab onto certainty' as earth, we 'avoid and push away' as water, we 'intensify and indulge' as fire, we 'get frantic and busy' as air, we 'zone out and become confused' as space." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to tantric/elemental practices who want an honest account of Dark Night misery intertwined with real-life trauma and habit-breaking.

author: Banned For waht?
summary: Idiosyncratic solo log tracking kundalini/chakra-based energy cultivation through self-described 'channels' and chakras, repeatedly claiming and then retracting arhat/4th-path-level attainment amid highly personal, non-standard terminology.
interesting_sentence: "Finally i destroyed my self." —Banned For waht?
helpful_sentence: "Breath in, then notice (very) subtle currents going to the head and then breath out and then try to notice currents going down." —Banned For waht?
who_should_read: Readers curious about kundalini/chakra energy practices and cautionary examples of self-diagnosing high attainments without external verification.

author: supermonkey :)
summary: Dense continuation log cycling rapidly through A&P, Dark Night and Equanimity with frequent daily cessations/fruitions, integrating Rob Burbea's dependent-origination work, Internal Family Systems parts practice, and exploration of formless and pure-land jhanas.
interesting_sentence: "here's another mind bomb: if there is no self or other, when we read 'somebody else's wisdom', we're really just recognizing our own." —shargrol
helpful_sentence: "we 'liberate' dukka by holding it compassionately within a caring and respectful awareness. then it releases, lets go, unknots." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners deep in post-stream-entry cycling seeking a granular account combining Burbea's dependent-origination teaching with IFS-style emotional processing and jhana exploration.

author: Michael
summary: A returning practitioner recounts past retreat experiences at IMS, including a striking 'perfect mindfulness' state a teacher labeled equanimity and a sit where aversion to severe back pain dissolved, alongside recent short breath-focused sits exploring distraction and dullness.
interesting_sentence: "I remember thinking something like the roof could cave in and it wouldn't affect this 'state' I was in." —Michael
helpful_sentence: "I remembering thinking something like, 'Hey old friend, back pain. What are you like right now?' And shortly after that, it really seemed like all or nearly all of the aversion disappeared." —Michael
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate meditators wanting relatable, low-key examples of working with distraction, dullness, and physical pain on and off retreat.

author: Niels Lyngsø
summary: Niels reports a 90-min x8/day retreat filled with vivid energetic phenomena (the 'body suit,' spinal rotor vibrations, a kundalini-like lump traveling up the spine) and anatta insights into attention, followed by weeks of continued energetic 'rewiring' and a long reflection on emotional repression and anger tied to his childhood and past relationships.
interesting_sentence: "It actually felt like there were being drawn some new fiber-optic cables inside my body, establishing new connections between gut (gut feeling), heart (heart felt) and head (thoughts/spirit/intellect)." —Niels Lyngsø
helpful_sentence: "Try imagining your \"energy body\" to be a bit bigger than your physical body, like a kind of loose space suit or something." —George S
who_should_read: Experienced yogis deep in Equanimity curious about kundalini-like energy phenomena and how energy work interacts with repressed emotion and anger.

log: Not sure what stage I am at - maybe you can help from the logs?
author: b man
summary: Describes a nearly unbroken twice-daily 20-minute sitting routine (tracked via a coach.me app) and a recurring week-to-10-day cycle of flu-like malaise, sadness, agitation, and post-sit depression that has intensified since increasing sit frequency.
interesting_sentence: "I seem to be cycling through these periods where everything is fine and then I will go through a week-10 days where things are really rough and I have what feels like a cross between a big detox and the flu." —b man
helpful_sentence: "For what it's worth, I'd guess post A&P with plenty of experiences of EQ and dark night." —x x
who_should_read: Practitioners noticing cyclical mood crashes tied to increased sit frequency who want a brief real-world example of dark-night-like phasing.

author: John Doe
summary: Chronicles short 20-30 minute noting sessions using Shinzen Young's six-sense-door model, describing how making the urge to check the meditation timer itself an object of noting dissolves the anxiety, alongside a note about running out of depression medication.
interesting_sentence: "By the time the 30 minutes are almost up, I start to doubt that I even set the timer at all, and the fear that I will stay stuck meditating for hours becomes quite strong." —John Doe
helpful_sentence: "Noting your urge to stop noting and doing something else seems to work." —John Doe
who_should_read: Beginners doing short daily noting sits who want a simple example of Shinzen's categories and working with meta-distractions like clock-checking.

author: Kyle T
summary: Details a 9-day, roughly 10-11 hours/day home retreat blending Mahasi-style noting with Culadasa-style concentration, reporting very deep jhanic absorption, a spontaneous 'Source' no-self insight around day 5, dark-night-like shadow material and a panic attack resolved via visualization, plus spinal energy sensations, but no A&P or cessation despite hoping for one.
interesting_sentence: "Source emanates sensations from itself and experiences them through the body. body and ego create a false sense of “self” to keep up the illusion of separateness to experience reality through the sense experiencing instrument which is the body." —Kyle T
helpful_sentence: "If a teacher notices a student struggling after intensive vipassana it can be benefial to switch back to concentration practices to find some bliss/meditative joy." —Kyle T
who_should_read: Dedicated TMI/vipassana-hybrid practitioners doing intensive home retreats who want a candid account of jhana progress mixed with dark-night shadow material.

author: Mike Monson
summary: A 64-year-old with a decades-long history spanning evangelical Christianity, Krishnamurti's 'choiceless awareness,' Zen, and KFD noting under Kenneth Folk recounts relapsing into alcohol/drug abuse and depression, then restarting noting practice which triggers strong involuntary jerking and days of intense misery, before settling into a lighter, calmer choiceless-awareness practice while re-examining an old, since-doubted claim of stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "I can relate to the experience: 'Reality stops cold and then reapears.'" —Mike Monson
helpful_sentence: "My own advice is to pick a reasonable amount of practice to do a day, like an hour of sitting. And then completely forget about practice for the rest of the time and just really enjoy/participate in your normal everyday life." —shargrol
who_should_read: Older practitioners returning to practice after long addiction-marked hiatuses who want an unflinching account of re-triggered kundalini-like phenomena and the interplay between psychology and insight practice.

author: Niels Lyngsø
summary: Niels logs a highly detailed daily samatha-vipassana practice (his self-designed 'SamVip'/'Just Sit' set-ups) cycling through named absorption states ('bottom of the ocean', 'floating in space'), adds walking meditation and later tantric semen-retention practice, and reports a joy-filled multi-day home retreat with vivid visuals but no cessation, all while remaining in an extended Equanimity plateau without reaching Stream Entry.
interesting_sentence: "In October/November 2019 I was on a Goenka retreat (my fourth since November 2017) with the expectation to get SE or at least some really deep jhana. I ended up in a psychiatric ward (you can read about it in my first post on DhO), which was the last thing I would have expected beforehand." —Niels Lyngsø
helpful_sentence: "really, a lot of this can be boiled down to naturally preferring already-self-arising knowing over tension-creating-future-oriented willfulness. resting in knowing, releasing willfulness: THIS is intimacy." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners deep in Equanimity who want a patient, richly phenomenological account of samatha-vipassana integration, retreat bliss, and life while waiting for Stream Entry.

author: richard blank
summary: Richard, about to start a 10-day Goenka retreat, describes 3 years of mixed Goenka body-scan and Shinzen-style noting practice, recalls past A&P-like energy/out-of-body phenomena and a dark-night-to-equanimity swing on a prior retreat, and asks the board which technique gives the fastest route to stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "First 7 days nothing but pain and misery...then boom:-) I could sit for hours and felt really relaxed." —richard blank
helpful_sentence: "Don't worry about obtaining stream entry. Just do the work and stream-entry will come naturally." —Bailey
who_should_read: Practitioners heading into a Goenka retreat who are weighing whether to stick strictly to body-scanning or blend in noting technique.

author: Lara D
summary: A newer practitioner following MCTB, Lara logs noting practice focused on pulse and breath sensations, reports lucid-dream experiences she interprets as A&P and a subsequent dark-night stretch of grief and fatigue, then works through jhana states (claiming a possible 4th jhana) while enrolled in an online Life Retreat course.
interesting_sentence: "I felt like I was traveling through a vortex, a little bit like going through a tunnel slide." —Lara D
helpful_sentence: "FYI - it's easier to tell what's going on in your sits (and to give constructive feedback) if you keep to a step-by-step phenomenological account rather than saying conceptual things like 'insight practice territory'." —Fitter Stoke
who_should_read: New practitioners curious how MCTB-based lucid-dream A&P experiences and jhana exploration intermix with early dark-night mood swings.

author: Mikey Oz
summary: Mikey restarts a daily one-hour commitment after two difficult month-long retreats that produced strong vibrations and lights but no map progress, logging recurring sleep paralysis, death-anxiety spirals tied to a new relationship, and a switch from Mahasi noting to Pa Auk-style breath concentration.
interesting_sentence: "Because I don't want to go through hell I fear death. Because I want to see AI's fruition I fear death. Because I love my life and girlfriend I fear death." —Mikey Oz
helpful_sentence: "Before I started noting, lots of things that my mind did were mysterious, and many were annoying, confusing, or even threatening. Noting let me see that the mind keeps doing very similar (often identical) things, again and again." —Martin V
who_should_read: Practitioners restarting practice after intense retreats who struggle with death anxiety and want a candid account of switching between noting and concentration technique.

author: nicolas C
summary: A Tibetan translator with ten years of inconsistent Mahasi-style noting practice logs a return to daily one-hour rising-and-falling sits, battling recurring torpor, and with forum feedback begins to suspect his quiet, vibratory, boundary-dissolving states may be vipassana-jhana or equanimity territory despite never having a recognizable A&P.
interesting_sentence: "Well it seems I'm still miles from A&P and it's still frustrating as I've been practicing more than 10 years now at, I really wonder if there is a wire unplugged somewhere in my brain or if I'm missing something in the instructions..." —nicolas C
helpful_sentence: "When you sit to meditate you do not start out at the stage of Mind and Body, rather, your starting point is the Arising and Passing... Then you quickly run through the Dark Night with very little stress or difficulty, then up into Equanimity and have a fruition." —Plume
who_should_read: Long-term Mahasi-style noters stuck without a recognizable A&P who want reassurance that equanimity-like jhana states can appear without classic nana landmarks.

author: Niels Lyngsø
summary: A dedicated home-retreatant works with a private teacher to move through High Equanimity into a hypothesized Stream Entry via an eight-day retreat, then logs months of intense chi/energy phenomena, unknowing events, a Rob Burbea jhana retreat, and a high-dose psilocybin sitting while trying to map his progress onto second and third path.
interesting_sentence: "I hypothesize that I got 2nd Path on my summer retreat of 2022, more specifically probably in the third sitting on Day 7." —Niels Lyngsø
helpful_sentence: "Many people assume an unknowing event is path, but it is actually A&P event or formless realms, and then when they return to work through the remaining aspects of dark night, they call that review." —shargrol
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry meditators wanting an extremely granular, teacher-guided account of chi/energy phenomena, retreat mapping, and a psilocybin-assisted sitting.

author: Mikhail Zybin
summary: A pre-A&P self-described beginner alternates freestyle 'noting aloud' ('shooting aliens') with TMI-style preliminaries and metta, logging vivid rapture, kriyas, and one unsettling ego-death-like session under a mentor's coaching.
interesting_sentence: "When my mother sneezed in another room, my body started to shake, I felt horror of not having a face (strange feeling, it passed quickly) and fell of my cushion." —Mikhail Zybin
helpful_sentence: "Jhana, Mantra and Kasina fall into a Samatha side of the practice and Noting (Mahasi or Goenka) falls into the Vipassana category where Khanika Samadhi (momentary concentration) is all you really need to do it." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Beginner-to-early practitioners curious how noting technique choices (aloud vs. silent, Mahasi-style) shape early A&P phenomena.

author: Pawel K
summary: A self-described post-4th-path practitioner describes manipulating 'neurons' and mind configurations, generating metta fruitions, sensing matter through crystals and metals, and clashes publicly with another poster who mocks his claims.
interesting_sentence: "I am pretty sure that yesterday I realized how to use Vajra Samadhi correctly and thus completed 10th Bhumi and 5th Mahayana path." —Pawel K
helpful_sentence: "One should not try to have permanent no-self experiences in either direction as having not experience of sense of self doesn't make person enlightened. Having genuine knowledge is what makes person enlightened." —Pawel K
who_should_read: Readers curious about idiosyncratic, esoteric post-4th-path accounts blending energy/neuron manipulation, Mahayana bhumi language, and forum moderation drama.

author: Niels Lyngsø
summary: A 52-year-old ex-Goenka practitioner logs extremely detailed daily sitting reports of body-scan self-inquiry and 'do nothing' noting, culminating in a calm 10-day solo retreat spent mostly in stable Equanimity/absorption without the dramatic A&P/Dark Night swings of his earlier retreats.
interesting_sentence: "Then a very clearly articulated sentence: 'The center cannot hold!'" —Niels Lyngsø
helpful_sentence: "In EQ it can be very helpful to very gently inquire: 'who/what is experiencing all of this?'" —Niels Lyngsø
who_should_read: Meditators wanting a highly granular phenomenological account of Equanimity-stage absorption and how a stable EQ retreat differs from earlier dramatic A&P/Dark Night retreats.

author: T
summary: A breath-counting practitioner with ~500 hours and an upcoming IMS retreat works through shargrol's structured drill of noting sensations, emotions, thoughts, and 'most obvious object' in sequence, discovering a head-centered pressure spot and a freeze response when told to simply let the mind do itself.
interesting_sentence: "I have heard many say that is where the mind places the 'I' in many cases." —T
helpful_sentence: "Notice everything, note one thing on the outbreath." —shargrol
who_should_read: Beginner noting practitioners preparing for a Mahasi-style retreat who want a concrete stepwise drill for separating sensations, emotions, and thoughts.

log: Leveraging Attainments: Fruition or Stream Entry
author: David Kyle Spencer
summary: A lifelong intermittent meditator describes solving the koan Mu in an all-night session, then chanting the Heart Sutra's closing mantra which triggered ego dissolution, a felt merging with Yaeko Iwasaki's deathbed letters, and a visionary meeting with a boy resembling Avalokiteshvara, asking the forum whether this counts as leveraging one attainment into another.
interesting_sentence: "My ego hadn't just dissolved, it had completely vanished, along with the stranger's reflection." —David Kyle Spencer
helpful_sentence: "These types of visionary experiences are dead ringers for A&P events... My advice would be to take the wisdom you've learned from the vision with you, without placing too much importance on any ideas about the vision itself." —Matthew
who_should_read: Meditators curious how an intense koan- and mantra-triggered visionary breakthrough gets diagnosed against the standard insight map.

log: nada nonsense: just say "I disagree" or "agree"
author: Todo
summary: A purely philosophical forum thread debates whether non-attachment is a developable 'property of cognizance' or an absence/void beyond cognition, weaving in Daniel Ingram's phrasing, the twelve links of Dependent Origination, Taoist and Zen texts, and the map/territory metaphor.
interesting_sentence: "We develop an appreciation that fundamental non-attachment is a property of clear congnizance." —Daniel M Ingram
helpful_sentence: "It is prajna or ultimate insight that leads to non-attachment... Cognition may in fact have to be abandoned before non-attachment can be attained." —terry
who_should_read: Practitioners who enjoy semantic/philosophical debates about non-attachment, cognizance, and dependent origination rather than practical instruction.

author: Noah
summary: Noah logs his post-third-path Review and the ensuing fourth-path insight cycle via near-constant daily-life Mahasi noting, tracking recurring nana cycles, bipolar-related restlessness, and a brief Actualism experiment, ending with his teacher Ron Crouch tentatively confirming a 'technical 4th path' after a spontaneous energetic release while driving.
interesting_sentence: "Let it be known that I am directing the One Law/ One Mind as me and through me, to reach 4th Path as quickly as possible!!" —Noah
helpful_sentence: "THE NON-SACREDNESS OF ALL PHENOMENA. No weight given to these phenomena over those phenomena." —Nikolai
who_should_read: Practitioners nearing or past third path who want a granular, day-by-day daily-life noting account of cycling toward a tentative fourth path.

author: Nihila
summary: A long-running day-by-day log of cycling through dark night misery, fear and disgust while working with left-side nadi/kundalini imbalances, breath counting, memory-release work, and medication changes, with occasional dips into high equanimity and rapture and forays into 3rd/4th jhana concentration.
interesting_sentence: "It really is like sitting in the middle of a donut, but the donut itself is made of almost no solidity, so it's like I have to just hold it very gently and broadly with my attention." —Nihila
helpful_sentence: "Also remember the curse of the meditator: 'suffering less but noticing it more'. Sometimes the meditator feels like they are in a worse place, but all their friends and family see a person getting healthier and happier." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners dealing with kundalini/nadi imbalances and prolonged dark night cycling who want a candid, granular day-by-day account.

author: Emil Jensen
