summary: Asks the community why he falls asleep almost instantly within 5-10 minutes when doing noting or kasina practice while looking for the three characteristics at night, and receives suggestions on posture, diet, timing, and investigating the drowsiness itself.
interesting_sentence: "Who is drowsiness happening to?" —Z
helpful_sentence: "What is drowsiness? What does it feel like somatically? Where is 'drowsiness' located spatially in your body? Is drowsiness hot, cold, solid, heavy, light or some other quality?" —Z
who_should_read: Meditators troubleshooting chronic drowsiness or dullness during noting or kasina sits.

author: Lewis James
summary: Traces a path from severe depression and a traumatic psychedelic 'bodhisattva' vision through Shinzen-style noting and Mahamudra into reports of repeated momentary cessation 'glitches' and a profound formless-realms jhana session with Michael Taft, while placing himself in murky territory between 2nd and 3rd path.
interesting_sentence: "It was like an ocean made of tiny sparks, jumping in and out of this void, and I could feel the emotional tone of each and every one." —Lewis James
helpful_sentence: "The best way is to get clarity on your goals (type of practice and what you want from it) and then to follow the instructions exactly." —Richard Zen
who_should_read: Practitioners moving from a psychedelic-triggered spiritual crisis into structured Shinzen/Mahamudra style practice who want an honest account of cessation-like experiences and post-path integration struggles.

author: Fernsage S
summary: Detailed daily 50-minute sits split between concentration and Mahasi-style noting, tracking a struggle with dullness and 'mechanical' labelling while learning to note emotional states and feeling tones, with a resumed entry nine months later showing extended sit length.
interesting_sentence: "I can remember almost deliberately repressing it during my first Goenka retreat because it was so intense." —Fernsage S
helpful_sentence: "You could try investigating the feeling of doubt itself, e.g. ask yourself what do you feel in the body when you doubt your ability to note emotional states." —George S
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate noters battling dullness and mechanical labelling who want to add feeling-tone and emotional awareness to their practice.

author: Guillermo Z
summary: A brief introductory post noting use of an online Vipassana course plus the Equanimity timer and Goal Streaks habit-tracking apps to build a more consistent sitting practice.
interesting_sentence: "Goal Streaks : based on Jerry Seinfeld's productivity hack, in which you set a goal to do something so many times a week." —Guillermo Z
helpful_sentence: "I use both to make me sit more regularly." —Guillermo Z
who_should_read: New meditators looking for simple apps and habit-streak tricks to establish a regular sitting practice.

author: Nihila
summary: Months of daily noting and shamatha practice cycling through dark-night misery, disgust and fear tied to old trauma and left-side kundalini energy, culminating in metta-induced bliss and a self-assessed shift into equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "Omg it's doubt!! I can't stop crying and laughing" —Nihila
helpful_sentence: "The best way to gain knowledge of the Nana's and to understand them at a deep level is through repeated practice and direct experience of them." —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Practitioners working through trauma-linked dark night material who want a candid peer account of emotional purging and eventual equanimity.

author: finding-oneself
summary: Tracks a return to daily open-awareness, do-nothing, noting and TMI-style concentration practice after a foot injury and time off work, repeatedly touching dark-night-like unpleasant somatic states and equanimity while integrating mindfulness into a phlebotomy job.
interesting_sentence: "I saw this laser burn line travel about and inch, the stop at a point, where it burned for .1 seconds longer than the line and it popped." —finding-oneself ♤
helpful_sentence: "So in a very real way, troublesome thinking/behaviors are just a projection of unfelt feelings." —George S
who_should_read: Meditators balancing recovery from illness/injury with returning to a demanding job who want examples of blending do-nothing, noting and concentration techniques.

author: Liz
summary: Describes a blissful meditation session while ill on antibiotics that may have brushed against the A&P, followed by a return of anxious hypervigilance once the antibiotics wore off.
interesting_sentence: "it was like, nothing could hurt me while there was no \"me\" to hurt." —Liz
helpful_sentence: "it's also feeling a lot easier to accept those little thoughts without believing them, and just let them float away." —Liz
who_should_read: New meditators curious about tentative first A&P-adjacent experiences and managing anxiety while restarting practice.

author: Nav
summary: A months-long, meticulously structured (Mindfulness/Attention/Positives/Hindrances) daily noting log tracking full-body vibratory phenomena, persistent technique-related doubt and striving, and a gradual shift from dark-night discontent toward effortless, equanimous present-moment noting.
interesting_sentence: "Whole field of experience started shaking life in a vortex." —Nav
helpful_sentence: "We need not to ponder or analyise anything as this mind seems very quick in learning from the momentary matter of fact observation." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Serious noting practitioners wanting a model daily log format and encouragement to release striving around technique while moving through the dark night.

author: nook nook
summary: A non-native-English-speaking practitioner recounts three guided vipassana-noting retreats plus a 9-day solo retreat, self-diagnosing repeated cycling through dark-night stages into early Equanimity, with detailed shargrol-guided troubleshooting of doubt, technique-switching, and painful involuntary breathwork during re-observation.
interesting_sentence: "Sometimes even the eyes were strongly pulled backwards into my skull by their own muscles, or wanted to elevate together with my forehead, it was even getting physically a bit painful." —nook nook
helpful_sentence: "Never try to use noting to make an experience go away or stop happening. Whatever happens is not an impediment to meditation if you can note it. If you can note it, you are mindful. Simple." —shargrol
who_should_read: Intermediate meditators confused about self-diagnosing insight stages who want a detailed peer discussion of balancing concentration vs mindfulness and re-observation breathwork difficulties.

author: Nihila
summary: A recap log tracing a CPTSD-linked 2016 drug-induced psychotic break/A&P event and a 2018 Mooji-triggered 'I AM Nothing' awakening, followed by renewed noting practice and a later relapse into opiate use and suicidal ideation.
interesting_sentence: "There was this sort of sucking inwards, completely letting go, and I had the realization that 'I know nothing, I AM Nothing'." —Nihila
helpful_sentence: "Instead of disbelieving the thoughts try and welcome the guests which arrive and pass away in your mind." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Practitioners dealing with trauma and addiction alongside meditation who want an honest account of how spiritual insight and psychological crisis can intertwine.

log: First thread, First practice log [Travis Gene McKinstry] [MIGRATE]
author: Travis Gene McKinstry
summary: A beginner's detailed noting log that progresses from a 12-day solo retreat through Mind and Body, Cause and Effect, and a clearly identified A&P, into a rocky Dark Night and light Equanimity while preparing for a future 10-day Goenka retreat.
interesting_sentence: "(It's not the despair. I can take the despair. It's the hope I can't stand. — John Cleese)" —Dream Walker
helpful_sentence: "The problem with concentration practices is that they repress these habits and when the benefits fade they just come back again." —Richard Zen
who_should_read: New noting practitioners who want a granular, mentor-supported record of moving from mind-and-body through A&P into equanimity.

author: Mark Sl
summary: After years of technique-switching and inconsistent practice, the author restarts with Shinzen's See-Hear-Feel noting and Do Nothing, reporting spontaneous body tremors and kriyas alongside a TRE practice.
interesting_sentence: "My jaw moving side to side and up an down rapidly, my spine rounding and flexing, shivers, spasms..." —Mark Sl
helpful_sentence: "does this additional practice I am doing contribute to my capacity to sit, relax and be aware?" —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Chronic technique-switchers who need encouragement to commit to a single formal practice again.

author: N ELB
summary: A detailed day-by-day account of a 10-day Goenka retreat moving through terror and depersonalization into what the author describes as a disappearance of the borders of awareness on day 8, followed by post-retreat nondual compassion experiences.
interesting_sentence: "the borders of awareness just disappeared without having had the correlated mental consciousness of it." —N ELB
helpful_sentence: "In my opinion, the pure technique would have led me nowhere, it's way too rigid and unrespectful of the natural flow of experience." —N ELB
who_should_read: Goenka retreatants curious how a classic 10-day sit can produce dark night terror followed by a cessation-like event and lasting nondual shifts.

log: Notes from a 9 day solo retreat
author: Emil Jensen
summary: A meticulous session-by-session solo retreat log cycling through sexual urges, terror, a sleep-paralysis fear episode, and re-observation rage into a long Equanimity plateau, ending the retreat without a new fruition.
interesting_sentence: "The yard outside was full of sparks from someone using the angle grinder to cut open a door leading into the yard." —Emil Jensen
helpful_sentence: "It has something to do with reactionary habit patterns of the mind, creating these states of struggle with what is, in the body and the mind." —Emil Jensen
who_should_read: Experienced yogis wanting a granular model of what a full Dark-Night-to-Equanimity cycle looks like session by session on a long solo retreat.

author: Nihila
summary: A newcomer to the forum works through months of intense Dark Night cycling (fear, misery, disgust, DfD, re-observation) alongside childhood trauma and a past psychotic break, reporting purging, kundalini-like energy shifts along the left side of the body, brief A&P and Equanimity touches, and support from trauma therapy.
interesting_sentence: "It's like a combination of excitement, misery, dread, fear, anger. All at the same time. And then the total dissolving on top of it." —Nihila
helpful_sentence: "Giving attention to your Earth element stuff is very helpful: good food, cute animals, friends and loved ones, favorite books, exercise, paying taxes and doing household chores." —Aviva HaMakom
who_should_read: Meditators with a trauma history cycling through Dark Night stages who want a candid, emotionally raw account with peer support and cautionary advice about pacing.

author: Eric D
summary: A former intensive Tantric Buddhist practitioner, now recovering from a painful guru-disciple rupture and years away from formal practice, restarts with daily 45-minute 'Shargrol Sits' (breath counting, noting sensations/emotions/thoughts), tracking chronic fascia tension, a possible nimitta, and low-grade background sadness.
interesting_sentence: "During the counting breaths phase I successively let the awareness soak into the nostrils, sinuses, throat, chest, diaphragm and lower part of the torso... At some point there was a kind of centralizing bright spot in the upper half of the closed eyes visual field." —Eric D
helpful_sentence: "emotions don't need to be loud, they're basically sensations with a bit more \"meaning\" and they can be fleeting and imperminant just like sensations... In the beginning things seem small, but when you really start seeing them they seem big, and eventually you get so good at seeing them that they become small again." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners restarting practice after a spiritual-teacher rupture or burnout who want a detailed model of structured daily noting sessions.

log: Newb starting again (15min daily for now)
author: Tony Norris
summary: A father of young children restarts a modest 15-20 minute daily do-nothing/breath practice amid household chaos and relationship strain, experiments with metta and forgiveness meditation, and signs up for a TWIM retreat after struggling to sustain consistency.
interesting_sentence: "I'm tried on the frame that meditation is kind of like having a go @ yourself, like standing up to 'yourself' in a way like you would a bully, to see what this person is really made of." —Tony Norris
helpful_sentence: "With practice, the cycles get longer (few reps in the same time)... If you keep at it, and get it to work, you will have a fairly easy example of how the mind can, in fact, be trained and reconditioned so it functions differently than before the training." —Martin
who_should_read: Busy parents or beginners struggling with consistency who want relatable, low-pressure encouragement to rebuild a short daily practice.

log: notes on my practice (inspired by actualism)
author: Beoman Claudiu Dragon Emu Fire Golem
summary: A long-running actualism practice journal documenting sensory-attentiveness exercises, PCE glimpses, jhana experiments, a 5-HTP sleep experiment tied to vivid dreams and possible A&P/dark-night cycling, and a breakup that becomes fodder for investigating despair and self-centered attachment via the actualism method.
interesting_sentence: "i felt some 'soft' pressure over my head, and just had the sensible idea - hey, i bet i can drop this! so i let it manifest, and with an act of intention that would be more accurately described as it happening on its own, there was a very noticeable discontinuity (fruition) and it was gone." —Beoman Claudiu Dragon Emu Fire Golem
helpful_sentence: "at some point i understood that naivete, in actualism, is the same as equanimity, in buddhism.. a not clinging to what is currently happening, a not clinging to what might happen, a not clinging to what happened in the past." —Beoman Claudiu Dragon Emu Fire Golem
who_should_read: Actualism practitioners interested in a detailed, discursive first-person account of sensuous attention, PCE-chasing, and using relationship despair as practice material.

author: Nicholas H
summary: Recounts a first 10-day Goenka retreat followed by a week logging roughly 11.6 hours of anapanasati across multiple daily sits, plus yoga nidra, buddho mantra, and metta.
interesting_sentence: "Based off of Daniel saying in a few podcasts that those who are 'seekers' have likely passed the arising and passing away stage at some point in their lives, I'm guessing that may have occured when I was 21" —Nicholas H
helpful_sentence: "This lets the mind return to the chosen object without worry or agitation." —Martin
who_should_read: Beginners who just finished a first Goenka retreat and want a candid week-by-week account of ramping up daily anapanasati practice.

author: genaro
summary: Explains abandoning John Yates' TMI due to creeping doubt, then treats Sila (morality) as a contemplative practice in itself while planning to try Brasington-style jhana and Burbea-style emptiness contemplation.
interesting_sentence: "Eventually i read to the end of the book and discovered that it describes a practice that requires constant topping up else you lose the results" —genaro
helpful_sentence: "It's a very humbling process - until you try to express something with clarity you don't realise how poorly you understand it." —genaro
who_should_read: Practitioners who stalled out on The Mind Illuminated due to doubt and want a wry, philosophical take on treating morality itself as a practice.

log: Look how it's Mahamudra and Tonglen
author: Papa Che Dusko
summary: Logs near-daily 45-minute walking sessions cycling between Kenneth-Folk-style Listening Mahamudra, Tonglen, and 'Look How Its' through an intense Dark Night marked by family conflict, body pain, nicotine/alcohol cravings, and spontaneous doerless non-dual episodes, closing with a shift toward equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "There was very much awake awareness of this hand movement doing itself, refering back to no one." —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "What ultimately goes away with enlightenment... is something that wasn't there in the first place." —shargrol
who_should_read: Yogis deep in a Dark Night cycle who want a raw, day-by-day account of blending Tibetan-flavored Tonglen and Mahamudra practice with real family stress.

author: Hugh Fox
summary: Logs daily seated and walking Mahasi-style noting sessions, refining vocabulary between breath-anchored and freestyle (Ken Folk/Stephen Heyes) labeling while reporting energetic rushes, twitching, and swinging moods.
interesting_sentence: "I sat 90 feet in the air on a small platform yesterday, and the thought and adjoining feeling arose that there is no division between the thoughts and the thinker." —Hugh Fox
helpful_sentence: "From my experience, it's the rate of sensations that determines whether full words, syllables, or simple noticing works best. There is a sweet spot, but that sweet spot is a moving target." —John M
who_should_read: New Mahasi-noting practitioners who want to see how labeling technique evolves alongside early energetic and A&P-like phenomena.

log: Polly Ester’s practice log - a pilot project
author: Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
summary: Chronicles a systematic shift from concentration-heavy energy-body and finger-touch exercises into Mahasi-style noting, working through dissolution and repeated A&P-like energy surges while explicitly relating meditative phenomena to her ADHD and autism.
interesting_sentence: "It makes me feel very... mortal. And fleshy, sort of. It’s repulsive." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
helpful_sentence: "Look for the 3 characteristics in everything. That's the keys to the kingdom." —Chris M
who_should_read: Neurodivergent (ADHD/autistic) meditators wanting a granular, sensory-level account of noting practice and how insight-stage phenomena interact with atypical processing.

author: SushiK
summary: A near-daily, highly structured Mahasi-noting log from Shanghai tracking body sensations, energy surges, and mood swings over several months, cycling between dukkha-nana-like frustration and stretches of stable, equanimity-flavored calm.
interesting_sentence: "well, this was a terrible session and you're obviously all fucked up and wasting your time." —Tim Farrington
helpful_sentence: "The breath is useful mainly because it will always be there when you bring your attention \"home,\" for whatever reason." —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: Vipassana noting practitioners who want a granular, day-by-day phenomenology of cycling through the dukkha ñanas toward equanimity.

author: C T W
key_words: identity collapse from atheism to spirituality
summary: An intense, unstructured noting log in which spontaneous kriyas and energy movements arise merely from watching an Ingram Dharma talk, followed by a self-diagnosed acute Dark Night of pervasive unpleasantness, addictive relapse behavior, and a radical identity collapse the author suspects caps a decade-long unpracticed dark night.
interesting_sentence: "That would imply (it just occurred) that this is part of the chicken herding process. Yesterday, I was watching the chickens, today, I tried to chase them." —C T W
helpful_sentence: "simply being aware of the fear and anxiety keep doing that, think of it as resolving itself as long as you stay aware and don't mess with it" —josh r s
who_should_read: Meditators experiencing spontaneous, unintentional Dark-Night-like breakdowns outside formal sitting who want reassurance and a map-based vocabulary for what's happening.

author: Stranger_Loop Stranger_Loop
summary: A months-long, near-daily log of noting, body scanning, and kasina experimentation by a self-described post-emptiness-insight practitioner who cycles through intense energetic phenomena and rapid insight-stage shifts en route to a self-diagnosed third path.
interesting_sentence: "I could also use that vision to look around in a sphere with a radius of zero. This was super cool because it showed me that there is no center from which I am looking." —Stranger_Loop Stranger_Loop
helpful_sentence: "The trick seems to be to soften eyesight and try to look below/above where I could normally see when actually I can look in any direction." —Stranger_Loop Stranger_Loop
who_should_read: Practitioners deep in post-stream-entry cycling who want a candid, high-volume account of energetic phenomena, kasina work, and the disorientation of self-diagnosing higher paths.

author: Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
summary: A long-time post-stream-entry Dzogchen/Vajrayana practitioner blends daily sadhana, trekcho, jhana via nada sound, and vipassana while cycling through dukkha nanas amid a serious winter depression.
interesting_sentence: "And there it is, the unfuckupable Dharmatta." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
helpful_sentence: "Concentration is the result of finding jhanic consciousness. When it is found it will replace current consciousness." —Pawel K
who_should_read: Practitioners blending Dzogchen/Vajrayana with vipassana insight-cycling who want an honest look at how depression, ADHD, and neurodivergence intersect with advanced practice.

author: SushiK
summary: A Mahasi-noting practitioner works with a personal coach through repeated A&P/dark-night/equanimity cycles, later blending in TWIM metta-jhana practice that produces vivid near-cessation events with head-drop and fear.
interesting_sentence: "I remember thinking "Oh it thinks it's dying"." —SushiK
helpful_sentence: "The only reasonable thing to do is sit with it and get comfortable seeing it for what it is - thoughts, feelings, sensations." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners combining Mahasi noting with TWIM metta-jhana who want a candid, coached account of cycling through A&P, dark night, and equanimity.

author: John Wilde
summary: A methodical concentration practitioner logs incremental breath-focus progress using the five hindrances and four frames of reference, ultimately choosing a Thanissaro-style shamatha-then-vipassana approach over Mahasi noting.
interesting_sentence: "Identity and relationship are inseparable." —John Wilde
helpful_sentence: "What was important for me personally to recognize was that for insight to arise, I needed a calm mind; I needed first to create the condition and space for insight to arise before it would arise on a relatively consistent basis." —Ian And
who_should_read: Meditators wanting a granular, day-by-day account of building stable concentration via breath and the five hindrances before moving into insight practice.

author: Neem Nyima
summary: A long-retreat yogi stuck at the Late Mastery/high-equanimity stage uses circumferential, awareness-taking-awareness attention to soften head-and-neck tension blocks and re-engage investigation of the three characteristics.
interesting_sentence: "And if your up in Equanimity like me keep going deeper, there is a bottom and you will sink to it, and drown in the wisdom of the beyond." —Neem Nyima
helpful_sentence: "Instead of backing off I increased the dosage to 3-4 hours of practice a night, and the day after the gnarly depression lifted, the practice started producing blissful concentration." —Matthew
who_should_read: Yogis stuck in late-stage equanimity/mastery seeking concrete body-based tactics — circumferential awareness and tension release — for breaking through subtle blocks.

author: Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
summary: A Tibetan-tantric practitioner logs daily Vajrasattva ngöndro, mantra, and yoga sessions under Lama Lena while managing depression, chronic pain, ADHD, and autism, reporting kriyas, piti, an 'impermanence cessation', and rapid A&P/dark-night/equanimity cycling.
interesting_sentence: "The process is my main teacher. It doesn't charge anything if I listen to it, but it costs too much when I don't." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
helpful_sentence: "If it feels uncomfortable, that is what needs your attention. That is the guide." —Bardo
who_should_read: Practitioners blending Vajrayana ngöndro and tantric visualization with pragmatic-dharma insight mapping, especially those juggling meditation with chronic illness or neurodivergence.

log: Time to force things (Adam's practice journal)
author: Adam
summary: Adam commits to keeping the eight precepts and daily reporting to force consistency, builds reliable access to a soft-to-hard 4th jhana via Thanissaro-style whole-body breathing, and experiments with 'juxtaposing' sensations and bare awareness inside jhana to dissolve tension and anger.
interesting_sentence: "I relaxed into 4th and then started paying bare attention to the whole body panorama and got this incredibly strange effect of being zoomed in like a microscope while at the same time seeing the whole body at once." —Adam
helpful_sentence: "I've found that you just need to keep probing at the edges of experience. Over time you are able to let go more. To trust more. Forcing it only seems to produce stress and fear. Easing into it bit by bit seems to be the way forward." —Bagpuss The Gnome
who_should_read: Concentration-focused practitioners wanting a candid, technical account of building strong access to 4th jhana and using bare attention within it to work with anger and tension.

log: Practice update: continued development of mindfulness
author: Adam
summary: Over four months Adam integrates bare-attention practice into daily life off the cushion, reporting improved relationships with his brother and mother, recurring depressive doubt about whether he's practicing correctly, and a growing sense that goalless direct observation is unshakeable by any experience.
interesting_sentence: "Practice is about melting ourselves into puddles of water through the fire of attention. We become formless, thus unharmable and we can start melting those around us." —Adam
helpful_sentence: "In the factors of enlightenment, I think metta is a huge precursor to passaddhi (relaxation or tranquility), and viriya (energy) and (piti) joy." —katy steger
who_should_read: Meditators trying to carry bare-attention practice into relationships and daily life who want an honest account of cycling between doubt-driven depression and stable, panoramic acceptance.

author: Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
summary: Linda ('Polly Ester') logs months of Michael Taft's Reversing the Stack nonduality course, working through Tourette's tics, trauma release, and autism-related sensory patterns via mantra, Chenrezig, and earworm practice, while explicitly declining to claim 3rd or 4th path.
interesting_sentence: "It was like the web of light, the flower of life, folded into itself and collapsed, not into a singularity but into vaste spaciousness that had no center." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
helpful_sentence: "These different ways of seeing don't have to stick around permanently, and you don't have to be absorbed in every aspect." —Not two
who_should_read: Neurodivergent practitioners and post-stream-entry meditators wanting a candid, embodied account of integrating nonduality practice into daily trauma work and disability.

log: Time to share and keep track, now that things are taking off.
author: Mind over easy
summary: A 19-year-old first-time insight meditator documents crossing the A&P within days of starting vipassana, then cycling rapidly through dissolution, fear, and equanimity-like states while receiving detailed nana-by-nana coaching from veteran poster Tommy M.
interesting_sentence: "It was dizzying, electric, almost sexual in an odd way, and powerful." —Mind over easy
helpful_sentence: "Cycling is exactly what it sounds like - You go 'round in circles over and over, but this is part of the process and leads to deeper insights as you repeatedly investigate, what appears to be, the same territory." —Tommy M
who_should_read: Brand-new vipassana practitioners wanting a play-by-play of the A&P and early dark night, plus a model of how a mentor talks a beginner through nana identification without over-claiming.

author: Daniel M Ingram
summary: Daniel Ingram reports a sudden, seemingly permanent shift in which a lifelong 'grayish veil' over perception vanished, sharpening visual/auditory clarity and blunting food-reward responses, prompting forum comparisons to PCEs, Actual Freedom, and vagal-nerve neurochemistry.
interesting_sentence: "...this sense of a grayish veil over perception that I didn't even know was there felt like it ripped through the back of my head and vanished, never to return..." —Daniel M Ingram
helpful_sentence: "I notice this during a PCE, it's not that it's not enjoyable but it seems like food becomes more functional than rewarding." —Tommy M
who_should_read: Experienced post-4th-path practitioners curious how baseline perception can keep shifting long after path attainments, and how AF/Actualism concepts get debated on a Theravada-insight-flavored board.

author: Sunny S
summary: Sunny S restarts a daily noting/Do-Nothing practice after burning out from OCD and anxiety following a 2020 A&P, experimenting between classic noting, breath-resting per Ken McLeod, and Recollective Awareness Meditation.
interesting_sentence: "What followed was a period of bliss, rapture, feeling awesome about everything, and thinking I had attained stream entry." —Sunny S
helpful_sentence: "I realized that I was being greedy about noting, wanting to note as much as possible to avoid failure and make progress." —Sunny S
who_should_read: Meditators with OCD or anxiety who want a candid account of relapsing into practice after burnout and rebuilding a gentler, more sustainable noting routine.

author: Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
summary: Chronicles a stretch of practice moving through Kundalini yoga, a tantric visualization retreat, and detailed jhana refinement while working through endometriosis pain, ADHD, and vitamin deficiencies during the review phase after second path.
interesting_sentence: "It was warm! I kept moving my hand through it." —Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
helpful_sentence: "The goal here is to hold the mind and attention loosesly enough that you allow it to do what it wants." —shargrol
who_should_read: Experienced post-stream-entry practitioners interested in fine-grained jhana phenomenology, energy work, and integrating chronic illness into practice.

author: Tom C
summary: A beginner's month-long daily Mahasi-style noting log tracking piti sensations, itch tolerance, and an emerging off-cushion concentration state triggered by watching TV and video calls.
interesting_sentence: "I name these 'head-gasms' and I can only count a few in my time (maybe 6-8 or so times in past year - normally when I am practicing daily)." —Tom C
helpful_sentence: "Instead of stopping with meditation (falling because of hindrance) include freestyle noting aloud 1-5 sensations a second, keep noting unpleasant, desire to get up, ... speak out with loud voice to really hear yourself." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: New meditators starting a Mahasi noting practice who want an honest, granular account of early piti, itching, and dullness.

author: Brandon Dayton
summary: Describes using vipassana to break apart the sensations of a ruptured eardrum and ear infection over a painful night, achieving partial but not high-level equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "I was just sitting on my bed with a goofy grin on my face while my wife was putting the kids to bed." —Brandon Dayton
helpful_sentence: "I find it useful to recognize that the pain sensations are only a tiny percentage of the sensations occurring at that moment." —Lars
who_should_read: Meditators dealing with acute physical pain who want practical peer accounts of applying vipassana and broadened attention to reduce reactivity.

author: supaluqi
summary: Tracks a shift from comic disidentification through fear-laden hara and head-tension work into a koan-triggered breakthrough the author calls passing through 'the gateless gate'.
interesting_sentence: "It seems I passed through the gateless gate about 5 days ago." —supaluqi
helpful_sentence: "If a technique or something works... I stop, my mind then critiques and shreds it to crap, I then go on looking for something else, forgetting the previous, only to remember months later and wonder why I stopped." —supaluqi
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in koan and self-inquiry combined with somatic hara work, and in candid accounts of fear surfacing during a breakthrough.

author: Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
summary: Chronicles a stretch of cycling through the dukkha ñanas toward third path, blending vipassana and hard/soft jhana exploration, hemi-sync binaural beats, lucid dreaming, and spontaneous movement practice, all while navigating ADHD, Tourette's, chronic pain, and a missing cat.
interesting_sentence: "It's like a spasm or cramp (and I have Tourette's, so for me it litterally is - the tics are all selfings made obvious)." —Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
helpful_sentence: "Awake awareness is how you experience reality when the mind doesn't make subject/object out of everything." —Sigma Tropic
who_should_read: Practitioners deep in post-stream-entry cycling who want detailed jhana and dukkha-ñana phenomenology alongside neurodivergent (ADHD, Tourette's) practice adaptations.

author: Papa Che Dusko
summary: A mostly off-topic personal log covering guitar jamming, aquarium and shrimp-keeping, and family life, with brief first-person reflections on a work-triggered PTSD-like reaction and on explaining the six realms and four-path model to his young son.
interesting_sentence: "We looked into each other's eyes and shook hands and chanted Om Tare Tu Tare Ture Soha so she seals the deal!" —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "I wondered if this experience would lead to a PTSD related panic attack if the mind was not trained in attention?" —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: DhO regulars curious about a veteran practitioner's daily life and casual reflections rather than dense meditation technique.

author: Tom C
summary: Returns to logging after a lull, combining Mahasi-style noting with newly-adopted non-dual self-inquiry inspired by Rupert Spira, including a sit where intense facial itching gave way to sensations of the limbs and jaw feeling unusually large.
interesting_sentence: "What sort of shocked me is I realise I have no internal dialogue." —Tom C
helpful_sentence: "Its better contiune noting longer than stop too soon!" —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Noting practitioners curious how self-inquiry and non-dual pointers can be layered onto Mahasi-style practice.

author: Michal
summary: Logs daily hours of candle-kasina practice combined with holotropic breathing shortly after being discharged from a psychiatric ward, seeking a meditation partner for accountability ahead of an upcoming Vipassana retreat.
interesting_sentence: "I am going to a Vipassana retreat - 19. 8. - 30. 8. I just got out of the psych ward haha." —Michal
helpful_sentence: "typical advice is to be careful with concentration if you are in a mental health situation." —Misha -
who_should_read: Practitioners combining intensive concentration methods with breathwork who want a candid account of balancing practice with mental-health recovery.

author: Gunnar Johansson
summary: Opens a practice log built around the Sankhitta Sutta, outlining a first month of right-effort training via Vimalaramsi-style forgiveness meditation and Lester Levenson's Release Technique to work through the five hindrances.
interesting_sentence: "As main practice I will use forgivness meditation as Bhante Vimalaramsi teaches it in Guide to forgivness meditation." —Gunnar Johansson
helpful_sentence: "And releasing five hindrences using Lester Levensons release method." —Gunnar Johansson
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in combining sutta-based right-effort training with forgiveness meditation and hindrance-release techniques as a structured monthly plan.

author: Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
summary: A dense, near-daily contextual log tracking ADHD medication, diet-driven food intolerances, and Kundalini-style energy surges alongside jhana, fire kasina, tantric breathwork, and structured attention/awareness balancing exercises.
interesting_sentence: "I put a tiny peanut butter jar on my altar as an offering just now, as a message to my craving patterns." —Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
helpful_sentence: "My suggestion would be that you use such phenomena, in case they occur as a bio feedback mechanism telling you to shift power towards awareness." —Adi Vader
who_should_read: Neurodivergent practitioners managing medication, food sensitivities, and energetic phenomena who want a granular model of how daily context shapes practice.

author: Papa Che Dusko
summary: Using Shargrol's structured 'S-meditation' (mantra, noting, radical allowing), Papa Che Dusko logs dark-night-flavored fear/disgust/misery ñanas, conceit, and a core fear-of-abandonment pattern while juggling new fatherhood and a job search.
interesting_sentence: "BTW, today while changing my son's shoity diapers I've noticed how luminous they were! Like all of it! The smell, the content, and the diapers too! All was luminous! All was MIND!" —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "And fear is basically an unwillingness to experience a sensation." —shargrol
who_should_read: Sleep-deprived new parents wanting a candid, humor-laced look at working dark-night material and core psychological patterns into daily life.

author: Truth Seeker
summary: In a mostly theoretical opening post, Truth Seeker outlines an original 'God Loop' cosmology synthesizing world religions and states an intent to begin jhana or moment-to-moment practice without yet having sat formally.
interesting_sentence: "You have Self (Unknown), The Flow, and the Known (Space-Time)." —Truth Seeker
helpful_sentence: "At a certain point though, that fuel burns itself out, and you realize that you're not going to get anywhere unless you actually develop a consistent meditation practice." —George S
who_should_read: Newcomers drawn to syncretic theorizing about religion and consciousness before committing to a formal meditation technique.

author: Puente
summary: Puente narrates claiming stream entry through MCTB-style rapid noting despite never attending a retreat, then tracks repeat fruitions, strings of rapid cessations, and a recursive 'who's watching the watcher' inquiry he suspects may be second path.
interesting_sentence: "Awoke last night from a very vivid dream to an EXCRUTIATINGLY loud noise that was completely unrelated to the dream (like a dial-up modem ripping reality apart)." —Puente
helpful_sentence: "There's always more to see/learn and some stages are great for building 'I am a great meditator' illusion." —Yilun Ong
who_should_read: Concentration-oriented meditators curious whether stream entry can arise from daily noting practice alone, without ever going on retreat.

log: Swag's practice log (and other stuff too I guess)
author: Swag Axe
summary: Swag Axe recounts moving from psychedelic-triggered 'oneness' experiences and a TMI Stage 4/5 plateau into Rob Burbea-style metta and samadhi work, reaching first jhana on a self-guided retreat and preparing for his first group insight retreat.
interesting_sentence: "Had one really kind of blow-out experience where I felt this oppressive feeling come on and suddenly disappear followed by a blinding white light, an intense existential fear and lots of piti." —Swag Axe
helpful_sentence: "Every yogi should have multiple techniques in their toolbox if they wish to attain Stream Entry" —Pepe ·
who_should_read: Trauma- and addiction-affected practitioners wanting a candid TMI-to-Burbea transition story before their first formal insight retreat.

author: Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
summary: Documents months of intensive daily fire kasina and Wu Chi/balloon standing practice, tracking the shift from murk and a bouncing dot to a stable photorealistic 'learning sign,' alongside dukkha-ñana cycling, kriyas, and OBE attempts.
interesting_sentence: "Weirdly enough, after that I hit both jhanic territory and a cessation." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
helpful_sentence: "Concern with what other people think of us is a feature of the fetter of 'mana' or measurement (mistranslated as conceit)." —Adi Vader
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry fire kasina practitioners wanting granular phenomenology of the learning sign, nada sound, and energetic standing practice alongside candid dukkha-ñana cycling.

author: Papa Che Dusko
summary: Chronicles switching between Shamatha and freestyle noting-aloud to fit a physically exhausting postal-service job, tracking body jerks, murk visuals, and a self-observed 'mind movement' of clinging while cycling the dukkha ñanas post-stream-entry.
interesting_sentence: "I really feel as if I was never practicing in my life and this is my very first sit ever!" —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "It's like really realizing that holding that hot coal really hurts and the hand just lets go of it." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Working practitioners balancing a physically demanding job with consistent practice who want a candid, detail-rich post-stream-entry noting log.

author: Truth Seeker
summary: A self-taught practitioner spends over a year building and repeatedly revising a personal conceptual 'awakening system' around cycling contraction/expansion, with only sporadic actual sitting practice and edible-assisted introspection experiments, before committing to jhana practice via fire kasina.
interesting_sentence: "To become and die like Jesus taking humanity's suffering and going you join with god is what you aim for." —Truth Seeker
helpful_sentence: "And since there is only sensations, there is no SELF to be found anywhere." —Kaloyan Stefanov
who_should_read: Conceptually-minded beginners prone to over-theorizing who want a mirror for how doctrinal rumination can substitute for actual sitting practice.

author: Stacia O'Neil
summary: A brand-new practitioner logs her first short concentration and noting sits (10-20 minutes) while being informally guided one-on-one through early MCTB chapters and a first Dzogchen sit by a mentor.
interesting_sentence: "I want to emphasize to everyone that rather than this be a traditional teacher role, I am imformally heloping someone with who I have a very strong bond to see if she has the interest and durability for a journey." —Sam Gentile
helpful_sentence: "Rigpa is everywhere" —Sam Gentile
who_should_read: Absolute beginners curious what the very first days of one-on-one MCTB-based mentorship look like in practice.

log: Switching to Fire Kasina after months of continuous dropping from Stage 11
author: Emma pham
summary: A monastic practitioner stuck cycling in and out of Stage 11/Equanimity for over 20 cycles adds Fire Kasina to her noting practice and works through forum Q&A on murk, boundless-light absorption, and whether single-object noting conflicts with 4th jhana awareness.
interesting_sentence: "Then a 3D symbol of a dhamma wheel appeared . then a trunk of time went off" —Emma pham
helpful_sentence: "It usually goes: Clear/Focused/Onepointed -> Unclear/Unfocused/Diffuse -> Clear/Spacious/Broad in my experience." —Ryan Kay
who_should_read: Advanced Equanimity-stage practitioners plateaued or repeatedly dropping who want practical troubleshooting on murk, boundless light, and single- vs multi-object noting.

author: Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
summary: Chronicles daily fire kasina, standing/reclining energetic-disentangling practice, and jhana experiments with a practice partner, alongside antidepressant withdrawal sensations, astral projection, and dream-teachings pointing toward Nirodha Samapatti.
interesting_sentence: "It looked like a colored illustration from a children's book, featuring a penguin with a red bowtie." —Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
helpful_sentence: "Get really really concentrated. Attention focused on the task at hand, awareness far and wide and brilliantly powerful" —Adi Vader
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners curious about fire kasina, dream yoga, and navigating energetic/kundalini-like phenomena alongside psychiatric medication.

author: Papa Che Dusko
key_words: Ken McLeod's Wake Up to Your Life
summary: Documents a return to noting-aloud practice after quitting nicotine, moving from confusion and dark-night-like agitation into a self-inquiry variant of noting inspired by Ken McLeod and Kenneth Folk.
interesting_sentence: "I'm officially confused. Have no fucking clue how to proceed or which direction to take." —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "The downside of noting is that it doesn't do much to chip away at the sense of an independent observer, which is where fundamental duality and fundamental unease is created." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners restarting practice after a habit change (like quitting smoking) who want practical noting variations for confusion and restlessness.

author: T
summary: Tracks a year-plus of daily noting and breath-focused sits punctuated by fire kasina, Wim Hof-style pranayama, recurring energetic kriyas, and repeated brief 'blip out' experiences the author eventually identifies as cessations.
interesting_sentence: "I also had a thought of something akin to 'hearing doesn't know sight' or somehow the idea that one is separate from the other entirely." —T
helpful_sentence: "So if you are observing the flow of air at the nostrils, try narrowing the focus to individual sensations in three or four points within each nostril." —Not two
who_should_read: Concentration-oriented practitioners wanting a candid, humor-laced account of fire kasina, Wim Hof breathing, and self-verifying cessation experiences.

log: Putting it out there: The journal of an investigation
author: Dauphin Supple Chirp
summary: A methodical, session-by-session noting log that tracks progressive vibration/A&P-like phenomena through equanimity into repeated cessations and phala samāpatti, leading the author to tentatively claim second path.
interesting_sentence: "I think I'm just going to start calling it second path." —Dauphin Supple Chirp
helpful_sentence: "It's this law that seems to govern so much of our practice: Whenever you want something, it's not going to happen, but once you have lost the desire for it, it will come easily." —Dauphin Supple Chirp
who_should_read: Vipassana practitioners who want a granular, minute-timed log tracking progress from stream entry through repeated cessations toward a second path claim.

log: Sylvain's practice log #1 after first 7 day retreat
author: Sylvain R
summary: Details daily sitting and walking practice at home and a Chan temple following a first 7-day retreat, tracking breath-location experiments, 'collapse' sensations, and careful safety-consciousness around possible kundalini phenomena.
interesting_sentence: "I wondered if the mind had exploded in the past from just being a small point in my body." —Sylvain R
helpful_sentence: "When things get complicated in meditation, I ask myself 'am I noticing the three poisons?' and 'am I being born in one of the six realms?'" —shargrol
who_should_read: Meditators returning from an intensive retreat who want a grounded, safety-conscious account of integrating energetic phenomena into householder life.

author: Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
summary: Detailed log of tantric pranayama, visualization and mantra practice under Michael Taft alongside candid LSD and foraged-mushroom trip reports, culminating in several impermanence-door cessations.
interesting_sentence: "It’s really fuckin absurd when you manage to take a shower while not believing in your bathroom." —Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
helpful_sentence: "A dharma friend explained that he prioritized stability over intensity." —Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in tantra-based practice (pranayama, visualization, mantra) combined with candid psychedelic trip reports and cessation phenomenology.

author: Yogi Bear
summary: Returning-to-practice log of daily breath-watching sits that surface waves of sadness/anxiety and hypnogogic imagery, ending with the author suspecting he has settled into a broad, effortless Equanimity-like state.
interesting_sentence: "Everything just feels like easy mode?" —Yogi Bear
helpful_sentence: "In fact, shargrol (a forum member) and Chris M suggested I try sort of a Do Nothing or Shikantaza approach this summer... and if anything the jhanic arc become more reliable." —Ryan Kay
who_should_read: Meditators returning to practice after a break who want reassurance about jhana accessibility and honest peer accounts of TWIM/Brasington-style techniques.

author: T
summary: Sporadic first-person log tracking Wim Hof breathing, nimitta and blackout/cessation-like experiences, persistent musical rumination, and a witnessing/void state explored via Loch Kelley-style unhooking, interwoven with combative philosophical banter with other posters.
interesting_sentence: "There was no me, and there was no body in the normal sense." —T
helpful_sentence: "I distinguish between the \"quiet,\" which this experience sounds like to me, and the \"silence,\" which is deeper and somehow \"alive.\"" —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: Meditators who enjoy irreverent forum banter alongside genuine reports of Wim Hof-style energy work, cessation-like blackouts, and witness-consciousness exploration.

author: synelg
summary: Progresses from 190 hours of Mahasi-style noting into Culadasa's TMI stage system, documenting a severe SSRI-related depression/suicidal crisis, chronic dullness battled with a self-devised 'tweezer' technique, and recurring purifications of old memories.
interesting_sentence: "I didn't sleep much as I was afraid my mind would escape from the top of my head if I did." —synelg
helpful_sentence: "If one decides to stop taking them, it is critical to taper the dosage over a long period of time (months or even years)." —Ward Law
who_should_read: TMI practitioners battling dullness and drowsiness, and meditators concerned about combining SSRIs with intensive noting practice, who want a candid account of managing both stably.

author: Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
key_words: stream entry confirmed by Michael Taft
summary: A neurodivergent yogi cycles through dark night terror, has her stream entry confirmed by teacher Michael Taft after several fruitions, and builds an elaborate personal model of fruition phenomenology while working with jhanas, Shinzen Young methods, and Unified Mindfulness coaching.
interesting_sentence: "Fruition really is mindblowing insights from just a blip of nothing." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
helpful_sentence: "It becomes fundamental suffering when there's a \"split\" - when the sensations of the cupcake and wanting it are not clearly seen, when the illusion of self is not seen through, when there's some struggle against the way things are right now." —J C
who_should_read: Practitioners wanting a richly detailed, phenomenologically obsessive account of the stream-entry process, fruition variety, and jhana mapping, especially neurodivergent meditators.

author: Jure K
summary: Logs daily noting-style sits while tapering off Prozac, tracking how quickly triggers from family conflict now resolve into equanimity, and briefly claiming then retracting a third-path attainment after getting no confirmation.
interesting_sentence: "I've just come off 40mg of Prozac. Not only have I experienced the worst of the withdrawal symptoms but I'm also somehow managing to just live my life with my stuff happening too." —Jure K
helpful_sentence: "Third path is the path people are most commonly wrong about because the road there is usually more fractal than anything they would have experienced before it." —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Meditators navigating medication tapering and family triggers who want a candid, unpolished look at applying equanimity practice under daily-life stress.

author: Saka Yuki
key_words: guru yoga of Padmasambhava and Yeshe Tsogyal
summary: A solo diary of taking up Open Heart Yoga, a pragmatic Vajrayana approach combining guru yoga, tantric visualization, and the "2-part formula" mantra technique to progress past a stalled Liberation-Unleashed-style emptiness insight.
interesting_sentence: "We could say that the path is a process of heating up that ice through a laser beam of clear seeing, melting it so that it becomes vapor, free energy that can be used and be in harmony within a space that opens up." —Saka Yuki
helpful_sentence: "guru yoga involves: (1) an action/intention: asking for a blessing from a master, or reciting a mantra associated with the guru and (2) attention: tuning into one's bodymind for the result of (1)." —Saka Yuki
who_should_read: Vajrayana-curious pragmatic dharma practitioners wanting a detailed, technique-by-technique account of guru yoga and tantric practice used to work past the first fetters.

author: William Quixote
key_words: Rob Burbea three characteristics guided meditation
summary: Restarting meditation after several years off, logs daily alternating concentration and insight sits, experimenting with body scanning, spaciousness, and not-self techniques, culminating in what he identifies as his first jhana via Leigh Brasington's smile-energy method.
interesting_sentence: "It built and built and spread filling my whole entire body with twitchy energy and bliss." —William Quixote
helpful_sentence: "If you can *accept* the frustration and welcome it gently into awareness then it will start to \"self-liberate\". It's a really powerful practice." —George S
who_should_read: Meditators restarting a lapsed practice who want a systematic, day-by-day account of alternating concentration and insight techniques leading toward first jhana.

author: Cristal C
summary: A brand-new meditator with synesthesia and hyperphantasia describes vivid, spontaneous imagery journeys (starfields, tunnels, control rooms) and bi-location-like awareness while meditating with a black-out mask, seeking a mentor to help interpret the phenomena.
interesting_sentence: "I sometimes explore ancient cities or other places like I am a drone or as if my awareness is attached to a drone." —Cristal C
helpful_sentence: "You should notice the 3C of everything you see during the meditation. These images are impermanent and constant changing - thus they are also impermanent - no self - suffering" —Emma pham
who_should_read: New meditators with unusually vivid inner imagery (synesthesia/hyperphantasia) wanting reassurance that intense visionary phenomena are a known, non-pathological part of deep meditative states.

author: Oliver Myth
summary: Describes a felicitous plateau reached through AYP practices (Kechari Mudra, nauli/uddiyana, loving-kindness, sensuousness) plus exploratory riding of the jhanas, with no formal insight-map framing.
interesting_sentence: "I rest in kechari for several hours a day at a minimum." —Oliver Myth
helpful_sentence: "Complete equanimity is found in receptive, non-reactive awareness. That is sati." —katy steger
who_should_read: Meditators curious about combining AYP/kundalini-style body practices (kechari, nauli) with jhana exploration and sensuousness as a path to sustained felicity.

author: Saka Yuki
summary: A detailed, near-daily noting practice log moving from concentration/visualization warm-ups into Mahasi-style vipassana, tracking energy phenomena, dreamy states, and mind-state labeling under mentorship from a fellow poster.
interesting_sentence: "I felt a ball of energy drilling down through the right channel into abdominal area, turning in direction and shooting upward. Quite painful." —Saka Yuki
helpful_sentence: "When I began to note seriously as a practice annica and annata showed up in an organic way without my having to parse them out from my experience, which led to stream entry shortly after." —Bill F
who_should_read: Self-taught noting practitioners stuck in dark-night-like territory who want a model of consistent, granular phenomenological logging with mentor feedback.

author: Qwentin Groth
summary: A vivid, self-aware log of involuntary and planned sits cycling through intense energetic phenomena, recurring thought-"walls" tied to parental images, and explicit uncertainty about being in Re-Observation versus early A&P.
interesting_sentence: "Reappearance of what I like to call 'the Matrix Wall'... compelled to lower my jaw to the ground and observe the sensations of what I like to think of as my teeth and jaw give up solidity and sink into the hardwood floor." —Qwentin Groth
helpful_sentence: "Most consistent meditators don't fall into problems of laziness, the fall into problems of trying to force things to happen the way they think it \"should\"." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners experiencing intense, unbidden energetic phenomena and repetitive thought-loops who want reassurance that easing effort (not forcing) helps navigate Re-Observation.

author: Tarver
key_words: Shinzen Young's Do Nothing / Five Ways
summary: A long-running, technique-hopping log (concentration, Goenka body-scanning, noting, fire kasina, Shinzen's Do Nothing) culminating in a self-declared "candidate" stream-entry moment during a 10-day course and a durable lift in long-standing depression.
interesting_sentence: "There are no Others, only Self. Are you willing to withdraw the projection?" —Tarver
helpful_sentence: "It is very common to start a practice, get agitated, leave the practice, look for a new practice, get agitated... To regularly note why one is doing the practice is as much an insight practice as anything." —katy steger
who_should_read: Technique-hopping practitioners balancing heavy doctrinal theorizing with practice who want a candid, long-arc account of grinding toward a self-assessed stream entry.

author: Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
summary: An exceptionally detailed, near-daily log blending Mahasi noting, shamatha jhana work, yoga/qigong, and fire kasina, reporting an explicit stream-entry claim with repeated fruitions and ongoing exploration of possible further path shifts.
interesting_sentence: "As far as I understand it, I had stream entry on March 20th this year." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
helpful_sentence: "This is not a mapping practice! It's a sensations practice!" —J C
who_should_read: Neurodivergent (ADHD/autistic) practitioners wanting an unusually granular, post-stream-entry log covering jhana cultivation, retreat experience, and honest doubt about further attainments.

author: Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
summary: Documents an intensive stretch of daily jhana practice in which the nada sound becomes her personal nimitta, repeated 'unknowing event' cessations lead her to tentatively claim second path, all while managing Tourette's tics, autism/ADHD, and allergy flare-ups.
interesting_sentence: "Oups. In merging with that vibrating sound, I followed it into cessation in the space between the sound waves, or something like that. Twice." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
helpful_sentence: "But once you are mindful and equanimious, try to get insight into the dependent arising of the tics." —Not two
who_should_read: Jhana practitioners hunting for a reliable personal nimitta and curious how cessation phenomenology can interweave with neurodivergence and chronic illness.

author: josh r s
summary: Chronicles months of Thanissaro-Bhikkhu-style breath jhana experimentation—reaching a self-identified 4th jhana and repeated PCE states—culminating in a tentative, self-doubted claim of sutta-style stream entry and a deep dive into the 'attention wave' model.
interesting_sentence: "the shift i am proposing to have had is sutta-style stream entry, i am aware this is a fairly extraordinary claim because i have been practicing for a relatively short period of time." —josh r s
helpful_sentence: "Important to do this kind of relaxing of cognition only when concentration is high, otherwise it tends to result in some kind of dullness." —End in Sight
who_should_read: Concentration-focused meditators drawn to Thanissaro-style 'figure it out yourself' breath jhana who want a granular, experimental account of jhana mechanics and the attention-wave model.

author: Brendan I
summary: A self-described decade-long 'dark night yogi' logs mahasi-noting sits marked by violent rapture, a primal fear episode, and open questions about investigating the three characteristics and his own emotional disconnection.
interesting_sentence: "Couldn't tell you where I'm at in terms of POI other than from all descriptions I've read, I've been a 'dark night yogi' for nearly a decade" —Brendan I
helpful_sentence: "Everything is game for observing the 3Cs and decomposing how your whole experience is built up out of raw sensations" —George S
who_should_read: Long-term dark-night practitioners seeking validation and practical tips on working with fear, self-actualizing urges, and emotional disconnection through mahasi-style noting.

author: Ricky Lee Nuthman
summary: Logs vivid singularity/vortex sensations during late-night breath concentration, works through anger and self-pity triggered by family and client stress, and receives extended community guidance distinguishing 'power realms' from equanimity en route to stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "Like my awareness spreads to contain the entire universe, then it is all being physically sucked into the meditation object (which is an infinitely small pinprick) at an ever increasing speed." —Ricky Lee Nuthman
helpful_sentence: "A&P is sex, but EQ is love." —shargrol
who_should_read: Meditators experiencing intense vortex/singularity concentration phenomena who want peer guidance on distinguishing 'power realms' from genuine equanimity on the approach to stream entry.

author: Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
summary: Linda logs sits centered on the nada sound, piti, and earth-kasina practice while rapidly cycling through the dukkha ñanas, reobservation, and tentative second-path territory, punctuated by kundalini-like kriyas and an episode of staying equanimous while vomiting from medication.
interesting_sentence: "I actually managed to, at least to some extent, stay observing while vomiting cascades (due to penicilline medication)." —Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
helpful_sentence: "Trying to see as inclusively as possible may keep the mind busy enough to limit story telling but when the story teller shows up, investigate those sensations." —Jason Massie
who_should_read: Meditators curious about rapid nana-cycling, kundalini-like kriyas, and earth-element kasina work near the stream-entry/second-path threshold.

author: Psi
summary: Psi logs years of daily formal jhana and vipassana sits with tallied minutes, tracking a gradual reduction of anger and craving, recurring glimpses of boundless-space jhana, and a self-described dropping of the self-delusion that he is careful never to label with a specific path.
interesting_sentence: "It is as if one were the Millineum Falcon going into Hyperspace as a consciousness, sans visuals." —Psi
helpful_sentence: "Conceit goes in three directions: I'm better, I'm worse, I'm the same as..." —shargrol
who_should_read: Theravada-oriented practitioners wanting a long-form, day-by-day model of combining samatha/jhana practice with the Ten Fetters framework and Dependent Origination in daily life.

author: Rainbow
summary: Rainbow tracks cumulative sit-hours while chasing stream entry through Shinzen-style noting and daily-life mindfulness, reporting bhanga-like bicep pain, a disappointing one-day retreat, and preparation for a month-long Mahasi retreat with Sayadaw U Pandita.
interesting_sentence: "Champagne bubbles all over the skin, scanning the body becomes easy as sensations begin to flow." —Rainbow
helpful_sentence: "Meditation becomes not resisting sitting and walking and sleeping on schedule, and not resisting what comes up while sitting and walking." —shargrol
who_should_read: Busy professionals early in practice who want a candid, hour-tracked account of striving toward stream entry alongside anxiety and a demanding job.

log: Technical Log of the Villain Harden
author: Reece Harden Richardson
summary: Reece narrates highly idiosyncratic concentration and phenomenological experiments (colored sparkles, 'signal bundles,' imagined-limb movement he calls Bouncethru) built on a personal terminology system, LWC Theory, blending fire-kasina-style practice with biblical exegesis and pop-culture references.
interesting_sentence: "So Behemoth, eater of grass, may be eating 3D cross-sections of a person." —Reece Harden Richardson
helpful_sentence: "At a high level, my practice is mostly concentration and phenomenological experimentation." —Reece Harden Richardson
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about idiosyncratic, jargon-heavy concentration practice that blends fire-kasina-like visualization with personal mythology rather than standard map terminology.

author: Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
summary: An autistic ADHD practitioner logs months of breath-focused and letting-go meditation mixed with yoga, reporting frequent involuntary kriyas, a possible cessation, and an extended forum discussion of 'formations' at the equanimity stage while navigating a family crisis involving her trans child.
interesting_sentence: "It felt both weird and normal at the same time. Somewhere in all this...there was a very sudden occurring that felt like some part of the brain was plugged into a power source and a large loudspeaker." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
helpful_sentence: "The impersonal nature of bodily activity becomes progressively more noticeable as your practice evolves." —Bardo
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in how kundalini-like energetic phenomena, neurodivergence, and family crisis intersect with equanimity-stage insight practice.

author: Psi
summary: Psi checks back in after a long absence, describing roughly two hours of daily samatha, insight, and metta practice, softened anger, and a forum discussion of whether piti and kundalini sensations are the same phenomenon.
interesting_sentence: "I used to call it 'the chills' when I was a kid." —Psi
helpful_sentence: "The most distinct thing I can observe that is actually consistent in my sits is the attentional shape (i.e. spotlight attention, broad attention, panoramic awareness, on rare occasion boundless awareness)." —Ryan Kay
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about the overlap between piti and kundalini sensations, or new DhO members wondering about the forum's server quirks and culture.

author: l l
summary: A concentration-focused beginner escalates from twenty-minute sits to four-hour marathon sessions, gamifying breath counting and fighting dullness and pain, while a mentor helps him recognize likely light A&P and reobservation/dukkha-nana territory.
interesting_sentence: "heaven is indefinitely fast, whereas hell is indefinitely slow." —l l
helpful_sentence: "Don't look at dullness as your enemy, or this will develop aversion which will make you turn away from dullness, then dullness will progress even more, then you will get frustrated because of lack of progress and eventually snowball into quitting." —Irakli Gorgadze
who_should_read: Concentration-oriented meditators wanting concrete techniques (breath-counting games, endurance sitting) for building focus and pushing through dullness and pain.

author: Josef C
summary: A medical student repeatedly restarts a beginner meditation practice, oscillating between concentration and noting techniques while working on consistency, basic morality, and habit change with active community feedback.
interesting_sentence: "I think I am more of a attachment kind of person wherein I want to experience new things all the time or get dopamine hits which isnt whats happening in meditation." —Josef C
helpful_sentence: "So the trick is to learn to feel the feedback of experience, and then, based on that, learn to turn the knobs of concentration and insight up and down accordingly." —Niels Lyngsø
who_should_read: Complete beginners looking for a relatable, honest account of starting and restarting a meditation habit alongside common early struggles like resistance and inconsistency.

author: Teemu H
summary: A detailed nana-by-nana account of samatha jhana and vibration-focused vipassana practice culminating in a fruition event, followed by weeks of investigating a recurring 'pulsing knot' sensation and a lasting shift in the felt location of the senses.
interesting_sentence: "the state got stronger and the whole mind vibrated and I was already waiting for the *blip* which then happened and the peaceful silent darkness appeared." —Teemu H
helpful_sentence: "Don't worry about the super-fine technical details like rates of vibration or anything like that, it's cool if you can perceive things at this level but try to stay with the bare sensations themselves." —Tommy M
who_should_read: Practitioners doing vibration/nimitta-focused concentration practice who want a granular, jhana-by-jhana account of approaching and passing through a fruition event.

author: Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
summary: A post-stream-entry practitioner cycles rapidly through sub-nanas and jhanas while blending vipassana noting with Tibetan Bön practices (Dzogchen, guru yoga, fire kasina) and dream yoga, tracking recurring blips and fruitions without yet feeling she has reached third path.
interesting_sentence: "In such periods, I haven't even been able to hug a friend in a swimming pool without my whole body floating up all by itself, and if I have been standing up, my body has become like boiled spaghetti." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
helpful_sentence: "Maybe cleaning up cat vomit would do the trick if you found that particularly unpleasant, for example - spot the emotion and the contraction, and let them continue to happen without solidifying into an anguished reaction." —Not two
who_should_read: Post-stream-entry yogis curious how Tibetan Bön techniques (Dzogchen, kasina, guru yoga) combine with vipassana noting to navigate late-stage nana cycling and repeated fruitions.

author: Andreas Thef
summary: A ten-year meditator combines present-moment awareness, breath meditation, and Trauma Release Exercises while tracking intensifying kriyas, kundalini-like energy movements, and a shift from early A&P phenomena into dullness resembling dark night territory.
interesting_sentence: "Yesterday in my evening meditation session I was silently barking like a dog and howling like a wolf (just the movement with no sound)." —Andreas Thef
helpful_sentence: "it is to acknowledge a sensation as 'concentrated' in a particular place and then to generalize your attention to the entire sphere of experience." —tom moylan
who_should_read: Meditators experiencing kriyas or kundalini-like phenomena for the first time who want a candid, detailed account of navigating early A&P energetic release alongside trauma work.

log: Random down a dirt path comes and goes
author: Will B
summary: A terse, poetic log describes releasing longstanding physical tension through present-moment noticing, culminating in a claimed first vipassana jhana and glimpses of empty, self-less awareness.
interesting_sentence: "Most tension is gone this morning. That's 25 years at least." —Will B
helpful_sentence: "mental sights and sounds are exactly like "real" sights and sounds. They share the same essence and the same texture." —John L
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to short, imagistic accounts of releasing chronic body tension through bare noticing and present-moment awareness.

author: Ryan
summary: Systematically works through the thirteen contemplations of the Satipatthana Sutta week by week, culminating in a vivid visual 'blink-out' cessation experience during the four-elements contemplation that leaves a lasting sense of non-stickiness in daily awareness.
interesting_sentence: "I saw it phase into solidity and it glowed with an intense brightness, and then faded away to NOTHING." —Ryan
helpful_sentence: "movement masks dukkha. Even though we are not usually aware of it, physical movement is going on all the freaking time, and mostly to alleviate some kind of discomfort." —Ryan
who_should_read: Practitioners wanting a structured, week-by-week model for working through the Satipatthana Sutta's contemplations who are curious how sustained body-based mindfulness can precipitate a cessation event.

author: Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
summary: Linda logs months of fire kasina, Bön guru yoga, and an online Dzogchen retreat practice alongside strong Kundalini phenomena, nada sound, and a probable third-path perceptual shift toward centerlessness, amid losing her research job and family stress.
interesting_sentence: "With regard to these experiences, there is no subjective agency." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
helpful_sentence: "The important thing isn't to get a A+ in class for staying on the breath, it's to learn about how your own mind works." —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in tantric/Dzogchen-influenced insight practice, fire kasina phenomenology, and navigating major life stress alongside advanced meditation.

author: Psi
summary: Psi returns to DhO after a long absence with a brief 26-minute formal sit and passing reflections on working through deeply conditioned patterns via weights and running.
interesting_sentence: "Did we all accidentally slip into a parallel universe?" —Psi
helpful_sentence: "Mindfulness, then, is the unfailing master key for knowing the mind, and is thus the starting point; the perfect tool for shaping the mind, and is thus the focal point; the lofty manifestation of the achieved freedom of the mind, and is thus the culminating point." —Psi
who_should_read: DhO regulars curious about a brief community check-in from a returning long-time member rather than detailed practice content.

author: Rashed Arafat
key_words: Progressive Stages of Meditation in Plain English
summary: Rashed logs near-daily 35-55 minute kasina sits over several weeks, cautiously working toward and repeatedly questioning whether he has reached 1st or 2nd jhana while wrestling with expectations and inconsistent daily-life mindfulness.
interesting_sentence: "I was aware of the fear I associate with being fully responsible for my actions in an existential sense -- even simple actions such as having to get up early, clearing my mind , and starting my day -- and I simply sat with that feeling of fear (it lessened)." —Rashed Arafat
helpful_sentence: "If you found that the attitude of being mindful helped your daily activities be more pleasant (in addition to whatever good effects it might have on your concentration), would that change things for you?" —End in Sight
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate concentration practitioners working with a kasina object who want a candid, cautious log of early jhana attempts.

author: Rob Gee
key_words: Shinzen Young Thinking Process guided meditation
summary: Rob Gee, three weeks into serious practice, logs near-daily 20-60 minute Access Concentration sessions alongside Metta/Refuge prayers, tracking his hero complex and dependent-origination insights while quitting alcohol and cannabis after a job rejection.
interesting_sentence: "I am starting to detect \"something\" deeper, \"something\" that connects with the universe at a primal and ancient level." —Rob Gee
helpful_sentence: "A lot of people get stuck trying to \"kill\" the ego, which is still a neurotic relationship with the ego." —George S
who_should_read: New meditators building a consistent concentration practice while working through addiction recovery and career-related emotional turmoil.

author: Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
summary: Tracks fire kasina, reclining shamatha, and Dzogchen practice alongside Michael Taft's 'Reversing the Stack' course, describing kundalini energetics, kriyas, dream yoga, and cycling through dukkha nanas while coping with autism, job loss, and a probable COVID infection.
interesting_sentence: "Note to (not)self: Kundalini as spiritual antabuse" —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
helpful_sentence: "There's a huge difference between listening to the sound, as if at a distance, and being the sound." —Linda ”Polly Ester” Ö
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in kundalini/energy phenomena, fire kasina, and how neurodivergence (autism) intersects with intensive meditation practice.

author: Stefan Stefan
summary: Chronicles 3-7 hour daily sits investigating attention and the Three Characteristics through the Progress of Insight, describing repeated cessations, an intense existential dark night, and a spontaneous doership-collapse insight.
interesting_sentence: "Fuck me. Nothing can be changed, only experienced." —Stefan Stefan
helpful_sentence: "Free will is a massively overrated concept." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners deep in dark-night cycling who want detailed first-person phenomenology of A&P, dissolution, and cessation experiences.

log: OK, Let's Do This - Robert's Practice Log
author: Robert McLune
summary: A beginner's log cycling through Mahasi-style noting, breath watching, and seiza-bench posture experiments, reporting swirling visuals and brief absorption states while building a consistent daily sitting habit over about two months.
interesting_sentence: "You really need to think carefully here about what you're going to do, because otherwise, as the ski instructor from South Park said, You're Gonna Have A Bad Time." —Fitter Stoke
helpful_sentence: "I sometimes amuse myself here with the image of a car that's broken down in a field and getting out and pushing (noting) it." —Fitter Stoke
who_should_read: Absolute beginners with ADD/attention difficulties looking for reassurance and practical troubleshooting on building a sitting habit.

author: Tom Tom
summary: Tracks daily 30-minute sits investigating the residual sense of agency and a persistent perceptual 'warp' while working from late-anagami toward arahatship, reporting agencylessness estimated at 95-99% alongside debate over combining Actualism with vipassana.
interesting_sentence: "I have mild schizophrenia and lately I have been experiencing absolutely no voices or symptoms (though on occasion still during dreaming or if the body is sick with a virus or something)." —Tom Tom
helpful_sentence: "The paradox of letting go is that, to attain it, you have to stop attaining. To finally be complete, you have to allow yourself to stay incomplete." —Not Tao
who_should_read: Advanced yogis working on late-anagami/pre-arahat territory who want detailed phenomenology of dismantling residual agency.

log: Ryan L's practice log: From Scratch
author: Ryan L
summary: A brand-new meditator with about 50 hours logged describes bootstrapping concentration through TMI-style breath counting after burning out from startup life and a prolonged illness.
interesting_sentence: "Too many words, mate! What are you actually practicing? Welcome to DhO!" —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "Sounds like you’re doing it right to me. Consistent, gentle practice, with a good head about what the goal is, and what it all means." —brian patrick
who_should_read: Absolute beginners just starting a TMI-style concentration practice who want a candid account of starting from zero.

author: Stefan Stefan
