summary: Chronicles rapid insight cycling - including a strong non-dual glimpse, work with the seventh-through-tenth fetters, and a near-death experience - while shifting focus from chasing cessations toward wholesome daily-life practice.
interesting_sentence: "After a near-death experience and absolutely no suffering arising in conjunction with events before, during or after, I'm very confident in how this path has unfolded." —Stefan Stefan
helpful_sentence: "The more open we are to distraction, the less possible they are. Once we are fully open, distractions are impossible -- how can one be wilfully distracted?" —Stefan Stefan
who_should_read: Practitioners well past stream entry who want a raw, poetic account of dismantling subtle fetters and moving from insight toward embodied wholesomeness.

author: Olivier S
summary: A wide-ranging two-year retrospective covering a probable stream entry with a month of cessation-like events, a dzogchen retreat rigpa opening, a Mahasi noting retreat at Panditarama Lumbini, and a self-retreat that seemed to end the author's decade-long spiritual quest.
interesting_sentence: "Baya ñana kept us up for a couple of hours of nighttime terror" —Olivier S
helpful_sentence: "So, my advice is to be honest, as I really suffered from getting inappropriate instructions for where I was." —Olivier S
who_should_read: Experienced yogis interested in cross-tradition retreats (Mahasi noting, dzogchen, mahamudra) who want detailed phenomenology bridging insight maps with non-dual pointing-out instructions.

author: Erasmas II
summary: A short log of anapanasati practice using Vimalaramsi's 6R method, where Daniel Ingram identifies the author's tension and cloudy-pressure experiences as the Three Characteristics (3rd ñana) stage.
interesting_sentence: "Likely related to the stage callee the Three Characteristics, aka 3rd ñana." —Daniel M Ingram
helpful_sentence: "just let the burning energy define the shape of attention, and see where that goes, and try that for other objects also, as fixing attention to some ideal will end up missing things, as the shape of attention changes with the objects of meditation, as it actually IS the objects of meditation." —Daniel M Ingram
who_should_read: Early-stage concentration practitioners working with the 6R method who want to see how subtle tension and energetic sensations map onto the early nanas.

author: Ryan C
summary: A beginner recovering from an ibogaine-triggered psychotic episode starts brief daily TWIM-style breath meditation sessions from dhammasukha.org while working on self-esteem and confidence.
interesting_sentence: "Ibogaine showed me two amazing beautiful women that I could of gone with only minor changes of my actions." —Ryan C
helpful_sentence: "Please stay safe yourself! A physical practice like tai chi might help get you grounded in your body." —Jane Laurel Carrington
who_should_read: Someone newly recovering from a substance-triggered mental health crisis who wants a candid, very early-stage account of returning cautiously to meditation.

author: Stefan Stefan
summary: A weekly log tracking a shift into open-awareness practice, detailed jhana experimentation guided by shargrol (culminating in 'minty' third-jhana bliss and boundless states), and a series of smooth 'mental blank' cessation-like events prompting self-diagnosis toward second path.
interesting_sentence: "I peered into the abyss. Not going to lie, it was very spooky." —Stefan Stefan
helpful_sentence: "Cool and minty is fairly classic third jhana. That feeling is described as bliss, rather than piti." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners confused about mapping jhana phenomenology near second path who want a granular, guided phenomenological account with expert feedback.

author: Oochdd
summary: An 8-year practitioner describes a no-self event during walking meditation on a Thailand retreat, months of cycling and confusion self-diagnosed with a teacher's help, deliberate jhana cycling through 1st-6th, and a disorienting ability to relocate the center of perception.
interesting_sentence: "I was perceiving everything (meaning body sensation, sight, sound, etc) from a plastic chair." —Oochdd
helpful_sentence: "we're on this retreat to learn how to do less clinging, so that includes not clinging to jhana's" —Oochdd
who_should_read: Practitioners confused about mapping cessation and fruition events who want a candid account of working with a teacher to self-diagnose progress.

author: Rebecca P
summary: A beginner tracks daily samatha, walking, and metta sessions while withdrawing from opiate addiction, cycling through a psychiatric hospitalization and a dark-night-like collapse before achieving sobriety through a 28-day rehab program.
interesting_sentence: "I am a filthy shell of nothing." —Rebecca P
helpful_sentence: "meditation is not medication; in other words, don't look on this path as a substitute for appropriate medical care." —Laurel Carrington
who_should_read: Meditators in early recovery from addiction or living with bipolar/depression who want a candid, supportive account of sustaining basic practice through crisis.

log: The panda is eating bamboo - practice log
author: Dave sdfsdf
summary: A beginner uses Headspace-guided body scanning and breath awareness, noting occasional floaty limbs and light phenomena, and begins attending a local Chan sangha and exploring a Tibetan sangha.
interesting_sentence: "Felt a slight drop, my breathing dissappeared, the darkness behind eyes became gray and there was small patches of light floating downwards." —Dave sdfsdf
helpful_sentence: "when I don't meditate for a period of time of more than 1 week, I get this. Everytime. Perhaps it's the mind getting back into the groove of paying attention." —Travis Gene McKinstry
who_should_read: Complete beginners curious what early Headspace-style body scan practice combined with joining a real-world sangha feels like.

author: Ryan Kay
summary: Daily light-kasina and Do Nothing sits chart a month-long cycle through J1-J5 jhana phenomenology, kundalini-like energetic pulses, and non-lucid dream states, while gradually adding noting practice ahead of an upcoming Fire Kasina retreat.
interesting_sentence: "meditation these days feels a bit like sitting at the top of a waterfall which leads into an abyss" —Ryan Kay
helpful_sentence: "Consider emphasizing consistency and quality of investigation instead of length of sitting." —shargrol
who_should_read: Jhana-focused practitioners curious about detailed phenomenology of shifting between concentration states and how choiceless noting can be layered onto samatha practice.

author: Stefan G
summary: A beginner restarts a practice log with short morning breath-counting and metta sits, mostly wrestling with dullness and low energy while rebuilding a meditation habit after illness.
interesting_sentence: "It was hard to connect and the main "lesson" this sit was acceptance - relaxing in and letting the dullness, mind wandering and lack of "connection" be ok." —Stefan G
helpful_sentence: "I tried letting the breath to its thing, not me controlling it." —Stefan G
who_should_read: Beginners restarting a meditation habit who want a candid, low-key account of returning to basic breath and metta practice.

log: Opening the Energy Gates practice log
author: Bames Jond
summary: A TMI-trained practitioner beginning a six-month meditation sabbatical logs whole-body-breathing sits and Opening the Energy Gates qigong, alongside an intense off-cushion episode of awareness-knowing-awareness accompanied by existential fear.
interesting_sentence: "There was a rising sense of existential fear - of immediate mortal danger around the knowing." —Bames Jond
helpful_sentence: "1-2 hours/day is already more than enough to make sufficient "progress" - everything above that is probably unnecessary and will most liekely result in problems." —Oatmilk
who_should_read: Experienced concentration practitioners considering an intensive multi-month retreat or sabbatical who want a firsthand comparison of TMI, kasina, and whole-body-breathing approaches.

author: rein drop
summary: A doubt-prone, over-conceptualizing practitioner breaks years of stagnant practice with a 5.5-hour breath-awareness marathon sit and later uses Gary Weber-inspired self-inquiry to dissolve bodily tension, while gearing up for a Goenka retreat.
interesting_sentence: "It's amazing how much my life is based on these concepts and what's left when they start dropping away." —rein drop
helpful_sentence: "So just do this one thing, e.g., counting the breath, and only that. Keep on coming back to the same instructions. Rinse and repeat, rinse and repeat." —sawfoot _
who_should_read: Practitioners stuck overthinking their practice who want to see how simplifying to bare breath awareness plus self-inquiry can break a rationalizing deadlock.

author: Jesse Cooper Levy
summary: Over several months of anapana-into-noting sits plagued by involuntary kriyas, the yogi self-tracks through dark night, works through sexual and relationship material via noting, and preps for a 10-day retreat aiming at first path.
interesting_sentence: "I remember distinctly the thought of having passed through the fabric of meaning and finding nothing on the other side." —Jesse Cooper Levy
helpful_sentence: "My advice is to hold the map lightly. By that I mean you consult the map, then you put it down and get to practice." —Fitter Stoke
who_should_read: Practitioners experiencing kriyas or fear around dark night territory who want a candid multi-month account of noting practice, retreat prep, and working with emotionally charged material.

author: Ryan Kay
summary: A month-long daily log cycling through Samatha jhanas via light kasina and choiceless noting, culminating in narrative-blob dissolution events, a possible Vipassana Jhana episode, and preparation for a three-week Fire Kasina retreat.
interesting_sentence: "Let it die, let it die..." —Ryan Kay
helpful_sentence: "Sit every day, it's like trying to swim upstream during practice and getting flushed downstream when you rest." —shargrol
who_should_read: Jhana-focused practitioners preparing for an intensive retreat who want a candid, non-dogmatic account of blending concentration and insight practices.

author: PP
summary: A meticulous five-month Mahasi-noting log blending a Taoist Qigong background with vipassana, tracking oscillation between Dark Night and Low Equanimity via a self-devised 'Aversion Parameter Evaluation' technique.
interesting_sentence: "For my surprise and amaze, actually I was seeing through the throat, like if it were empty, and those pulses were coming from the spine." —PP
helpful_sentence: "So, for me, the three main signposts of practice are: A+P [energetic phenomenon]; Dark Night nanas [slightly wider focus]; and Equanimity [really wide focus]." —Alan Smithee
who_should_read: Noting practitioners who want a granular, technique-experimenting account of grinding through Dark Night into Low Equanimity.

author: Original Mike
summary: A short samatha/metta log wrestling with chronic anhedonia and grief-linked head tension, in which metta practice measurably improves the quality of a subsequent concentration sit.
interesting_sentence: "It's as if the pleasant energy had tried to arise and leave my body through my head but upon encountering the blocking tension it got stuck, stagnated, and turned into an intense heat." —Original Mike
helpful_sentence: "For the sake of argument, I thought, let's say this feeling lasts forever. What would it take for me to be happy if is a given?" —Original Mike
who_should_read: Practitioners struggling with anhedonia or grief who want an honest account of metta's effect on subsequent concentration practice.

author: relbatnrut
summary: A brief noting log combining Mahasi-style walking meditation with a newly adopted TWIM forgiveness practice, tracking frustration, clinging, and aversion during daily sits.
interesting_sentence: "I've had a number of pretty powerful psychedelic experiences (including one quite traumatic one 4 years ago) and one lightning bolt of energy style experience on retreat in June 2019 (at the time I thought it might have been stream entry; this seems pretty unlikely now)." —relbatnrut
helpful_sentence: "You may want for yourself and us to put next to the date, the duration of the meditation. You may want to consider listing some bodily sensations." —Sam Gentile
who_should_read: New noting practitioners feeling stuck between concentration retreats and daily life who want a candid, unpolished walking-meditation log.

log: Thoughts on Vipassana: a practise log?
author: Kevin Andrew
summary: A years-long Goenka Vipassana body-scanning log centered on a persistent, trauma-linked facial and cranial sensation nicknamed 'TTOTSOMH' that gradually dissolves and spreads into full-body kundalini-like activation with growing equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "It is ooblek all the way down, not turtles." —Kevin Andrew
helpful_sentence: "Even if you have been through the A&P before, it's very common to have a strong A&P-like experience with the continuity of practice on retreat." —shargrol
who_should_read: Long-term Goenka practitioners seeking a detailed phenomenology of trauma-linked bodily 'sankhara' resolving through years of daily body-scanning.

log: Ryan's Practice Log 4.0: Post Retreat Edition
author: Ryan Kay
summary: Tracks roughly ten weeks of daily post-retreat noting, samatha jhana cycling, and fire kasina sessions as a retreat-triggered loss of a 'pushing and pulling' controller-sense gradually fades and partially resurfaces, including a probable low-grade cessation and several near-misses.
interesting_sentence: "it was as if the whole conscious space was hooked up to a dimmer switch, and it dimmed down into nothing." —Ryan Kay
helpful_sentence: "The number one skill is learning how to experience felt-sensations, including the felt-sensations of moods and memories that arise during meditation." —shargrol
who_should_read: Post-retreat practitioners integrating a fading insight event into daily home practice, especially those tracking near-misses and low-grade cessations against the progress-of-insight map.

author: Pablo . P
summary: Documents about nine months of home practice built on Shinzen Young's Focus In/Out/Rest/Flow/Gone noting taxonomy, cycling through A&P, Dark Night, and low Equanimity while testing a self-devised diaphragmatic-breathing and breath-retention technique to release an energetic blockage at the solar plexus.
interesting_sentence: "Does this means that enlightenment is a fabricated experience?" —Pablo . P
helpful_sentence: "I found that the key to success is to watch/hear/sense the periphery and not the center." —Pablo . P
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in Shinzen Young's noting taxonomy who want a granular, technique-focused account of cycling through A&P, Dark Night, and low Equanimity at home.

author: Oskar M
summary: Recounts, partly from memory and partly in real time, a multi-year series of Pemako 'bhumi opening' insights triggered by the two-part-formula technique and sustained through guru yoga and central-channel energy work, describing recurring centerless, timeless non-dual states alongside a persistent throat-based fear knot and cycles of compassion practice.
interesting_sentence: "It felt like I was consumed by presence, like I completely lost the 'grip'." —Oskar M
helpful_sentence: "Your mind already has plenty of experiences of this kind of clarity, it's just that they tend to get pushed away out of fear or ignored in favor of concepts that sound more interesting." —George S
who_should_read: Vajrayana/Dzogchen practitioners curious how a non-Ingram bhumi-based awakening model compares to the four-path framework, especially those balancing guru yoga with fear and trauma work.

author: Ryan Kay
summary: Ryan records daily 25-60 minute sits alongside Muse EEG data, cycling through samatha jhanas and Do Nothing practice, mapping jhana 'attentional shapes' to nana territory and describing a probable Grade B cessation and ongoing post-retreat shifts in self-view.
interesting_sentence: "BAM. Hard J1 into J5 (just a space of consciousness with no boundaries or edges) over the course of what I think was maybe 20 seconds." —Ryan Kay
helpful_sentence: "It's not so important to know definitively if it is hard-J4 or light-J5, the important thing is to notice where there is subtle dissatisfaction that still remains." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in correlating jhana and nana phenomenology with EEG data, or navigating post-retreat integration and cessation classification, will find this detailed, science-minded log especially useful.

author: PP
summary: Pablo blends Shinzen Young's five noting strategies with Taoist/martial-arts energy work and a tummo-like breath-retention practice, cycling repeatedly through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity while fitting brief sits around a newborn and a 3:30am work schedule.
interesting_sentence: "You become a single unit with the opponent by dissolving oneself." —PP
helpful_sentence: "I found that the key to success is to watch/hear/sense the periphery and not the center." —PP
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in combining Shinzen Young's noting system with Taoist/qigong energy practices, or fitting practice around new-parent sleep constraints, will find useful phenomenological detail here.

log: Overcoming the Hindrance of Doubt - Log
author: Dan Ed
summary: Dan Ed, a primary-school teacher returning to practice after a decade-long break, cycles between Tejaniya's light awareness, Shinzen's See Hear Feel, and Mahasi noting while working through kriyas/TRE-like abdominal spasms and chronic doubt about which method to commit to.
interesting_sentence: "I've also gotten into TRE which seems to be in the same weird-exorcism-like ballpark as kriyas." —Dan Ed
helpful_sentence: "It's important not to use spirituality to repress or deny reality... and sometimes the reality is work is difficult and stressful." —shargrol
who_should_read: Meditators paralyzed by method-hopping doubt, especially those balancing a demanding job like teaching, will relate to Dan Ed's struggle and benefit from the community's grounded advice on trusting one practice.

author: tircle _
summary: tircle narrates two retreat-center sits combining spine-aligned Burmese posture, fast body scanning, anapana, and metta chanting, with attention to closed-eye colored visual noise and phone-driven distraction off the cushion.
interesting_sentence: "Whole visual field filled with noise. A lot of violet going to red and pink or to blue and intense green-yellowish." —tircle _
helpful_sentence: "This way the mind is faster than the automatic reactions to spine or shoulders discomfort and continuity is preserved." —tircle _
who_should_read: Readers wanting a brief, concrete snapshot of retreat-center sitting combining body scanning, anapana, and metta chanting will get a quick, low-key glimpse.

author: Ryan Kay
summary: Daily EEG-monitored sits blending samatha jhana up through J6/J7 with vipassana noting, tracking a recurring 'welcoming' practice that dissolves subtle craving and produces vivid equanimity-insight-visions (a self-stuff whirlpool, a Homer-Simpson watcher dream) without a clear cessation.
interesting_sentence: "Jhana knows how to jhana. Thoughts about how to do jhana don't actually DO the jhana." —Ryan Kay
helpful_sentence: "Don't worry too much about more time (notice the subtle greed/ambition in the desire) but just focus on maintaining non-heroic consistency day to day and real intimacy with what arises." —shargrol
who_should_read: Jhana-focused practitioners who want granular, EEG-annotated phenomenology of cycling through equanimity-insight-visions and using dependent-origination awareness in real-life conflict.

log: Richard Zen’s Concentration and Metta Log
author: Richard Zen
summary: Long-running concentration log pushing from 1st jhana toward the formless realms via sky-gazing and letting-go of attention, interwoven with metta, cognitive therapy, and MBTI-based self-analysis to reduce reactivity at work and in relationships.
interesting_sentence: "It's really a reminder that we can do Brahmaviharas as much as we can but we must include some compassionate wrath where we can look at least a little dangerous or else we look like easy targets for bullies." —Richard Zen
helpful_sentence: "The way to deal with it is to see thoughts as objects arising and passing away. By not adding to it and trying to look for something permanent in thoughts all you find is unfindability so repressing it or adding to it is pointless." —Richard Zen
who_should_read: Concentration-oriented meditators who want a long-view account of combining jhana practice with metta and cognitive therapy to integrate insight into daily work and relationships.

author: Patricia M
summary: Detailed daily noting log with heart-rate/HRV measurements documenting a restart of formal practice after nine years, including a mini home retreat and a brutal week of grief, self-criticism, and coping-drink relapse before easing back into calmer sits.
interesting_sentence: "it appeared like a dark, viscous mass moving around slowly and loosing a bit of mass when finally starting to move upwards" —Patricia M
helpful_sentence: "The basic noting practice is to note whatever comes in through the six sense doors (the five physical senses, and the mind, i.e., thoughts), in the light of the three characteristics." —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: Meditators restarting a noting practice after a long break who want an honest, quantified (HR/HRV) account of cycling through dark-night territory and self-critical relapse.

author: Yellow Tree
summary: Fast-moving log of a business owner cycling repeatedly through A&P and Dark Night via noting, body scanning, and Rob Burbea-style sky-gazing while practicing almost continuously through the workday and openly processing childhood trauma, attention-seeking, and unusual sexual/somatic phenomena.
interesting_sentence: "Right as I'm about to cross over into sleep state, everything goes back, then the only thing that's left is the ringing in my ear, everything else dissapears, and then suddenly everything is just a bright white light, and then I get terrified." —Yellow Tree
helpful_sentence: "You're doing noting exactly how it is supposed to be done - noticing how the mind creates stress for itself via aversion-clinging-ignoring in all its myriad forms." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about rapid state-cycling via noting and sky-gazing woven into a full workday, especially those also processing childhood trauma and unusual somatic/sexual phenomena during practice.

log: Ryan’s Practice log 7.0 Steady onward to nowhere
author: Ryan Kay
summary: A near year-long daily log of jhana and vipassana practice tracking nana cycling, hip pain, and EEG-recorded sits, culminating in a probable cessation/dependent-origination collapse on May 23rd and a shift to agenda-less 'welcome everything' shikantaza before a planned 10-day home fire kasina retreat.
interesting_sentence: "The bastard moves! It jumps to different sense doors or sensations and keeps reattaching." —Ryan Kay
helpful_sentence: "The path consists of investigating problemness and eventually finding out it wasn't the problemness you thought it was." —shargrol
who_should_read: Serious daily-practice vipassana/jhana practitioners navigating cessation ambiguity and stream-entry uncertainty will find a detailed, honest phenomenological model of the insight cycle.

author: Richard Zen
summary: Explores an Actualism-influenced practice of noticing and releasing preferences at work and in daily life, alongside reflections on perfectionism, proto-anger, and recurring episodes of senses 'fading' during concentrated states.
interesting_sentence: "I've been debating what clinging, resistance, or preference is, but it appears to be anger." —Richard Zen
helpful_sentence: "By embracing the shame of imperfection with the attitude of 'done is more important than perfect' or 'do not let the perfect be the enemy of the good,' there's a lot of healing with that." —Richard Zen
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in blending Actualism-style preference investigation with Buddhist psychology, especially around perfectionism and workplace stress, will find practical reflections here.

log: Pawe? K practice log (mostly thinking outloud) [Pawe? K] [MIGRATE]
author: Pawel K
summary: An idiosyncratic log mapping personal 'vispassana jhana' states onto chakras and describing repeated 'blip'-style fruitions with diminishing afterglow, alongside self-invented techniques like sensation 'sandpapering' and hemisphere-splitting perception.
interesting_sentence: "It was like trying to eat poison." —Pawel K
helpful_sentence: "Try going outside and looking out into the distance and opening your visual field to include everything into one thing and quiet the mind." —Dream Walker
who_should_read: Meditators drawn to idiosyncratic, systems-oriented phenomenology (chakras, self-devised jhana taxonomies, visualization-based insight work) will find an unusual but detailed account of repeated fruition-like events.

author: Daniel Johnson
summary: A day-by-day account of a 9-day Goenka-style retreat marked by three days of severe dark-night despair, including suicidal ideation, that gave way to ease and an anicca insight on day 5, sparking extended community debate about whether the 13-year practitioner has unknowingly already attained stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "There were thoughts of suicide, deep depression and despair and quite a bit of hopeless agonizing suffering." —Daniel Johnson
helpful_sentence: "If you can keep attention on a 'solidified' object (breath, kasina dot) for an hour without engaging/floundering in any content then that would be first samatha jhana." —Tom Tom
who_should_read: Long-term dry-insight/Goenka practitioners who feel stuck cycling through dark night without a recognized fruition will find validation and detailed technical debate about equanimity and stream-entry diagnosis.

author: Todo
summary: A self-taught, teacherless practitioner of over 40 years, driven originally by a visceral fear of death, argues for a non-dual 'clear cognizing' framework in extended philosophical debate with the DhO community rather than describing concrete meditation sessions.
interesting_sentence: "I would literally see my body after death bloated and being eaten by worms and this will sometimes wake me up in the middle of the night." —Todo
helpful_sentence: "When the mind needs to be 'inclined', it is already too slow." —shargrol
who_should_read: Philosophically inclined practitioners interested in non-dual framing of Delusion and 'clear cognizing,' and in how a self-taught outsider engages a technique-focused forum, will find this thread of interest.

log: Ryan’s Practice Log 8.0: The Bastard (i.e. the self) Moves!
author: Ryan Kay
summary: An at-home 8.5-day Fire Kasina retreat log where the author develops his 'watching the bastard move' practice, tracking not-self investigation of the observer/witness, papanca, and drifting equanimity through daily journal entries.
interesting_sentence: "It's a six inch knife that wants to cut a forty-eight mile cake." —brian patrick
helpful_sentence: "The feeling here is more like solving a koan than finding a thing." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring notself/witness dissolution via fire kasina and shikantaza who want a candid day-by-day account of post-retreat integration.

author: Richard Zen
summary: A multi-year noting/Shikantanza practice log cycling through A&P and reobservation into a long, stable equanimity, integrating Actualism-style HAIETMOBA, dependent-origination contemplation, and Freudian/CPTSD psychological work, without ever claiming a specific path or a confirmed cessation.
interesting_sentence: "My experience is telling me \"fuck cessation\" and \"direct path all the way baby!\"" —Richard Zen
helpful_sentence: "No one has ever known a cessation has happened until afterwards." —shargrol
who_should_read: Long-term equanimity-stage practitioners wanting detailed phenomenology of integrating dependent-origination contemplation, Actualism-style practice, and psychological (CPTSD/Freudian) work into daily life.

author: Mind over easy
summary: The OP tries an Actualism-style, PCE-oriented practice for about an hour, reporting an immediate A&P and passage through jhana/vipassana-like stages up to equanimity, which sparks an extended debate about how PCEs relate to nanas and to actualist theories of belief and feeling.
interesting_sentence: "I got an A&P right away when I tried that, not sure how that or any other nana factors into the PCE deal." —Mind over easy
helpful_sentence: "The most important part about the PCE is the experience of the actual world." —Beoman Claudiu Dragon Emu Fire Golem
who_should_read: Meditators curious how actualism's PCE concept differs from progress-of-insight nanas, plus a deep dive into actualist theory on beliefs and feelings.

author: Ricardo Hz
summary: A first-person account of a 24-day Mahasi-tradition retreat in Penang, Malaysia, covering weak concentration, a pain-tolerance breakthrough, noting-label distraction, and the stress caused by trying to maintain continuous mindfulness.
interesting_sentence: "It was like being shown a drug you're addicted to, remembering your intention is not to take it and then consuming it nonetheless." —Ricardo Hz
helpful_sentence: "Relax. When we think about our practice as a thing, we suffer." —Chi
who_should_read: First-time retreatants curious about a Mahasi-style silent retreat and the ordinary frustrations of pain, poor concentration, and self-imposed pressure to be continuously mindful.

author: Tommy M
summary: An eleven-year practice log describing a stable, six-month-old shift to a 'fully non-dual' baseline built on Tonglen, Bodhicitta, and Tummo, followed by deepening Vajrayana practice — Amitabha deity yoga, mantra-based dream yoga, and cautious Tummo/tsa-lung energy work.
interesting_sentence: "I can barely even write this down without welling up because of the strength of my devotion to him." —Tommy M
helpful_sentence: "Tummo is a deep , rich , condensed heat, 4 finger widths below the navel & close to the spine." —Noah D
who_should_read: Vajrayana-oriented practitioners wanting detailed phenomenology of Tummo, deity yoga, and dream yoga integrated with a post-stream-entry non-dual baseline shift.

author: Ryan Kay
summary: A software engineer's highly detailed six-month log tracking spontaneous kundalini-like energetic phenomena (clonic spine movements, chakra sensations, eye movements) alongside a daily practice of walking metta and jhana-focused sitting or laying meditation.
interesting_sentence: "I was Mr. "Chakras are bullshit" until awareness started localizing to them and weird shit started happening (energy pouring out of the top of my head, muscle contractions, etc.)." —Ryan Kay
helpful_sentence: "It is always safest to return back to the body sensations and start from there ... body sensations will lead to interesting insights about mind states, mind images, thoughts, assumptions etc" —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about kundalini-type energetic phenomena and the interplay between attention intensity and involuntary bodily movements during jhana-oriented practice.

author: Alessandro Migliori
summary: A brief journal describing daily ~40-minute sits that reliably cycle through A&P, dukkha nana, and equanimity, including one session with vivid tantric/mandala visions and a breakthrough insight into the constructed nature of emotional selfhood.
interesting_sentence: "Also some days ago i got very deep and seen mandalas / Tantric deities having sex, deknotted a lot ot the tangle of sensation behind the eyes and recognised clearly the "knowing"" —Alessandro Migliori
helpful_sentence: "When seeing space around emotions happens now you see that also them are mind objects coming and going and don't create a self so that no emotions is coming from behing and imposing how you must feel" —Alessandro Migliori
who_should_read: Meditators who overpracticed into instability and want a concise example of dialing back intensity while still cycling cleanly through A&P, the dukkha nanas, and equanimity.

author: Rick M
summary: A meticulous month-by-month log of concentration and Mahasi-noting practice that tracks a clear A&P crossing followed by an extended Dark Night marked by dissolution, fear, and full-body vibratory convulsions, richly annotated by experienced forum members.
interesting_sentence: "It felt like I fell down a dark elevator shaft and was then wrapped in a jhanic blanket." —Rick M
helpful_sentence: "The task right now is not to pick up on every last detail of each ñana. The task is to master the technique and to get good at treating everything that comes up as just more sensations." —Fitter Stoke
who_should_read: Insight practitioners moving through their first A&P and Dark Night who want a granular, nana-by-nana account plus seasoned troubleshooting from experienced noters.

author: Troels T
summary: A newer practitioner's detailed 4-day solo retreat report describing a violent kundalini-like energy release up the spine followed by weeks of near-zero anxiety, then a subsequent dip in motivation he suspects is an early Dark Night.
interesting_sentence: "After about 90 minutes I felt it: a massive hump of energy at the base of the spine, crawling upward slowly. When it reached the middle of my back, it stopped, and then everything snapped. Violent tremors shook my whole body." —Troels T
helpful_sentence: "In my experience, meditation gets really interesting and profound one it starts to suck (due to boredom, anxiety, exhaustion, distraction, whatever)." —Alex N
who_should_read: Goenka-tradition practitioners curious about kundalini-type retreat breakthroughs and the motivational dip that can follow, along with concrete body-scan troubleshooting tips.

author: Sam s
summary: Documents several weeks of daily Mahasi-style noting practice mentored by forum member Noting Monkey, alternating fast vibratory noting with a softer 'feeling space' concentration practice while trying to locate himself on the map without a clear dark night.
interesting_sentence: "I don't know whether I can observe a thought stream without interrupting it, as they usually break down into colours and sounds when I do" —Sam s
helpful_sentence: "Work with the sensations you have in the present it is enough you don't need to look for more." —Noting Monkey
who_should_read: Vipassana noters uncertain about their map location who want a candid, mentor-guided account of daily practice troubleshooting.

author: Mike B
summary: A beginner restarts a stalled meditation habit with a mix of Just Sitting, breath awareness, and metta, logging a first week of 220 minutes across six days with steady but unremarkable mind-wandering work.
interesting_sentence: "If one doesn't indulge or fight with experiences they cessate on their own." —Richard Zen
helpful_sentence: "The impulses cessate on their own if you just watch them and not indulge or fight with them." —Richard Zen
who_should_read: Complete beginners struggling to establish a consistent sit who want reassurance and practical beginner tips.

author: Andre Pais
summary: Lays out a detailed 5-day, 13-hours-per-day solo night retreat schedule alternating sitting and walking noting practice with post-meditation 'not-self' and 'impersonal field' contemplations during breaks and meals.
interesting_sentence: "I have a feeling you're trying to do so much because you're trying to achieve some result (such as enlightenment)." —ivory
helpful_sentence: "It's important to be very well-rested and get enough sleep during retreats." —J C
who_should_read: Practitioners designing an intensive self-directed home retreat who want a concrete day-by-day schedule blending noting with not-self inquiry.

author: Robert Paul Thompson
summary: Announces intent to track a Unified Mindfulness-based practice aimed at building fluency in the first four shamatha jhanas while monitoring vipassana jhanas, developed partly in collaboration with Claude AI ahead of upcoming retreats.
interesting_sentence: "But initially, wanted to share a practice I've been collaborating with Claude AI on." —Robert Paul Thompson
helpful_sentence: "My goal was to develop fluency with the 1st four Shamatha Jhana's through intuitive exploration, and also to maintain awareness of how and when I may be passing through Vipassana Jhana's as my practice deepens (again)." —Robert Paul Thompson
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about combining Unified Mindfulness jhana training with AI-assisted practice planning might use this as a starting point to follow later updates.

author: Robin Woods
summary: A practitioner who attained stream entry and 2nd path years ago returns seeking guidance on approaching 3rd path amid AI-related existential and economic anxiety, and later reports spontaneously noticing dependent origination and a shift to bare sense-door awareness.
interesting_sentence: "Typing words on phone - red autocomplete phone emoji appears - laughter - see laughing emoji in autocomplete- more laughter- sound of cat fountain - mental image of fire in pizza van from last night - itch beside eye- mental image of gateless gatecrashers book." —Robin Woods
helpful_sentence: "The resistance is the real kicker, but you can gain relief by turning the resistance from a subject (the self you see the world through) into an object (not a self, just another part of the world)." —John L
who_should_read: Post-2nd-path meditators grappling with real-world anxiety who want peer perspectives on techniques for progressing toward 3rd path.

author: Sam N
summary: Twice-daily vipassana/noting practice with mild A&P energetics, kasina experiments, and vivid hypnagogic visions that resolves into cycling between re-observation and equanimity with markedly reduced day-to-day suffering.
interesting_sentence: "Then I a saw a huge figure, a menacing villain just smiling at me and saying “No.”" —Sam N
helpful_sentence: "Work with including any discomfort into the totality of your experience." —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about energetic/kundalini-style phenomena and the felt texture of cycling between re-observation and equanimity in daily sits.

author: chris mc
summary: After a decade of self-diagnosed dark night, takes a year off work to pursue stream entry, does back-to-back Goenka and Satipatthana retreats, then pivots away from Theravada noting practice toward a Vajrayana group.
interesting_sentence: "there came a period shortly after where it felt like something in the way I had been seeing practice and the world slipped out of the body and there was a sense of release and completion." —Bill F
helpful_sentence: "Certain ways that I had been configuring reality that were not in line with clear seeing, and were causing pain, began to be seen more clearly, and a really incredible period of ease, well being, and calm was ushered in before what is referred to here as "stream entry" happened." —Bill F
who_should_read: Long-term practitioners feeling stuck in dissatisfaction who are considering whether a change of tradition (Theravada to Vajrayana) might unstick their practice.

author: Peter de Waal
summary: Combines 45 minutes of walking meditation with 1-2 hours of seated noting/open-awareness practice, minutely describing foot-lifting sub-sensations and a brief awareness 'stutter' he suspects was a small cessation, while self-locating in equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "The very minute sensations of balancing, lifting en putting the foot down are noticed in a rapid tempo (about 5-10 per second)" —Peter de Waal
helpful_sentence: "Next time this happens (or a similar experience) try to describe it all in a more detailed way." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Practitioners who want a granular, moment-by-moment phenomenology of walking meditation and a candid equanimity-stage self-assessment.

author: Robert L
summary: Following lessons with Kenneth Folk, an A&P electrical kundalini-like sensation is followed by dark-night nightmares and disgust, then a shift to samatha/do-nothing practice yields a spacious 'room with no walls' equanimity and a fleeting no-self glimpse, later moving into tummo and Dzogchen.
interesting_sentence: "The seen was there, like always, but Rob wasn't." —Robert L
helpful_sentence: "there is nothing to do in EQ but let it deepen into even more emptiness--- the dark night tends to burn out our greed to get Somewhere Else, and experience Something Else, and exposes those efforts as misery-making." —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: Meditators interested in kundalini-style A&P openings and how a shift to samatha/do-nothing practice can settle dukkha nana turbulence into spacious equanimity.

author: Tony Takitani
summary: Chronicles early concentration practice moving from breath-stopping pleasurable jolts toward Kenneth-Folk-style noting triplets and Culadasa's structured TMI intervals, while experimenting with mindfulness during chess and kettlebell training.
interesting_sentence: "It was mingled with excitement, and it felt like I had just taken a hit of some drug." —Tony Takitani
helpful_sentence: "if you want to do jhana which can be practiced as both, you might want to find a subtler and stabler sense of wellbeing/fullness in the breathing to begin building concentration on." —josh r s
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate concentration practitioners curious about early jhanic signs and Culadasa's staged TMI approach.

author: Sam Gentile
summary: A near-daily Mahasi at-loud noting practitioner logs months of detailed body-sensation sits moving through Reobservation and brief touches of equanimity while coping with a devastating Parkinson's-spectrum diagnosis, IBS, and financial collapse.
interesting_sentence: "It's like a Noah meditation report from the ark on day 35: 'A little rain. Animals restless. Prayed to God.'" —Tim Farrington
helpful_sentence: "Just simple noting and accepting all weather its pleasant, neutral or unpleasant." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Practitioners curious about sustained Mahasi-style noting practice while navigating chronic illness, medication side effects, and financial hardship.

author: Chris
summary: A newer vipassana practitioner recounts an early full-body pulsing A&P event followed by months of depression and physical pain in the Dark Night, then describes settling into equanimity and, in a final post, an apparent fruition marked by a heartbeat spike and reality "re-engaging."
interesting_sentence: "It very much felt to me like feeling my body pulse in and out of existence." —Chris
helpful_sentence: "I've never come anywhere close to that and it didn't stop me from getting to 4th path. The most I can get is 2-3 / second." —J C
who_should_read: New vipassana practitioners uncertain whether an early pulsing/A&P-like experience and subsequent depression fit the map will find this recognition and reassurance useful.

author: Hac Phi^2 Vita
summary: A dedicated dual vipassana/jhana practitioner logs granular Mahasi-noting sits alongside daily Ashtanga yoga, describes a Fire-Kasina-triggered A&P and recurring "black frame" gaps that a consulted teacher interprets as possible fruitions in Review, and later reports a vivid hard-jhana breakthrough after studying Leigh Brasington's instructions.
interesting_sentence: "...all of the head buzzing suddenly went right into the point between my eybrows, and then...exploded. This explosion occured in three senses exactly simultaneously... a blue/white cartoon-explosion. Sort of like a 'POW' speech bubble from batman in the -60s." —Hac Phi^2 Vita
helpful_sentence: "Path moments don't occur because you are thinking about cessation. They occur by surprise after intense effort." —Not two
who_should_read: Serious dual-track vipassana/jhana practitioners wanting granular phenomenology of cessations, path-moment structure, and jhana development guided by close teacher feedback.

author: Rocket _
summary: A self-described 4th-path practitioner journals his post-attainment life as a financially struggling artist, describing scanning-technique body pain release from past Goenka retreats, heart-center-opening PCE-like moments, Actualism-influenced practice, and periodic self-retreats to reset from unmindful stretches.
interesting_sentence: "Colors become muted, I contract into an isolated existence, compassion falls to new lows - then the master reset is hit in retreat." —Rocket _
helpful_sentence: "the trick is to do it off retreat and during a rather intense burst of work/creative output. these things proceed faster if some stress is imposed, induced, a gradient so we flow in proper directions." —Rocket _
who_should_read: Post-attainment practitioners navigating financial precarity and integrating claimed 4th path into an unconventional creative life.

author: Tyler Rowley
summary: A practitioner nearing a year of sobriety through Recovery Dharma details daily fire-kasina and slow-noting sits rich with nimitta phenomenology, names a recent event an A&P, and expands into Tibetan Vajrayana practice (Vajrakilaya empowerment, Tara devotion) while candidly tracking his bipolar and psych-ward history against the experiences.
interesting_sentence: "If I ever got close, it's because I crashed through the entrance in a stolen spiritual Ferrari, and Shiva happened to be the bouncer that day." —Tyler Rowley
helpful_sentence: "Suffering does not stop when we avoid it, it's actually the opposite. Suffering is caused by our avoidance, by our selfish search." —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Practitioners with a bipolar or psychedelic-adjacent history exploring how Vajrayana devotional practice and MCTB-style noting can coexist safely with careful mental-health self-monitoring.

author: Sam Gentile
summary: Sam logs twice-daily concentration-then-noting sits while coping with Parkinson's, financial hardship, and community support, reporting a strong teacher-confirmed Arising & Passing event followed by a confirmed Dark Night (Dissolution) and later ambiguous jhanic/itching phenomena.
interesting_sentence: "I am definetly in the Dark Night, the Dukka Nanas. From the profound, deep, intense joy of the A&P, i have come crashing down into depresion and not wanting to do anything." —Sam Gentile
helpful_sentence: "Concentration is more pleasant/healing. Noting can be turbulent/destabilising. Considering your health you must be the judge." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Meditators managing chronic illness or financial hardship who want a candid daily account of moving through the A&P and into the Dark Night under close teacher guidance.

log: Piers Retreat Report(s) and practice log
author: Piers M
summary: Piers recounts an intensive 60-plus-day Mahasi-style retreat at Panditarama under Sayadaw U Pandita, describing exhaustion-triggered jhana-like drops into piti and sukha, a mid-retreat collapse into what a teacher's talk called 'chronic yogi syndrome,' and recovery through deliberate acceptance practice.
interesting_sentence: "You won't find that instruction in a typical Dharma manual." —Piers M
helpful_sentence: "The contracted mind cannot note properly or continuously. Objects are missed. Sati does not develop. Without Sati, Samadhi cannot arise. Without Samadhi, Yogi is bored and frustrated and wants to leave." —Piers M
who_should_read: Yogis weighing an intensive Burmese Mahasi retreat who want an honest account of exhaustion, concentration breakthroughs, and the 'chronic yogi' plateau along with practical retreat-logistics detail.

author: Rod C
summary: Rod, a returning meditator, methodically works up through the four Samatha jhanas via breath-count concentration, layering in Mahasi-style noting and off-cushion HAIETMOBA practice while navigating work stress, energetic 'kriya' jerks, and uncertainty about his position pre-A&P.
interesting_sentence: "I have had a growing desire to just go and get peace and quiet - leave everything (I won't do this of course) but much less tolerance for noise and complexity. Its as if I am driving me rather than me if that makes sense." —Rod C
helpful_sentence: "There are stages when you feel like you could meditate forever, and then later it seems difficult to count ten breaths in a row. This is normal and part of the ebb and flow. Don't fight it and try not to get frustrated." —Dodge E Knees
who_should_read: Concentration-focused beginners curious how to methodically map and stabilize the four jhanas before layering in insight/noting practice.

author: Val Keinen
summary: Val logs seven years cycling in the Dukkha Nanas, combining Mahasi-style noting with qigong and TMI methods while working through PTSD-driven near-psychotic rage and despair, kratom use, and brief but recognizable tastes of Equanimity via walking and daily-life noting.
interesting_sentence: "I have a non-serious dream that one day I'll make it through this and make an extreme metal album called \"Reobservation.\"" —Val Keinen
helpful_sentence: "When these thoughts and behaviors/urges come up, try to focus on the underlying feelings as physical sensations in the body. The thoughts & behaviors are really just the result of the feelings trying to get attention. The more you can give attention to the feelings directly, the less need there is for reactive thought/behavior patterns." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners navigating a long, psychologically complex Dark Night alongside trauma or mental illness who want a candid, non-idealized account of cycling between Reobservation and Equanimity.

author: Sam Gentile
summary: Daily noting-and-concentration log tracking a shift from subtle skin-only sensations through Low Equanimity to a bliss-and-tears episode the author's teacher confirms as Stream Entry, alongside ongoing Dzogchen study and forum pushback on his claim.
interesting_sentence: "I THIINK I JUST HIT STREAM ENTRY!" —Sam Gentile
helpful_sentence: "'welcome each count-breath as your ONLY guest' and see it/listen to it/welcome it as the most important thing there ever will be." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Noting/POI practitioners curious about a candid, blow-by-blow account of a claimed Stream Entry and the ensuing teacher-confirmation and community skepticism.

author: plantguy *
summary: Weekly 'sprint' log of MIDL breath-relaxation practice working through gross dullness, doubt and restlessness, paired with the Ideal Parent Figure Protocol, with growing non-reactivity and openness noticed in daily life.
interesting_sentence: "Then the visual field darkened, and it felt like i was being sucked into a tunnel. Fear came up, and I dropped out of it." —plantguy *
helpful_sentence: "if you doubt you are not doing it right, the best and safest is to \"return back\" to noting actual body sensations." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Meditators combining a dry-insight/MIDL practice with inner-child or attachment-focused psychotherapy who want a systematic weekly progress log.

author: Sameer
summary: A ten-day self-directed Mahasi noting retreat log detailing intense leg pain, a coffee-withdrawal sleep-deprivation experiment, and iterative troubleshooting between aloud/silent and eyes-open/closed noting styles.
interesting_sentence: "Then there was a vision of a rectangular spiral staircase from the top and sheer terror with the fear of falling down." —Sameer
helpful_sentence: "Sleep deprivation (even if you don't feel like sleeping) has a way of creating big problems. It's one of the biggest warning signs that you are trying to do too much and are using too much effort." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners planning their own self-directed noting retreat who want an honest account of the pain, sleep issues, and negative-emotion cycles involved.

author: Vasily Andreev
summary: A single opening sit report describing self-sense fading, attention spontaneously roaming through body and space, and gently fluxing wave-like sensations while the 3 characteristics became more vivid.
interesting_sentence: "Then the space around me as well as any sensations on my \"body\", which had lost it's form and felt like sensations, began fluxing together very gently and easily like waves coming and going, up and down." —Vasily Andreev
helpful_sentence: "I'm also actively trying to quit mapping as much as well as relaxing and letting the fields synchronize, as Daniel puts it, and watch how things arise and pass away on their own." —Vasily Andreev
who_should_read: Newcomers curious what an early, unmapped sit touching A&P-like perceptual shifts and self-boundary loosening can feel like.

author: Sam Gentile
summary: A stream-entered noter chronicles daily Concentration/Noting sits alongside a growing Dzogchen and non-dual (Michael Taft) practice, while cycling through severe chronic pain, financial crisis, and ultimately a fatal pancreatic cancer diagnosis before passing away.
interesting_sentence: "It''s officIal. The doctor called. I have advanced pancreatic cancer and advanced liver cancer. I think its over." —Sam Gentile
helpful_sentence: "I have a process I like to use for things I'm feeling aversive to: promise myself to just do it for say, five minutes, and then if I totally hate it, I'm allowed to stop." —Logan G
who_should_read: Practitioners facing serious illness or terminal diagnosis who want a candid, unfiltered account of sustaining a mixed noting/Dzogchen practice through pain and grief.

log: Nothing to see here, move along [John M.] [MIGRATE]
author: John M.
summary: Documents a rapid, largely spontaneous progression from access concentration through the rupa and arupa jhanas using a relaxation-based (rather than effortful) entry method, including a brief centerless samadhi state and an A&P event during a dream.
interesting_sentence: "Recently I entered a brief state -- maybe 5 seconds tops -- that seemed to be completely centre-less." —John M.
helpful_sentence: "It isn't produced by letting go, and letting go doesn't help jhana factors arise. Rather, the factors themselves ARE the letting go." —Not Tao
who_should_read: Concentration-focused practitioners curious about a systematic, relaxation-driven approach to progressing rapidly through the jhanas into the arupa states.

author: Platu
summary: A restaurant worker uses continuous, unstructured written self-inquiry (heavily influenced by Jed McKenna) rather than formal sitting technique to dissect fear of public exposure, work-related resistance, guilt, and shame.
interesting_sentence: "I don't remember when I have felt so much fear in my life." —Platu
helpful_sentence: "It will probably be extremely uncomfortable at first, but with practice it's something you can get comfortable doing and eventually you will notice that the reservoir of old shame is slowly draining out of your system." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to raw, confessional self-inquiry as a practice, especially those wrestling with work-related resistance, shame, and fear of public vulnerability.

log: Samsaric Breaks: Mystic Referee's Practice Log
author: mystic referee
summary: A medical intern using TMI stage-based concentration practice logs a slide from Stage 4-5 back to Stage 3-4 amid irregular hospital shifts, mainly wrestling with early-morning dullness and noticing mindfulness lapses in daily hospital work.
interesting_sentence: "Also trying to notice that every form of suffering is something caused by a thought about the past or the future, and that there's absolutely nothing miserable going on in the present moment." —mystic referee
helpful_sentence: "Progress has always been extremely nonlinear for me, and thinking too much about stages, ranking sits/experiences, maps/models, and progress in general has been detrimental to my practice during the actual sits." —Ryan Kay
who_should_read: Busy working professionals or medical trainees seeking realistic strategies for sustaining TMI-style concentration practice around irregular schedules and morning dullness.

log: Video Killed the Radio Star - Papa's log 4
author: Papa Che Dusko
summary: Papa Che Dusko logs daily noting-aloud and Fire Kasina sits alongside grueling seasonal manual labor, tracking recurring bodily agony he attributes to the 3C's, a cessation from the prior year, and an emerging sense of impersonal, watcher-less 'knowing' off cushion.
interesting_sentence: "Burn at this temperature for a while, turning into nothigness every time something comes up, wanting only zero, and you end up with something like equanimity, except that you don't particularly want equanimity anymore, it's a fucking bait and switch, obviously, and so on into nothingness." —Tim Farrington
helpful_sentence: "I follow speed noting until I feel absorption taking place and there is more clarity and mind-body seems more locked into each other, and if there is natural curiosity taking place and interest into an object then I do Investigate that more." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Advanced yogis navigating post-path embodiment, physical adverse effects, and prolonged 3C's/Dark Night territory while juggling demanding manual labor and family life.

author: Sam Gentile
summary: Sam logs near-daily 30-60 minute noting sessions paired with lightweight Vipassana-Jhana concentration practice under teacher Abre Fournier, repeatedly cycling through boredom, dullness, and Dark-Night-flavored doubt and "Desire for Deliverance" without reaching a clear cessation or path claim.
interesting_sentence: "I'd rather do "that other thing" than wake up every morning and face this life." —Sam Gentile
helpful_sentence: "Body sensations are really the safest place if you assume to be "desiering mind states" just so you could note them!" —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Noting practitioners stuck in repetitive dullness/boredom cycles who want a candid, granular daily record of working with a personal teacher through Dark-Night-type territory.

log: Nothing to see here, move along
author: John M
key_words: energetic pressure at bridge of nose
summary: John M logs about five weeks of daily concentration sits progressing from access concentration through the first several jhanas into brief centerless, boundless states, ending with a self-described A&P event followed by Dark Night territory.
interesting_sentence: "It took a good while to return to normalcy, and I'm still missing a pair of socks." —John M
helpful_sentence: "Relaxation = jhana." —Not Tao
who_should_read: Concentration-focused beginners curious how jhana practice can spontaneously shade into insight-map territory like A&P and Dark Night.

author: Sat Chit
summary: Sat Chit, who experienced stream entry in India at 23, resumes serious noting practice years later with DreamWalker's guidance, inventing a fast shorthand labeling system while tracking his progress through the early nanas toward a self-set goal of second path.
interesting_sentence: "There was a visual represenation of an observer that is like the nucleus of an atom, whereas the electron cloud around it represented the sensations." —Sat Chit
helpful_sentence: "I find that passive notes, like "feeling", "hearing" and "seeing" work really well to keep my anchored, but not when I am doing something active." —Sat Chit
who_should_read: Practitioners restarting practice after a long lapse who want to see how a shorthand noting vocabulary is built up while re-approaching the nanas after an earlier stream entry.

author: Sam s
summary: A practitioner logs roughly five months of 30-45 minute daily noting sessions, oscillating between vipassana and jhana attempts while reporting perceptual distortions, finger-joint pressure, and torso vibrations, before shifting to Reggie Ray-style somatic breathing practice.
interesting_sentence: "I sometimes get intense pressure in mainly my finger joints which I eventually have to move, as it feels like they're bending backwards." —Sam s
helpful_sentence: "The last thing I wanted to mention is that if I've learned anything after being on this forum and through my practice is never lose your meditation object." —Travis Gene McKinstry
who_should_read: Beginners uncertain whether to pursue vipassana or concentration practice who want a candid, meandering account of early exploratory practice.

log: Noting Aloud + Ki breathing - Papa Che Log 3
author: Papa Che Dusko
summary: A highly detailed daily log of freestyle noting-aloud combined with Ki-breathing (Aikido style), cycling through re-observation and equanimity within the 3C's stage of second path, marked by persistent body jerks, visual murk phenomena, and family/illness interruptions.
interesting_sentence: "This was agony in its purest!" —Papa Che Dusko
helpful_sentence: "The lesson in 3C's is to trust the attention and sensations doing its thing and let go of the notion that \"I\" am moving the attentuon from one to the next sensation." —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in granular noting-aloud phenomenology within second-path territory, especially those combining noting with breathwork.

author: Plne
summary: A reflective, philosophically dense log blending Shinzen Young-style noting, mantra 'pulsing', and Actual Freedom-influenced daily attention practice, exploring lost motivation after a relationship began and recurring dream and self-inquiry themes.
interesting_sentence: "It was highly addictive and compelling for me and sought to enslave me." —Plne
helpful_sentence: "namely: that even the good stuff is in essence not satisfying and although pleasant must also be seen as just more stuff that we cling to and which holds us back." —tom moylan
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to philosophical self-inquiry and daily-life attention practice, especially those wrestling with motivation loss during equanimity-like phases.

author: Seamus O
summary: An intensive practitioner logs rapid progression from probable stream entry through second and third path within a couple of months, using rapid sound-based noting, deliberate concentration technique, and jhana cycling, alongside recovery from a depressive episode.
interesting_sentence: "my heart started racing, then I kept chasing the observer, and reality fucking crashed." —Seamus O
helpful_sentence: "Read Dan's book on the three doors and see how you can get there via the different doors of the 3 characteristics." —Richard Zen
who_should_read: Serious, intensive practitioners wanting a granular, technique-by-technique account of cycling through the nanas and multiple path attainments in rapid succession.

author: Sam s
summary: Sam logs months of daily 20-45 minute Vipassana noting sessions (feel/see/hear), oscillating toward and away from jhana attempts, and repeatedly reports vibratory body distortions, finger-joint pressure, and brief equanimous-feeling states without matching them confidently to the map.
interesting_sentence: "At that point I reached the point of no return; the Arising and Passing of phenomenon, or A&P." —Travis Gene McKinstry
helpful_sentence: "In my experience the only way to deal with restlessness, anxiety, or doubt is to just sit." —Travis Gene McKinstry
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate noting practitioners uncertain where they sit on the map who want a candid, unresolved account of body-distortion phenomena and jhana-vs-vipassana indecision.

log: Noting with muscle memory (with charts) Practice Log
author: Anton
summary: Anton logs roughly twenty numbered 20-60 minute open-monitoring noting sessions tracked via a custom muscle-memory app (see/hear/feel/mental image/think/emotion), charting noting-speed shifts alongside piti, dark-night-type dips, non-arising contemplation, and repeated equanimity-labeled states.
interesting_sentence: "Yikes! That's how self first constructed" thought. —Anton
helpful_sentence: "It took about 15 minutes until I no longer had to look at the buttons and several more days until it became as effortless or easier than labeling by verbal thought." —Anton
who_should_read: Data-minded practitioners curious about quantifying noting practice and correlating sensation-label ratios with shifts in insight stage.

author: seth tapper
summary: seth tapper posts three short free-verse poems using imagery of a whale hunt and a bounding shell to describe self-chasing dissolving into a felt sense of completed liberation, closing with the classic 'nothing left undone' arahant formula.
interesting_sentence: "I am the whale all I chased was my tail" —seth tapper
helpful_sentence: "Brush lines in a letter to a dear friend turn out beautifully. A moment of joy." ~ryokan —terry
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in poetic, artistic expressions of post-path realization rather than technical session-by-session detail.

author: Voje Sambar
summary: Voje Sambar logs a shift from casual solo Vipassana to daily 1-3 hour sessions combining breath-counting concentration, metta, and noting alongside regular Zen-center sittings, reporting a multi-day bout of resistance, the disappearance of chronic anxiety, and unexplained physical tension/energy in the genitals.
interesting_sentence: "I do wonder something, about my balls. I am very tight in my balls." —Voje Sambar
helpful_sentence: "Morality is the basis for concentration, which is the basis for insight." —T DC
who_should_read: Newer practitioners navigating motivation swings, spiritual ego, and questions about combining Zen and Theravada-style concentration/insight practice.

author: Sam S
summary: Practices Zen huatou ('Who am I?') within a Philip Kapleau-lineage residential sangha, reporting minor Equanimity signs alongside Dark Night-related tension and computer-lock procrastination, before shifting focus to stabilizing first jhana via breath and pitisukkha.
interesting_sentence: "This practice is better than heroin." —Sam S
helpful_sentence: "Have found that there's 2 kinds of bliss so far: the pleasure of the breath itself, and the blissful feelings that come up from focusing on that pleasure." —Sam S
who_should_read: Zen practitioners curious how huatou/kensho practice interacts with the A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity map and jhana cultivation.

author: charon
summary: Chronicles a shift from samatha to MCTB-style vipassana noting following an anatta shift with an online group, tracking intense background vibrations and dark-night cycling, observer-investigation, and reflections on childhood hallucinations shaping his practice.
interesting_sentence: "I remember many times crying at the thought I was insane because of how loud, transient, and out of my control such thoughts were." —charon
helpful_sentence: "Retrospective analysis of my sits, with all these points in mind, have made me realise how I’m striving for SE in the same way I was striving for jhana in my previous practise - and how such striving is creating content I’m getting lost in." —charon
who_should_read: Vipassana practitioners deep in dark-night vibration/noting territory who want a granular, unfiltered account of observer-investigation and striving pitfalls.

author: Arena Heidi
summary: Journals an ongoing post-2022 shift into effortless access to 'vastness'/the unknown as her meditation focus, describing how sitting with the unknown while relocating to an isolated mountain home surfaces and dissolves ego fear, grief, and entanglement.
interesting_sentence: "Don’t think you can sit in the unknown. The unknown sits the unknown!" —Arena Heidi
helpful_sentence: "I am learning to feel okay with the ego’s discomfort and and seeing the humor in the ego once its distress dissipates." —Arena Heidi
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring nondual 'unknown'-as-object practice who want to see how it interweaves with major real-life upheaval and ego fear.

author: walking rain
summary: Documents a disciplined vipassana noting practice tracking the three characteristics across many sits, moving from flickering/bubbling impermanence through dissolving observer/doer sensations, brief non-dual off-cushion experiences, and equanimity-like ease with pain and restlessness.
interesting_sentence: "Like somebody talks and it felt like I was talking, a car passes and it felt like I was making that noise." —walking rain
helpful_sentence: "Impermanence: reality is made up of shimmering particles that fade from experience as soon as they are experienced." —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Vipassana noters who want a granular, month-by-month record of three-characteristics practice edging into equanimity and non-dual glimpses.

author: Siavash '
summary: Chronicles roughly eighteen months of intense kundalini-style energetics, flying dreams, and fire kasina practice cycling through A&P, Dark Night, and Equanimity while starting a new office job and losing his father.
interesting_sentence: "My head feels very heavy and I can not relax it, but fuck it, who cares." —Siavash '
helpful_sentence: "It's important for Dark Night Yogis to not worry that a strong A&P is going to lead into a terrible Dark Night." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in richly detailed kundalini-style energetic phenomenology and long-term nana cycling while balancing full-time work and family grief.

author: Paul Smith
summary: Recounts recovery from a psychotic episode triggered by intensive Mahasi noting, then cautiously rebuilding practice with Goenka-style technique, Culadasa's triplet noting, and nada-sound/light phenomena while staying on medication and in therapy.
interesting_sentence: "My thoughts were still vibrating with extremely high speed and I remember not being able to use language - its expressive power was too weak." —Paul Smith
helpful_sentence: "Meditation should be good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners wondering how to safely resume meditation after a practice-induced psychotic break, and how to balance psychiatric care with intensive noting.

author: Wings
summary: A ten-year Goenka-style vipassana practitioner describes shifting attention from bodily sensations to the quality of awareness itself, using a 'fish tank' metaphor for a still-versus-turbulent field of equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "It feels like I am sitting in a fish tank, sometimes a small one, just some 10 cms around my body, sometimes much bigger." —Wings
helpful_sentence: "So, when I sit at home I now actually direct my attention to this quality of awareness instead of at the sensations." —Wings
who_should_read: Vipassana practitioners curious about shifting attention from individual sensations to the quality of awareness itself within Goenka-style technique.

log: Sharing the Practice with Good People
author: George S Lteif
summary: Documents rising-falling noting sessions in the Ajaan Tong tradition that produce spontaneous neck-twisting and crying-laughing cycles, which Daniel Ingram identifies as classic first-jhana phenomena, followed by months of calmer chest vibrations.
interesting_sentence: "Then my mind focused on my teeth (strange) and I got disgusted and started crying, then saw myself crying and started laughing and entered into a cycle of crying-laughing for several rounds." —George S Lteif
helpful_sentence: "As long you stay long with the calm sensation you stop the progress as you follow your desire for the pleasurable mind state and you avoid learning." —Noting Monkey
who_should_read: Meditators curious about classic first-jhana phenomenology such as spontaneous shaking, crying, and disgust arising within a rising-falling noting practice.

log: Walter's Practice Log: Striving for Stream Entry
author: Brato Ganibe
summary: Detailed session-by-session notes on breath counting, SHF, and Mahasi noting while preparing for a solo retreat, including two moments where the internal visual field abruptly blanks out like a 'reset button.'
interesting_sentence: "It was almost like something pressed a reset button in the middle of the noting." —Brato Ganibe
helpful_sentence: "You haven't experienced post Stream Entry or a Fruition, so you don't know what that is, and you can't know it unless you experience it, so it's not reasonable to crave for something that you don't know what it is." —Siavash '
who_should_read: Practitioners preparing for a concentration/insight retreat who want granular session notes on breath counting, noting, dullness, and watcher investigation en route to stream entry.

author: Siavash
summary: A multi-month log mixing fire-kasina and murk-visual practice with intense energetic/kundalini sensations, sleep disturbances, and a resurgence of panic-attack-like breathing anxiety during the COVID-19 lockdown, gradually settling into calmer, more equanimous states.
interesting_sentence: "Darkness, darkness, darkness and darkness. Lost, hopeless. No more energy. No energy for anger." —Siavash
helpful_sentence: "Just remember, reobservation is trying to show you all your triggers (good) yet is also trying to seduce you into reacting (bad)." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in fire-kasina visual work and kundalini-type energetic phenomena who want a candid account of practicing through anxiety and physical illness.

author: Pepe
summary: A dense, highly analytical log tracking a shift from non-conceptual awareness and Self-Inquiry practice into systematic noting of vipassana-jhanas and micro-aversion/greed patterns, culminating in plans for an intensive six-hour-a-day home retreat after quitting his job.
interesting_sentence: "'I am I' emerged... and when I was thinking of repeating the phrase as if it were an Inquiry in itself, when I said 'I' the subconscious responded 'IS'." —Pepe
helpful_sentence: "It was my mind, making massive assumptions based on previous memories and experiences with the objects in my attention, and projecting this process to consciousness." —Chris M
who_should_read: Pre-stream-entry practitioners doing intensive Self-Inquiry and vipassana-jhana work who want a granular, mentor-guided account of refining concentration and noticing subtle reactivity.

author: Shari Tresky
summary: A new DhO member and professional counselor introduces herself, describing 25 years of off-and-on meditation, a grief-driven rediscovery of practice through MCTB2 after her partner's death, and her first day of simple noting.
interesting_sentence: "It's a poker game for the highest human stakes, and you're already all in." —Tim Farrington
helpful_sentence: "Notice the 3 characteristics of every sensation that you experience, in real time." —Steph S
who_should_read: Complete beginners to insight meditation who want a warm, community-driven introduction to starting a noting practice on DhO.

author: warble head
summary: An extremely detailed, near-daily log of long corpse-pose sits combining chakra scanning, mantra, and astral-projection-style experiments with a systematic technique for dissolving the self, observer, and sense of 'doing,' reporting frequent probable cessations while pursuing second path.
interesting_sentence: "I felt myself moving towards the dukkha door. It felt like some sort of Lovecraftian being had seized me and was now pulling me in." —warble head
helpful_sentence: "I realized that awareness and focus/concentration are two different things." —warble head
who_should_read: Concentration-focused practitioners interested in chakra/energy work and systematic self-dissolution techniques who want a blow-by-blow account of rapid, repeated cessation-chasing.

author: Siavash
summary: A software engineer in a pandemic-era daily log battles chronic work-avoidance and recurring COVID-like sickness while logging kundalini-style pains and vibrations, lucid-dream practice, and brief tastes of equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "Then a feeling started moving up from the base of spine, like water moving in a pipe, it moved a few inches up and stopped, then there was a storm of sexual energy." —Siavash '
helpful_sentence: "I tend to have what I call spiritosomatic pain. Surprisingly many times it has turned out that some pain that seemed so very physical disappeared totally as soon as I had an insight related to the chakra where the pain was." —Linda "Polly Ester" Ö
who_should_read: Meditators dealing with kundalini-like energetic symptoms, health anxiety, and work-avoidance who want a candid, unpolished daily log rather than a tidy insight narrative.

log: Practice Log - Changes from Sutra to Tantra
author: lightbodyseeker_
summary: A former MCTB-style practitioner switches to Amrita Mandala tantra, using shouted-mantra Dynamic Concentration and shamanic soul-retrieval work to open successive 'bhumis' while unwinding subliminal programming, root trauma, and autism-linked dysregulation.
interesting_sentence: "This irritability and frustration turned out to be caused by external entities (demons) that I'd picked up by doing demonic magick several years ago from a popular book on Amazon." —lightbodyseeker _
helpful_sentence: "The pleasant sensations/bliss of jhanas and other meditative absorptions come from the meridians activating and stirring up the traumas and selfishness stored in those meridians." —lightbodyseeker _
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to Mahayana tantra and trauma-informed post-awakening work who want a detailed, if unconventional, account of translating sutra insight language into tantric practice.

author: Shaun Steelgrave
summary: A Mahasi-noting and color-kasina practitioner catalogues vivid third-eye HUD visuals and nimitta variants while mapping cycling through A&P, re-observation, and multiple layers of Equanimity, and personifies dark-night patterns as 'the Inquisitor.'
interesting_sentence: "I feel like maybe some old lamas are trolling me from the celestial homes in the van allen belt." —Shaun Steelgrave
helpful_sentence: "The tricky thing is the more you try to \"catch\" it, the more power it gets. But the more you allow it and study it with curiousity (even a kind of appreciation and respect) then the more it falls apart." —shargrol
who_should_read: Yogis who enjoy richly described visual/energetic phenomenology and playful, self-aware personification of dark-night mind-states while still pre-stream-entry.

author: Pepe
summary: A near-daily speed-noting practitioner logs granular ñana-by-ñana phenomenology—mental inertia, 'watching the watcher,' and self-described vipassana-jhanas—while consolidating a stable, panoramic pre-stream-entry Equanimity over several months, capped by a multi-day home retreat.
interesting_sentence: "Vipassana Jhanas!! Until now 'vipassana jhana' (VJ) was a concept." —Pepe ·
helpful_sentence: "Soak in jhana, soak in spatiality, soak in feelings of continuity. Your mind will probably want to \"do something\" but notice this is a very subtle aversion and a very subtle fear of giving up control." —shargrol
who_should_read: Detail-oriented noting practitioners in Equanimity who want granular, jargon-rich phenomenological mapping and substantive back-and-forth with experienced yogis like shargrol.

author: Siavash
summary: A long-running, densely detailed log of intense kundalini-like energetics, visual phenomena, and dream awareness alternating with severe work-related procrastination, despair, and grief over a beloved singer's death.
interesting_sentence: "it felt that the heart wants to come out of my mouth" —Siavash
helpful_sentence: "You will always have enough time for practice, one way or another." —Tim Farrington
