who_should_read: Practitioners curious about raw, extended kundalini-type energetic phenomenology intertwined with real-life work-avoidance and depression.

log: Seeking guidance from non-sufferers:  10 day solo retreat  :D
author: Claire O
summary: Day-by-day log of a 10-day solo self-retreat combining noting, RAIN, and relaxed-body instructions from a mentor, moving from anxious speediness to piti/jhana experiences and repeated no-self/emptiness insights.
interesting_sentence: "Piti errupted haha flooding the body with waves of glee, also happiness/sukka too." —Claire O
helpful_sentence: "Retreats can be helpful or they can set back a meditator even further -- they are like playing with fire." —shargrol
who_should_read: Anyone planning a first solo retreat who wants a candid, mentor-guided example of managing mania/anxiety while building a noting practice.

log: Practice Log - I managed to get myself up to speed for the second time
author: Attila Olah
summary: A beginner tunes his noting pace across several sits, debates whether breath-focus counts as samatha, and reflects on cravings via a smoking-cessation and ice-cream anecdote.
interesting_sentence: "I realised that cravings cannot be satisefied by feeding them, they can only be postponed but you will have to deal with them in the end." —Attila Olah
helpful_sentence: "What if you were paying attention to the arising and passing away of that one object? Watch it flutter. Watch it appear and disappear." —Chris M
who_should_read: Beginner noters fine-tuning their noting speed and deciding between object-focused and open-field vipassana.

author: Shawn Rene Ingram
summary: A single one-line entry noting an attempt at a three-hour sit, with no further detail, replies, or follow-up.
interesting_sentence: "Trying a longer sit this morning. Three hours." —Shawn Rene Ingram
helpful_sentence: "Trying a longer sit this morning. Three hours." —Shawn Rene Ingram
who_should_read: Of little use to any reader given the single-line entry and absence of further content.

author: Siavash
summary: Siavash logs months of intensive fire-kasina and visual-static practice interwoven with deep processing of childhood trauma, family guilt, and recurring anger, supported by detailed peer counseling from George S on inner-child work and the six realms/elements model.
interesting_sentence: "Poor fucking idiot. This technology has turned us into these little selfish arrogant bastards." —Siavash
helpful_sentence: "The advantage of combining the two is that you can make faster progress because you are really working on your core reactive patterns, which tend to show up more clearly in stressful daily life situations." —George S
who_should_read: Kasina practitioners interested in pairing intense visual-concentration practice with trauma-focused emotional processing.

log: Seeking Truth - Practice and Contemplation Log
author: Truth Seeker
summary: Truth Seeker spends over a year testing syncretic Christian/Buddhist concentration frameworks — breath, perceived light, and even a basketball-shooting posture as a kasina object — frequently practicing while high on marijuana, without ever confirming he has reached access concentration.
interesting_sentence: "This shit scares me sometimes. Im scared of my own mind at times and the places it goes. This practice feels like an umbrella from the storm, a rock admist the sea" —Truth Seeker
helpful_sentence: "If something scares you while meditating label it as \"fear\" and connect the dots! How does it feel in the body? Where in the body? etc ... connecting the breadcrumbs so to speak" —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Eclectic practitioners curious about blending Christian contemplative prayer with Buddhist concentration technique and unconventional kasina objects.

author: M C
summary: M C (addressed by repliers as Trial and Error / Nick N.) chronicles a 10-day Goenka retreat that triggers intense fear and repressed-emotion release, followed by a year of cycling through dark night, equanimity, and reobservation, culminating in a nothingness experience and a Zen-flavored 'mind opening' moment, though cessation has not yet occurred.
interesting_sentence: "I, and the things around me started to lose their substance. They seemed more and more empty and flickering. They were like illusions formed on nothingness. Like paintings on air, or digital formations." —M C
helpful_sentence: "I would spend some time looking/feeling into the sensations that occur and see if you can sense a quality of permanence or some kinda overlay of solidity." —Dream Walker
who_should_read: Practitioners cycling between dark night and equanimity after an intensive Goenka retreat who want phenomenological detail on the transition plus self-inquiry practice.

author: Sheldon Frith Nicholson
summary: Sheldon, a 19-year-old absolute beginner, recounts a 2012 life-changing experience he suspects was A&P, then a self-directed week in the woods attempting to attain first jhana through breath concentration, followed by a demotivation slump he wonders is the Dark Night.
interesting_sentence: "I did not care what happened to me, or humans, or life because I was thinking from the perspective of the Universe." —Sheldon Frith Nicholson
helpful_sentence: "This sure sounds like narratives I've had in Dark Night experiences. I'd say keep it simple and keep practicing concentration." —Adam
who_should_read: Absolute beginners wondering whether an intense unexplained peak experience might have been an A&P event, and who are struggling with post-experience motivation.

author: Siavash
summary: A months-long, near-daily diary of intense body energetics, visual murk phenomena, and cycling through self-diagnosed dark-night states (fear, disgust, despair, anger) interspersed with periods of equanimity, alongside ongoing work with childhood trauma and a destructive habit.
interesting_sentence: "An hour ago a perception arose that the experience, whatever and however that is, is complete or perfect, can't be any different and doesn't need to be any different." —Siavash
helpful_sentence: "You are right about the need to really feel the feelings. The problem is the second arrow - the mental despair and suffering which gets overlaid on top of the physical and emotional pain (the first arrow)." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in a granular, trauma-informed account of cycling between dark-night states and equanimity alongside kundalini-like energetic phenomena.

author: Simon T
summary: A single dense retrospective post describing a psychedelic-catalyzed intensive practice period that culminated in a sudden 'camera-flash' shift releasing all body tension, followed by self-mapping of subsequent days onto dissolution, fear, misery, disgust, re-observation, and equanimity.
interesting_sentence: "I felt like a rag doll left on the bed." —Simon T
helpful_sentence: "It seems that there is three modes to dissolution: In the first mode things feel pretty normal except that I'm lost in my thoughts a lot." —Simon T
who_should_read: Practitioners curious how psychedelic use can intersect with and accelerate the classic insight-stage cycle, told through detailed self-diagnostic mapping.

log: Practice Log Aiming for 1st Jhana, Stream Entry & Beyond
author: Ryan Jaidsedha Burton
summary: An aspiring yogi preparing for a 60-day silent retreat at Panditarama documents early Anapanasati/jhana attempts, mistakes intense bliss for near-absorption, and after forum and teacher feedback pivots his retreat plan from Samatha jhana to Vipassana noting.
interesting_sentence: "I was on the verge of Jhana as a bright light had appeared in my mind and the 5 Jhana factors strengthened to an incredible almost unbearable extent." —Ryan Jaidsedha Burton
helpful_sentence: "Yogis can get into some weird / fearful places when going \"solo\" or having inadequate guidance as the purification of mind happens." —Tina Rasmussen
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate yogis planning long silent retreats who want a candid look at technique-switching doubts and the difference between deep access concentration and true absorption.

author: Shiva R Wheeler
summary: A brand-new practitioner's first log entry after a 10-day Goenka course describes a 60-minute body-scan sit with tingling, wave-like sensations, a dissolving sense of center, and an effort to stay equanimous with the exhilaration that arose.
interesting_sentence: "The sit became more spacious and I seemed to dissolve and be without a center." —Shiva R Wheeler
helpful_sentence: "I affirmed to stay in equanimity." —Shiva R Wheeler
who_should_read: Brand-new Goenka-method practitioners curious what early body-scan sits and their subtle energetic sensations can feel like.

author: William S
summary: A returning-to-practice yogi investigates the physical sensation of anxiety and has what other posters identify as a Mind-and-Body/A&P shift, then spirals into an unusually severe depressive dark-night episode compounded by COVID-related small-business stress.
interesting_sentence: "...starting and basically instantly getting clobbered by some Thor hammer of existential depression." —William S
helpful_sentence: "Be tough as nails and gentle as a feather." —Tim Farrington
who_should_read: New practitioners wanting a candid, forum-validated account of tipping from an A&P-like insight into a genuine dark-night depressive episode amid real-life stress.

author: Siavash '
summary: The seventh installment of an intensely detailed log dominated by painful kundalini-style energetics, insomnia and visions, interwoven with work-from-home procrastination, financial stress, and Bradshaw-style inner-child grief work.
interesting_sentence: "It made the whole body jump up, with an involuntary mini-scream kind of sound, that all happened in less than a second." —Siavash '
helpful_sentence: "All I can say is that if you can remain present with the core shame/guilt/anger long enough then it starts to run out of fuel." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in kundalini/energetic phenomena colliding with real-life procrastination and childhood trauma work during a prolonged Dark-Night-like period.

author: Simon Willis
summary: A beginner's breath-counting concentration log tracking gradual entry into a recurring 'closer stage' absorption state, chair/bench/kneeling-chair experiments to manage arthritic knee and hip pain, and a shift toward noting after forum advice.
interesting_sentence: "Its like throwing a ball at a wall and expecting it to stick." —Simon Willis
helpful_sentence: "Concentration can best be built up by 1) applying the technique of noting, 2) meditating a couple times a day [spacing them out], and 3) noting off the cushion." —Alan Smithee
who_should_read: Beginning concentration practitioners dealing with physical pain during sits who want a candid account of the transition from breath-counting to noting.

author: finding-oneself ♤
summary: Recovering from a broken foot, the author logs a multi-month noting/TMI practice arc cycling through loneliness, depression and cannabis experimentation into increasingly stable Equanimity, framed by grief over a brother's fatal overdose and burnout from medical-field work.
interesting_sentence: "I literally felt demonic aliens approaching (shudder)." —finding-oneself ♤
helpful_sentence: "There's only a problem when thoughts are taken to be somehow 'more real' than they actually are relative to the other senses." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners cycling through Dark Night territory who want an honest, unfiltered account of depression, addiction-adjacent coping, and eventual stabilization in Equanimity via TMI-based noting.

author: Shri
summary: A meticulously systematic twice-daily noting log tracking TMI-style progress, recurring piti and buzzing energetic waves, and candid self-doubt about progress, enriched by detailed technique coaching from experienced DhO members.
interesting_sentence: "It seemed the eye, the one looking flipped and fell inside the body, somewhere in the torso, and then was looking in the upward direction seeing/feeling the sensation of tightness in mouth etc in my head." —Shri
helpful_sentence: "So before the sit say this to yourself; Will I ever get any attainments or insights? I dont know. But I do know what is happening RIGHT NOW!" —Papa Che Dusko
who_should_read: Noting practitioners early in their practice who want a model for detailed, structured daily logging and honest troubleshooting of technique doubts.

author: William Quixote
summary: A beginner's daily concentration log documenting progressively reliable entry into first jhana via Leigh Brasington's smile-and-shift technique, tracking recurring white-light and bliss phenomena across varied conditions (rooftop, airplanes, jury duty) before pivoting toward noting.
interesting_sentence: "After a while I saw a light behind my eyelids that was whitish tinted yellow and felt a pleasant sensation partway between the warm feeling after you hear a good joke right before you laugh (80%) and the feeling of leaving a party with someone hot (20%)." —William Quixote
helpful_sentence: "my technique for dealing with songs in my head... is to switch my focus to the song, calmly trying to hear every single subtle change in the sound. This usually makes the song slow down, break up, repeat a little, warp, etc." —Seamus O
who_should_read: Concentration-practice beginners curious what reliably entering first jhana feels like and how to trigger it using a smile/pleasure-shift technique.

author: Siavash
summary: Daily log blending intense kundalini-like body energetics, lucid flying/out-of-body dreams, and deep psychological work on childhood shame, anger, and insecurity alongside bed-based and sitting practice.
interesting_sentence: "I felt like an evil force is trying to possess my body." —Siavash
helpful_sentence: "Repressed emotions never stays completely repressed and so it comes back in an altered way as anger." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners experiencing intense kundalini-like energetics and lucid dream/OBE activity who want a candid, psychologically-integrated account of cycling through the dark night.

author: Smiling Stone
summary: A roughly 78-day loose home retreat of 3-6 hours daily Goenka-style body scanning explores vipassana jhanas, deepening equanimity and spaciousness, and later fire kasina, framed through elaborate music/harmonics and Abhidhamma theorizing.
interesting_sentence: "the witness does not witness, it processes!" —Smiling Stone
helpful_sentence: "You have to trade clarity for inclusiveness." —Jason Massie
who_should_read: Long-term concentration/body-scan practitioners settled in stable equanimity who want a philosophically rich exploration of jhana, space, and the 'last step' toward cessation.

author: Chris Coleman
summary: A complete beginner starts short breath-and-noting sits after a possible midlife crisis and wonders whether his lifelong sense of detachment from thoughts and sensations already reflects early Mind-and-Body and Cause-and-Effect insight stages.
interesting_sentence: "If 'Dark Night' feels anything like a mid-life crisis coupled with a constant restlessness and frequent feeling of being pissed off, I'm there." —Chris Coleman
helpful_sentence: "Maintaining concentration for a long time may seem unmanageable, but it is always manageable to stay with the next breath." —Teemu H
who_should_read: Complete beginners wondering whether a lifelong intellectual detachment from thoughts and sensations already reflects early insight-stage territory.

author: Shuxin Chu
key_words: lucid dreaming and astral projection goals
summary: A university student opens a 14-session summer log of breath-awareness meditation aimed at exploring lucid dreaming and astral projection, using sits to work through a childhood-trauma trigger tied to elevator noise and footsteps.
interesting_sentence: "I held the previous belief that if I truly go into my full potential, then I will be 'shine too bright' and embody my full truth, then I won't be palatable or lovable by the people around me." —Shuxin Chu
helpful_sentence: "Be careful here. It looks a bit like going from one fixed view to another fixed view." —Ryan Kay
who_should_read: Beginners wanting to see how trauma-processing and self-worth work can be woven into a simple breath-awareness practice from the very first sits.

author: The Woofy Hermit
summary: A long-running TMI practitioner cycles through a persistent stage-4 concentration plateau, experiments with fire kasina, metta, and body scanning, undergoes a harrowing high-dose mushroom trip, and later reports a severe crisis of worsening mental health, job loss, and dark-night-like mood cycling.
interesting_sentence: "squidward tuesday cucumber elf phelph winter surface mushroom smoke food rocket drink sunday coward" —The Woofy Hermit
helpful_sentence: "You can't actually speed up noticing, but, you can get better at noticing more. Noticing more is achieved through relaxing and getting better at staying with metacognitive awareness." —Bahiya Baby
who_should_read: Practitioners with underlying mental health struggles considering psychedelics or dry insight practice who want an unflinching cautionary account of meditation intersecting with a personal crisis.

log: Siavash's Practice Log (Fire Kasina and more)
author: Siavash
summary: Siavash tracks nightly Fire Kasina candle-gazing sessions in detail—nimitta colors, molten gold, vibratory piti—while trying to map recurring bouts of fear, sadness, and dullness onto the A&P/Dark Night/Equanimity nanas without ever claiming stream entry.
interesting_sentence: "That does sound like the molten gold, which, as you say, often isn't gold, but somehow we just call it that, as it often does start as bright yellow for some, and a more dull gold for others, but for me it has been hot white, bright purple, and all sorts of irredescent colors." —Daniel M Ingram
helpful_sentence: "It is very common prior to path, whiere someone goes slowly/moderately up through the nanas and quickly back down and slowly/moderately up and quickly down and up and down." —shargrol
who_should_read: Concentration practitioners curious about Fire Kasina nimitta phenomenology and how to map energetic and emotional cycling onto the progress of insight.

author: Smiling Stone
summary: Smiling Stone chronicles years of Goenka-tradition Vipassana retreats and daily body-scanning practice, describing intense metta-driven bliss states, an evolving 'layers of consciousness' theory, and a deliberate distrust of naming attainments.
interesting_sentence: "I was surprised by the intensity of the bliss and realized that the warm, encompassing, velvety vibration was the expression of the purity of my love (in that special moment)." —Smiling Stone
helpful_sentence: "Something that gets noticeably better with years of practice is resilience: the swiftness with which balance is restored after it was lost for a while." —Smiling Stone
who_should_read: Goenka-tradition Vipassana practitioners wanting an introspective, non-map-focused account of body-scan retreats and metta-driven bliss states.

author: J J
summary: James logs a shift from long-standing jhana practice into vipassana, describing a session with dissolving self-sensation and speculating he is cycling through the A&P after an earlier breakthrough tied to feeling he was the Tathagata.
interesting_sentence: "The fact is, I am alive, I am Awake, I am well. But I am not a part of this community, it thus defeats the elitism present here." —J J
helpful_sentence: "Rather one must eliminate unwholesome states and cultivate wholesome ones to bring about more happiness." —J J
who_should_read: Jhana-focused meditators curious about a candid, philosophically digressive account of shifting into vipassana and grappling with a grandiose post-A&P self-narrative.

author: Sigma Tropic
summary: Sigma Tropic documents years of TMI-based samatha/jhana practice under a Culadasa-lineage teacher, tracking stage progression, frequent fruitions, a teacher-confirmed 2nd path shift, and a parallel struggle with drug-addiction recovery.
interesting_sentence: "What I did led to Arhatship and therefore my documenting about it is dharma." —Sigma Tropic
helpful_sentence: "I've noticed that this thing we call \"concentration\" really shouldn't be thought of as a \"me\" attending to an object, rather as letting the object be of it's own accord without grasping, and noticing the grasping that introduces the sense of self and superimposes it onto an object." —Sigma Tropic
who_should_read: TMI practitioners wanting a granular, stage-by-stage account of jhana development, fruition tracking, and the messy overlap between meditative bliss and addiction recovery.

author: spatial
summary: Chronicles a year of oscillating A&P and Dark Night nanas, deliberate jhana and formless-jhana experimentation, and repeated insights into the physical, constructed nature of self and suffering, without settling on a path claim.
interesting_sentence: "...it was like I saw that my suffering was actually under my control (because it was the product of the story in my head), but also simultaneously not under my control (because it was just a conditioned response). This was really shocking, and I literally felt as if I had seen a glitch in the matrix." —spatial
helpful_sentence: "A big part of the challenge of the dark night stages is that you can't make it into what it isn't." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners deep in dark-night cycling who want a raw, introspective account of self-inquiry, jhana experimentation, and the felt sense of self dissolving.

author: Steph S
summary: Continues a detailed dependent-origination discussion with fellow practitioners, describes a multi-day non-dual/emptiness experience during choiceless awareness that later reverted, and shifts into sustained brahma-vihara and fire kasina practice.
interesting_sentence: "It's like there's this stopping point in the center of the head. It feels like a very tiny tension... I even thought something along the lines of, I will keep looking at this even if my head explodes if that's what it takes." —Steph S
helpful_sentence: "Take two different people in the same room with the same incoming sense data and they will have different conscious experiences because of their different conditioning." —George S
who_should_read: Practitioners wanting a rigorous, community-sourced breakdown of dependent origination alongside brahma-vihara and fire kasina practice reports.

log: Practice log No Second Arrow (comments valued and appreciated)
author: No-Second-Arrow Z
summary: A practitioner managing lifelong depression and seasonal affective disorder works through inconsistent first- and second-jhana attainment, vibratory and mortal-fear phenomena, and noting practice, while processing the death of a beloved cat.
interesting_sentence: "I was mortified by fear of dying... and suddenly I felt incredibly fast vibrations horizontally, all across my chest and my shoulders." —No-Second-Arrow Z
helpful_sentence: "Fear is deeply rooted in the body. As you become more sensitive to the body this is going to come up. You are in effect releasing it." —Chuck Kasmire
who_should_read: Meditators managing depression or seasonal affective disorder who want an honest, incremental account of building jhana and noting practice amid mood fluctuations.

log: skin in the game: log x
author: Tadhg
summary: Chronicles months of rapid dark-night/laughter cycling punctuated by frequent brief cessations and sharply diminishing craving, alongside deep engagement with Pali sutta phenomenology and a heated exchange with a hostile poster.
interesting_sentence: "Skin in the fucken game cunt. Bahiya, will you whisht with yourself." —tttttttt hhhhhhh
helpful_sentence: "Non dual experiences are experiences; they are not experience itself. Experience itself is the thing." —tttttttt hhhhhhh
who_should_read: Advanced practitioners in late dark-night/third-path territory who want a raw, humor-laced account of rapid cessation cycling and dissolving craving.

author: Yamazaki
summary: Documents an intensive, daily-logged TMI-stage practice with a profound no-self breakthrough, subsequent dark-night mood cycling, a psychedelic (shrooms/DMT) detour, and a first ten-day Goenka retreat.
interesting_sentence: "I was confronted with that fact that I was going to die one day, and then be gone forever... It felt as though my whole life was just a distraction from this problem of my unavoidable annihilation." —Yamazaki
helpful_sentence: "Unfortunately, sometimes you have to be tough. Not always, but keep the wimping out to a minimum and it actually becomes easier to not wimp out." —shargrol
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate TMI practitioners wanting a candid, day-by-day account of building consistency, facing dullness, and a first retreat.

author: spatial
summary: Spatial narrates visionary retreat phenomena (grid patterns, vibrating light, a suspected third-path shift) alongside real-time struggles with self-inquiry, agency, and a spontaneous confession of romantic feelings.
interesting_sentence: "I just told a really good friend of mine that I have feelings for her, over the phone. I didn't want to do that. I couldn't stop myself." —spatial
helpful_sentence: "That inquiry into 'the observer of the state' is high-energy practice. Something that can only be done a few times during a sit... This is advanced stuff so don't try to treat it like a basic technique that you repeat lots of times during a sit. Less is more." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners deep in post-stream-entry territory who want a raw, blow-by-blow account of visual/energetic phenomena and the confusion of judging one's own path progress.

author: Steph S
summary: Steph works through the middle chakras and brahma vihara jhanas, reporting disorienting non-sequential nana cycling and fielding forum speculation about whether her descriptions indicate third path.
interesting_sentence: "Do I keep looking for the ice cream? Is the problem the ice cream? Or is the issue that I keep looking for the ice cream even though it's not actually in there?" —Steph S
helpful_sentence: "What I found is that focusing on a more neutral meditation object (like the breath) and ignoring the chakras actually allowed them to start unblocking themselves... focusing strictly on the breath keeps the doer out of the way of the chakras." —George S
who_should_read: Jhana-vipassana hybrid practitioners curious how chakra-focused investigation and brahma vihara practice interact with nana cycling near the third-path threshold.

log: Practice Log of Anicca Dukkha Anatta
author: Anicca Dukkha Anatta
summary: An Indian householder practitioner logs a long, candid cycling through dukkha nanas marked by gory visions, energetic jolts, and grief, while forgiving childhood abuse and setting boundaries with family, gradually settling into an equanimity-flavored indifference.
interesting_sentence: "I am not coming back, have been pushed beyond point of no return." —Anicca Dukkha Anatta
helpful_sentence: "Equanimity should have a flavor of kindness, ease, and space. False equanimity is often actually aversion and indifference." —shargrol
who_should_read: Householder yogis balancing intense dukkha-nana cycling with family caregiving duties who want a detailed, non-mystified account of the process.

log: Small steps on a long path
author: Small Steps
summary: A martial artist logs a disciplined daily regimen combining wu ji standing qigong, walking, and choiceless-awareness sitting, tracking a gradual shift toward effortless, centerless open awareness supported by piti and the seven factors of awakening.
interesting_sentence: "It literally stopped me in my tracks, and after that moment it feels like getting to the center-less, open awareness hasn't really been difficult in most circumstances." —Small Steps
helpful_sentence: "Piti is like the excitement of receiving a present on Christmas day. Sukha is like the happiness of having and enjoying the present one has received." —Small Steps
who_should_read: Martial artists or movement-practice meditators wanting a model for integrating qigong standing practice with formal vipassana and open-awareness training.

author: Airo D
summary: A beginner Goenka-tradition practitioner logs early attempts to build access concentration on the breath, noting frequent distraction and a brief post-sit wave of piti-like vibration and calm.
interesting_sentence: "Meditation can destroy your ambition. I used to go to the Zen center to hear talks and for group meditation sessions... I thought they were crazy ... until I did it too." —Jim Smith
helpful_sentence: "I cannot stay focused on my breath longer than 30 seconds without being interrupted by distractions (mainly thoughts)." —Airo D
who_should_read: Absolute beginners to Goenka-style vipassana who want a relatable, unpolished account of struggling to build access concentration.

author: spatial
summary: Explores the distinction between craving and clinging via dependent origination in extended dialogue with forum members, while practicing breath-focused concentration, watching the felt sense of the doer, and investigating synchronized vs. unsynchronized vibrational awareness.
interesting_sentence: "This feels like how in Zelda II: The Adventure of Link, the final boss is Link's own shadow." —spatial
helpful_sentence: "Craving is that bit of tension you feel after a feeling of pleasantness, that contracted tense feeling." —Steph S
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in parsing the craving/clinging distinction through dependent origination and moment-to-moment self-observation.

author: Mind over easy
summary: Outlines a one-month intensive plan of three daily jhana-plus-vipassana sits to attempt stream entry in October, reporting reliable jhanas 1-3 (occasionally 4th) and A&P crossings within 5-10 minutes.
interesting_sentence: "I'm ready to see what all this talk of nirvana is about!" —Mind over easy
helpful_sentence: "Mindfulness at the senses is accessible, is always there, and can cause such amazing peace and concentration --- just doing regular ol' stuff." —katy steger
who_should_read: Practitioners planning a structured, intensive push for stream entry who want a concrete jhana-plus-vipassana daily schedule.

log: Practice log of Stuart Charles Law
author: Stuart Charles Law
summary: A 60-year-old, self-taught practitioner working through MCTB reports a persistent multi-hour soft-focus, 'day-dream'-like altered state resembling light-to-medium access concentration following three one-hour daily samadhi sits, trying to identify it as passaddhi.
interesting_sentence: "Hell's teeth, i've come a long way and most of it alone." —Stuart Charles Law
helpful_sentence: "It's been said that for every hour spent in samadhi , you can subtract needing an hour of sleep." —Ian And
who_should_read: Older, self-taught beginners curious about how sustained samadhi practice can produce lingering passaddhi/access-concentration-like states in daily life.

author: YCR
summary: A former TMI (Stage 6 concentration) practitioner turned noter chronicles cycling through repeated A&P experiences, dark-night-style psychological/POI conflation, vivid light-bulb-kasina visions, and one uncertain brief equanimity-like release, while debating whether to return to concentration practice.
interesting_sentence: "It's like this path taunts me with false cessations." —YCR
helpful_sentence: "The idea of progress of insight is when you have the complete knowledge of the nana, then you know how to accept the nana with equanimity and you move through the nana." —shargrol
who_should_read: TMI or noting practitioners who struggle to distinguish psychological turmoil from dark-night POI phenomena and want a candid, detailed account plus expert dark-night guidance.

author: spatial
summary: spatial practices a self-devised "thank you for X" noting technique while naming a flood of difficult emotions and sensations amid daily crying since Christmas.
interesting_sentence: "the notes that disappear before they can turn into words" —spatial
helpful_sentence: "That sounds hard. I hope it gets easier soon." —Martin
who_should_read: Practitioners exploring gratitude-based noting during emotionally overwhelming periods who want to see how personal life stress intersects with meditation.

author: Doctor Avocado
summary: Wing (Doctor Avocado), a neuroscientist doing 4-7 hour daily strong-determination vipassana, switches from Goenka body scanning to full noting, investigates breath/CO2 retraining to address chronic anxiety, and reports intense neck sensations and repeated cessation-type discontinuities without persistent aftereffects.
interesting_sentence: "It feels like some high-pressure energy has been contained and must burst through the walls of a dam, or maybe that some alien creature is about to hatch in my neck and break through the skin (please no)." —Doctor Avocado
helpful_sentence: "If you have pain as-it-is, there is no suffering. If you have pain plus attraction, or aversion, or ignoring -- then that is suffering." —shargrol
who_should_read: Intense long-sitting vipassana practitioners stuck oscillating between dark night and equanimity who want practical advice on balancing effort with ease.

author: Pjotr Hill
summary: Pjotr transitions from Goenka body scanning to Mahasi-style noting ahead of a retreat, reports strong bliss and a persistent third-eye sensation, and later wonders whether post-retreat anxiety and motivation dips signal dark night.
interesting_sentence: "I later realized that the sensations just wants to be experienced." —Pjotr Hill
helpful_sentence: "Try not to follow it and just do the practice." —Noting Monkey
who_should_read: Beginner-to-intermediate vipassana students preparing for a first Mahasi-style retreat who want a relatable account of technique-switching and post-retreat dark night uncertainty.

author: Jyet
summary: Jyet cycles through fire kasina, mantra, and Reggie Ray-style body-centered practices while processing deep shame stored in the solar plexus, reporting vivid kriyas, energy-body vibrations, and repeated AnP-like manic states across a multi-week log.
interesting_sentence: "my tongue stuck out by itself/kriya and i got rid of stuck energy with a hissing sound" —Jyet
helpful_sentence: "I guess you could explain it as cycling between the dukka nanas and AnP, or purification as I'm more used to refer to the cycles." —Jyet
who_should_read: Practitioners drawn to fire kasina and body-based trauma-processing techniques who want an unfiltered account of kundalini-type energy phenomena and shame work.

log: Yes Ma'am, It's my first practice log
author: Sax Ma'am
summary: Sax Ma'am works through The Mind Illuminated stages 2-4, alternating focus between the breath and a closed-eye visual field while repeatedly sitting with acute grief over a sibling's suicide alongside covid recovery and other life stress.
interesting_sentence: "my grief came up with the thought 'my brother shot himself'." —Sax Ma'am
helpful_sentence: "Every time you leave your focus on right now and then return, you're developing a more consistent mindfulness." —Chris M
who_should_read: Beginners working through TMI stages 2-4 who want a candid model of sitting with acute grief and distraction without abandoning formal practice.

author: supermonkey :)
summary: A nearly five-year practitioner cycles through re-observation, equanimity and fruition while doing intensive IFS-based shadow work on shame, restlessness and the I AM fetter, reporting jhana-access difficulties and repeated brief cessations.
interesting_sentence: "Like a tree gives birth to apples, Nirvana gives birth to Samsara." —supermonkey :)
helpful_sentence: "Meditation not going smoothly is often a sign that the meditator needs to address/investigate the cause." —Chris M
who_should_read: Practitioners blending IFS/shadow work with insight meditation who want a detailed account of shame, restlessness and jhana-access struggles post-stream-entry.

author: Sonchai Jitpleecheep
summary: A new 90-day daily-practice commitment opens with a detailed first sit exploring bodily sensations, aversion to pain and the illusory sense of a solid body, earning Daniel Ingram's assessment of Three-Characteristics/early A&P territory.
interesting_sentence: "There isn't really a 'body' in terms of direct experience; instead there are various 'pockets' of sensation that seem to flicker on and off." —Sonchai Jitpleecheep
helpful_sentence: "Sounds relatively 3 Characteristics to early A&P territory." —Daniel M Ingram
who_should_read: Beginning vipassana practitioners starting a structured daily commitment who want a model of clear, granular sensation-level noting.

author: yodayeti
summary: A self-directed 3-day metta retreat at a Thai forest monastery surfaces toxic shame and self-aversion while directing loving-kindness toward a girlfriend, with sleep deprivation and community friction limiting formal sitting.
interesting_sentence: "I ended up being happy about being capable to make myself happy." —yodayeti
helpful_sentence: "One of the main practise elements was conscious and intentional smiling which, in a very subtle way, increased my well being." —yodayeti
who_should_read: Practitioners interested in metta practice or a self-directed retreat account who want to see how loving-kindness work surfaces shame and relationship-based sadness.

author: spatial
summary: A meticulous, months-long solo vipassana and Goenka body-scanning log tracking posture struggles, jhana-adjacent absorption, and a slow deepening from dark-night fear into sustained equanimity, without reaching a cessation event in this installment.
interesting_sentence: "Help...I'm trapped inside a giant robot! I'm very small, and very fast. The robot is lumbering and awkward. I don't even know exactly how big I am. There's a force that makes me want to connect with the robot." —spatial
helpful_sentence: "Your job is to use as much effort as it takes to touch the peanut and still allow it to bob up and down unrestricted. Just enough presure to feel the texture of the peanut shell on your finger pad as it bobs around freely." —shargrol
who_should_read: Meditators refining posture and concentration technique who want an unusually granular, months-long account of early-to-mid insight cycling without a definitive attainment claim.

author: supermonkey
summary: A densely phenomenological, months-long noting-practice log cycling repeatedly through dark night and equanimity territory, reporting over a hundred spontaneous 'fruition' resets, kundalini-like energetic symptoms, and integration of IFS and trauma work alongside formal Mahasi-style noting.
interesting_sentence: "...you are not even in control of yourself! You are a horse yourself!" —supermonkey :)
helpful_sentence: "Only through directly experiencing the needless suffering that arises due to confusion and reactivity does the mind become unconfused and non-reactive. The way past the dukka nanas is by truly going through it." —shargrol
who_should_read: Practitioners doing sustained noting practice who want a granular, honest account of cycling through dark night, equanimity, and repeated cessation events en route to second path.

author: Steven E Barnes
summary: A brief two-post log describing insomnia and piti-like limb twitching during sits, and rebuilding driving confidence after a month of recurring panic attacks.
interesting_sentence: "I have anxiety about anxiety; a collapse in my self confidence." —Steven E Barnes
helpful_sentence: "Today, I did some driving, and shopping, reminding myself that I have done this thousands of times with no accidents or freakouts." —Steven E Barnes
who_should_read: Meditators dealing with panic attacks or meditation-related sleep disruption who want a candid, brief account of regaining confidence.
